Stephanie doesn't reply. The locksmith returns with the key and its copy, and she pays him. Out in the parking lot, she dons her sunglasses, gives you the copy of the key, and looks away.

"Look, it's not a club," she says. "You don't have to fill out an application. I'm not saying you're not a friend. I'm not saying anything. But we've got guy friends who aren't part of this, even the ones who know about it. Oh, and by the way, I really wish you wouldn't say anything about it."

"I won't. I mean, I haven't."

She grunts, scrapes the back of her hand across her nose. You tense as you wait for her to speak.

But when she speaks again, she just says, "I'll see you at Catherine's."

* * * * *

The Muskovs don't live in the toniest part of town, but they live in a very nice neighborhood in a large house with plenty of parking. That's good, because there are lots of cars on the street when you pull up.

The door is opened by two giggling girls who are leaning against each other to keep from falling over. They look familiar, but you can't quite place them, and they don't seem to recognize you. "Oh hey," the blonde one says. "Come on in."

"Is Katy here yet?" you ask. "Katy Conlee?"

"Yeah, she's upstairs. Oooh!" The blonde one seems to see you for the first time, and her eyes go very wide. She grins, and pinches her friend in the side. "She's upstairs," she tells you again over the other girl's squeals.

"Thanks." You glance around, and move toward the staircase while trying not to blush. It's hard, especially when the other girl bursts out in shrill laughter a few seconds after your back is turned.

There's a broad hallway at the top of the stairs, but it's not so broad that three people, standing in a knot, can't clog it. One is Fred Hildown, a tall, good-natured kid who like Catherine is on the track team. You don't know the other guy, but he's in shorts that show off thighs knotted with muscles, and he's got his deep chest thrown out, so even though he doesn't throw you to the floor and give you a wedgie, you're pretty sure he's a jock. Like Meghan Velasquez, who they're talking to, they're holding and drinking from plastic tumblers. Fred nods at you with a "Hey Will," and Meghan smiles brightly as you squeeze past them.

Girls' laughter floats out of a bedroom, and inside it you find Katy and half a dozen other girls, including Catherine Muskov herself. She's a tall girl with a mane of brunette hair that falls softly to her shoulders, and a calm, merry expression. She's in shorts and a tank top, showing off long, strong limbs—much like the limbs you dreamed about last night, you realize with a start. Just the kind of limbs you'd like to find embracing you.

But it's Katy who surprises you with a quick hug. She springs over from the windowsill and gives you a quick squeeze. "Hey, you found it," she says. "You know Catherine?" She turns to your hostess. "You know Will?"

"Hey Will," Catherine says. Her eyes glint in a friendly way. "You've been out before, right?"

"Uh, no, I don't think so."

"Why not?"

Your eyes flit around the room. You recognize most of the girls, but you never talk to them, let alone hang out with them, for they're of the athletic/popular sort. "I don't know," you say. "Just haven't."

"Catherine has parties all the time," one of the girls says. "Just start showing up."

"I don't have them all the time," Catherine protests. "But you got someplace I can text you?" she asks you, "for the next time I have one?"

You give her your contact info, then nervously add, "My friend Caleb's going to show up, if that's okay."

"Don't know why it wouldn't be," Catherine says.

"Come on," Katy says, "I'll take you downstairs, get you a snack or something." She squeezes your forearm briefly, sending shivers all over your body, and draws you out into the hall and past that blockade. At the head of the stairs she glances back, then snuggles close to you. "Is it on account of Meghan you asked your friend out here?"

"Uh huh." You gulp.

"That's great," she says, and squeezes your hand again.

* * * * *

And it seems pretty great for everyone as the afternoon progresses. Caleb arrives not long after you do, and you and he and Katy and Meghan wind up down in the living room with a mess of other people from school, sprawling on the floor and sofas while the TV shows a football game for the more jock-like guys (who are actually pretty friendly with you) while everyone else consults their cell phones and passes them around to share videos and memes and social posts. It's all tremendously casual, with girls draped across and over guys and guys leaning against girls. So it's nice—though you guess it doesn't mean much—when Katy props up against you and accepts your arm around her torso.

You draw her out in gossip, and because there are several other girls nearby who are part of her and Stephanie's group, you soon get an earful about Hannah and the girls who supports her. Despite Stephanie's chilly attitude, you privately jot down three names of girls who would be promising as "gaslighting" impersonations.

Tara Weston is another troublemaker on the soccer team, a rival of Anita Nuevo's, and she backs Hannah in everything Hannah puts her mind to. Anne Starkey is a long-time friend of Hannah's, and was one of the first girls to welcome her to Westside when she made the move. And Jenny Taylor, the captain of the Eastman girls' varsity soccer team, is another good friend of Hannah's.

You're chewing on this information when Stephanie arrives. Or maybe she's been here for awhile and you've just not noticed her, for she comes charging downstairs. She stops cold, her face very red, when she sees you. To your disquiet, she summons you over with a jerk of her chin.

"That thing you were talking about earlier," she mutters at you angrily when you're alone in the laundry room. "If you're gonna do it, do it as Catherine."

You now have these choices:
11113441411112213122221121312.title: Popular Settings

choices: Send a Catherine-lackey back to Stephanie's
Send a Will-lackey back to your place
Send a Will-lackey to your place, and you go out at Catherine
111134414111122131222211213124.content:
"What happened?" you ask Stephanie.

She glares past your shoulder into the hallway. "You can do whatever you want, Will. I'm just telling you that if you do something—"

She catches herself, and darts you a piercing look. "That thing you made. It obeys you, no matter whose, uh, mask you put on it?" she says. You nod. "So if you put a mask of Catherine on it, you could make it, um—"

"Yeah, if you want it to do something to Hannah, I can, uh—"

Stephanie nods. "Then you want to help? That's a way you can help." From the way her jaw works you can tell she's clenching her teeth.

She still hasn't explained why she is now so hot to let you help, and to use the lackey to do it, but you don't press her. But you do point out some of the complexities.

"Okay, I've got a mask made, readyto use, but I don't have it here," you tell her. "I'd have to go get it and come back. And putting it on someone is, well, kind of tricky."

"How so?" Stephanie's voice is very hard.

"Well, like I explained, you have to get it onto their face. Like, uh, this." You mime pushing a mask into Stephanie's face. "And then it knocks them out for about ten or fifteen minutes or so. Like, totally knocked out. They're breathing, but you can't wake them up."

"Can you get the mask off them, wake them up that way?"

"No, because it goes right inside them. Like, it completely vanishes. You have to wait for it to come out. And after that they're still asleep and it's another ten or fifteen minutes before they wake up. So you have to get them by themselves."

"How'd you do it with Coach Schell?"

You feel yourself blushing. "I found her in her office. I showed her the mask and I pretended like I found it. Then I just sort of, uh, shoved it at her. I snuck out afterward before she woke up."

You expect Stephanie to act shocked, but she only chews her lip. "And," you add, "if we're going to send the lackey out, we need some clothes."

"Okay, you can leave it to me. I'll get the mask done, get some clothes together. When I'm ready we'll meet up at my pop's store and get the thingie ready."

"It doesn't have to be Catherine," you tell her. "Katy and Meghan were talking about Hannah, and it seems like there's some other girls—"

Stephanie shakes her head. "Let's do Catherine."

"Uh, any reason in particular?"

"She just pissed me off, is all." Stephanie stalks out of the laundry room. A moment later, you follow.

* * * * *

It's closing on five o'clock, and some people are talking about ordering pizzas in. But one thing leads to another, and instead you and Caleb along with your ... girlfriends? is it possible? ... go out to eat at Cluck-for-a-Buck, a local KFC knockoff. There's another couple with you: Or, at least, they're as much of a couple as you and Katy or Caleb and Meghan are a couple, you suppose. Joel Wharton and Mandy Tiller aren't holding hands or embracing, but they seem pretty comfortable brushing up against each other.

Joel is that guy you saw in upstairs hall with Fred and Meghan. He's on the track team, though he looks more like a wrestler. He's bluff and gruff and has an intense gaze, and you can't shake the feeling that, if he wanted, he could kick your feet out from under you and pin your shoulders to the ground while holding (oh, say) a triple-scoop ice cream cone in each hand without dropping anything. But though he doesn't smile he seems friendly enough. After awhile you notice that he doesn't curse, not even a "heck" or a "darn." An explanation, perhaps, emerges over a greasy meal of fried chicken and fries when Katy casually reveals that his dad is the pastor at the Tenth Street Assembly of God.

Mandy is the not-blonde girl who opened the door to you when you arrived. She's a softball player, but other than being an athlete she's the opposite of Joel. She has long brunette hair; his blonde hair is closely cropped. She is small and bosomy, with a pronounced hourglass figure; he is burly. She's a smiler, a gusher, a giggler, and a chatterbox; he mostly confines himself to grunted answers. She wants to know all about you and Caleb; he only asks a handful of questions. But though Joel leaves the interrogation to Mandy, he listens with an intensity that suggests he's filing away not only every answer, but even your stammers and pauses. Between Mandy's talking and Joel's listening, you're soon exhausted.

"We gonna head over to my place after this?" Caleb asks you when the meal's over, and the girls—to your mild astonishment—have excused themselves en masse to the restroom. You stare at him until you remember that you're supposed to stay at his place tonight and ride in with him to school in the morning.

"We don't have to do that until later," you reply. "Don't you want to—" You glance at Joel, but he's occupied with his cell phone. "Hang out with Meghan a little longer?"

He shrugs. "You wanna hang out with Katy?"

"Actually, I'm supposed to meet up with Stephanie in a little while. Um, a school project we're coordinating on," you add as his eyebrows go up.

"Well, whatever," he says. You glance again at Joel. You're dying to know what Caleb is thinking of Meghan—she seemed to be hanging on his every word over dinner, and at Catherine's she treated him like an easy chair as they shared cell phones back and forth. But you don't want to say anything in front of him.

You're rescued from an awkward silence by the girls returning. Your company drifts into the parking lot, and when no one can come up with any compelling ideas for an activity, you break up into pairs, with you driving Katy back to her house.

"Mandy liked Caleb," she tells you when you're in the cab of your truck. "If he's going to ask her out on, you know, a date, he should do it pretty soon."

The near-wreck you suffer on the road—almost rear-ending the car in front of you—is nothing on the screeching, crashing, clattering collision inside your head. Thought one: Really? People are seriously thinking Caleb and Meghan as a couple? Thought two: Are you telling me this because you want me to tell Caleb? Thought three: Are you telling me this because you want me to ask you out on a date? You mumble something, and when Katy asks you to repeat it you say, "I can mention it to Caleb."

"It was fun today, right? Catherine has people over all the time. So, you know—" She squeezes your arm. "You don't have to be shy about dropping in."

"It's not my usual kind of crowd, you know?"

"Like how not?"

"All kinds of ball players." You glance over to find her beaming at you. "I'm not a jock."

"Don't be a goose, Will. No one cares."

Yet you have to wonder: If no one cares, why did it matter that Mandy liked Caleb?

* * * * *

Stephanie texts you just before you drop Katy off, and orders you to meet her at her house. She's waiting out front, and leans into the cab to press a bulky plastic bag into your hands.

"You're gonna have to hurry," she tells you. "I can't go with you, I'm stuck here. But tell the, uh, thing, when it's ready, to come out here. Oh wait, you're gonna have to drop it off, aren't you?"

"I guess. But I'm supposed to stay over with Caleb tonight, remember? And my twin was supposed to go home instead of me, and go in for school for me tomorrow?"

"Mm. I forgot about that." Stephanie rubs her nose.

The front door opens, and a skinny man in cut-off shorts steps out and calls her name in a sharp voice.

"Coming!" Stephanie yells back. "Look," she tells you, "I'll leave it up to you, okay? Put Catherine's mask on the thing and send it here, or put your mask on it and do whatever. But either way, you're gonna have to hurry. Text me when you decide." She pats the side of your truck and hustles back indoors. You stare after her until the front door shuts, then put your truck in reverse and back out into the street.

* * * * *

Argosy Furniture looks like it's about to close when you arrive: the showroom lights are dim, and the place feels deserted. But the times on the window tell you that you've got thirty minutes or so. You scurry through the back offices and into the hideout without meeting anyone.

From the bag you unpack a mask and a set of clothes: gym shorts, a tank top, flip-flops, and some underthings. The first thing you do is seal up the mask—the brain band is already attached to its inner surface—and study Catherine's name. All you have to do is put the mask onto the lackey, give it the clothes to put on, and you'll be on your way, and you'll have shown Stephanie that you're to be relied upon.

Or you can put your own mask on the lackey and have it drop you off near Caleb's house while it goes home for you and covers for you at school. That was your original plan.

But then a variation occurs to you: Send a copy of yourself to stay over with Caleb, and you put on Catherine's mask. That way you and Stephanie are both happy. Why didn't she think to propose that?

Oh, right: Probably because she wouldn't like the idea of you inside Catherine's body.

Which means that if you do the latter, you would have to pretend to be the lackey and not tell Stephanie who you really are.
111134414111122131222211213124.title: Doubles and Double Dates

1111344141111221312222112131243.content:
You heft the mask thoughtfully. If you put it on, it won't be the first time you've gone out in disguise. You spent an afternoon as Coach Schell, after all, and pretended to be her with other people, including Stephanie.

But this feels different. This is a schoolmate, and if Stephanie finds out what you've done ...

Ah, fuck it. Playing it dangerous with Stephanie just might be half the fun.

You strip off your clothes and, shivering hard against the chilly air and the cold concrete floor, you stretch out on the ground and drop the mask onto your face.

A heavy warmth spreads through your limbs.

* * * * *

You're woken by a horrible nausea, and you quickly roll over onto an elbow as your guts roll up into the back of your throat. You're shaking all over with cold, and your head spins. The fuck?

You brush your hair out of your face, but aside from that you hold very still until your guts have calmed down. But hard goose bumps race up and down your body. It feels like you're inside a freezer. That must be why you feel so sick. Ever since you were a little girl, the cold makes you want to throw up.

You cradle your stomach and think to yourself: Oh my God, did I just remember being a little girl?

Yes you did. Because you've now got tits.

Big ones, too. Great bags that hang down, aiming their rock-hard teats at the icy concrete floor.

You gulp, and push back the soft hair that has again fallen into your face. And when you think of what else you've got, those goose bumps harden and prickle all over.

You clamber to your feet and clasp your arms against the cold. Clothes. There are clothes around here, right? The light is gray and dim, and you have to squint to bring anything into focus. Shit. Your contacts. Catherine isn't blind, but she's pretty damn near-sighted. But you find the bag and pull out the clothes. They must be Stephanie's, because you don't recognize them. They're skimpy, too, just some running shorts, panties, and a t-shirt, and no protection against the chill. There's not even a bra—the hell?—and your breasts droop and rub uncomfortably against each other inside the shirt. The sooner you get out of here, the happier you'll be.

Not until you've dropped the "Will Prescott" mask onto the lackey do you stop to wonder how the thing will know to obey you—or if it will obey you at all.

Your duplicate does a double-take and scrambles up and backward onto his elbow when he sees you. "King Hell?" he says.

You put your finger—a long, slim thing with a curved, manicured nail—in his face. "Listen, I don't want any shit from you, okay? I'm you, got it? I mean, I'm Will Prescott. The real guy."

"Uh huh," he says, and looks you up and down with a hungry expression. "I don't know why you want to say that, babe, considering you look like—"

"I'm wearing a mask, okay? You know how those work."

"Sure, sure." He licks his lips.

"Don't make me take it off to prove it to you."

"Hey, neither of us wants that." He leans forward and strokes your arm, and you jump back with a little squeal. "Come on," he grins. "As long as you're looking like that, you and me could at least—"

"Why are you so disgusting?"

He sniggers. "Ask yourself that." Then he laughs. "Oh come on, boss. I'm just giving you shit. I knew it was you the moment I saw you."

"You did?"

"Sure." He shrugs. "Don't ask me how. I just knew. But, uh—" He stands up, and to your horror you see that his cock is fully rigid and blushing. "I'm not holding it against you."

* * * * *

As he gets dressed, you explain to him what's going on. He smacks his lips over your impersonation plot. "Wish that was something I could do," he says. "Think maybe we could try that out? We could, like, try putting that coach's mask on me to see if I can wear it."

"Maybe one of these days," you tell him as you lead him out of the empty space and lock the door behind you. "Right now, you just need to remember that, uh, you're supposed to be me and I'm supposed to be your lackey." You hand him the key.

"Oh, fuck me," he gasps. "No, seriously," he continues. "Fuck me. If I was your boss I'd—" He presses up close against you.

You push him back. "Pretend you've got scruples."

"What kind? You're gonna have to be more specific," he says, and puts his face into your hair.

You shove him back more firmly. "You don't want to take advantage of me," you tell him, and you hate the prim tone that has crept into your voice. "You're just going to drop me off at Stephanie's, like she asked, then drive out to Caleb's to spend the night. Oh, and you're going to go in to school tomorrow for me."

"You're as bad as Dad," he grumbles. "Go to school and don't have fun."

He tries to chatter on the drive out, but you make him be quiet as you try getting more firmly into character. I'm Catherine Muskov, you remind yourself, and that part of it isn't hard to keep straight. It's knowing—remembering—that you're a fake that is going to be the challenge.

So, you woke up in a dark, abandoned storefront next to Argosy Furniture, you tell yourself. You were cold and nauseous and confused. That much is easy to remember, as it it's the truth. But you were even more confused, you explain to yourself, because Will Prescott was leaning over you and leering.

No, you correct yourself. He wasn't leering. He was just looking at you. Which was kind of creepy, but—

But you knew who he was. That surprises you a little: Catherine Muskov knows who you are, and she knew who you were even before you showed up at her house. She even knows who Caleb and Keith are. What's so funny about knowing who people are? a little voice inside you says. It's not hard. Maybe not for Catherine, you reflect, even if she's got people coming and going through her house all the damn time.

You're caught short as you suddenly remember the afternoon from her point of view. You showed up uninvited, which felt funny to you, but it didn't seem at all funny to her, since her friends are always bringing random people over to her place to hang out. And it wasn't so random in your case, since Stephanie had told Catherine that you and Caleb would be coming over to hang out with Katy and Meghan, on account of—

Stephanie had shrugged at that point, which was just the point that Catherine got interested. "Katy and Will?" she asked. "Did you put them together?"

"No, I'm just keeping an eye on them." She wouldn't say any more than that, even after Catherine has pressed her. So Catherine had found Mandy at some point in the afternoon—

Fuck! Catherine's the one that got Mandy interested in you and Caleb, got her to follow you out to dinner, to talk to you, feel you out, figure out what kind of a guy you (and Caleb) were, to see if it looked like you and Katy (and Caleb and Meghan) would go good together. And the texts she sent back—

You feel for your cell phone, then grimace when you realize it will be with the real Catherine. It's like having your arm cut off.

"You okay over there?" Will asks, and you realize you've been groaning and clutching your head.

"I'm fine," you say, and straighten back up with a sigh. "Just thinking things through."

"Yeah? What's there to think about?"

You give him a sidelong look. "You and Katy." You can't help smiling a little as his cheek pales. "Go looking for her at her locker tomorrow," you order him, "and ask her to go out with you Friday night. Dinner and dancing."

He gasps. "I can't dance! You know that!"

"It doesn't matter. She won't care. And besides, on Friday it'll be me, not you, with her, looking stupid on a dance floor. Just make sure that you ask her out, and that she understands it's dinner—your treat—and a dance club after that. I'll set it up so that you'll meet some people out there."

"You will? How will you manage that?"

You're about to reply that you'll just text some people and set it up so you and them will be at Legends that night. Then you remember that you're not Catherine Muskov. "I'll talk to Stephanie about it," you say. "Speaking of which—" You reach over tug at the phone in his pocket, and he jumps. "I should tell her we're almost at her house."

* * * * *

She's standing out front when the truck pulls up, and your mood darkens at the sight of her. The mood between you wasn't happy when you last parted.

She doesn't look happy to see you either, and she brushes past you to lean in to talk to your doppelganger. "Everything go okay?" she asks him.

"Sure," he replies, and you cringe at how defensive he sounds, and at the way he curls up a little under her gaze. "Why wouldn't it?"

"Is she going to do what I tell her?"

Will looks between you and her. "Hey Catherine," he says. "Do whatever Stephanie here tells you."

"Whatever you say, boss," you reply, and give Stephanie your sweetest, most insincere smile.
1111344141111221312222112131243.title: The Fake Fake

choices: Keep things light and innocent: Are you a virgin?
Ask a probing question: Are you trying to break up Hannah and her boyfriend?
Maneuver her into a dare: Kissing or more.
11113441411112213122221121312431.content:
Stephanie pulls you into the house and hustles you through the living room. You just have time to say "Hi Craig" to her sixteen-year-old brother (a junior at Westside) who is sprawling on the living room couch before Stephanie is dragging you upstairs and into her bedroom. She slams the door shut and turns on you with a feverish light in her eyes. You smile placidly back at her.

"So how much did Prescott tell you?" she asks in a husky voice.

"About what?" you ask, and fold your arms across your chest. It's the first time you've done this, and you get a thrill at the way they bump against your bust.

"About what's going on," she says. "About—" Her voice turns huskier. "About what we're doing."

"You mean how you're practicing black magic?" you retort before you realize what you're saying. The color drains from Stephanie's face. "I assume that's what you'd call it," you continue, having given Catherine's instincts their head. "Jesus, Stephanie, after what you guys did to Hannah at the party Saturday—"

"That was just to freak her out."

"And that makes it better, what you're planning to do now?" You give Stephanie a disapproving smirk. "What did she do to you to deserve it?"

"She's a cunt," Stephanie mutters. She wipes her palms on the sides of her shorts and dodges your gaze. "She comes swaggering in like she's special shit and can just take whatever she wants."

Like Marc? you almost say, but catch yourself. That was probably the comment that made Stephanie mad enough to copy Catherine for this "prank."

Everything had been going great at the house—everyone was relaxed and hanging out, just the way Catherine liked it. Then she'd found Hannah's Tumblr post, with the photo of the garter snake she'd found in her bedroom. She showed the phone to Stephanie. Which one of you guys snuck it in there?

Stephanie flushed. We didn't do it, she said.

You sure? Seems like something you'd do.

Not your business, Catherine.

Then why'd you bring your business into my house? Catherine sniffed.

Because it's the only time we— Psh!

Catherine almost dropped it at that point—you can feel her regret that she didn't—but then she muttered, Marc's gonna kick your asses if you're not careful.

It was like putting a match to a Roman candle. It's not his business either! Stephanie had nearly shouted. If he'd kept his stupid self out of the tryout—

He's her boyfriend, Stephanie, Catherine snapped, then added, more in mischief than because she thought it was true, Is that what this is about? You're pissed he's going out with her instead of you?

Stephanie had turned white, from her temples to her throat, and stalked out, leaving Catherine to wonder if maybe Stephanie really was jealous of Hannah's relationship with Marc Garner.

"Look, I don't want to fight," you tell Stephanie now. "I'm not even here, I'm not even the real girl, you know."

As you say it, you put your hand out to stroke Stephanie's arm. She jerks away.

"What's wrong?" you ask. It's a stupid question, as you almost instantly realize. "Oh, it's because I'm not— I'm not real. Is that it?"

"This is just really weird," Stephanie says. She hops onto her bed, and she still won't look at you. "Let's just hang out till tomorrow. Then we'll do the thing, and then it'll be over with."

"Do what thing? Will only told me you want me to stir up some kind of trouble, but he didn't tell me what you want me to do."

Stephanie rubs her palms on her knees. "I don't know. I'll have to think about it. It'll have to be after school, I'll have to check and see, you know—" She shrugs. "What Hannah's doing and what's happening with her."

"So what am I supposed to do till then? Where am I supposed to sleep?" You know what you want: to sleep next to Stephanie. "What am I supposed to wear?" You also know the answer you want to that: You want to sleep in the nude next to Stephanie. "And jeez, Stephanie." You cradle your boobs. "Couldn't you send a brassiere along with Will?"

Stephanie starts, and rakes her eyes across your rack. "I didn't have anything big enough for you. But can't you, like, go in a corner and turn off or something?"

"I'm not a machine. I don't have an 'off' switch." You can't resist adding, with a giggle, "Just an 'on' switch. Michael knows where that is." That would be Michael Allen, Catherine's boyfriend.

"Jesus," Stephanie mutters. "You're disgusting."

"What's disgusting?" you cry out. "You've heard me talk like that before. Well, you've heard the real girl talk that way, I guess." You gasp at a sudden idea. "That's what we could do!" You leap onto the bed, and Stephanie shrinks back. "We could talk. Tell each other secrets." You giggle. "I could tell you all the things the real girl"—you crook your fingers around the phrase—"has never told you, and you could tell me—" You walk your fingers across the bedspread toward Stephanie's thigh, and she presses herself back, trying to get away from you. "All the things you never told Catherine."

"What the fuck would I do that for?"

"Because it wouldn't go any further than me. That's why you don't tell people things, right? 'Cos you're afraid they'll spread it around? That's why I keep some things back."

"Like what?" Despite her guarded tone, Stephanie looks piqued.

"Truth or Dare." you suggest. "Except I'll have to tell the truth. Will told me I have to do what you tell me anyway, so the dare part wouldn't have much of a point for me. But you—" You flick a fingertip across Stephanie's bare thigh, and she flinches.

"Nnh. So tell me something you haven't told me," Stephanie says. "Something Catherine hasn't told me."

"Pff. Fine. I will. But let's do it like Truth or Dare. We'll take turns. Okay? If it's going to be like a two-girl slumber party—"

"Tell me something."

"Blurgh. Okay." You fall onto your side and stretch your legs out—your long, strong, track-trained legs—and wiggle your toes. "Danny Russo's brother, Charlie? You know Danny Russo, right?"

"Lacrosse guy? Hyperactive?"

"That's him. But his brother, Charlie, I let him— Well, I guess you could say I let him tittyfuck me."

You shrug in the shocked silence that follows, and try to adjust your boobs, which without the support of a bra are dragging you down. When that doesn't work, you fall onto your back.

"When was this?" Stephanie finally asks.

"Oh, a couple of years back. It was before I moved here from Eastman. It was at a party, I had a couple of wine coolers and we wound up in a bedroom. It seemed like kind of a fun idea—"

You break off as you remember Charlie looming over you. He was shirtless, showing massive pecs and shoulders and biceps. It was early autumn, but he was in shorts and flip-flops (until he peeled them off) and squatted over you, pushing his sausage-like cock between your boobs. He'd cum pretty fast, and left you even more quickly, leaving you to clean yourself up.

"This was before you and Michael started—?"

"Oh, a long time before that. I never told him. I never told anyone. Except you, now."

Stephanie sucks in a deep breath.

"Okay, my turn," you say. You half smile as Stephanie visibly stiffens, and you let her twist as you try to figure out something to ask. You settle on asking her about yourself—it'll seem like an easy, innocent question while actually being something you're interested in. "Your friend Will, and Katy. Why are you trying to set them up?"

"'Cos Yumi asked me to."

"Is that the only reason? You and him have this 'magic' thing going between you."

"That's something different." Stephanie rubs her forehead. "I don't know how that happened. Will showed it to me. I think he was trying to impress me or something."

The words bubble out of you. "Why? Do you think he's interested in you?"

"God, I hope not."

You feel the smile fall off your face. "Why not?"

"Well, he's supposed to be interested in Katy."

"Is that all? He's not your type?"

She sighs and rolls her eyes. "He reminds me of Craig, okay. Only stupider. Anyway, I guess it's my turn now." She cocks her head. "Have you ever cheated on Michael?"

It takes you a moment to recover from the shock of that "stupider" comment. You cover it by stammering, "What counts as cheating?"

"Don't be smart. You know what counts."

"Well, there was something I did early on, before it was really clear if we were, you know, going to be exclusive or anything. That's why I ask what counts as cheating."

Her eyes narrow. "What did you do?"

"I sucked off Luke Richardson." That's one of Michael's own friends—they're on the baseball team together.

"This was at another party?" Stephanie asks. "Too many wine coolers?"

"No, totally sober, down by the river. It was just us, and we were talking about— He'd just broken up with Sophie Van Den Berg, and I wasn't sure if Michael and I were really a thing. So it's like we were trying to make each other feel good. My turn?"

Stephanie shrugs.

You're not sure where to take it. She might get suspicious if you continue to ask about herself. She might also get suspicious if you ask about Hannah.

But you could you get her to open up more? And if she backed off, if she called for a "dare", could you use that as a way to get at least a little bit physical with her?
11113441411112213122221121312431.title: Confidence and Confidants

choices: You're bored, not depraved. Leave the spell alone
111134414111122131222211213124314.content:
You lean in on Stephanie. "Who do you really have the hots for?"

It's an obvious question, so it's an innocent question, and so it's a question that Stephanie shouldn't freak out over. But it's one that could stir her up, get her excited; get her a little rowdy, maybe, and if you worked at it ... a little raunchy?

She rolls her eyes. "What are we, back in middle school all of a sudden?"

You giggle. "You play it so close, Stephanie. I have no idea who you like, or even what kind of guy you like. Isn't there anyone?"

She sighs. "Dare."

You groan. "Dares are no fun," you pout, "and I'm no good at coming up with any. At least, none we can do here when it's just us." Actually you can think of a dare that would involve just the two of you, but you judge it's too early for that kind of thing. "So I'll change my question. Is there anyone who gets you all, you know, twitchy?" You tickle her, and she jumps.

"Dare," she repeats.

"Oh God, there is, isn't there?" you exclaim. Stephanie, who is already pink, turns a deeper crimson. "Why don't you want anyone to know? But you can tell me. I can keep it a secret, remember?" You grin at her.

Stephanie falls onto her back and stares at the ceiling. She pinches the bridge of her nose. She takes a couple of deep breaths.

Then she says, "No. I can't say it out loud."

"Why not?" You suck in a sharp breath. "Is it a girl?"

Wow! If a look could scorch, the glare Stephanie shoots at you would have burnt your face off, mask and all! "No," she says.

"Oh, don't take it like that," you laugh. "I'm never more than about four wine coolers away from making out with a girl at a party."

Stephanie's eyes widen. "Seriously?"

You nod, and roll onto your stomach to give her a close stare. "Go on," you say. "Ask me." You grin. "That's my Dare to you. Ask me who I've made out with."

Stephanie's nostrils flare. "Who?" she asks in a croak.

"No one!" You loose a gale of laughter into the room. "Never had a chance! But I would," you add more earnestly. It's not a lie, not exactly, you're putting into Catherine's mouth. "There wouldn't be as much pressure, you know?" You curl onto your side and hug your knees to your chest. Your bare legs are warm and smooth, and you thrill inside and out as you hug them. "I figure it would be hot, but alos kind of relaxing, more like a cuddle. Don't you ever want to do that? Just to cuddle with someone? To hold them and stroke them?"

Stephanie's gaze goes distant. "Nnh. I guess when I think about it I go straight to the fun part."

You suppress a little gasp of excitement. "Well," you drawl, "I guess it could go on for a little more than just a cuddle. Depending on how fun it was." You titter. "Your turn."

But Stephanie says nothing, and continues to gaze past your shoulder. After a few minutes of silence, she stirs when someone downstairs calls her name. "Be right back," she tells you as she hops off the bed. But it's actually closer to a quarter hour, and her mood has cooled by the time she returns.

* * * * *

So that's the end of the game, and when she comes back she tells you to amuse yourself on her laptop while she checks her phone. She does join you, though, when she notices that you've logged in to Catherine's social media accounts, and she puts her arm around you as she leans in to run the cursor around the screen as she reads.

"Lookit," she says, "Catherine's setting up a thing with the track and baseball teams for after school tomorrow. She'll be busy with them, so you can get together with Hannah then, maybe."

"How will I do that?"

She looks at you. "How would Catherine set it up?"

It's a good question, and you mull it for a minute. Then you open up Snapchat.

Stephanie stops you. "Hang on, don't do it through Catherine's account. Do it through—" She wraps her arms around you and taps at the keyboard. "Here. This is a, uh, second account I keep. For special stuff."

As she types, you reach up to lightly clasp and stroke her arm, letting go only when she straightens up again.

"What should I tell her?" you ask.

"Just that you want to meet her. Tell her it's important, though, and, uh, kind of sensitive. Just you and her."

"Where can we meet?"

"Cherry Brook Bakery. It' just up the street from my pop's store."

"And what am I supposed to do when we're together."

"I'll tell you tomorrow. Just send her the invite now."

You shrug, and lean in toward the laptop camera. "Hey Hannah," you say, speaking quickly so as to squeeze the full message into the tiny video snippet. "I'm gonna be at Cherry Brook Bakery tomorrow after school, could you meet me there around four, four-thirty? Just us, 'cos it's kind of sensitive. And don't tell anyone. Really." You flash a brief frown. "We need to talk."

Stephanie grunts over it, and forces you to record it a few more times until she's satisfied that your message will draw out Hannah without alarming her and causing her to say anything to the real Catherine at school. Then you send it. Your co-conspirator is looking a little pale in the face when you stand up.

"It'll be okay," you tell her, and draw her into a light embrace. "I know I was kind of a bitch earlier about it all, but now I figure that if you're going to all this trouble to get back at her, you must a good reason." You stroke her back.

If you thought your words would reassure her, you were wrong, for Stephanie looks even more stricken when you release her.

* * * * *

When it's time for bed, Stephanie changes into pajamas. She's not shy about disrobing in front of you, and you have to pretend very hard not to be interested as her shirt and shorts and underthings come off. She has strong, smooth limbs, and her flat, taut stomach faintly shows the lines of her abdominal muscles. After she's changed, she hands you a set of flannel pajamas. You pull off the skimpy clothes you've been wearing, enjoying the feel of the cool air on your breasts and hips, but are slow to put the other things on.

"You know," you tell her as you unfold them, "I don't normally wear PJs when I sleep."

"What, you sleep in the nude?" Stephanie replies as she pulls back the sheets of her bed. "I sometimes do too."

"It's nice, isn't it? Just feeling the sheets against you. Do you mind if I, uh—?"

"Do whatever you want," she grunts as she slips between the sheets. "Come on, I have to get up early tomorrow."

So you slip into bed with her, with only her pajamas between you. She puts out the light, and for a long while you lay there listening to the sound of her breathing. Your heart works its way higher and higher into your throat.

"You know," you finally croak out, "when I was talking earlier about ... cuddling?"

A pause. "Uh huh?"

"Well, I'd kind of like it if— I mean, wouldn't it be fun if—"

You break off at the long sigh that follows. "The hell is wrong with you?" Stephanie mutters.

You wince, but persist. "Nothing. But I'm not real. You know I'm not. And—" You pluck at the side of Stephanie's pajamas. "It's like when we were doing Truth or Dare. We could tell each other things we'd never tell people, because it can't go any farther than us. Well, we could also—" You gulp. "We could also do things that—" You trail off as your words are swallowed into the oppressive silence.

"I don't want to do anything," Stephanie says. Her tone is rough and impatient. "I'm not into that. It doesn't have any appeal for me." Under her breath she adds something that sounds like, "Especially not with a sex doll."

"So you're exclusively interested in guys?"

"Yeah, pretty much."

Still you press: "And even though it's dark in here, you can't close your eyes and pretend that I'm a guy?"

"I'm only interested in one guy." Her voice is strained. "And it's pretty hard to pretend you're him."

It's a shot in a million, but you take it anyway: "It's not your friend Will, is it?"

She snorts. "Oh, Jesus. Just shut up and go to sleep. Remember, you have to do what I say."

* * * * *

Stephanie is up early the next morning for a jog. You'd go with her—a first for Will Prescott though not for Catherine Muskov—but you've no running shoes, so instead you take a very long and soapy shower with lots of time to rub and finger yourself. After she's back and showered, Stephanie gives you a fresh set of gym clothes and also an old iPod Touch that used to belong to her older brother (now off at college) so you can keep amused during the day. She drops you off at Argosy Furniture on her way to school, and you make your way back into the workroom.

It's not long before you're bored out of your skull. With nothing better to do, you pull out mask supplies and resume working on them. Six mind-crushingly dull hours later, you've completed and attached two more brain-bands to two more polished masks. Your only respite comes at around two, when Stephanie texts you with an alert: Her mother needs her to run errands after school, so "Will Prescott" will instead be meeting you at Argosy after your meeting with Hannah.

With nothing else to do after finishing those masks, you return to the grimoire and study the new spell—the one that calls for a person to be set on fire. You're so bored that you can't help thinking about it, and how you might execute it.

And who you might use when executing it.
111134414111122131222211213124314.title: Chances Taken

choices: Hold off and confess things to Stephanie
1111344141111221312222112131243141.content:
So the next spell uses a person as an ingredient. But it's not really asking for a human sacrifice. You're sure of that. Why would it? Probably it just wants to, you know, borrow some of the person's "life force" or something. Nothing bad would happen if you used, say, Hannah Westrick in the spell. Would it?

Of course, if that were true you could use yourself in the spell instead of someone else. But just because you're sure the spell is totally safe doesn't mean that you're stupid.

You kill the rest of the afternoon by prepping the ingredients. Ten minutes after school lets out, you shoot Hannah another little video, asking her to meet you at Argosy Furniture instead.

* * * * *

"Hey!" You give Hannah a wide smile and put your arms out for an embrace as she gets out of her car. Remarkably, she found a metered parking space on the street.

Hannah puts out her arms, and you pull her into a close hug. She's a big girl—though not in a bad way—and fills your arms. She is very warm, too.

Hannah Westrick is one of the stars of Westside girls' varsity soccer team. If you—that is to say, Catherine—were forced to confess, you'd say that she's the best player, and by a fair margin too. She's tall—almost six-foot—with strong thighs and calves and a build that manages to seem husky even while preserving an hourglass shape between her large bosom and her wide hips. You wouldn't want to see a girl that size charging across the field at you, whether the ball was between her feet or between yours. She wears her hair, which coils up in tight, frizzy curls, in a long, thick ponytail that trails almost to the small of her back. Her face is wide and flat and handsome. Maybe it's her size, and the mane-like shape of her hair, but there's something cat-like, even leonine, about her visage.

"So you got changed in a hurry," Hannah says now. She pulls back but continues to stroke your back while smiling into your face.

You shrug. "Well, you know, couldn't wait to get comfortable. So how was your day?" You tuck your arm into hers and hold her hand as you lead her into the store.

"God. Fuck. Excuse my language." She covers her mouth with her fingers as you almost run into a woman her small child just inside the front door. Into your ear she murmurs, "Would you talk to Dominique Hughes for me, please? I'd talk to her myself, but I can't even."

"Why, what's she doing now?"

Hannah snorts. "Look, that shit she pulled at the party was one thing. You know I'm not blaming you for that, right? Where are we going?" she interrupts herself, and looks around.

"A place in the back we can talk."

"What was wrong with the bakery?" She looks around, as though she's just noticed what kind of store she's in.

"There's more privacy here. You won't mind. It's kind of cozy."

But Hannah stiffens as you pull her along toward the back offices. "What did you want to talk about anyway?" she asks.

"That can wait 'til whenever." You squeeze her hand. "What was Dominique doing, like, today?"

"Oh, well, you know that headscarf she wore to your party?"

"The red one? Oh my God!" The words come blurting out before you can stop them. "If I thought I could pull off a look like that!"

"I know, right?" Hannah squeezes your hand back. "That's only one of the reasons I friggin' hate her, because she can do that whole exotic gypsy thing—"

"Did you notice she wasn't even wearing earrings the other night?" Still Catherine's thoughts come tumbling out of you. It's easier to give in to her excitement than to stifle and second-guess them.

"What about her earrings?"

"She wasn't wearing any. At the party. But didn't it seem like to you she was?" You cluck your tongue enviously. "Big, gold, hoop earrings, to go with the headscarf. You know, like a gypsy."

"I didn't notice, but why are you making a deal about—? Was she wearing earrings or wasn't she?"

You roll your eyes. "She wasn't, but it totally seemed like she was, right? It didn't seem like that to you? It did to me. I mean, that's how perfect that headscarf was for her, all by itself it gave her that totally exotic look like you were saying."

"I that's why I hate her. She doesn't have to try to look great. I guess it's that Mexican-Indian look she's got," she adds with a cluck of her tongue.

"She is half Hopi," you remind her.

"Well, here's half hoping she dies in a fire."

"Hannah!"

"I'm sorry. No, I take it back, I'm not. What is this?" Hannah asks as you unlock and open the door to the abandoned store front.

"It's the place. But what were you telling me about Dominique? What did she do today?"

"Oh, nothing. Just irritated the hell out of me." Hannah wrinkles her nose and clucks distastefully as you usher her into the dim, dusty room. "She wore that headscarf to school today, just to spite me, I think. I don't remember ever seeing her in it before, but today it was like every time I turned around I saw her out of the corner of my—" She trails off. "The hell, Catherine? Why'd you wanna come here instead of—?"

"Because I wanted to show you this." You pick up one of the masks. "I didn't want to take a chance on losing it in public." You hand it to her.

"What is it?"

"It's a mask, silly. A thing for Halloween. It needs some eyeholes punched into it, but otherwise it's, you know."

Hannah turns it over a couple of times. "Okay, it's pretty cool, I guess. But come on, let's get out of—"

"Try it on," you urge her. "I got it thinking it would suit your looks," you continue as she looks dubious. "With your hair—"

You press up close and put your arms around her to pull at her ponytail, loosening it and pulling the strands free. You're close enough that you can smell her breath—cinnamon. Your lips twitch into a smile. You weren't lying last night when, as Catherine, you confessed to Stephanie that you were only ever a few drinks away from making out with a girl. You'd love to make out with Hannah. To open your mouth and take hers in yours ...

You let go of her but you don't step back. "Try it on," you say. "Just put it up to your face." You grasp her wrists, and she only lightly resists as you push her hands to her face. "We'll take a picture and you'll see." You titter. "Maybe you'll look spooky enough we can send it around to freak out Dominique."

That turns the trick. Hannah lifts the mask to her face. It only touches it before it vanishes, and she sinks to the ground, taking you with her.

* * * * *

You had two good reasons for forcing a blank mask onto Hannah. One was that you wanted to knock her out so you could perform that spell on her. Probably you couldn't have talked her into it! And Stephanie would probably dig having a copy of her nemesis—a bonus on the kind of trouble she wanted you to do anyway.

But now you're really glad to have a copy of Hannah on hand. It turns out that spell was a lot hinkier than you were counting on.

So while she was knocked out, you lofted her onto one of the tables, pulled her clothes off her—and found she's got a bush down below that almost's as much of a jungle as the one atop her head—and dumped the rest of the ingredients on her. You almost backed out at the last moment when you went to set a flame to her, for you remembered how long it took to "cook" the lackey, and it would be bad—for lots of reasons!—if Hannah Westrick was spread out, on fire, for the next week in the empty storefront next to Argosy Furniture. But then you reminded yourself that surely this new spell wasn't going to be a duplicate of the previous spell.

Well, the good news is that Hannah wasn't on fire for more than half a minute, if even that. The bad news is that ... the new spell is pretty much otherwise a duplicate of the spell that made the lackey.

The difference is that it has turned Hannah into a lackey.

At least, that's what it looks like to you. You are cold with horror all over as you study her.

She is the same color as the lackey, for a start—an off-white, like a water-stained marble statue. She is also stiff all over, though maybe her flesh yields just the tiniest little bit when you push a fingertip into her. But from the tips of her toenails to the tips of her hair, she really does look like she's been transformed into a statue.

So it really is good that you've got a copy of her in the mask that you lifted off her face just before you put the match to her. All you have to do is put on the mask, put on the clothes, and Hannah Westrick can go home without anyone knowing anything happened to her. Or you can get your own lackey out here, put Hannah's mask on it and resume your own life. That would be quickest and easiest. And as for what to do with this thing you've made ...

You jump as the iPod touch chimes. It's a text from Stephanie: on my way twshop wehn u redy meet me thre.

Shit!
1111344141111221312222112131243141.title: Getting Hinky with Hannah

choices: Assume Catherine's identity, and dodge Stephanie
Put on Catherine's mask, and pretend to be the lackey with Stephanie
Put Catherine's mask on the lackey, and meet with Stephanie
1111344141111221312222112131243142.content:
So the next spell uses a person as an ingredient. But it's not really asking for a human sacrifice. You're sure of that, at least. Why would it? Probably it just wants to, you know, borrow some of their "life force" or something. Nothing bad would happen if you used, say, Catherine Muskov in the spell.

Of course, if that were true you could use yourself in the spell instead of someone else. But just because you're sure the spell is totally safe doesn't mean that you're stupid.

As you put the ingredients together, you try to figure out a way to get Catherine out to Argosy alone. The trouble is that Catherine hates to be by herself—she doesn't even like driving anywhere without at least one person in the car with her, and she prefers to be with two or three.

Fortunately, you're wearing her mask as you work, so you're able to use her own brain to fashion a plan that would appeal to her.

* * * * *

hey this is will prescott, you text Catherine shortly after school will have let out, using the iPod touch that Stephanie left you. heard theres a thing happening after school? Normally you'd never dare send even this kind of disguised self-invite to a girl you barely know, but at the moment you're not feeling very normal. (You smile to yourself as you brush a long, dangling lock of soft brown hair out of your eyes.)

Two minutes later: Hey Will that's right, meeting at Stewarts Hole. Woohoo. U should come.

will katy b thr?

Could be. Ask her. A smiley emoji follows.

You already shot Katy a text asking to meet her—and then texted your doppelganger to get the hell out of school without bumping into her—but you don't tell Catherine that. Xlent will do.

Then, after sending further instructions to your lackey, you stretch out on the table and remove Catherine's mask ...

* * * * *

... so that ten minutes pass in what feels like ten seconds, which is better than killing the time in any other way. You text Catherine with one hand while with the other you peel off the shorts and girl's underwear that Stephanie gave you for the Catherine disguise: hey this is will again im stuck at argosy furniture could u cm pick me up?

After another delay: Sure, Michael and I can pick you up.

Ten minutes later—and dressed in the sloppy t-shirt, shorts and flip-flops that Stephanie lent to fake-Catherine—you're loitering in front of Argosy and anxiously watching for Catherine. There's a white pick-up truck parked across the street, and you scowl at the straw-headed kid who pokes his head up long enough to look around. Your doppelganger returns your scowl with an identical one of his own, and ducks back down again.

An SUV finally pulls up, double-parking with the motor running in front of Argosy. The passenger-side window goes down and Catherine smiles out at you. "Hey Will, get in."

But you stick your head through the window to address Michael Allen instead. "Hey man, can I borrow your girlfriend for a few minutes? Promise to give her back."

"For what?" he asks. He's not a muscle-bound jock like most of the bullies at school, but he's got a broad, deep chest and meaty forearms, a product of working out for the school baseball team. Somehow his scruffy blonde hair, which he keeps jammed under a ball cap not unlike the one you affect, gives him a manlier look than you get from a similar 'do.

"I got my truck working so I don't need a ride," you explain, "and I'm s'posed to pick up Katy. But, uh—" You turn puppy-dog eyes on Catherine. "Can I give you a ride out there too? Out to Katy's first, then out to the park?"

A little light goes off behind Catherine's eyes—just as you knew it would. "Okay," she says, and turns to Michael. "Meet you out there in a few. Don't let— Mmph!" Whatever she was going to say is swallowed by Michael as he jumps in to kiss her. She murmurs something at him after they break off, then slips out of the SUV. You pretend not to notice the quick, hard glance that he gives you before driving off.

"So what are you doing here?" Catherine asks as she follows you. "And where's your truck?"

"Parking lot back of the store," you tell her. "I was doing a favor for Stephanie."

"I don't remember seeing you around Stephanie much before," she observes. "Or Katy either."

You grunt, and keep your answers brief and clipped as you hurry through the store. Catherine's doing exactly what you hoped—she's nipped at the prospect of talking pritavely to you about Katy, and your plans for and about her—but you're suddenly very nervous about what you're doing and what you're going to do, and if it weren't too late to change your story, you might back out. You were a lot braver while you were wearing Catherine's mask, and before you saw her boyfriend flex his fingers around the steering wheel when you asked to "borrow" her.

Also, as you hurry through the cold furniture store, you're very conscious of just how skimpy your clothes are. Yes, Catherine, in her midriff-baring sports tee and hip-hugging jogging shorts, is just as underdressed as you, but it looks a lot better on her.

Not until you're fumbling at the door to the empty storefront does Catherine ask again where you're going and what you're doing. "It's the thing I have to pick up for Stephanie," you explain. Your voice quavers; you hope Catherine puts it down to the chill in the air. "Some furniture. Oh, hey." You pretend to have surprised yourself as you pick up the mask you carefully set out on a table just inside the doorway. "There's this thing here I want to show you too." You raise the mask up, say "Oops" in a voice that is a trifle too loud, and fall toward her. It splats right into her face.

* * * * *

So, slamming that mask into Catherine's face turns out to have been a really good idea!

You assumed it would be—that's why you did it. But your first and only idea was that you wanted to knock her out so you could try the new spell on her. It's not like you could have talked her into laying on a table and letting you sprinkle chemicals over her before setting her on fire!

But it was a bit of a gamble on its own. It wasn't one of the new masks that you put onto her, but the mask you've been wearing all day. You weren't sure what would happen if you put it back on her, but it did knock her out long for you to get her prepped for the spell.

But now you've another reason you're glad you copied Catherine into a mask. It turns out that spell was a lot hinkier than you were counting on.

So while she was knocked out, you lofted her onto one of the tables, pulled her clothes off her, and dumped the rest of the ingredients over her. You almost backed out at the last moment when you went to set a flame to her, for you remembered how long it took to "cook" the lackey, and it would be bad—for lots of reasons!—if Catherine Muskov was spread out and on fire for the next week in the empty storefront next to Argosy Furniture. But then you reminded yourself that surely this new spell wasn't going to be a duplicate of the previous spell.

Well, the good news is that Catherine wasn't on fire for more than half a minute, if even that. The bad news is that ... the new spell is pretty much otherwise a duplicate of the spell that made the lackey.

The difference is that it has turned Catherine into a lackey.

At least, that's what it looks like to you. You are cold with horror all over as you study her.

She is the same color as the lackey, for a start—an off-white, like a water-stained marble statue. She is also stiff all over, though maybe her flesh yields just the tiniest little bit when you push a fingertip into her. But from the tips of her toenails to the tips of her hair, she really does look like she's been transformed into a statue.

So it really is good that you've got a copy of her in the mask that you lifted off her face just before you put the match to her. You've got a lackey waiting outside right now. All you have to do is get it in here, strip your mask off it, and put Catherine's onto it.

Before you can pick it up, though, the iPod touch chimes. It's a text from Stephanie: on my way twshop wehn u redy meet me thre.

You cuss under your breath. Stephanie is counting on meeting the lackey-fake of Catherine, to ask about that meeting with Hannah.

Shit! You completely forgot about that thing. You look at the time and see that it's too late to meet with Hannah now.

And you've got the petrified Catherine Muskov to get rid off.
1111344141111221312222112131243142.title: Making a Mess with a Muskov

choices: Send the lackey out to replace Catherine
11113441411112213122221121312431422.content:
You can worry about Catherine later. Right now you need to keep this mess under wraps from Stephanie. You throw yourself onto the floor next to what's left of Catherine and jam the mask onto your face.

* * * * *

Your head is aching when you awake, and you're sick to your stomach with the cold. You glance around the dark room, wondering how you got on the floor and where Will Prescott scampered off to, but the confusion lasts only a moment. You rub your face and haul yourself to your feet. You squint at the blurry world and frown.

Then you think: My contacts.

You make a face, and gingerly touch and pluck at the surface of your eyeball. Catherine's used to it, thank God; you yourself have never touched an eyeball directly, neither your own nor someone else's. But it takes you only a few seconds to grimly conclude that Catherine's contacts did not transfer into your own eyes when you put the mask on. So where are they?

Your gaze falls upon the petrified Catherine Muskov. You bend over her face and peer into her eyes.

Something glints there, something you would have missed if you hadn't looked very closely.

Carefully, holding your breath, you pluck her lenses out of her petrified eyes. They are still pliable, but too stiff to put into your eyes. She didn't bring her book bag in with her, so you don't have any contact solution.

So you go looking for a restroom and a sink, which you find must off the Argosy back offices. You're rubbing your eyes, lubricating the water-wetted lenses with your own tears, when a voice behind you calls your name: "Catherine?"

You shriek, jump, and whirl. It's Stephanie, standing in the doorways. "Jesus," she says. "Calm down. Where's Will?"

You are breathing very heavily as you grip the sink behind you, and your heart is beating hard. In a whirl you wonder, Does Stephanie think I'm the real Catherine? Does she know that I called her out here? Does she actually know it's me, and what I did to her friend?

Stephanie frowns and snaps her fingers in your face. "Hey," she says. "You okay? You meet with Hannah yet?"

Hannah? Oh God! You let out a deep breath. She thinks you're the lackey!

"Oh, um, no, we weren't able to hook up," you say in a trembling voice. "We missed each other or something."

"Fuck." Stephanie puts her hands on her hips. "We can't try it again tomorrow," she mutters.

"We can't?" you gasp. "Maybe we could?"

"I don't know where to put you tonight. Shit." Stephanie sighs and falls silent.

You can't find anything to say, so you jog from foot to foot until she looks up again. "Where's Will?" she asks.

"Um, I think he's out looking for a parking space," you improvise. "He dropped me off, sent me in here to wait for you."

"Well, let's go put you away. We can't have you running around in case someone sees you." She gestures for you to follow.

To your horror, you realize she's leading you back to the abandoned storefront, where the real Catherine is splayed out on a table.

Five steps, ten steps, fifteen steps ... Your mind is a whirl-a-gig of terror as Stephanie advances on the door of doom. Words try to form in your throat, but you can't force them past a tongue that has gone thick and parched. Stephanie grasps the metal knob; a hoarse shout dies in your throat ...

The knob rattles but refuses to turn. Stephanie jerks at it again, then grunts. "Got the key?" she asks.

You almost faint, but retain enough presence of mind to pat at your pockets. "No," you lie. "Will forgot to give it to me."

"Never mind, I've got my own copy." Stephanie feels at her own pockets, then frowns and mutters. "In my bag," she says, and brushes past you back toward the furniture shop.

You grab her by the arm. "Wait, I'll go find Will. We need to get together anyway, right? Be right back." Before she can argue, you sprint off back the way you came.

At first you think that your doppelganger must have run off, when you don't see him behind the wheel of the truck. But it turns out that he's just ducked down in the cab, playing a mobile game on your phone, and he jumps as you throw yourself into the cab of the truck. "Hey babe," he smirks. "Got your thing done? 'Cos if you don't need me now, Caleb is talking about—"

"I need your clothes," you snap, "and your face, too. My face, I mean. Take your clothes off!" you order as he gapes at you. He jerks into motion as you crouch in the passenger-side foot well and pull off your top and bottoms. You look up when your double makes a weird groan, and find him ogling you hard. His cock is rising like the spar of sailing ship as he twists himself out of his pants. He squeaks as you loft yourself onto him, pressing your boobs against his chest, and he goes stiff all over as you grasp his face, mutter some arcane words, and pull.

When you settle back, there's a blob-like slab of rock bent between the driver's bench and the steering wheel. Your eye instinctively falls to the "crotch" of the thing, and are relieved to see that there's no bone-like spur sticking out of the thing's lap.

You settle back into the foot well, and pull at your own face.

* * * * *

You're woken by an insistent ringtone, and raise a muzzy head to look around. The world is a blur, and your eyes hurt as though they've been scratched and gouged. You rub at them with the heel of your hand, but that only makes it worse. You fumble at the lumpy clothes on the truck bench until you find your phone, wrapped in a shirt. You have to hold it out very far from your face before you can begin to make out the text on the screen: meet me n stor w kee, it seems to say.

Fuck. You'd much rather rack out in the truck for a few more minutes of sleep—maybe your eyes are hurting because the phone woke you up—but you hustle into your own clothes and hop out of the truck. You squint against the sunlight, and an unexpected car honks at you as you dash across the street.

"Will!" a voice shouts, and you spin. A blurry figure trots up to you, and she has to get quite close before you recognize Stephanie. "Didn't you see me?"

"Huh? Oh. Guess I had, uh—" You look around. Everything is a smeary blur, and you hope the blobs that are moving are people and not furniture. It feels like you've got something stuck in your eyes—

Oh yeah.

"Hey," Stephanie says, and she shakes you. "Pay attention."

"What?" you snap. You desperately want to get back into that bathroom so you can take Catherine's contacts out.

"I said, Where's Catherine?"

"Oh. I, uh, pulled the mask off the thing, left her— Uh, it, out in the truck."

"Well, put it back on her. It. We need to put it back in the storeroom. The place."

"I'll take care of it. Listen, just hang on a minute, I need to use the restroom." Without waiting for her to argue, you run off, caroming off the corners of sofas and tables as you stumble blindly into the back offices.

And after you're back out again, you find that Stephanie has vanished on you. With your heart in your throat you check the door to the abandoned store, but it's locked. Your relief is short-lived, though, when you return to the front of the furniture store and find Stephanie leading ... Catherine? ... back in through the front door.

"You got the key?" Stephanie asks you.

"The what?" you reply, trying not to swallow your own tongue.

"The key, Will, to the place." She rubs her nose and frowns. "Catherine went back to get it from you."

"Oh." You pat your pockets, which are empty. "No, they must have fallen out in the truck," you truthfully reply.

Stephanie rolls her eyes hard. "Fine. Just wait here, I'll go get my set from my bag." She turns on her heel.

You give the lackey a sidelong glance. It looks just like Catherine, and it's dressed in those same cast-off clothes that Stephanie gave to you, and if that's not enough to settle its identity, there's a greenish tinge behind the ghastly smile she turns on you. "What's going on?" she says. "I feel like I should be down at Stewart's Hole with all my friends, but I also feel like I should be—"

"Listen, you have a set of keys on you?" you ask. She pats her pockets, and pulls out the key to the abandoned storefront. "Trade you." You give her her contacts and take the key. "Go put your lenses in," you say as you push her toward the back. "Close the door and lock it and don't come out until I tell you." Your heart is racing, but you think you finally see a way out of this farce.

You're loitering out front when Stephanie strides back in with a very determined expression on her face. "Oh," you tell her with studied casualness. "I found my key after you left and I put the lackey back. We're all good."

Stephanie grunts. "Except she didn't get to talk to Hannah."

"Well, that was a cock up," you agree. "You want to try again tomorrow?"

"I think we need to just give up on it. No offense, Will, but this magic shit you found is more trouble than it's worth."

You're actually inclined to agree, but you keep quiet.
11113441411112213122221121312431422.title: The Key to It All

1112.content:
Caleb frowns as he studies the book, trying to turn its pages while fingering the mask. "Pretty freaking weird," he says for the fifth or sixth time that afternoon.

"I'm not making it up."

"I'm not saying you are. I mean, here's this thing," he says of the mask, "and a pretty damn good likeness it is too."

Actually, it's just a generic face, with a brow and nose and cheekbones and chin and the faint impression of a mouth. Still, it is far more subtle than anything you could have made on your own.

"The thing is," you say, "it's not good for anything until it's been polished, and I keep rubbing it and nothing happens."

"What, like Aladdin's Lamp?" Caleb asks sarcastically. "Maybe it's like getting scratches out of plexiglass. You have to rub it forever before it's smooth."

"Fuck, I haven't got time to work at it that hard."

"Whoever said magic would be easy? Look at computers and how much programming goes into them."

"Are you volunteering to polish it until it finally works?"

"Nah, let's make another one." He starts pulling out the ingredients you'd shown him. "I wanna see this magic stuff in action."

"Hang on," you sigh. "I got in trouble when I made this one. It makes a horrible smell." You think a moment. "Let's do it at the clubhouse."

Caleb blinks, then grins.

The "clubhouse" is actually the basement of an abandoned elementary school a few blocks from your house. Two years ago you found an outside door leading down into a basement storeroom. No one seems to go in there—though parts of the old school are now used as a community center—and you padlocked the door with a lock of your own. You'd shown it to your friends Caleb and Keith, and last Halloween you guys even hung out down there, telling ghost stories and drinking cheap liquor. It's been many months since you've been back, but it would be the perfect place to experiment.

Quickly you gather the supplies and take them out to Caleb's car. Your dad's face shows relief as you pull out of the driveway.

The basement is undisturbed, and Caleb watches as you set up tables and fans under the windows that peek out just above ground, but he stops you before you can begin. "Lemme try," he says. "I wanna see if this magic stuff only works for the guy who owns the book."

So under your guidance he mixes the ash and ice and other whatnot; he leaps back as it smokes and pops; you hold your noses and gag at the stench. Caleb dumps the mix over the upturned mirror and puts the match to it; he picks up the shell after the flames wink out, and it twists in his hand just as yours had.

He's breathing heavily. "I said I believed you weren't making it up. But actually I kind of didn't." He gulps. "But now I really do."

To be honest, part of you regrets he can also do the spells. But mostly you are relieved to have someone to share it with—and to help you decide what to do with it.

* * * * *

"You know, if these things work, we could pull some awesome pranks," Caleb says.

It's Wednesday, and you're sitting in the elementary school's semi-abandoned playground, each with a mask, polishing away. You'd discovered that intense rubbing at a single spot would buff a mask from a dull gray to a brilliant blue, so you and Caleb have gotten together each day after school to talk, horse around, watch a movie, or otherwise waste time while finishing off the masks. It's tedious work, but when you are done you will have two masks, doubling the amount of fun you can have.

"Except the book's really vague about how they work," you remind him. "I mean, how do we copy people into them?"

"I assume they're like a mold," Caleb says carelessly. "Like, you get up close to someone—" He mimes shoving his mask into your face.

"That's another thing," you reply. "How do we get close to the people?"

"Depends on who it is. Got any ideas?"

Well, there's Geoff Mansfield for a start; he's been hanging around Lisa lately and it would be nice to ruin his chances with her. There's also lots of guys at school you could pretend to be, so as to get close to their girlfriends: Marc Garner, Ricky Golia, Ethan Nieves—

"What about Jeremy?" says Caleb.

"Richards? Why?"

"Because he's a dick."

Well, there is that. Jeremy Richards was one of your friends in middle school, until he hit a growth spurt and tried out for the basketball squad. Now he hangs out with the jocks. He doesn't jeer at you and his other former friends, exactly, but he treats you like you're not cool enough for his company any more.

"And we could use him to get close to some other guys," Caleb continues, warming to his idea. "Like, clone yourself as Richards, then get close to Javits and clone him." He leans back with a grin. "Then call Cindy. So hey, wanna come over and suck my cock tonight?" he says, dropping his voice to imitate the basketball player's.

"Oh, God." You close your eyes and put your head back. The thought of that bosomy, willowy cheerleader slurping all over you, even if it's because she thinks you're her boyfriend ... "But how do we get the original people out of the way while—?"

Caleb shrugs. "We'll think of that when the time comes."

* * * * *

The time comes, as far as you're concerned, the next day, when you stumble onto Geoff and Lisa outside the cafeteria. You instantly take in the scene: Her, gazing into his face with the kind of rapt expression you wish she had looked at you with; him, gazing back with a cool half smile. "Guys," you mutter, and try to dodge past.

"Hi Will. Have a good weekend?" Lisa asks with careless grace.

"It was four days ago, I don't remember," you growl.

"What a busy life you must lead," Geoff says with light sarcasm.

The hair on the back of your head goes up. "What're you doing next weekend?" you ask Lisa; you'll be damned if you're going to let Geoff just scoop her up. "Wanna see that new CGI movie with me?"

"You don't want to see a cartoon, do you?" Geoff sneers.

"I'm really not into those," Lisa says, and your jaw clenches.

"How about golf?" Geoff suggests to her. "My uncle has a membership at the country club."

"Can Will come along?" she asks.

"I can only bring one guest."

"That's okay, I don't know anything about golf," you blurt out, then mentally kick yourself hard.

"We could all go see a movie on Sunday," Lisa suggests brightly. "That'd leave Saturday for something else." She smiles up at Geoff.

"We'll give you a call," Geoff tells you. He starts to leave, then pauses to look expectantly at Lisa. She follows him without even saying "Bye" to you.

* * * * *

You're at the clubhouse furiously buffing a mask when Caleb catches up to you. "Got an idea for who you wanna use it on, huh?" he says with gentle sarcasm. You tell him of your encounter with Lisa and Geoff. "Jesus, Will, this is bigger than them. You need to expand your thinking. What's a little revenge—?"

"It's not revenge! It's about saving her from him! The guy's a jerk and she's a— a fool if she can't see that!"

He laughs. "So you're gonna clone yourself as Geoff and act like a really big jerk, so she'll understand that he's a big jerk."

"Yeah!"

"Oh, Christ."

"Look, don't try giving me advice! It's not like you've been a lot of help.!"

"Shut up." His tone catches your attention, and you look up to find him glowering at you. "I've been on your side, you know, so don't give me any shit."

His tone suggests there is more here than just anger at the way you snapped at him. "What do you mean?"

He looks at you steadily, and when he speaks he very calm, with none of the stuttering tone he's used before when discussing Lisa.

"She was never into you," he says matter-of-factly. "She just thought of you as someone to hang out with over the summer. But now school's back, and it's her senior year, and she's looking for a real boyfriend."

"Well, if that's your opinion—" you say, face flushing.

"It's not my opinion. Lisa won't tell me anything, but I've talked to Eva—you know, her best friend—and that's what she says."

"Well, Eva is a cunt."

"Maybe, but that's what she says Lisa told her."

You and Caleb stare at each other without blinking. When you look away, it's because you have to blink back hot tears of rage and humiliation.

Caleb leans forward. "Look, dude. Lisa's not worth it. I know she's pretty and she's usually nice. But there's something missing inside her. It's weird, but she'd be nicer if she were an actual bitch, like Kelsey and them. But she's not really that different."

You toss the mask away. It suddenly seems pointless. If Lisa really is that kind of girl, then who are you to come between her and Geoff? They deserve each other.

Caleb puts the mask back in your lap. "So she's not worth it," he says. "We can have fun with other people with these."

* * * * *

Caleb hasn't finished his mask when you meet in the clubhouse on Saturday, but yours is done, and he whistles appreciatively at it. "So who do we try it on?"

You've been giving it some thought, now that the point has come. "We don't know how they work. It could be dangerous."

"So who—?"

"Let's try it on Geoff. We could do it in the movie theater in the dark."

"You said it could be dangerous!"

"Yeah, so I think—"

"And I wont let you throw it away on Mansfield. Let's call Richards."

"So if it's dangerous, let's try it on ourselves."
1112.title: A Conspiracy of Two

choices: Counter with a plan of your own
11121.content:
Caleb's eyes bulge. "Are you crazy? We don't know what will happen!"

"Exactly. It would be—"

But you can't finish the thought. For if it's unethical to test them on other people, it would be unethical to indulge all the other plans you've been fantasizing about.

"Well, if you want to be your own lab monkey, go ahead," he grumbles.

"Right," you say. "I'll try it out, and you watch to see what happens. Jump in if it looks like, you know, something's going wrong." Despite your attempted bravery, your stomach flops about at the thought of "what might go wrong."

"Alright," he says after taking a deep breath. "How do we do this?"
