He shrugs helplessly. Close questioning shows that he also doesn't push as hard as he could. "Keep after them," you tell him. "Castillo, Vargas—"

"They don't have the money."

"'Cos they don't know what they can get from you!"

You dismiss them soon afterwards, for you've the other meeting to get to. You hope the disguised originals of the dipshits aren't as useless.

* * * * *

"I don't think I can do it," Chris Trantham says quietly. "I mean, I don't think Trantham could. No one would buy him as a seller, not a serious one. He's too much of a dude. You know. 'The Dude'." He makes air quotes.

You stare at him with dull frustration. But he's got a point. Trantham is soft and round and flabby, an unbaked roll of biscuit dough that sags to the touch. "So who over there would be believable as a hard-core dealer?"

He thinks a bit. "Terry Fitzgerald, maybe. More than Trantham would be. Paul Zametti. Adam Karter, maybe. Or one of those basketball players. Uh, are you thinking about giving me a new identity?"

Honestly, you're not sure. It might be best just to pull Evans back into Westside, and to develop a brand-new contact over at Eastman. First, though, you'd have to figure out who that could be.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Business Before Pleasure

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Okay, you remember those metal strips I showed you last week," you tell Trantham. "I'm going to bring you—" You silently groan at the work you'll have to do. "Three of them. All you have to do is get them onto people, onto their foreheads, the same way we got these masks onto them."

"Onto whose foreheads?"

"Lemme see that list again." You study the creased paper describind the students at Eastman.

You wish you didn’t have to do their thinking for them. You wish Evans—the idiot who's pretending to be Chris Trantham—could use Trantham's brain to figure out who's worth copying. Above all, you wish you didn't have to waste time with this meeting at Hochstetter Park.

But, dimly, you recognize that if they were smart enough to think on their own, they'd be smart enough not to let you bully them around.

"This girl, this guy and ... oh, one more of your choice," you say. "What I want are people who hear things, who know things, the gossip and the rumors, the underground stuff. The people who can guess who at Eastman is dealing and who is using, and most of all who they suspect is dealing. We need to find someone plausible for you to be, and besides the ones you mentioned—who sound about as plausible as my Aunt Sue—we need a list of who that might be. You get those strips back to me by Friday, and we'll dig into them to see who might work, because these guys' brains will have that info." Then, since he'd warned you he had a gig to practice for, you dismiss him.

That leaves you with Hall. "Okay, make me happy. Where's the football team getting their shit from?"

"You'd have to ask Carstairs. Or get inside his brain," says Mendoza, the guy playing the junior football player. "He's the one who brings it in, parcels it out."

"Fuck. And what do you mean he parcels it out? He's giving it away?"

"No, but he says, ' Today you can buy, but you can't,' you know, telling people when and how much. He doesn't let Kevin do it that much, only as a reward for sometimes."

"So why don't the other guys come to us when Carstairs stiffs' em?"

"They do, sometimes, right? Even Erik sometimes buys from me. From Mendoza, I mean." He looks very uncomfortable for a moment. "Also, he sells it for cheaper than we do." His discomfort deepens. "Thirty-three for an eighth."

"Thirty-three? Are you—? You can't get it on the street for less than forty, even with a discount for bulk!" A horrible thought hits you. "Is he getting it from a distributor direct? He can't be subsidizing the whole fucking team at ten, fifteen an eighth! Fuck, the way they're playing this season, they're smoking a bale of the stuff a week!"

"Hey, you know I'm playing on the varsity squad these days."

"Shut up." You grip your forehead. "It's gotta be— Wouldn't be my guy supplying him, he's too careful for that shit."

"So put Carstairs under questioning, find out how it's working."

You look at him. Mendoza isn't as reckless as Thomason, but he's the smartest of the trio, and he's probably nailed it. But you've another question for him: "What would Carstairs say if Kevin Hall—" You jab him in the breastbone with a knuckle. "If you were to start distributing on your own down in the junior class?"

He blinks, and stammers. "I don't think he'd like it. It's not a money-maker for him. Like, it's about team rewards, not—"

"Okay, I get it. So if you can't deal, who down there can? I mean, if you had to guess which of the juniors was—"

"Oh, that's easy. Ethan Wade."

"I don't know him."

"Sure you do. We had him in that stupid General Business class last year."

"I only got so much room in my brain, I flushed that shit out while I was in it. But this Wade guy, has he got the guts to handle a set up?"

"Well, he walks around all the time like this." He struts like a rooster with a massive boner. "Kind of like you, actually."

"I should fuck you up for that. But it sounds like he might work."

Hall's reaction is studiously neutral. "Are you moving me, like you're thinking of moving—"

"I didn't say I'm moving anyone, and I don't think I'm moving you. No, what I'm thinking is—" But you haven't thought it yet, and it takes you a few minutes to think it out. "I'm thinking we want him to do the work, we'll just supply him and help out. We'll sell him eighths at forty and tell him he can sell them at fifty. Like, you and Thomason and Evans have been doing."

"Am I still supposed to be selling three ounces a month—?"

"No, your job'll be to help Wade sell three ounces a month. Not instantly, either, but help him ramp it up, quick. You're gonna be the one supplying it to him, not me. He'll believe it, coming from you. Fucking football team, the crowds at your games get a contact buzz when you run out on the field. You sell him the stuff and you help him move it, but not by selling it direct, you just sound people out if they're interested, point them toward this Wade fuck if they are. That way your hands are clean if Carstairs gets wind of it. Oh, and tell Wade that if anyone asks where he's getting it, he should tell them to go fuck themselves. It can't be traced back to the football squad, okay? But if he really gets put on the spot, like giant screws are being twisted into his eyeballs, he can tell them it comes from Matthias. We'll even set up a little dump, a place Matthias likes to go to—"

"You mean that loose board under that portable?"

"You know about that?"

"Whole football team knows. Matthias puts his own stash there."

"God damn it, that idiot."

"Yeah, what's the deal with him these days? Is anyone really gonna believe it's Matthias selling into the junior class? He's cleaning himself up."

"That's why they'll believe it. He's still getting it but he's sworn off it, so he's selling it. But you show Wade that loose board and tell him he can blame Matthias if it gets really hairy. I think that'll cover us."

"Why are things suddenly so complicated?" Hall asks, and his voice acquires a whine.

"You think this is complicated? You got no fucking clue."

* * * * *

When you're done at the park you make a run to the grocery store, but after that you're released from further obligations until eight o'clock. There are things you should be doing, but when you get to the elementary school, where you were going to look over the next spell in the book, you discover that you've left it behind. Fuck it. You call Kirkham, but he's busy.

And that, for what it's worth, is how you wind up back at the water towers with Call and Spencer and Horner.

That'd be Joshua Call, Jeff Spencer, and Nicholas Horner. Not three guys Gary Chen often hangs out with—and Will Prescott never in a million years would—but your itchy butt took you back to the towers, and this trio of dropout delinquents were the only faces there you recognized. "Whyncha bring a party," Call jeers at you. "You always got a baggie of fun with you."

"Or a fortune cookie at least," laughs Horner. He's the smallest of the bunch—the designated butt-kisser—and his grin falters when you glance at him.

But you let it drift. Call takes a swig from his flask and pointedly doesn't offer it to you when he passes it around to his friends. Not that you'd take a swig anyway. The cigarette is enough for you, and the flask looks greasy. Call does too. It's easy to imagine black grime under his fingernails and motor oil between his fingers, probably because, with his tight skin and ropey muscles, it's easy to imagine him wielding a tire iron. Doubtless the stains—of oil or of blood, depending on the particular job he made of it—wouldn't show on his black t-shirt.

You just grunt. "Well, when you guys are next in the market, talk to my boys," you tell them.

"They never have anything," Horner says in his sneering whine; the rat must be feeling brave because you didn't chastise him. But he does flick a nervous glance at Call; he never says anything, that you've ever noticed, without looking to his bruiser pal for permission or approval. "I asked Tanner if he had a toke, and he said—"

"When was this?"

"Coupla weeks ago. And he said—"

"They're gonna start having some anytime you ask, starting next month," you say. "We picked up some more supply, gonna—"

Call laughs hoarsely: a bark like an elephant seal. "Sounds like some of those bullshit business classes are starting to kick in for you, Chen. Shit, I coulda told you—"

Spencer interrupts: "Why don't you get a whore, start pimping her out?" he asks you. His tone is sullen, as is his stare. "Not that I'd touch anything a gink like you'd—"

"Would you touch Lin Pol," you shoot back before he can dig himself a deeper hole. Not that Spencer would care: he sucks in oxygen and blows out racism. "Bet you would," you continue as he expression hardens. "Bet you'd bend over and let her spank you, but she'd fuckin' scare you so bad you wouldn't even get hard."

"Fuck, man, that's the way to think," Call says, and laughs again. "I don't mean Lin, I mean make yourself fucking useful, set up a stable of ponies for us to ride." He claps a hand to his crotch. "Fuck, if you could talk Jelena Petrovich into—"

"She's gay!" Horner whinnies.

"So? Gimme fifteen minutes with her and—"

The repartee that follows is obvious and boring. But you're thinking about Spencer's suggestion: between the stone -bots you've made and the masks, you've basically got an instantly deployable "build your own whore" kit.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Too Many Dumbasses

choices: Go on to the next spell
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
It's a tempting thought: Make a fembot and whore it out. But life is complicated enough. You delete the thought and return to the conversation, which mostly consists of Call and Spencer and Horner fantasizing lewdly about Jelena Petrovich and Sienna Goldman and other girls of the tattooed persuasion. When talk turns to Andrea Varnsworth, though, you suddenly feel shy and even a little sick, and abandon them with a quick "Later." They don't even give the courtesy of a return farewell.

* * * * *

You are feeling ragged and bleary the next morning as you trudge across the parking lot toward the school. You were up until three in the morning doing and homework and finishing up a trio of mind bands for Trantham.

But there's a quickness to your step nevertheless, for you are keenly anticipating the minutes between 9:00 and 9:10, when the apparition of Andrea Varnsworth is due to appear outside the windows that look toward C wing. Yes, it's Joe Thomason inside that lovely body now. But still, that means you'll be able to talk to that body now, to stare at it openly when you meet to talk about business. So with increasing impatience you shift in your seat as the clock edges closer to the usual time. The tension become nigh intolerable.

And then she appears. Walking slowly, eyes down. She's wearing light purple shorts and flip flops and some kind of sweatshirt. Her hair, as always, gives the impression of being damp. What's her schedule, you wonder, for now you can ask why she always appears this way at this time of day. Does she have study hall first period, and she spends it in the natatorium? But if so, why does she always take this route back into the school? It would be the long way around, wouldn't it?

"Gary, Steve," says Ms. Gladstone. "Put your eyes back in your heads and turn them this way."

* * * * *

During lunch you make a circuit of the school to spy out the state of your various replacements. In the cafeteria you spot Gordon Black sitting at a table with some of the football players—including Kevin Hall—laughing and making goofy faces, apparently without a care in the world. At another table—the alpha table—sits Chelsea Cooper, wrapped inside the arm of Seth Javits. She's laughing and talking a mile a minute; the other cheerleaders at the table look very uncomfortable, though, and you don't even spot Cindy. You guess that "snap vote" must have gone off as Chen wanted.

Then you make a pass through the hallways and outside the wings, peering into classrooms for those who don't have fourth period lunch. Inside Gladstone's classroom you spot three of them: Will Prescott, Caleb Johansson, and Andrea Varnsworth. All three are gazing attentively at the front of the room. So is Matthias when you scope out Walberg's room. Today's he's wearing a snazzy-looking sweater. Where is he getting the money to buy this stuff? You assume he's buying it, since you've never seen Matthias in these clothes before, and you don't remember finding them in his closet when you briefly played his part.

You catch up briefly with Evans and Mendoza. "Where's Thomason," you ask, and they shrug.

Fucker. He's also not in fifth period. Andrea must be too scared or too freaked out by her new life. You wonder if she's hurt herself, but bury the thought before it can fester into guilt.

The rest of the day passes without excitement, though not without some interest. The girls in Orchestra are buzzing excitedly in a corner, then explode when Eva Garner comes in. They practically swarm her. She is tight-lipped, however, and most of her responses are just variants on "I don't know" and "That's not my business." But from the content of the questions you gather that something even bigger than the snap cheerleader vote happened.

Eventually you glean the facts: Cindy Vredenburg, having muffed her coup, quit the squad. That was followed by a housecleaning of sorts: Lin Pol and Yumi Saito are also out, and so is Michelle Estrich. And to Audrey Briscoe's horrified, "What about you and Jessica, are you still going to be on it?" Eva will only answer with a very clipped, "We haven't decided."

You'd be interested in hearing more, maybe from Chelsea herself, but you've not got the time, and there's another girl you have to talk to.

But you don't have her number, so you stop by her locker after the final bell. The sidelong look she gives you is a little more tremulous than you'd expect from this self-confident girl. "I wanna meet at four," you tell Andrea. "Where?"

She doesn't immediately answer, and before she can Charles Hartlein appears at her other shoulder. "Oh my God, Drea," he gushes. "I know you're not interested but it's too delicious not to share!" He catches sight of you, and his chortle turns soft and malicious. "Hey Gary, there's a freshman disrespecting you over in A wing. Better go find him and flush him down the toilet."

Andrea stares into her locker.

She won't say anything in front of Hartlein, so you speak: "Text me later, girl. Lookin' forward to it."

You catch the phrase over-compensating, obviously from Hartlein as you move off.

Ten minutes later comes a text: CVS by McD's.

Weird, but it'll work.

* * * * *

The pharmacy is just across the corner from the McDonalds where you'd parted with her yesterday. You recognize Andrea's car out front; inside, you find her looking over some hair care products. "Prepping for a date?" you say as you fall in next to her.

She gives you that same brief, sidelong glance as at the locker. "You're really not the kind of guy I'd be talking to," she says quietly. "I figure here it looks like we ran into each other if someone sees us."

"Like in a spy movie, huh? What's wrong with your place? Your mom and dad wouldn't like my face either?"

"Dad's someplace west, mom's working, they wouldn't care," she says as she picks up a box of hair coloring. "But Hartlein's always popping in over there." She clucks with distaste: possibly over Hartelin, possibly over the hair coloring, possibly over both or over some third unnamed thing.

"What was he dishing about at your locker?"

"Do you care? I didn't. This girl didn't. Cheerleader shit."

"Yeah, that's a hot topic. You got anything to report? You looked fine this morning on your way from— Where were you coming from at the end of first?"

"Swim practice. I didn't know you could see me."

"Whole line of guys in two fucking wings get an eyeful of you every day, girl. Power of their erections could run a fucking factory for most of a month."

"Don't be disgusting. But I don't have a lot to report. I'm settled in pretty good." She tucks a strand of hair behind her ear, a little self-consciously. "Andrea's mom isn't around much, works strange hours. I've got people always coming and going from the house, though." She moves down a few feet, kneels to examine some more products. "Give me a few eighths tomorrow and I'll start your advertising campaign."

"Sweet, but you gotta pay for 'em. Is this girl good for it?"

"Easily."

"Really? She get a big allowance?"

"Mom's a waitress/manageress at Ristorante Locarno. She leaves tip money all over the house."

"Interesting. How much of it could go missing without her noticing?"

"If you want that kind of action, shouldn't you have gone for Kelsey Blankenship instead of Andrea Varnsworth?"

"Don't sass me, bitch. I just mean, would this baby's mama miss thirty-two dimes?"

"No. Most of it would go right back into her kitchen drawers after I sold the shit, right?"

"You're not selling, remember? But, uh, how much is laying around at her place?"

A pause. "Between twelve and thirteen thousand."

Your knees buckle, and you drop into a crouch beside her. "Seriously? Doesn't her mom believe in banks?"

"She does on the fifth of the month. And even then only if she's started losing the silverware inside all the loose cash. The money that's there now is what's left after she dumped a lot of it in the bank last week."

You grab a shelf to keep from falling over. "God damn, she could finance our whole expansion!" You've been worried about how to buy more stuff from Dwayne without increasing the debt to Mathis.

"As long as most of it gets paid back within a few weeks," Andrea says. She gets to her feet again.

"Well, this is great to know anyway. Meet me tomorrow in the library before class, I'll pass a baggie to you."

"I have swim practice at seven. How about after first?"

"That'll work too." You look her up and down. "The exercise looks good on you."

"And it feels even better," she says with a slight groan.

* * * * *

Maybe rockbot orders have an expiration date: Grandfather is being quarrelsome when you get home. But he's not too loud, and you don't want things looking suspicious, so you leave him to the nurse and to your mother as duck into the bedroom with some leftovers from Panda Garden. You want to glance through the grimoire before going in to work.

You find confirmation that grandfather is now a –bot of some kind, like the one still in the elementary school basement. Meanwhile, the next spell uses the same ingredients as the previous two, but with some significant twists: It uses only a few ounces of graveyard dirt, but no body; and the product is to be painted onto the inner surface of a mask—one that has a brain band attached. There is no explanation for what it will do. But there never is.

Hmm. You've got lots of masks around, but they're riding around on people. You could experiment with one of those, even with the mask you're wearing now. But what if you break it?

Maybe you should wait until you can meet with Caleb again.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: A Meeting with Mata Hari

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Whether you do the next spell with Caleb or not, you'll need all the supplies on hand, so on your way to work you swing by the cemetery and pick up a sack of dirt.

Meanwhile, other plans are also ripening. During your smoke break you slip the three new brain bands to Trantham and catch up on a text from Kevin Hall: Talkd w/Wade, he's intrested. Talk 2 me after school tomorrow? Heres Wades pic, remember him? The attached photo does jog a few memories. He's a small kid, but wiry, with dark bangs that sweep rakishly over narrow eyes. There's a feral, rat-like quality to his intense gaze, and you could easily imagine him growing up into another Dwayne Macauly.

* * * * *

You meet Andrea in the library stacks after first period, and after exchanging a baggie for an envelope full of fifties and twenties you ask if she's seen Joe Thomason's ugly face around. She shakes her head.

Nor does a circuit of the school at lunch locate him either. You do spot Horner and Spencer, though, which surprises you, since you'd heard that they had stopped coming to school at all. And yet there they are in the hallway of C wing, sneering and leering and chortling at some joke only they are in on. Spencer in particular looks pleased, and there are few things uglier than Jeff Spencer when he's looking pleased about something. His dull, Neanderthal eyes get a gleam, and his dome-like forehead gets a filmy sheen of sweat; and if you're close up to him you might even glimpse a little bit of anticipatory spittle at the corner of his mouth.

You saw that little bit of spittle up close your sophomore year when Spencer and some of his friends caught you—Will Prescott, not Gary Chen—in a school restroom. You were just trying to get to the sink when Spencer shoved you into a wall and held you there. He'd sniggered and breathed heavily into your face—his breath stank like a gym shoe—and you saw that little gob of spittle just before he hocked it and a whole lot more into your face. All of his friends had laughed at that, and one of them whapped you on the side of the head, but Spencer let you go and just looked very pleased with himself.

You tried consoling yourself later with the sniveling thought that it must be terrible to be so stupid that spitting in someone's face can make you happy.

Right now, though, you just turn your head and push past Spencer without saying anything.

* * * * *

But after glimpsing Horner and Spencer, you're not surprised when you see their pal Joshua Call after school. You're waiting behind the portables, gazing past the fields that belong to the school and into the fields beyond that don't, when hear footsteps and turn. It's Call, in his usual wardrobe: heavy work shirt, dirty jeans, scuffed boots. Not so different from your wardrobe today. Only he hasn't got a ski cap atop his head, just greasy black hair in a ragged bowl cut. "Fuck are you doing here," you ask. "Thought you stopped coming to classes."

"Didn't have nothin' better to do." At the sound of a high laugh he glances back toward the school. "The fuck is that?"

"Hennepin and Semple, those guys." They're audible from the other side of the portables, and they're happy since you'd just sold them two eighths a few minutes ago.

"We should go fuck 'em up," Calls says, and you can tell by his expression that he's serious.

"Nah, they're okay."

"So why aren't you hanging out with them? You're queer like them."

"So? You like hangin' out with queers too. You'd hang out with Jelena," you jeer back.

"That cunt," he mutters, and glowers at you as though you've something to do with it, whatever "it" is. Then he stalks off again. You let him go.

It's coming up on four, and you're still in your spot, when Kevin Hall rounds the corner. "Hey, what are you doing here," he says in a loud voice. "Thought you'd be back there with Hennepin and them."

"Felt like thinkin'," you say, and contemplate the burning end of the cigarette. "How was practice?"

"Brutal." He drops to the ground with a groan, and drops his voice too. "So I talked to Ethan Wade yesterday."

"So you said. Details, man."

"Well, he's never done this kind of thing before. 'Cept hustling candy bars his sophomore year, part of a 'band candy' scam."

"Fuckin' amateur. Lemme guess, he got busted."

"Yeah, but he was running it in the open where people could check on his story."

"Fucking noob. He's not gonna fuckin' run this one in the open. Is this kid dumb or is he even more retarded than he sounds?"

"I don't know," Kevin sighs. "You're not on the hook if he fucks up."

"Like fuck I'm not. But what experience has he got besides a dumbass band candy scam?"

"Well, he says he's bought some on the street before."

"He says? Fuck 'im. You believe him?"

"He says he got it at the Warehouse, he and his friends pitched in a couple of times to buy an ounce. He said it cost 'em three-sixty, which I think means he's telling the truth."

"No, it fucking means he talked to someone who knows what he's talking about. So according to him, he and how many friends bought one ounce how many times?"

"Him and two or three guys, and they bought maybe three times or four. He said they didn't smoke it all themselves, and he even sold two of the joints to a guy he knows."

You sigh. "Does that fucker have a name?"

"Yeah." He checks his phone. "Anthony McFarland. I talked to him. He said he bought two joints off Wade and Dason Mitchell, and if they ever had more—"

"Wait, who? Dason Mitchell? The fuck kind of name is 'Dason'? That French for 'gaywad'?"

"I don't know. But McFarland said he'd definitely buy more from someone if he knew where to get it."

"Fucking great. So we can sell an ounce a month down in the junior class. Open wide, I'm about to jizz."

"I think he can sell more than that. He talked like he was really excited about that sale he made, 'cos he made a forty dollar profit on it."

That catches your ear. "Forty? You mean twenty profit each on two eighths that he bought for thirty-six an ounce?"

"Uh. Yeah?"

Click click click. Ka-ching! "He sold those two eighths to this McFarland dick for sixty-five each?"

"If that's what, uh, forty bucks' profit works out to."

"Christ, and we've been giving them away for fifty!"

"It might just be this McFarland kid. He's kind of dumb looking, and I think he's got money."

"Doesn't matter. Rising demand means rising prices." Suddenly you feel feverish. "Okay, tomorrow I'm gonna give you six eighths to pass on to this Wade fuck, no charge. We can gamble this far on him. Tell him he's not to smoke the shit himself or give any to his friends. But he is going to give them away, to other people, even this McFarland dope. He's to pass the shit around to whoever he thinks might be interested, he's trying to get a list of prospects. He's gonna tell people he got this stuff free, and he's sharing 'cos he loves to share, and he's to ask these dipshits if they want more in the future, but that if they want more they'll have to pay 'cos he's pretty sure he'd have to pay. He's to find out what they'd be willing to pay. In a week, he's to talk to you about what he's learned, names and prices, tentative demand level. If this works and he doesn't shit all over our plans with his fucking stupidity, in two weeks we'll have a supply for him. Here."

You hand Hall the paper you'd prepped for this meeting. "First month, we'll sell him twelve eighths at fifty-five per. Second month we'll sell him eighteen at fifty-one per. Third month we'll sell him twenty-four at forty-eight per. After that the cap comes off, but the more ounces he buys, the cheaper his per-unit cost is until it drops to thirty-nine per. That's our floor. So, the more he sells, the more gross he realizes, and the cheaper his per unit cost is so his profit grows in two directions. Bang, gives him an incentive to grow the market. But we're capping him at the start because we don't want him going crazy. Last thing we need are posters going up on the junior lockers. This is all very quiet. He's gotta understand that."

"I think he will," says Hall, though he looks a little dizzy.

"Good man." You clap him on the shoulder. "You handled this great. Ten percent of what he pays us will be yours for handling the business."

He looks very gratified by that: Obviously he's not nearly as good as you at calculating just how little ten percent of the take will be.

* * * * *

After he leaves, you consult the memorandum book you keep on the phone, adding updates and entering new info.

And right now you're looking at your balance sheet:

Inv. 10 That's the number of eighths you've left on hand.
Allowance. $9.50 That, in hundreds, is the amount of cash you've on hand from sales. It will cover the rest of the month's "tips" to your parents, with a little left over.
IOUs. $3.60 (TE) In hundreds, what Evans owes you.
Bank $16.15 In hundreds, what you need to pay Mathis to fully complete next month's order, and you won't be able to generate that from your remaining inventory while still contributing "tips" to the household budget. And you'll need a lot more than that to carry out the expansion Chen is demanding.

So you're receptive—for business reasons, not only social—when Hennepin shouts at you as you're walking back to your car. "Hey, we're goin' out to Andrea's in a bit. Wanna go along?" That draws guffaws from the rest of the crowd.

Andrea's got a giant cash till you can dip into.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Noises Off

choices: Hang out some more at Andrea's
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Sure, I'd love to," you tell Hennepin and his friends. "I've never been to Andrea's."

Looks of incredulity—mingled with horror—are exchanged by Hennepin and Semple and some of the others. But it's too late now. You have been invited, and you have accepted.

You've no idea what kind of people show up at Andrea Varnsworth's house, but if you had to wager, you'd bet on this crowd: Karl Hennepin, Adrian Semple, Connor Davison, Andrew Harding, Lee Reynolds. The first two, of course, are regular customers of yours. The others, though scruffy, have always politely turned you away. Maybe they're genuinely not interested in weed. Or maybe they just never wanted to associate with Gary Chen.

This afternoon may prove a test of them. How will they react to Andrea Varnsworth if she pulls out a joint?

* * * * *

Happy whoops break out as you walk into Andrea's. They're not for you, though, but those with you, and a brief damper falls over the celebratory sounds when you cross the threshold.

Andrea's house is a couple of blocks west of the downtown district, not far from a sketchy neighborhood, and it looks like a migrant from that neighborhood. It is smaller than its neighbors and sits on a smaller lot. But it is tidy and well kempt, and the living room just inside the front door is very sparsely furnished with a futon, a bookshelf, and a desk and chair set. The floor is wood. In all, it looks like the kind of place that's been permanently laid out for a party. It's already crowded enough for one.

Music is playing on an old-fashioned boom box: something with a steady beat, but it's not playing so loud that it would disturb the neighbors, and the beat is muffled by the bodies of the guests. Besides the recent arrivals—yourself, Hennepin, Semple, Davison, Harding and Reynolds—you catch glimpses of Eric Harlen, Rebecca Sykes, Sienna Goldman, and Roy Booth. Jelena Petrovic and Fatima Zahedi are sitting on the futon, flanking the queen of the crowd, Andrea herself.

Her eye flicks with amusement over the faces of the newcomers, and she almost misses yours. But just as she's turning away she catches sight of you. A tiny smile flashes over her face, but it doesn't linger, and it vanishes as she leans over to listen to something Fatima is saying to her.

It doesn't look like a stimulant party: you only see colas out. Mostly it feels like the start of a late-afternoon hangout session. More people are apparently expected, for you hear snatches of conversation, like "Yeah, Tackett's coming as soon as he picks up Kevin" and "I'll ask Brewer when he gets here."

No one snags your elbow for a conversation, and no one offers you anything, so you duck through a doorway and down a very short hall into a kitchen. There are two friendlier faces here: Brad Murphy and Spencer Osbourne. "Whoa, hey, it's Chen," the latter gasps; his mouth widens into a grin. Brad just blinks like he's been slapped; but he always looks like he's just been slapped, and has been surprised by the assault. "Why'd they let you inside?" Osbourne teases.

"They heard you were coming and knew you'd need disciplining." Osbourne laughs at your gibe. "When the fuck are you gonna start going to classes again?"

"Soon as they start showing the lectures in IMAX. When are you gonna start ditching, start hanging out more with me and the Bradster?" He slaps Murphy in the chest; his friend blinks again, and looks startled. "You give us a buzz."

"Yeah, but I got parents who give a shit. Don't yours? What does your dad do?"

"Nothing." He scratches his cheek against his shoulder; his bristly blonde whiskers are almost as long as his bristly blonde hair. "But Bradley's dad's an airline pilot, you know," he adds slyly.

"Huh?" says Brad, and jerks a little.

"Huh?" Osbourne snickers. "Isn't he always at, like, thirty-five thousand feet?"

"What?" Brad's breathing turns labored, and a little panic appears in his eye. "No, he works at Target. He's a floor, uh, something."

"He's a floor? A tile floor?" Osbourne grins, and nudges you.

"No! He's— Are you fucking with me?" Brad's nose and cheeks are raw from acne, and they flush more deeply with confusion.

"Hey Murphy, does your dad, uh—?" You mime taking a toke.

"No!" Murphy looks genuinely offended. "He like, goes to church and stuff!"

"Then why does he always seem like he's up in the clouds?" Spencer asks. "Must be a genetic thing," he adds when Brad only gapes back. "Oh, but speaking of things that take you up high." He glances back into the living room before leaning in with a lowered voice. "You got any—?" He waggles his eyebrows.

"Talk to me tomorrow, can get you as much as you need. Which is how much?"

Spencer rolls his tongue in his cheek, then holds up four fingers. Then his thumb pops out. "How about you, Bradical Man?"

"Huh?"

"Huh? I'm gettin' five from Chen here, for me and Wooten and the weekend. How much do you want?"

"How much what?"

"Jesus, man. Ganja. How much do you want to buy?" Spencer carefully enunciates each word over a sudden upsurge of noise in the living room.

"Oh, I don't have any on me."

"Just give me a number. I'm getting five."

"Then I'll take one."

"That'll hold you through the weekend?"

"The weekend?" Brad's expression of perpetual panic deepens. "Oh. No, I better stock up on, like, uh— Five?"

Spencer gives you a look. "Better bring all you got tomorrow. I'll handle the transactions for both of us." He half turns at the sound of a door slamming from a room beyond the kitchen. "Ah, sounds like Reston's finished watering the garden."

"Fuck, is Reston here?" you say. "I'll go mingle with the others. I don't need to hang out with no midget bassist." Osbourne only shrugs.

You back out down the hallway. There's another door there, and it opens as you pass: Rebecca Sykes comes out, bumps into you. "Sorry," she says through a tight smile, and pushes in front of you and into the living room.

Dammit, she's a cute thing. Tiny. The top of her head barely touches the top of your shoulder, and that's with the high heels. She looks like she's prepped for clubbing, with a leopard-pattern jacket over a tight black dress, inside of which her butt waggles invitingly as she walks away. Her dark red hair, which is flat and straight on the top of her head, curves into loose curls as it falls below her shoulders. You suck in a sharp, lustful breath as she squeezes between classmates and tucks herself under the arm of Eric Harlen.

More people came in while you were in the kitchen. Hartlein is no surprise to see; nor is the sight of his fellow theater whores Christian Padilla and James Brewer. Hartlein is laughing shrilly, and he doesn't change expression when he sees you, though he does turn away and says something to Padilla, who in response glances in your direction.

But you don't care. You slide over next to Roy Booth, who's by the boom box, bobbing his head. You put your mouth by his ear. "I'm bringing in a couple of baggies tomorrow. You in the market for some?"

"Nah, I get mine from someone else," he says. He jerks his chin at a scene behind you. "Maybe you should talk to Andrea, though."

You turn. Andrea, still on the futon, has a burning joint between her lips, and is looking up with a cool, quizzical smile at Semple. Fatima is staring at her with a scandalized expression. It turns to one of disgust when Andrea casually offers it to her. Jelena, who is frowning deeply, gets up. Connor Davison promptly flops down in her place. Andrea, smiling, offers it to him. He takes it awkwardly, smiling nervously back at her, and takes a hit that is clearly experimental.

"You give that to her?" you ask Booth.

"Nah. Maybe it was Reston. I didn't think she'd be into it. Try cultivatin' a relationship with her, man. Or maybe I'll tell my guy."

"She doesn't like me. I shouldn't even fucking be here."

* * * * *

You linger for a bit, then search for a private area. You find it in the back yard, which is dominated by a massive vegetable garden. You text Andrea, telling her where you are.

You wait a quarter hour before the swing door opens and she comes out. "You like my garden," she asks. "Matt Reston did. I should tell you about it. It's the only reason I'd be talking to you."

"I need cabbage," you say. "Four thousand. By the middle of next week."

"For how long?"

"Seven, eight days. You'd have it back by the fifth."

"That'll work. Remind me to bring it in."

"You don't want me coming back here?"

"You don't fit in. And I don't want people thinking you're where I'm getting—"

"I'm already denying it. You say your mom keeps the money in her silverware drawer?"

"Most of it."

"Then tomorrow, start telling people that a lot of it went missing, and you think I took it. Put me in the dog house. Also, do you know where Booth gets his bash?"

"From me. Well, Joe Thomason. What?" she asks when you laugh sharply.

"Nothing." He only suggested that Thomason should cultivate a "closer relationship" with Andrea. "I won't take the cash now, but you tell people—" The door behind her starts to open. "Need me to weed your garden, Andrea," you smoothly improvise as Padilla steps onto the back porch. "Get a good root growin' down in your bushes?"

"Get back inside, Chen" Andrea replies. "I want you where people can keep an eye on you."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Court of the Doppelganger Queen

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You don't linger at Andrea's, for you can sense the hostility from the others, which you suspect has deepened since she pulled out a joint. You toy with driving over to Kirkham's. But then you'll get to talking about girls and the various things you can do with them. That is on your mind after Andrea's, but not in a way that would leave you enjoying Kirkham's kind of talk.

Why can't you get a girl? You ask yourself this question knowing full well that there are two people you can ask it of: the person you really are (Will Prescott) and the person you are pretending to be (Gary Chen).

Chen, naturally, has an easy answer to both questions. Will Prescott, in his estimation, is a sad little nobody who couldn't show a girl a good time if he had ten thousand dollars, a long weekend in a private hotel room, and a how-to guide. And all the girls sense it, so why should they waste time with him? Gary Chen, meanwhile, is the wrong ethnicity, even for cunts like Lin Pol and Yumi Saito, who are looking for—and, thanks to fuck-heads like Jordan Cooper, can get—action with a white guy.

And what do you—Will Prescott—think? Of yourself, you only have a very confused notion of what the problem might be. Chen has a point, you glumly admit: You have no idea what to do with a girl, and you're awkward and ungainly around them, and you find it very easy to get distracted by friends and computer games and pictures of perfect girls that you can find on the internet. Real girls are messy and you don't understand a freaking thing about them except that they always seem to be looking for someone else over your shoulder when you talk to them.

Thing is, you could say the same thing about Caleb, but when you were playing Caleb's part with Eva Garner, she seemed to respond. So what is it about your skin that it comes between you and a girl, in both directions, knocking the girls away and knocking you yourself off balance? You don't think you're any worse looking than Caleb; in fact, you don't think it's false pride to regard yourself as rather better looking than him.

Maybe Chen's right: Maybe if you ditched the funny clothes, got a haircut, stood up straight, put on some muscles, lowered your voice by half an octave, showed some serious cool—

Maybe if you turned yourself into a totally different guy, you bitterly think, then girls would like you. Or that guy, they'd like that guy, the guy you're pretending to be. You, they're not interested in. But him, that other guy—

Oh, and for your fucking information, Chen, you silently yell at him, since you've worked yourself up into a yelling kind of mood, the girls don't avoid you 'cos you're Chinese-American. It's because you're a fucking asshole, and are rather famous for being a fucking asshole.

Jesus, maybe if you could find a spot somewhere between these two morons you've got inside your head—yourself and Chen—you could actually get it on with some attractive girls.

But Chen can't afford to be nice. And not even Gordon Black, it looks like, can change Will Prescott's reputation.

You glance around, and notice that you've somehow wound up on Plumtree Road, way west of town. You cuss and spin the Jeep in the other direction. You've driven two miles or so back toward town when you recognize a car pulling out of a side road, and honk at it. You gesture at the driver; and after flashing a peace sign back at you, he follows.

* * * * *

"How do you know about this place?" Justin Roth asks. He's followed you to the old elementary school—maybe a foolish place to have guided him—and the two of you are sitting under the monkey bars. There's a small playground and an extensive field behind the school, but it's always empty, for it's mostly old people living in this part of Acheson these days. So you and Justin have the soft autumn night to yourselves. The sky has deepened in color, but the lowering sun has lit the high fluffy clouds with vivid pinks and purples. One very late cricket—the last, hopeless but still hopeful virgin of the year?—is singing in the distance.

"I dunno, just stumbled on it once, a long time ago," you say with a shrug. "Too many shits hanging out at Hochstetter Park, too many old people at South Creek. This isn't a place for partying, so I come here when—" You trail off.

"Yeah," Justin drawls. "You know, Tsosie lives, like, a mile, mile and half, off that way, toward the river."

"I didn't know that."

"Yeah. Of course, Perry—" He grins. "Perry lives off in that fucking so-called 'Wilderness' development that way." He waves his arm in the other direction, where Chelsea and Gardinhire have their houses.

"Why 'fucking so-called'?"

"Because it's not fucking wilderness," he says, his voice cracking with outrage. "You know what the definition of a subdivision is, right? It's a place where they cut down all the trees and then name the streets after them. Fuckers!" he screams at the people who live out in that subdivision. "God damn it, I shouldn't lose my temper," he grumbles, and pulls a half-smoked joint from a jacket pocket.

"Set you up with some more of those tomorrow? I'm bringing more in."

"Nah, I'm good," he says, then rubs his nose. "No, truth is I'm tapped out."

"Get Small to cover for you."

He fixes you hard with his eye as he lights the joint. "I'm not getting Perry mixed up with you, Chen. In fact, I've told him to stay the fuck away from you."

"Am I not welcome behind A wing anymore?"

"Hang out as much as you fucking want." He falls onto his back and crosses one leg over the knee of the other. He takes a small hit, exhales through his teeth. "There's what's business and there's what's personal, you know that."

"Thanks, I'm glad you understand. Not many people do."

"People are idiots. People are stupid. People shouldn't be allowed to fucking exist." He slaps at a bug on his chest. "Give it back to the fucking mosquitoes. Bears, cougars, bacteria. Whatever."

"I thought you liked people."

"I fucking hate people, man. Oh, there are a few I like." He swings up sharply, leaning in on you with a wicked grin and gleam in his eye. "I tell you about Deanna Showalter? Last Saturday?"

"No, what about her?"

He laughs and flops onto his back again.

"Oh, fuck you."

He laughs again, louder, then his voice turns dreamy. "Down by the river," he says. "I wasn't even looking for any, I just thought Semple would be down there, doin'—" He raises up. "You know, you can sell whole fuckin' bales down there, most nights and every weekend."

"Cops know it too, they're always cruisin' along there," you tell him. Actually, that's Dwayne's territory, so it's off limits.

"They ain't never fucked with me," Justin says. "But Deanna." Now he actually cackles. "So, I'm drivin' along by the river, and I spot her and summa her girlfriends walking along, and they all start teeheeing when they see me, so I pull over and get out and we all talk for a bit. Then after I'm pretty sure I read it right, I go off to look over the river, give 'em a chance to decide which one of 'em's gonna, you know. So Deanna wins the coin toss or whatever, she comes over, and she's nervous. I hate a fucking nervous girl, you know, don't you hate talkin' to 'em when they get that quaver in their voice? Shit. So I pull her close and we kiss and—" Another draw on the joint, then he pinches it out. "Lucky I cleaned out my backseat about ten days ago, so there weren't any problems."

"Is that about how often you get laid, every ten days?" You try to keep your voice neutral. Inside, you are frothing.

"Pfft, fuck, no."

"Every seven days then? Every two?"

He lifts his head to squint at you. "I don't get laid that often. Only every couple of weeks. I'm sure you get a hell of a lot more than me."

"What makes you say that?"

"Because I never get any!" His voice again cracks with emotion as he sits up. "It's not even every couple weeks, it's maybe once every two months. Fuck, I got some action over the summer 'cos there wasn't any school. But before that? Gotta go back to before spring break."

"Why don't you get a girlfriend?"

"No one wants a dope addict for a boyfriend," he retorts. "Why don't you get a girlfriend?"

"No one wants a Chinese guy for a boyfriend."

He blinks, then grimaces. "That's bullshit."

"Try it some time. Okay," you add when he glares. "It's because no one wants a dope peddler for a boyfriend."

"Well, now, that makes sense," he says. "So you just do lots of 'em, one at a time."

"I told you how often I think you get laid. How often do you think I get laid?"

"Okay, I'm not playing that game with you," he says, and gets to his feet. "No one ever gets as much as they want, and that's too bad."

"Why do you say that? People get laid more than you want them to."

"I didn't say that!"

"Sure you did. You said people shouldn't exist. How do you think they all got here?"

He makes a face at you and wishes you a good night.

* * * * *

Well, that was pointless, you decide afterward. But it did have the effect of working out some of your neuroses, just by putting them into words of some kind.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Tuesday in the Park with Justin

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You don't have a lot of homework, so after getting that out of the way and after practicing the viola you make an early night so you can make an early start of it in the morning.

In fact, you make such an early start that the family is all still asleep when you get up. You leave a note saying you have to go to an early soccer practice, then make a beeline for the old elementary school, taking along the grimoire.

You double-check the ingredients for the next spell and find that you have all the supplies you need, except for hair. You pull off Chen's mask—

* * * * *

—and snip off some of your hair, and put Chen's mask back on—

* * * * *

—and after rubbing away a sudden rush of exhaustion, you dump everything into a bowl and put the bowl onto the open book. You light the pile, and after it fizzles and burns for a minute or two you find you've made a thin but sticky paste. You've got a mask of Martin Gardinhire going to waste, so you coat the inside of it with the newly made goop. It goes on quick and dries fast, and when you're done, the letters of his name still float over the inner surface even though the inside of the mask is now the color of the rock-bot lying nearby. You glance between it and the mask speculatively before setting the mask onto the book.

The page now turns. There's a large paragraph of writing on the opposite side.

But what first draws your attention is the page showing the newly uncovered spell. It makes your eyes hurt.

The top of that page looks normal, with ornate but neat script arranged in the manner that the book likes to list spell ingredients. But the bottom— Oh!

It's like an out-of-order computer monitor. When you look away from it, you seem to see letters and script, but when you look directly at it, the letters fuzz and bloom and skitter away. No matter where you lay your eye, the ink jitters and scatters.

The hell is going on?

Only when you peer closer do you resolve the mystery. Just below the list of ingredients is a faint line, one that is rough beneath your fingertip. Rough like a torn piece of paper.

That's it, you hiss to yourself. The top half shows the ingredients, but the bottom half, the part with the sigil, has been torn away, and the fuzzy thing you're seeing is the bottom part of the spell that comes after. And since the book doesn't want you seeing any spells until you've completed the spells that come before, it's messing up the writing of that subsequent spell.

You pound the table. All that work and this is where it ends. No sigil means there's no completing the next spell. This one, the one that makes the new kind of paste, is going to be the last one you can use, because some previous motherfucking user went and defaced the book. Way to ruin it for everyone, asshole!

Well, there's no point raging, not when you've only an hour before classes start. You bring up the browser on your phone and start translating the back half of the spell you just completed.

* * * * *

The book doesn't use the word "rock-bot"—"animated trunk" seems to be the best translation of the actual Latin—but it says that the latest spell puts a rock-bot inside a mask. And when placed on someone, it turns them into a disguised rock-bot.

Maybe. The damn translator scatters clauses and phrases all over the place in confusing ways.

But as you look at the painted mask, and at the rock-bot, and turn the mask this way and that in your hand, it seems to make sense to you.

The cemetery stuff makes a rock-bot. If you put a mask on the rock-bot, it turns into that person. The resulting person-bot has to obey the guy whose hair is inside the rock-bot.

If you mix the cemetery stuff with a person, it turns the person into a rock-bot. Put a mask on it, and the resulting person-bot—which hides the rock-bot—has to obey the guy whose hair is inside it the rock-bot.

Now, you muse, you've put the cemetery stuff inside a mask. If you put the mask onto a person ... The cemetery stuff will cover them and turn them into a rock-bot? Then the mask, which is already on the person, will turn that rock-bot into the person-bot that's inside the mask?

That makes sense, doesn't it?

Well, it makes as much sense as anything else. You put Gardinhire's mask onto the rock-bot.

It's like dropping a stone into a pond. The rock-bot briefly ripples, and when those cease—

Gah! You raise your eyes from Gardinhire's naked crotch to his face. It's already turned to stare at you with fear, worry and puzzlement. It doesn't move.

"Sit up," you tell him. He obliges, looking like a man who's being pulled up by string and hooks. He glances down, notices his shame, and with a deep blush covers his dick with his hands. He swallows, and his eyes water as he stares back at you. "What's your name?" you ask.

"Martin Gardinhire," he stammers. "I think." He looks around. "Uh, I'm feeling a little confused." He swallows again.

"Do you have a boss?" That's the really important question: Does this thing have to obey anyone? Does it have to obey you?

"Uh, there's Mr. Stilwell at the country club—"

"I don't mean him, dipshit. I mean, is there someone whose orders you have to obey? Absolutely?" You lean in to fix him with a close stare.

He seems puzzled for a moment, then his eyes clear, and real fear shows behind them. "Yes," he says with quiet horror. "You."

Good enough in this case, you think, and you tell him to be still while you pull the mask off him. The rock-bot reappears, now bent and sitting up where Gardinhire had been. You still have another test you want to run, though. One that makes you very nervous.

* * * *

"Anything else," Gary Chen sullenly asks.

"Yes. Don't tell anyone that you're not really me."

Gary Chen—and it looks and acts just like him, and is dressed just like him—makes a face. "You fucking think I'm going to go up to people and say 'Hey, I'm not that—'"

"Don't call me names. And you know what I mean. If anyone has any questions for me, tell them you'll talk to them later. And don't forget to bring Caleb back here after school."

"I won't. Can I got now? I'm going to be late."

"Yes. And when I say 'Caleb', I mean—"

"I got you the first time," he yells, and runs up the steps and vanishes before you can think of anything else to tell him.

Finally! You have a fully functioning Chenbot!

There was one hairy moment when he lunged for you, but he stopped dead as soon as you told him too. A few humiliating commands—like the order to hit himself—soon convinced you that the rock-bot inside the mask overrode the rock-bot underneath. So you have an obedient Gardinhire-bot and an obedient Chen-bot anytime you need them.

Hallelujah, this could finally be the solution you were looking for! You just have to figure out how to turn it to your advantage.

You pull on those old track pants you found last Saturday, and sit down to do some furious thinking.

The simplest and most radical solution is to return everyone to normal. That means tearing masks off Chris Trantham and Kevin Hall and Andrea Varnsworth, and off Tanner Evans and George Mendoza and Joe Thomason; off Seth Javits and Caleb Johansson; off Gordon Black and Dane Matthias and Chelsea Cooper.

Everyone, except for Gary Chen.

Because that's more or less what this solution is, the solution that let's you put everything back to normal without pissing off Chen. Because—

Well, there's one more test you'd have to run, but if it worked out—

If it worked out, all you'd have to do is put that Chenbot mask onto him, and then Gary Chen would turn into your obedient Chenbot. You could order him to take his life back while everyone else got theirs back.

Okay, that's not the most ideal solution. The ideal solution is one where Gary Chen doesn't get roboticized, but still leaves you alone. Thing is, you doubt seriously that's going to happen.

But maybe you should still hold off on turning him into a Chenbot anyway. Things are a mess, but no one is really suffering, not yet. You still have a chance of talking Chen into accepting a return to normality, provided you improve his operations as he's asked you to. Why, with a few strategically placed rock-bots—if they work as you suspect they will—you could get those operations humming real fast! Meanwhile, the Chenbot could handle the really grubby work you don't like, such as grandfather duties and the country club.

* * * * *

You get your first chance to decide what to do sooner than expected. It's a little after eleven when you're startled when the Chenbot barges back in. "What the—?"

"I ran into a problem," it says. "And I said, Fuck it, let Prescott solve it, since he's the boss of me. Thomason showed up at school, finally."

"You mean Andrea, the real Andrea?"

"Yeah. We talked a bit. He or she or whatever said he wasn't going to work for me anymore. No more buying ounces, no more selling eighths."

"You straightened his shit out, right?" You say it without even thinking.

He grimaces. "Thomason said if I tried anything like that, he'd go to the cops, because he'd get off a lot more lightly than I would."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Return of the Chenbot

choices: Let her quit the team in peace
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You tear the mask off the Chenbot and jam it onto your own face. The move is instinctual, almost.

And that was a really stupid thing to do, you realize after you've woken up again. If the mask now turns people into Chenbots, you just ran the risk of turning yourself into a Chenbot.

The realization gives you several bad turns. The worst, of course, is the thought of what you almost did to yourself. Almost as bad is the discovery that you're probably mistaken about how the rockbots-in-a-mask actually work, which means some of your daydreams from earlier might not pan out.

But after recovering from your momentary shock and horror, you'd settled down to reliving the Chenbot's memories of the conversation with Joe Thomason.

It happened just before first period, when it found and confronted Thomason. The Chenbot had acted just like you would have in its place, demanding hat the hell and why the hell Thomason been absent. Thomason was very placid, and said he'd been sick. Chenbot said they needed to talk about Thomason's sales for next month. Thomason said he'd be quitting. Chenbot asked how healthy Thomason would be feeling if he tried that. Thomason coolly replied that if Chen or Kirkham or anyone tried any shit, he (Thomason) would have lots of testimony to give to the police, that he'd come real close to going to the police anyway, but decided not to because it would be "too much trouble," and that Chen would see the wisdom in letting Thomason quit. The Chenbot had let it go at that, and gone to find you.

You fall into a chair, feeling ill and angry. You have to hand it to her: Andrea has guts, more guts than Thomason ever had. Or maybe Thomason was just sadly greedy for the dimes and nickels he could make peddling six or seven sad little joints a month around school. He and the others were always too frightened to ever make a real go of it, and you worked with them only because it kept you from spreading yourself too thin. So you never worried that a chickenshit like Thomason might ever tell you 'No' or think about going to the cops; but you're not dealing with Thomason anymore, you're dealing with Andrea, and after finding her feet she's apparently decided not to follow the script you'd given her. Well, she can just—

You put your face in your hands, tighten your guts, sit up straight. You're not Gary Chen.

No, but you don't need things unraveling, not today, not until you get a better sense of what you're going to do and how you're going to do it. If it wouldn't hurt to hide Gary Chen inside a rockbot for a few weeks or months, it wouldn't hurt to hide Andrea Varnsworth under one for a few days or weeks.

If that's the way the –bot masks work. You're not so sure of it anymore.

It's lunchtime at the school, so you call the person holding Thomason's phone.

"No, I'm not meeting you anywhere," he tells you bluntly. "I'm not stupid. You're psychotic, and I'm not—"

"I just wanna talk."

"I don't want to talk either. I told you what I wanted to say, and that's all."

"We need to talk because you don't want to do anything stupid! Look, you wanna meet in public, that's fine. Hochstetter Park, let's say. In the parking lot. You don't even have to get out of your car, I'll talk to you through the window. Is that okay?"

"I don't want to talk. You can't—"

"Just one conversation. That's all."

A long silence ensues, then he grudgingly agrees to meet you at five o'clock.

You consider returning to the school inside the mask, but decide to continue working in the basement, where you've been preparing more brain bands and masks on the theory that it's good to be well-armed. You send the Chenbot back in your stead.

* * * * *

The next time the door to the basement opens you have a warning: the sound of rough male voices approaching. You're staring at the door with a wide grin when it opens and Seth Javits comes in. He stops halfway down the stairs when you call. "Hey man, glad you could break away! I didn't want to take you away from any hot Chelsea action!"

He boggles, and whips around to stare at the Chenbot, which visibly seethes. "What the—!"

"Yeah, check it out, I got the Chenbot working! I sent it out to be me this morning—I mean, to be Chen this morning—and I stayed down here and worked on masks!" You hold them up. "Come on in, and I'll show you what I did."

But Caleb remains wary. "Last time we fired up the Chenbot it tried to kill you," he says.

"I told you, I fixed it."

"How?"

"A new spell. It makes up this paste that you put inside a mask, and then the –bot has to obey the paste you put in the mask."

"Huh?"

Caleb flinches as you run up the stairs to pull the mask off the Chenbot's face; that rock slab—still in Chen's clothes—materializes on the staircase. "See, I told you it was that rock-bot thing underneath. Come on down."

Caleb now seems somewhat more convinced that nothing too funky is going on, for he follows you. He peers inside Chen's mask when you turn it over. "This is that mask we made of Chen," you explain. "See, it's got the crazy Chinese characters and everything. But see how the color is different, it's white like the rock-bot over there?" Caleb glances between the mask and the rock slab, comparing them. "The new spell, it makes up this paste, and you put it inside mask. But I used my own hair this time, and—"

"This is that spell that uses a 'perfect body'?" Caleb asks. "Fuck, I knew you weren't going to wait for me. Hang on." He turns very pale, and his eyes widen. "What happened to the body? Jesus, Will, did you grind someone up into powder?"

"No! This is another new spell. I got past the other one, this is the spell that came after."

"You did two new spells? What happened to the one with the body? Who did you use? The old man?" A little green shows in his face.

"Yeah, him, he's fine. The, uh—" You swallow around the lie that you're rapidly composing. "The dirt and hair and chemicals, they just seeped into him or something. He still looks the same and acts the same. Only he has to do what I tell him to do."

"You mean like he's got a rock-bot down inside him?"

"Uh, yeah, I guess that's what happened. Anyway, that's what it's like, and he has to obey me." Close enough to the truth, if you dodge the details.

"Well, that's cool I guess," says Caleb dubiously. "But how useful is it? Are you telling him not to shit in the bed?"

"I haven't tried that," you say. "But I can tell him to quiet down, stop screaming, stuff like that. Makes the home life easier, I'll say that."

"Huh." He stares quizzically into Chen's mask, and over at the rock-bot. "So let's see. This stuff inside the mask is the same kind of thing as the rock-bot is made of?"

"Yeah, except there's no person involved in the spell, and only a little cemetery dirt. It's real easy to make and apply, about ten minutes to cook it and stick in the mask. Then I put the mask on the rock-bot, and whang, it was totally my slave. The stuff inside the mask, I guess, overrides the stuff inside the rock-bot, so my hair overrides yours."

"So I don't have a rock-bot slave anymore," he observes. "Does that mean you can't wear Chen's mask anymore?"

"No. I mean, yes I can. I tried it. I guess you can't be your own slave, because I put it on and I was fine. It was just like wearing the mask before I put the paste in."

"So what happens if—?"

"Here, let me show how to make the stuff. I got it all set up." You pull him over to the prep table. "See, it's exactly the same stuff as in the rock-bot spell, but with a lot less dirt. We pour it all into the bowl and—" You snap your fingers. "Hair. We'll use some of yours so that, you know, you got some of this stuff you can use to make a— I know, we'll put your stuff inside Javits's mask, that way I'll have a Chenbot and you'll have a Sethbot."

Caleb blinks a couple of times, as though trying to figure out a very difficult sum. He gives you a wary look, but slowly sinks into a chair. "If we're going to—"

"Just hold still," you say, and grasp his face. He grabs your wrist, but you've already got a purchase on him, and rapidly mutter the spell: Seth Javits's mask comes away and Caleb Johansson's head lolls onto his chest.

That's when you put Chen's mask onto him.

* * * * *

"No, I'm gonna go hang out with Chelsea," Caleb says. "She's being a bitch, but— Have you thought about how to use this paste stuff with Chen?"

"A little. Come back here tonight when you're done with her, and we'll talk more."

"I thought you said you had to go back out as Chen."

"I do, for a little while, but I'm sending the –bot in to work for me. Just come by here after eight." After he's gone, you turn to the rock-bot, which he'd helped you move onto the desk.

The quick test with the Chen mask confirmed your suspicion: Once it was on him, Caleb acted just like the Chenbot, and had no idea where Caleb Johansson was or had got off to. The personality in the mask—and its program to obey you—was all that remained. Then you'd torn it off him and proceeded to make up some rock-bot paste with his hair. So he never knew of the experiment.

But now you know it's safe to use the paste on Andrea Varnsworth, and thereby bring "Joe Thomason" back onto your team.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Substitution Game

choices: No, 'bots aren't the way to go
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Hochstetter Park hasn't really got a parking lot, only an indentation in the street where one can parallel park without fear of getting sideswiped. Thomason is already waiting when you arrive, and he rolls the window down halfway when you motion to him. "Give me two minutes, and I can change your mind about the whole thing," you say as you lean in.

Then you strike, twining your fingers about his bony temple. He reacts more forcibly than Caleb did, but you're faster with the magic words, and when he wrenches your hand from your face it accomplishes your victory: You pull off the mask of Joe Thomason, and Andrea Varnsworth topples sideways.

You rush to the passenger side and climb in; in a trice you've the inside of the mask painted and dried with the rock-bot paste. Andrea hasn't even stirred before you get the mask back onto her. As happened with Caleb, the new –bot revives instantly, and rears back from you. You hope that's Thomason's instincts, not Andrea's.

"Calm down, Thomason," you tell him. "You're my friend." He blinks, but visibly relaxes, which surprises you a little. Your words were just a formality, but it almost looks like he's taken them to heart. "That's right, you're my friend," you repeat. "You like me, you like working with me. You do everything I tell you to do. Right?"

"Yeah, naturally," he agrees, though looking a little confused on the point.

"You told me this morning you weren't going to sell any more stuff for me. Are you going to keep selling for me?"

Now he looks uncertain. "I don't know."

"You are. You will buy bags from me, and you'll sell them. Starting next month, you'll buy four bags from me. You'll give me twelve hundred for them."

"I don't have twelve hundred."

"That's okay, you'll just pay me every time you sell some. All the money you take from a guy, you pass on to me, starting today. Also, starting today, prices are going up to fifty-five for an eighth. If people ask why, tell them it's because of demand, you got too many people asking for it, so if they want theirs, they have to pay more to be sure of getting it. Does that make sense?"

"Sure. But I already sold all mine."

"Great. Tomorrow we'll get together to talk about who you're selling to, and we'll get you some more to deal. Bring me all the cash you got at home so you can pay for it. How much you got on you now?"

He pulls some crumpled bills from his pockets. "Twenty-two and some change."

"Gimme this ten, just to show you like me, and you can keep the rest. We're friends, Thomason, don't forget that. Just keep going on with your life. Do your homework, hang out with friends, whatever. You're Joe Thomason. Just do what Joe Thomason likes to do."

He shrugs and gives you a half smile.

"Good man." You clap him on the arm and get out of his car. From your Jeep you watch as he drives away.

You're glad you don't have to use the same trick on Evans and Mendoza.

* * * * *

You return to the old school, rearm the Chenbot, and send it off to the country club. "Come back here before you go home," you tell it. It rolls its eyes but doesn't argue. You had swung by Chen's place before going over to the elementary school, so you're dressed in some of Chen's things. It's odd: Everything you've got on you've worn before in your guise as Gary Chen, and it felt totally normal. But now, in your own skin, the sleeveless tank-top, the floppy gym shorts and the flip-flops feel like someone else's clothes, no matter how much you try settling into them.

You'd finished one mask and one brain band before breaking off to meet Thomason, and you're expecting Caleb soon, so you pause to clean up. You label the containers that hold the rock-bot paste, marking which one is yours and which is Caleb's; you make up some more mask sealant and label that container; you sort supplies and tools for making masks and brain bands. Then, when Caleb still hasn't arrived, you pull out Chen's homework and work on it for him. The English lit assignment is easy, since you were taking the same subject from the same teacher (just at a different time), and so is the bullshit "Marketing Fundamentals" assignment. You do what you can with the AP Stat and Physics homework, using what you are able to remember of the subjects outside of Chen's mask. You notice it gets harder as you go along, though, and the abilities and memories that you had inside Chen's mask seem to evaporate. By the time you give up on the final problems, you have ceased to understand the answers you'd given to the first.

You have an easier time remembering the stuff you have to do to manage masks and deals, though: Maybe it's because it's because you were working those angles yourself, with stuff you were bringing to the merger of your mind and Chen's, but the notes to yourself flow easily across the page: Check with Trantham on Friday about those brain bands you gave him; check with Hall tomorrow about developments in the junior class; check with Andrea about her "advertising campaign"; pow-wow with Evans and Mendoza and the again-compliant Thomason tomorrow at lunch about their prospects.

* * * * *

Those subjects are still rolling around thoughtfully when you hear a crunch of wheels outside. A minute later Seth Javits comes in, looking like a very satisfied teenage male. He stretches his arms as he swaggers down the steps. "Oh, fuck, but when Chelsea Cooper wants to make a guy feel good she really knows how to do it," he says.

"The secret ingredient is Gary Chen," you tease.

"Yeah," he sighs. "But he's pretty good at making you forget that. Oh, I ran into Tilley just after seventh period," he chortles. "Literally."

"Oh, Jesus, what did you do to him?"

"What did he do to himself, you mean. So I'm at the sink in the D wing bathroom, stylin' my 'do, and I turn around just as one of the stalls me behind opens up. It's Tilley, and he bumps right into me as he comes out. He looks up, totally freaks out, jumps back four feet like I'm blazing hot. Which I am. K'shzzz!" He licks a fingertip and touches his flexed bicep. "Anyway, he jumps back into the stall, falls on the floor, puts his hand inside the toilet. I just gave him a look and left, but I was laughing so hard on the inside."

"Dude, that's horrible," you exclaim, but you're laughing too. Then you sober up. "You think maybe we oughta bring him in on this?"

Caleb does a double-take. "We don't want a dumbass like Tilley mixed up with these masks! You're twice as smart as him, and look how you've fucked everything up."

"I fucked you into Chelsea Cooper's cunny."

"The secret ingredient is Gary Chen," he retorts. "But no, we're not bringing him in."

"Is that Caleb Johansson or Seth Javits talking," you ask. "And why does Javits hate him so much anyway? You've got an inside take on things now."

"I dunno," he sighs. "It's irrational. I feel it when I look at him, like he's a pair of fingernails scratching at a chalkboard. I think he just sets Javits off." He shrugs. "But it's me who says we need to keep all this away from Tilley," he adds firmly. "So what have you figured out about the paste stuff?"

Suddenly you feel very vulnerable outside Chen's mask. This is Seth Javits talking to you, and though you can feel Caleb's innocence and goofiness behind the mask, he looks exactly like Seth: Strong, straight teeth, strong nose, hard eyes, a powerful torso under the letterman jacket. And you've got a confession to make.

"Well, the way I figure," you tell him, "these rock-bots are suddenly portable, and we can use them to hide people."

"What do you mean?"

"Alright, I'll confess. While we were changing your mask out this afternoon, I ran a little test on you. I put Chen's mask on you. You know, it has my rock-bot inside it, and I put it on you and, uh, it turned you into a Chenbot, totally under my control."

The color drains from Caleb's face.

"I took it right off you, it was just a fast test, and I didn't want you arguing with me, so I— What are you—?"

Caleb—Seth Javits, to all appearances—grabs you by the throat. "You fucking did what to me, you little puke?"

"It was just a test," you gasp. "I took it right off you! I mean, you know that, right? No harm, no— Urlpgh!"

His fingers tighten, and his lips peel back. But he drops you. Eventually.

"Alright, you tested your thing," he snarls. "And you put that stuff, my rock-bot stuff, in Javits's mask?" You nod: your voice still hasn't returned. "And if I put it on you then you'd turn into what? A Sethbot who has to obey me?"

"Ninety-nine percent chance I would. If you want to try it, I'll let you, to make up for—"

He waves you to silence. "No, we're good, I guess. But what if I put this mask on Javits? Would he turn into a rock-bot, or would it just go inside him again, like when we put it on him the first time?"

"I don't know," you admit. "But I think he'd turn into a Sethbot. What are you thinking?"

He starts. "I dunno. What are you thinking?"

"Well, I'm thinking of just going on like this for now, except sending the Chenbot out to work. And you're having fun, right?"

"Yeah," he says slowly. "But it sounds like you could turn Chen into the Chenbot, and we could put everyone back to normal."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Friends in Fake Places

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"You could turn Chen into the Chenbot, and we could put everyone back to normal."

That's Caleb's suggestion, but you're no more inclined that way than you were before. Luckily, it's easy to talk him out of it: "Does that include turning Seth Javits back to normal?"

Caleb turns very red under your steady gaze. "Okay, maybe Seth could stay where he is."

"Sure. How about Chelsea? Does she go back to normal?"

"Uh—"

"Because if we turn Chen into a Chenbot, she has to go back to being herself. Is she gonna stick with you then, or go back to Gordon, who'd also be back to being himself? How's Cindy these days, is she talking to you again?" you add before Caleb can answer any of that blizzard of questions.

"That doesn't— Look, this takes care of Chen, right? You don't wanna be stuck like this, do you?"

"No, we can turn him into a bot. Be my pleasure to. But then Gordon has to get turned loose, doesn't he? And then Dane'll have to get turned loose, and then it'll be Chelsea coming out from under a mask, and we're back to 'Is Cindy talking to you these days' because Chelsea—"

Caleb swings at you; not hard, and you duck.

"Calm down, cowboy. I'll leave it to you. We turn Chen into a bot, and you go back to being yourself, or we leave everyone where they are. 'Cos that's basically the choice."

"Fine," he says gruffly after a minute of struggling. "But it's not like I'm going to stay like this forever, and then you better be ready to put that mask onto Chen. I guess we can let things go on this way until I get bored." You suppress a grin: Caleb seems deeply ashamed of this decision, which is why he is pretending he might ever get tired of being Chelsea Cooper's new jock-star boyfriend.

You finish another mask-and-band combo in the time after he leaves and before the Chenbot returns. It looks worn out, and you too feel the exhaustion after you've changed back into the mask. Now you spot the flaw in your plan to dodge work: When you're back in the mask, you not only feel the stress and grind of your own evening, but the stress and grind of Chen's. It's all you can do to copy out the homework you'd completed, and to complete the homework you hadn't.

* * * * *

The Chenbot did well in the marketplace yesterday, selling the last of your stock to Osbourne and Murphy; and he managed to lay it on them for fifty-five an eighth with the excuse that it was the last he had. But the real good news comes today, during second period, when Marc Garner pulls you away from a practice scrimmage to say that the AP fucks are still in business with you, and will be buying six and a quarter ounces next month. "There's an extra weekend in November," he explains. "So don't get used to it. It'll be back to five the month after that."

"Bump it up to eight ounces and keep it there permanently," you tell him. "You get a discount at that level."

Garner's eyes narrow.

"Look, it's not my idea," you tell him. "I told you, I just procure for whoever asks me, collect a percentage. My guy just jacked his price up to four-forty an ounce, but this stuff is like anything else, if you buy in big enough bulk, you get a discount.
