He puts his price break at eight ounces, cuts it to three-sixty per." You watch as Garner tries to calculate prices in his head. "That's twenty-eight-eighty for eight ounces with the discount," you tell him when it becomes too painful to watch. "If you buy only six and a half, you'll get one and a half ounces less, and you guys will save all of twenty bucks. Next month, if you buy five ounces at four-forty, it'll cost you twenty-two hundred. But with the discount, an extra seven hundred for three ounces carries a marginal cost of two-thirty an ounce. You'd be picking up the extra baggies at half price."

Garner looks horribly confused—he's an AP fuck, not a genius—and objects that eight ounces is more than half again what they're used to consuming in a set period. "So lay the extra aside, save it for the month after. Or throw a bigger party, more friends. I'm telling you, it's smarter to get the discount. How many people are you buying for? Ten, twelve? Tell them they can pick up a shitload more pot if they each throw in a few more bucks on top of what they're already contributing."

"For this month only," he finally agrees. "We'll take eight if you can guarantee that discount."

"I'll lock it in this afternoon, text you to confirm," you say.

Then it's back to the game.

You do "lock it in this afternoon," with a memo to yourself on your phone: Dope for dopes, 8 for 28.80.

* * * * *

"You hear about Josh Call getting' his balls kicked in?" Roth asks when the conversation lags.

You're eating lunch with him and Small again; it's become a regular thing lately since you don't much like hanging out with the fake Evans and Mendoza and Thomason. You haven't been paying much attention to their talk, and normally you wouldn't trouble over anything about Joshua Call, but it's the third time he's drifted past view in recent days, so you turn a slightly distracted ear toward the conversation.

"What happened?" Small asks. "He mouth off to the wrong truck driver?"

"No, it was here at school."

"What was he doing at school? Someone told me finally dropped out, thank God."

"I dunno what he was doing up here, unless it was something to do with the person who kicked his balls in." Roth grins. "Jelena Petrovich."

"No!" Small exclaims, and even you feel piqued. Something happened between Call and the dyke rocker he has that crush on? "What's the deal?"

"I didn't see it," Roth says, "but what I heard was Call was getting' handsy with her, had her pressed up against a locker. Anyway, Sienna Goldman came along and grabbed Call, and while he was dealing with her, Jelena tried making a field goal with his nutsack."

Small cackles. "When did this happen? This morning?"

"Nah, day or two back. I only heard about it this morning. Sienna was out at the portables before classes, looking for guys to come hang out at the warehouse this weekend, in case Call and his friends show up and make trouble." Justin turns to you. "You should go out there, hang out, try to selling 'em some stuff."

"They don't like me."

"That's tough."

"What, are they're looking for bodyguards?" Small asks, his tone dripping with derision.

"I think they just want lots of guys hanging out while they practice," says Roth. "You know, just to be on hand in case." He falls back on his elbows. "I guess I could go up there, it'd be fun. I just hope Call and them don't show up."

"Why, you scared of 'em?" you ask.

"No, 'cos I hang out with 'em sometimes. Be fuckin' awkward, socially speaking, if I had to get between them and Jelena."

* * * * *

At your locker after the final bell rings, you text Kevin Hall to meet you by the practice fields at five-fifteen, which will allow enough time for all the afterschool clubs to break up. It also gives you a chance to text Andrea, then to talk to her when she calls you back at around four thirty.

"I'm on the house phone," she says. "No chance of anyone finding your number on it. That fag Hartlein loves to borrow Andrea's cell and snoop through it. Creep."

"I thought you loved him, Drea," you tease.

"It's all I can do keep from vomiting on him," she says. "Can't you take him out for a beating?"

"Keep the personalities out of it, Thomason," you say. "You're no use to the team unless you are Andrea Varnsworth."

"Mm," she says. "I think I am, at least where and when it counts. I helped Bethany Lewis dry her hair today. I think I turned her on while doing it. I know it turned me on. Oh, I also started talking about a lot of money going missing at the house, so expect to be unpopular real soon."

"I'm used to it. How's the popularity of the product?"

"It varies. With Sienna and Fatima and Jelena, I think I'm going to get an intervention if I keep it up."

"Fucking troublemaking hos. They had a run in with— Oh, you probably heard. But I saw Connor Davison toking with you at the party. I never heard he had the habit."

"Me neither. Lee Reynolds and Andrew Harding got a buzz too."

"Nice. What about Winkler?"

"Fucking twerp. No, he was like Fatima, tried giving me a lecture. Mew mew mew mew mew, I hate weasely little fucks like him. But I think Andy Tackett was interested. Am I supposed to be selling anything?"

"No, just share and make it look sexy, the way Andrea makes everything look sexy."

"I can definitely do that." Her voice turns amused. "I've been practicing in front of a mirror. God, I was turning myself on, I invented this trick of just barely putting it between my lips, then looking up under my eyebrows with this sexy little twinkle in my—"

"Okay, great," you say, taking a deep breath and trying to steer your attention back to where you need it to be. "So just keep doing that in front of Davison and Reynolds and—"

"Are we really going to be selling a lot of shit to these dumbasses?"

"Maybe not, but every little bit helps. And you don't need to be worried about sales number. Just be out there looking good with that trick." You catch your breath with a groan after hanging up.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Fragments of a Day

choices: Use the new guy to check out the warehouse
Put the new guy on autopilot, do other stuff
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You spend the rest of the day and night at home: practicing the viola, doing homework, and laying long-range plans.

Andrea Varnsworth is a long-term advertising play, and Thomason will probably be happy to play that part until he graduates. The goal has to be to nudge more of her friends—and people who want to be her friends—into partaking of the same recreational substance. But there will have to be a sustaining infrastructure.

Infrastructure: That's what makes it so nice to deal with the AP fucks. They regularly pay for a regular supply, and it all gets handled within sixty seconds. No hustling for nickels and dimes among the school outcasts.

Now you want a similar infrastructure for the group that Andrea is influencing. The trouble is that they are all a bunch of independent types with irregular demand. Even if Andrea added to their numbers, that would just mean a few more haphazard buyers for you to keep up with.

Well, for Chen to keep up with, once he took direct control of this stuff again.

If he ever did.

You put away the question of how long you will be doing this. It's easiest to pretend it will be forever. That way, when you hand it off, it will all go smoothly.

Okay, so ideally your irregular customers would set up a regular ordering system and regular consumption venue. Guys like Davison and Tackett, maybe even Winkler and more, might then be seduced into making their consumption habitual because they would want to attend these regular parties.

But where to hold them?

* * * * *

Friday is boring. That doesn't mean you relax. You have soccer practice and two hard classes; and when you spot Evan Wade going into a bathroom, you follow him in and pick a fight, just to see what he's made of. He's scrappy and he's got a few moves, and he doesn't yield when you hassle him. But he still ends up with his head inside the paper towel disposal while you explain that the B wing boys' room is your property after fifth period, and that if he doesn't like the smell of wadded up paper towels he'd better find another toilet to use after fifth.

You send the Chenbot to the country club while you lazily make and polish a few more masks. You're not sure why you're making more, except to have them ready to go in case you need to make a switch. After all, you're just about set on moving Evans to some new position at Eastman; you'll know better after you get those three brain bands back from him.

But the Chenbot brings more than the three bands when it returns close to midnight. On the cell phone is a text from Chelsea Cooper's number: Tomorrow, 11, my place. Bring cash.

You make a face. She must mean the receipts from the month's sales. You doubt she wants to talk about the cheerleader tryouts.

Yes, that's been the big news of the last week: Open tryouts for six spots on the team, come one, come all classes. You've barely paid attention: you've enough of Gary Chen's life to worry about without adding Chelsea Cooper's to it.

But as for the cash: You have a thousand you can pass on to her; the rest of the hoard has to be saved for the house as "tip money" from the country club. Chen is not going to like being short when it comes to placing the final order with Mathis. What if he asks how you're going to cover it? You don't want to tell him about the cash you mean to "borrow" from Andrea. It would revive his "rich twat" idea.

* * * * *

"What do you mean, I should cover it?" she sneers. "You think Mathis takes credit cards?"

"Can't you get the cash from your folks? Tell 'em you wanna buy—"

"They're generous, but they're not complete shitwits. It's all done on plastic so they can see where the money's going."

As with your other meetings, you're sitting out by the pool; and since the weather has turned chillier, your erstwhile boss is well bundled up, so that not even her bellybutton is showing. But she still looks gorgeous in the lustrous black sweater and matching knitted head band as she counts the bills tucked inside the fat envelope you'd given her.

"It's not much of a shortfall," you point out. "With that there, and the three-sixty Evans owes me, you're only short two-fifty. Don't you spend that much on clothes every weekend?"

"You trying out material for a comedy club?" She grins sharkishly. "You and Johansson skimmed some."

"Naturally. Frog tongues and bat wings don't come cheap, and you had me pop four guys into new positions. Also, I passed out free samples to get new assholes interested. Call 'em capital expenses and greater-than-expected marketing costs."

"So get the money from the three fucketeers. Between them, they gotta have the cash laying around in their new digs."

"They don't."

"Don't they?" she asks in a small, innocent-sounding voice. "I heard a nasty rumor that four thousand dollars disappeared from Andrea Varnsworth's house."

Fuck. You should have known that rumor would get back to Chelsea. "Yeah, alright, that's why the shortfall won't matter."

She grins. "So now you're finally on the team. I told you that stealing some—"

"I'm not stealing, I'm borrowing."

Her face falls. "The fuck?"

"Andrea's mom's got a buncha cash laying around. But I figure we can put four thousand of it to work and pay it back out of the first big sales before she notices it's gone."

"Why can't we keep it? We already said it was stolen from her house."

"We don't wanna leave traces, especially when Gary Chen is the accused. If we repay it, she can say that it was mistake, that the money didn't get stolen." You point to your face. "Why are you trying to put your own head in a noose?"

"Hmph. Well, when you get the money from her, give her another job. We're having cheerleader tryouts, you know? Jesus, Prescott, you're not all faggy under my face, are you, you have noticed those signs we've been putting up?"

"I heard rumors," you allow. "Six slots you're trying to fill? Way to fuck up the squad, man."

"I've got it exactly the way I want it," she snarls. "Just me and my friends, and everyone who comes on is going to owe me. It's open competition, and there's going to be a line around the block for those spots. Everyone will be kissing my ass, and don't think I'm not gonna make the most of it. Fucking Vredenburg bint thought she could take it away from me, I'm stronger than I was before! One of 'em I want competing is Andrea Varnsworth."

"I don't think she'll be interested," you say, and wonder at the coincidence that Chelsea should want her trying out for the squad when that was the excuse you used to trap her for Thomason.

"You tell her to be interested."

"I think that would be out of character. She's really married to the swim team, and I think the practice times conflict."

"I didn't say I was going to pick her," Chelsea says. "Though God I'd love to see her out of that one-piece with her arms and legs out to here." She clumsily splays her limbs. "But it's just to cement things. I want Andrea Varnsworth competing hard for a spot on the squad, and I want to fucking turn her down, so everyone will know what kind of power I've got."

If it weren't for the vulgar talk about Andrea, and most of the cuss words, you'd wonder if Chelsea's personality wasn't taking over Chen's. But then, based on your behavior inside Chen's mask, you're pretty sure this is Chen working out his pathologies in a different form.

"I don't know what excuse I could give her—"

"Don't be dumb. Tell her you want her in the girls' locker room after cheerleading practice so she can secretly take pictures."

* * * * *

You're pretty sure that "Andrea" will kick hard at the cheerleader scheme—you want to kick hard against it—so instead of calling her you send a text to set up a meeting. Besides, you want to see her again. Hell, you just want an excuse to be close to her.

But the reply is disappointing: She'll be spending the afternoon with her girlfriends the Warehouse.

Ah yes. The Warehouse. Now that's a place you need to check out closer. With his schedule, Chen rarely has the time to get in; and, anyway, Dwayne has warned him off it the same way he's warned Chen off the river as a sales location. But thanks to those brain bands Trantham brought you, there's another avenue for you into the Warenouse.

Yeah, those brain bands from Eastman told you a lot of what you needed to know. Two of them were useless, though through them you learn that the other high school will be a hard market to cultivate. It's a much more genteel place, much more in the grip of well-scrubbed AP types and honor-code athletes. But in the third brain band one you hit the jackpot. In the character and personality of Adam Karter you find a kid with the drive and amorality of Gary Chen and the charisma of Justin Roth. If anyone could set up a racket at Eastman, this Karter kid could.

In fact, he's got so much potency that you've pretty much decided to not put Evans inside a mask of Karter; with Karter's personality as a crutch, he might become a formidable rival.

So you'd been thinking of Karter as someone you'd have to rockbot-ocize. But for this afternoon it might be a good idea to take him over as an impersonation. He hangs out at that warehouse, so no one would blink if he showed up, and you could check out the scene directly.

And if Andrea is there? Even with Thomason running the controls—especially with Thomason running the controls—the girl is not going to touch Gary Chen. You wonder if Karter would have better luck with her.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: What the Boss Lady Wants

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Yes, a rockbot is just the way to handle the Eastman traffic: dependable and subservient. And this guy Adam Karter is just the one to set up an Eastman trade for you. He's a popular guy, but a raffish troublemaker; easy to imagine as a dealer, but not the scary kind like Chen. Just a party-hearty dude who knows how to lay his hands on a bale of weed every month, and generous enough to bring everyone in to share it.

And under his face, you scope out that warehouse where you want to set up the other Westside party scene.

So you call Trantham: "Set me up a meeting with Adam Karter this afternoon."

"I think he's busy on Saturdays."

"I know for a fact he is, and I know for a fact he'll listen if you call it a business proposition."

"He hasn't got a car."

"I know that too, so give him a ride! Jesus! Meet me up at the Westside student parking lot. Oh, and bring all the bash you still got hand. And all the money."

* * * * *

It's close to one on a bright (but slightly chilly) October afternoon, and you're tensed up in the Jeep when an old Plymouth rattles and sighs into the Westside lot: Chris Trantham's car. He's only a few minutes late, so didn't have long to beguile yourself with the thought that Andrea—the new Andrea—must've been out here a few hours ago for the Saturday swim. How Thomason must be loving his new athleticism.

You get out and approach the Plymouth after it's parked. Two doors open and two figures get out. But you concentrate on the driver. "Take a walk out to the portables and back," you tell Trantham. "Slow. Lean against my Jeep when you get back, wait for me to get out and talk." He nods and shuffles off, and you turn to other guy."Let's talk in my friend's office," you say, and drop behind the wheel of the Plymouth.

Karter follows, and turns to you, and you get a good look at him. He's got a blonde, shaggy mop of hair that despite its crudity still manages to look stylish in a ragamuffin kind of way. Maybe it's because of his face, which is brown with angled cheekbones and a strong, narrow nose and chin. His impish grin is wide and sharp at the corners, and his teeth are white and mostly straight.

"So you're Adam Karter," you say as you unzip your backpack. From it you pull a blank mask.

"And you're Gary Chen," he replies in a clear but mischievous tenor.

"Pleased to meet you," you say, and hold out your hand. He takes it.

And with his good hand clenched in yours, you can keep him from blocking you when you jam the mask onto the front of his head. He bounces off the headrest and falls forward. You catch and prop him up, and light a cigarette while waiting for the mask to finish copying him. Briefly you regret not talking to him before ambushing him; but there will be plenty of time for later for another kind sociability once you're under his face.

You flick ash out the window, and when a glow appears on Karter's face you flick away the cigarette itself.

You catch the mask before it can fall into his lap, and you've got the rockbot paste out already, so it's only a minute or so before you've got his mask finished up and fitted back onto him. You're not totally sure this is going to work—will it turn him into a rockbot, or will it only go into him, then reappear? But you think you've got the answer when he instantly stirs. "The fuck?"

"Quiet. Hit yourself. Hit yourself in the face."

There's only a fractional hesitation before he complies. And he hits himself again, harder, when you order him to. A third time, and you're satisfied when he curses in a frightened voice.

"You work for me, Karter," you tell him, shifting around to stare at him directly. "You don't tell anyone, but you work for me. You will answer every question I ask., and you will do everything I tell you. " You almost tell him that he won't argue with you, but you decide that argumentation might be a good thing, if it keeps you from making any mistakes he could warn you off of. "Do you understand?"

"Yes," he says, sounding very resentful. You can fix that, though.

"You like me, Karter," you tell him softly. "You like working for me. It's a blast. It's the most fun you've ever had. But don't tell anyone you work for me. It's a secret. You get it?"

"Sure," he says, sounding much happier about it now.

"Great. Now come with me. When Trantham gets back, we're gonna drive someplace and get very intimate with each other."

So he follows you and gets into the Jeep. You wait for Trantham, who isn't long in appearing. "Karter and I are going off to talk," you tell him. "I'll take him home. You can head off to wherever."

So he leaves, and you drive back to Acheson to the elementary school.

* * * * *

"Sweet!" you exclaim as you study yourself in the basement mirror. You grin over your shoulder at the seething Chenbot. "Don't hate me because I'm cute, dude."

"I don't, I fucking hate you because you're -- "

You snap a finger at him, and the shuts up. You go back to admiring yourself.

Adam Karter -- and that's you now -- is finely constructed from crown to toe. Slender without being skinny, with muscles that show without showing off. Brown all over from the sun under golden hair that is unkempt like Will Prescott's without looking unmanaged; and that is tousled without looking styled. Over this body you've thrown a short-sleeved plaid button-down shirt that's as thin as a cotton rag whose tails hang over a pair of jeans cut off just below the knee. Sockless feet -- like boney canoes -- are thrust into ragged canvas shoes. A plastic wristband is the only other adornment. (Like Karter's body needs adorning.)

Yes, he's very cute on the outside. Utterly rotten on the inside, but that's not something you need to dwell on. Happier to look forward to an afternoon at the Warehouse in the company of his friends -- of both sexes.

"Okay, let's go," you tell Chen, and dart past him for the stairs. "You just need to drop me off. I'll call you tomorrow when I need picking up. And you -- " You climb into the passenger side of his Jeep like a long-limbed spider monkey. "You just do what Gary Chen needs to do. Homework, housework, hanging out with Kirkham, whatever. Understand? Good." You buckle yourself in, turn on the radio to a station that Karter likes and Chen hates, slap his hand away when he tries to change it, and groove along to the beat while slapping the canvas top of the Jeep with your palms.

This really is a lot more fun than being Chen, as long as you beat back the horror of what's waiting at the mobile home where Karter lives.

* * * * *

The Chenbot is taking you to the Warehouse, where Karter spends most of his weekends, and many of his weeknights, it being a much more congenial place than the trailer home where his mother lives. It would be congenial even if the home life weren't so horrible, as it's a party scene, music scene, and social hangout all rolled up into one. It's the place where you can have the most fun in Saratoga Falls, if you've got nerve and guts. Also, youth. No one over nineteen is allowed inside.

No one knows how it all started or came to be. Oh, the building itself has a history that is easy to trace. "The Warehouse" is just that: a warehouse, on the northern edge of the industrial district, in the worst part of town. It was built next to the railroad tracks, plainly as a facility for loading, offloading, and storing goods, but has been abandoned for as long as anyone remembers. At some point -- in the early 1980s, if not earlier -- the city's teenagers began to congregate there, rearranging the inner walls, bringing in furniture, holding impromptu parties. These got larger and larger, and it became a regular thing for garage bands to perform there on weekends, as a tryout venue before attempting to play in real bars and nightclubs. Habit gradually hardened into tradition, and now bands play there almost every Friday night while Saturday nights are given over to dances and a more nightclub-like atmosphere. The whole things runs on the basis of a kind of spontaneous order, where everyone just seems to know what jobs need doing. There is some kind of administration and enforcement -- older kids who have been mysteriously instructed in how things are supposed to work, and ensure that they do work that way -- but there are never any adults around, and those people who run the weekend parties also arrange for high school muscle -- football players, mostly -- to be on hand to repel invasion by any outside gangs roving the streets. As there are never any incidents at the Warehouse, the police never involve themselves in it.

Still, there must be some kind of tentacle inside the Warehouse, for Dwayne Macauley has warned Chen off of trying to deal inside the place, and that suggests that some other dealer or group of dealers -- maybe Dwayne himself -- has staked that territory, for drugs and drink are openly dealt there. But Karter -- whose habits, like Chen's, are restricted to tobacco -- has never inquired as to who supplies the concessions. But he knows who to ask, and that means you do too.

But is that why you're going there? Or are you going there to hit on Andrea?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Another Agent for the Organization

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
The Warehouse sits behind a whitewashed cinderblock wall, almost invisible from the street, and doesn't come into view until the Chenbot pulls up next to a sliding gate made of chain link. "Remember, stay out of trouble, and call me if something comes up you can't handle," you tell it, and it rolls his eyes.

You'd fix its attitude, but it's kind of fun kicking it around.

You've got Karter's cell out before you've even hopped onto the street. Karter couldn't afford his own, but he's so popular with his peers that a collection got taken up to buy him a cheap model and subsidize a service plan. You're about to call inside for someone to open the gate when you spot a figure hunched over by the front door. "Yo!" you call, and whistle. The boy looks up, then hurries over to the gate. He's got long, curly brown hair under a backward-turned ball cap, and the eager manner of an underclassman desperate to please an upperclassman.

Which is exactly what he is.

"Hey Bell. They got you on door duty, huh," you laugh as he unlocks the gate and pushes it back. He looks immensely flattered that you know his name. "Who's inside?"

"Drew and Lane are taking care of things," the Eastman sophomore replies. "Except Drew went out a little while ago. They called me this morning, asked if I could help out." His face is pink with pride and pleasure.

"Cool. Anyone else inside? Any girls?" You waggle your eyebrows.

He blushes. "Some. Oh my God!" His grin almost wraps all the way around his head. "One of them -- !"

"I'll see if I can talk a couple of them out here for a smoke, help you on door duty, give you a chance to shine." You slap him lightly in the chest, and with a wink trot up to the main door to the place.

The walls of the Warehouse rise about forty feet above the ground, behind an acre of asphalt parking lot that is empty except on weekends. The walls are blank and weather battered, and under the eaves are some narrow and very dirty windows, most of which are broken. Massive sliding doors in the left wing open into a larger space that holds the stages and dance floors, but you head for a kind of concrete pillbox sticking out of the middle of the warehouse: this is the main entrance, and it once held -- and still does -- a small reception area. Once inside, you yell and whistle. "Yo! Lane!"

"Back here!" a voice calls, and you follow its sound into the wing opposite the dance floor, which is stocked more like a traditional bar. Lane Overstreet is there, moving crates of beers behind an improvised bar built of battered cabinetry. He starts to say, "You need any help?" but breaks off when he sees it's you.

Lane is dark of face and hair -- he's mostly of Italian-Hispanic ethnicity -- and looks all the darker in his leather jacket, leather pants, and leather fingerless gloves. A fringe of beard runs down the side of his jaw to his chin, and he's got piercings in his ears, nose, and lip. He also sports a penis ring, according to report, but you leave that to his boyfriends to confirm or refute. "Where's Drew?" you ask.

"Making a food run. I thought you were him coming back. What're you here for?"

"Thought I'd hang out, give you something to lust for. Who's making that noise?" you continue as a wailing guitar lick floats in from elsewhere.

"Lady Steel. Practicing. They're playing next Friday."

"Fuck. I was looking forward to next Friday till you told me that."

"When're you guys playing next?"

"I dunno. Paul's being a dipshit. I think it's a girl bothering him but he won't talk to me. Oh, speaking of girls, Bell said something about some slamming hotties hanging out in here." You lean your elbows on a cabinet. "Can you confirm?"

"Well, there is one you'd like."

"Yeah?"

"Tall, brunette, slim, tan all over. Built like a swimmer, but I bet her breasts cut into her time."

That would definitely be Andrea, and you grin hard at his description of her. "Sounds like she turned you on."

"Fuck you, and that's not a come on."

"Uh huh. Who's your latest fantasy?" You slowly unbutton the top button of your shirt. "You can tell me." You loose another button, and rub a fingertip on your breastbone. "Who's your latest crush?"

His eyes darken with satire. "Durras."

"Oh, fuck." You button yourself up again. "Asshole."

"He really cramps your style, doesn't he?"

"He hasn't got any style! Dumbass son of a bitch. Have you even talked to him? I've known fucking Irish setters smarter than him."

A secret smile spreads over Lane's face. "The one I like is smart."

You sneer. "The one you like is -- " The penny drops. "Hang on. You're not talking about Frank are you?" You howl as Lane grins and winks. "Oh, you gotta make a pass at him! You got him for any classes?" You grab Lane by the lapel of his jacket. "You've fucking got to make a pass at him! Do it in front of his brother!"

Lane pushes you back. "I'm not going to do that."

"He's gay, you know."

Lane starts. "Where did you hear that?"

"I didn't. It's obvious. What happened to your gaydar?"

"It's fully functional, and it doesn't go off around him."

"Only 'cos Frank Durras is too deep in the closet for you to pick it up. He's got to be gay. I mean, look at the evidence. He doesn't date, he doesn't even smile at girls, not that I've seen." You lightly grasp Lane's lapel. "Just get him quietly alone, tell him how you feel. I bet anything he's so bottled up he'll bust out crying with relief before you even get a second sentence out. You're sexy enough for him." You pat Lane's jacket down.

But Lane just smiles and goes back to unloading beers. You muse over the easy way you just teased Overstreet -- a guy you yourself have never met -- about some Eastman students you've never even heard of; then, with a shrug pad into the next room, where a lot of makeshift booths and tables -- as in regular bars -- are set up. It's empty. You crane your neck and consider going up the stairs in the back, to the second floor and the loft with the makeshift walls and the mattresses where --

All over your body, muscles tense pleasurably and relax, leaving you with a tender, half-mast erection. If you could get Andrea up there --

You shudder with anticipation, and dread.

Karter could make the approach. He's got the guts to overleap that first moment of hesitation, and the daring to dart through the obstacles, and the confidence to pursue the fleeing hart. But you know it's not Andrea Varnsworth in the next wing. It's Joe Thomason in an Andrea disguise. And would Joe Thomason fall into bed with a guy? No, if it got around, got back to Chen, that Andrea Varnsworth had fucked a dude at the Warehouse, he'd be hazed mercilessly as a fag. You'd see to that, once you were back in Chen's mask, and Thomason would be too smart to run the risk of word ever leaking out.

No, it's best to use this trip to scout out the scene, figure out how to infiltrate it, make some money off it. You return to the bar, where you hoist yourself onto the counter next to Lane. "So I need to talk to somebody about some weed," you say.

He straightens up. "You?"

"It's not like that. I'm not in the market to buy. I've got some I'm trying to offload."

"Not around here, you don't."

"Come on, I know it gets dealt. You sell it from behind this counter yourself, I've seen you."

"Because I've got a license. You don't. C'mon, Karter, you know how it works."

"I don't. Enlighten me."

"If you don't then you don't. So -- The fuck is that?" He rears away from the baggies you've just dropped on the counter.

"It's what I'm trying to sell. I got it for nothing. Nothing. Some asshole trying to be nice to me. I'm not gonna smoke it so I'm trying to turn it into cash."

Lane pushes it back toward you. "I'm not buying."

"Don't you make a profit off your 'license'? You'll make a bigger profit off -- "

"Until someone hears what I did for you, and then -- Karter, I like pain, but not that kind and in those quantities."

"Then tell me who you buy from, and I'll try selling to him."

"Sell it on your own, but not here. That's a fucking serious warning, Karter. " He jumps at the sound of a door slamming somewhere. "Get that fucking stuff out of here -- "

"No, tell me who to talk to, or Drew's gonna find those baggies sitting here between us."

Lane turns a bright red. "Fine. Reston. Talk to Reston, and he'll -- "

"Reston?" You laugh. "You're fucking with me. Okay!" You sweep up the baggies and pop them back into your shirt pocket just as a door opens and a half-naked figure comes in, lugging a cardboard box. "Catch up with you later. Yo! Jeffries!"

"Karter!" Drew Jeffries sets his burden down. He's dressed in baggy shorts, no shirt, and a backward turned cap. You exchange a complicated handshake. "Pay you a buck fifty to help lug some groceries in," he says.

"Forget the cash. Just gimme a ride after we offload your shit."

The deal is struck. "Where to?"

Well, it depends. Do you visit Matt Reston -- a bass player who is apparently linked to the marijuana concession at the Warehouse -- in the genial guise of Adam Karter, or the threatening guise of Gary Chen?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Business End of the Warehouse

choices: Tell Macauly about the football team
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You can catch more flies with honey than vinegar.

It's not a proverb that Gary Chen takes as guidance, but Adam Karter does. "I need to see Reston. You know where he lives?"

Jeffries nods. "Sure, I can take you out there. Does it have to be now?"

"Whenever. I'm in no hurry." You go out and spend the next five, ten minutes moving box after box of salty snacks from Jeffries' van to the bar. Another twenty minutes passes with you gossiping with Jeffries and Overstreet about Eastman stuff. At every moment you expect Andrea Varnsworth to walk through, but she never does, and when you follow Drew outside you still haven't confirmed that she's even at the Warehouse.

* * * * *

It's not a long drive to Matt Reston's place: He lives in a bombed-out residential section not far from the industrial zone, and not that far from Andrea's. It's a decrepit apartment complex that looks like it was once a hotel: three wings, each two stories tall, enclose a parking lot that might have once had a swimming pool in the middle of it, and the rental office occupies exactly the spot where a hotel office might have stood. The place is treeless, airless, and even in the cool October air feels hot. Jeffries pulls up in front of a door and points: Unit 147. "Want me to wait?"

"Can Reston give me a ride back?"

"Don't know."

"I'll chance it. I know some people who live close by."

Jeffries cocks a skeptical eyebrow, but lets you out. You have a feeling that unfriendly eyes are watching from behind the curtains in some of the rooms opposite, but you don't let it faze you.

A scrabbling at the door comes in answer to your knock, and Matt Reston looks through the crack. His smile is tight but friendly. "Ay, Karter, what's up? You visiting or inviting me out?"

"Either one. Got some stuff to talk to -- "

Reston pulls you inside. He puts a chain on the door and locks two bolts plus the little twistie lock in the knob. "Want some refreshment? All I've got's tap water."

"That's okay." You look around. Even the tap water in this place is probably disgusting.

There is a single bed with only a mattress, topped with a tangled sleeping bag. In the corner is a dorm-sized refrigerator and next to it a low cabinet with a hot plate. A stink of mold and garbage permeates the air.

You let out a low whistle. "How much you pay a month for luxury like this?"

"Fifty, and I'm looking for a roommate. My last one -- Well, he got himself free room and board someplace else."

"I'll think about it. I've got some business I want to talk over with you." You push the sleeping bag aside and sink onto the mattress. Reston keeps aloof by the door, even though you smile at him.

Matt Reston is not a name known to Will Prescott. He's a musician, of fair caliber, playing bass in a band called Tiger Driver. He was at Westside until the end of his sophomore year, and at Eastman through the middle of his junior year, which is when he stopped going to school altogether. As a physical specimen he's unimpressive, being short -- very short -- with a big nose, pockmarked cheeks, and long blonde hair that's had some of its locks dyed black. He makes up for these shortcomings with a nervous energy, a loud-mouthed forwardness, and a bantam-weight speed and quickness of fist. He is, you reflect with Adam Karter's intuitions, both the most likely and least likely guy to be moving marijuana inside the Warehouse.

"Somebody gave me something I don't have any use for," you say, and pull the baggies from your pocket. "Somebody else told me you might have a use for it, so I wanted to see if you'd give me something in trade for it." You toss the baggies onto the corner of the bed.

He only glances at them. "I don't have a use for that."

"You don't? Because somebody told me -- "

"Somebody's wrong."

"Then what am I supposed to do with them?"

Reston shrugs.

"Well fuck, man. I tried selling it in the Warehouse -- "

"You what?" His tone turns dangerous.

" -- and no one would touch it. They said 'Talk to Reston'. I picked up this stuff for free, but even when I said I'd -- "

"For free?" His eyebrows work. "Yeah, I can take it from you for free."

"Well, it was free to me is what I meant. Can't you offer me something for it?"

"A glass of water."

You make a face. "You know what I mean."

He sighs. "Where'd you get it?"

"I'm not going to tell you. I'm not trying to deal, man. I just wanna monetize something I don't got any use for. It was a one-time thing, I'm not gonna get anymore from -- Whoever. I just want -- "

"Alright. Fuck." He puts his hands on his hips, and an expression halfway between annoyance and fear shows on his face. "Do you have anything on you?"

"Just that." You point to the baggies.

"I mean cash. Shit," he says when you shake your head. "Because the way we do this is, you give it to me for free. You just give it to me. Then I sell it back to you, then point you in a direction where you can sell it at a profit by acting as my courier."

"The fuck? Why do we have to -- ?"

"Because that's the only way I'm touching something that doesn't come from my supplier. By not paying you for it myself."

"I'm not going to tell anyone," you protest.

"I don't care." His thrusts out a belligerent lower lip.

"Alright," you sigh after a moment's thought. "Will you buy that stuff from me in return for some information?"

"What information?" His voice drops to an octave lower than his own bass guitar could probably strike.

"Tell me how I can start dealing inside the Warehouse."

Reston's eyes blaze. "You said you weren't in the business! You said -- "

"I'm not! But apparently you're the only guy who can get me in. I've seen it coming out from behind the bar. Overstreet and Jeffries hustle it along with the beer." Reston's eyes become very hard as you continue. "How do I get a license like they've got?"

Reston stares at you very hard. "You get it from me," he says, and that's all.

You shrug. "Then as far as I'm concerned, that's good enough to trade you for this." You toss one of the baggies to his feet. "For the rest of them, let's do what you suggest. I don't have any cash on me now, but I know how to get some. How much do you want for these?" You wave the other baggies at him.

But Reston doesn't answer. He stares intently at the baggies, as though trying to will them into leaping off the bed to join the other one at his feet. "Trade you more information for the rest of it?"

"What information?"

"How to get a license from me, to deal in the Warehouse?"

"Yeah? Okay."

"'Cos I don't think you can get any use out of it. But here it is. You get a spot inside, a little corner of your own, and you don't sell outside that spot, and you sure as fuck don't sell inside any corner belonging to any of the other guys. Like, you don't deal in the bar. That's Lane's area. You also buy a set amount from me at the start of every month, and you sell at a set price, no discounts. I hear you're giving discounts, and I tell someone, and he breaks your legs."

"Harsh, man."

"Gotta be that way."

"This area, what kind of area is it? Is it like a physical space, or -- "

"A physical space."

"Time limits? Like, only certain days?"

He turns wary. "Weekends are the only time when people are around. You can't sell -- "

"But what if I got some friends to start hanging out there on weekdays? Late afternoon, early evening. Would I be able to bring stuff in, sell it to them? Or would I have to let Overstreet -- "

Reston sucks in a sharp breath. "We've never had to think about that," he says. "And people don't like going to the Warehouse on weeknights."

"I know that. But I also know how to make people comfortable." You grin cockily.

Cupidity plays in Reston's eyes. "I'd have to think about it," he says. "But maybe. If you're serious about this."

"I'd have to think about it too." You toss all but one baggie at his feet. "So how about we go pick up that money so I can pay you for this last baggie? Then you can tell me who I can sell it to. Unless you want to just tell me who I'd be selling it to, and then I'll just give it to you now?"

Reston visibly tenses, and gives you the wary look of an animal that can't decide if it's being tempted into a trap. For a very long moment you're convinced he's going to curse you and maybe fly at you. But he suddenly relents with a shrug. "Probably you couldn't sell it to them anyway," he says. "You don't know them, they go to Westside. Best if you just leave it to me. You eat yet? I'll buy you supper around the corner, show my appreciation for our deal."

"Sure, thanks. But who do you know at Westside who buys?"

"A football player named Carstairs. He sometimes picks up a little from me when we run into each other at the Warehouse. That's who I'd send you to."

* * * * *

You let Reston buy you a cheap meal at the nearby Waffle House, then have him drop you at Hochstetter Park, where you call the Chenbot. Back at the elementary school you fit yourself into Chen's mask to think over what you've learned, and what to do about it.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Inside Dope on the Inside Dope

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You call Dwayne Macauley -- Chen's supplier. He's skittish about talking and even more skittish about seeing you, but when you tell him it's about Westside's football team, he relents and sets up a meeting.

* * * * *

Paranoia is your friend when dealing with Dwayne, and paranoia is Dwayne's friend too: you don't park in front of his place, but the next block over, at the address he gave you. It's the near-twin of his own house -- a cinderblock shack, this one augmented with a rusted-out bathtub out front -- and the door is opened by a skinny woman who is probably in her thirties but has the face of a sixty-year-old. She lets you into the living room and at a grunt from Dwayne goes out the back door.

Dwayne is twitchy, and he glares at you from a broken sofa, but you plant your feet firmly and return his look with a hard one of your own. "What's this bullshit about the Westside football squad?"

"What bullshit's that?"

"You know what bullshit I mean, cocksucker, or you wouldn't be talking to me about it now. Westside's my territory, I get to break the face of any other fucker who tries dealing there. So how come I find the football team's getting their ganja from someone else?"

"That's your problem. Find out who it is and break his face."

"I could and I would, but I don't go off half-cocked. I gave it a little thought, and deduced that you'd be unhappy if I removed Matt Reston's jaw with a baseball bat."

And Dwayne blinks. Once, but hard. His eyes water a little, and he looks away. "Okay, this isn't about Westside, Chen."

"The fuck it isn't! Those -- "

"Are the football players dealing at Westside?"

"I'm not talking about that, I'm talking about the cocksucker who's selling to them!"

"It matters because this isn't about Westside. They're not buying inside your territory, they're buying at the Warehouse, like any asshole can. If they bring it onto school property, that's their business, same as anyone who buys at -- "

"Then the Warehouse is a fucking back door into my property! I want it closed!"

"I'm not closing the Warehouse!" Dwayne's eyes blaze. "And I'm not -- !" He masters his anger. "You go to the Warehouse, Chen?"

"No. Well, not often."

"The Westside football squad acts as security there. Part of the deal is they get to buy product at a discount."

Your eyes narrow. "And Reston handles the actual sale?"

"Now you've got it. Technically, he buys their bundle from me at the same price he gets, then sells it to the squad at cost, so he's not making a profit, he's just passing it along." Dwayne spreads his hands. "It's not a back door, not if they're not reselling it into the school, and if they are, tell me so I can straighten it out. But if I cut 'em off, they won't go to you for a supply." A thought strikes him. "Unless they do already. Do they?"

"Sometimes," you grudgingly admit.

"There you are. You're not losing any business, not any business you're entitled to, because they're not buying at Westside, they're buying at the Warehouse, and that's mine."

"You actually run it? That's your enterprise, the Warehouse?"

Dwayne maintains a stony silence. You sigh deeply and stare at the wall over his head. "Okay, I get it."

"Good. Then if there's nothing else -- "

"Hang on. I might have a proposition for you."

He waits patiently while you run some numbers and simulations through your head.

* * * * *

A lot of stuff that puzzled you now makes sense. You'd learned from Mendoza that Carstairs was buying weed at a steep discount, the kind of discount that wouldn't make financial sense to a dealer. Now it does: the discounted weed is payment for services rendered elsewhere. At the same time, you want --

Or Chen would want, and you're acting as his agent.

-- to sew up Westside tightly as your own game preserve. You are also casting covetous eyes on the Warehouse as a field for expansion, if in no other way than as a place where an extended Westside user base could regularly party, thereby giving you a regular and easy-to-supply demand point. You could solve both problems at a stroke if you could take over the Warehouse franchise from Reston.

After a few minute's thought, you turn your attention back to Macauley, who has started to shift impatiently on the sofa.

"If I asked Reston to describe his financials, what would he tell me?"

"Beats the fuck out of me. Why don't you ask him?"

"Because I'm here, not there, and I'm betting you could tell me and he might not." You hazard tipping your hand a little. "I might be interested in staging a merger of interests with him."

"Merger of -- ? Where the fuck do you learn to talk like that? But Reston's not gonna be interested," Macauley snorts.

"What about a buyout, then? Tell me what I'd be taking over, and I'll run the numbers to see if I can make it attractive to him to sell out to me."

Now it's Dwayne's turn to take his time answering. He scratches his neck and rubs his nose. He looks all over the room except at you. "He buys sixty ounces a month from me," he says at last.

"Same as me, then."

"Same as you, but he only sells it into the Warehouse. Standing deal with the football squad is that they get six ounces for sixteen hundred. Reston sells the rest into the Warehouse at -- Well, it's up to him, but he's making a profit." Dwayne holds your eye. "And on top of what he pays me for the supply, he pays me fifteen hundred a month to service the money I advanced him to set it up."

Ah. So Dwayne's branching out into Karol Mathis's line of work.

You lean against the wall and tug at your lip while running the numbers in your head. If Dwayne is being truthful -- and he probably is; you've threatened to talk to Reston to get the real numbers -- then Reston is turning a profit of about thirty-five hundred a month on his operation at the Warehouse, and a personal profit of two thousand a month after all business expenses. He's living in a lot more squalor than he'd have to suffer for two thousand a month. Either he's got a serious monkey on his back, or he's scrimping like mad so as to pay off the debt to Dwayne.

Either way, you could run his operation more profitably than him since you wouldn't have his living expenses to worry about. You could relieve him of his debt -- which would probably ease his mind -- and even give him a monthly payment in return for selling you his right to manage the Warehouse. You could probably even get him to accept that monthly payment in the form of a management fee.

"If I could talk him into selling out to me," you ask Dwayne, "would you have a problem with me taking over his Warehouse operation? Including servicing the football squad on your terms?"

"You'd be spreading yourself too thin."

"Not if I left Reston to run it. Here, I take over his debt, so he doesn't have to worry about payments to you. I buy from you on the same terms, sell to him at, I dunno, three hundred an ounce. Whatever he makes selling into the Warehouse is profit to him, free and clear. But that give me enough to service the debt to you and still drop a profit into my pocket."

"He'd be taking a pay cut."

"I've seen the way he lives. I bet it would feel like a pay raise if he didn't have to worry about servicing his debt to you. And, no offense, man, but I bet he'd sacrifice a little to get you out of his life."

Dwayne smiles faintly. "If you can talk him into it, it doesn't make any difference to me. Hell -- " He stands up with a sigh. "You're more dependable than him anyway."

"I ain't making a formal offer, you understand," you tell him. "I was doing the figuring off the top of my head, and Reston -- "

He waves you off. "Sure. We'll talk. But sooner rather than later, okay?" He fixes you with a sharp eye. "I don't like uncertainty."

"Then what are you doing in this line of work?"

He grimaces. "Beats the fuck out of me."

* * * * *

Your proposal to Dwayne would give you what you want, and you think Chen would be pleased to see such a deal go through. Of course, the simplest way of managing Chen's finances would be to turn Macauley into a Dwaynebot. Fuck, you could turn Karol Mathis into a Mathisbot, which would simplify everything. But you have the sinking feeling that the top of each pyramid is just a facing stone in the side of a bigger pyramid, so that if you turned even Mathis into a 'bot you'd just have his finances to worry about. No, the simplest way to fix up Chen's operation is to get it into a shape that he'd be happy with.

Trouble is, you doubt anything would keep him happy for long. Case in point: You still haven't said anything to "Andrea" about competing for a spot on the cheerleader squad. It's a stupid demand Chen has laid on you; and if he's stupid and grasping that way, there are a lot of other ways for him to be stupid and grasping -- more ways than you can possibly satisfy.

You go home, deal with the parents a little while, and make an early night of it. In bed, in the dark, you concentrate on the fact that you're trying to get away from Chen. You can do that with the 'bot masks, if you're willing to steal someone's life. Just return everyone to normal, and disappear into some other life.

At the same time, you can't help nursing the proud thought that you're handling Chen's business better than he himself ever did.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Takeover Artist

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
It's Sunday, a little after noon, when you give Reston's door three hard knocks. You've been awake and busy since a little after eight, and all morning you've been itching to talk to him. But your gut tells you he's not the kind to be out of bed until after eleven, at least.

But when he peers out through the narrow crack between the door and the door frame, he looks like he's just dragged himself up. He also looks hung over. "Yo, I wanna talk to you," you hiss at him. "Don't make me haul you out here to do it."

You see his Adam's apple bobble. After a long hesitation, he opens the door. You step in, removing your sunglasses. That gives you a better view of the scene inside. Most obviously, that would include Reston, in a tank-top and underwear; a tangled-up sleeping bag; and the girl inside it.

It's Sienna Goldman. She glares at you.

"I wanna make this alone," you tell Reston.

He doesn't answer before Sienna does. "Then take it outside," she growls.

You keep your eyes on Reston. "Don't make me drag her out into the parking lot."

She answers again. "Just try it, motherfucker."

"Can't we talk someplace else?" Reston asks. He is very white, and his hair is lank and there are dark bags under his eyes.

"No, because it's about business."

"I bet whatever business you've got to talk about -- " Sienna starts to say. But in that moment you leap at her.

It's an instinct, almost. You brought a blank mask with you, in case Reston proved recalcitrant, and in one bound you're next to the bed and you've got the mask out of the bag slung by your side. Sienna half-twists away, but you jam the mask down onto her face and she goes limp and silent. You spin back on Reston, who's advancing behind you, and send him reeling back with a hard punch to his chest. The blow feels like it shattered your own arm, but you pretend not to felt anything, and get in Reston's face with a snarl.

"You wanna take it outside, we can take it outside. Or I can shut you up the same way I shut your whore up. Which is it gonna be?"

To say that Reston looks confused would be an understatement. His eyes are wide and the rest of his features look like they're trying to fly off his face. "What did you -- ?"

"She'll be okay. She's just gonna be out for a bit. We'll leave her here and take a ride, she'll be happy about that when she wakes up, but she'll think twice about giving me any lip back the next time she sees me. Now get cleaned up, get a shower or something, get her bitch-stink off you. Get on!" You shove Reston toward the alcove where the bathroom will be. He half-stumbles into it and shuts the door. A minute later the shower water turns on.

He takes a good long time, which gladdens you, for it gives the mask time to come back out of Sienna. You cuss yourself for wasting it on her. Now you'll have to be persuasive with Reston if you're going to make a deal with him. Maybe your show of force will work to your advantage; or maybe it will make him truculent and resentful. You search his face when he reappears, but his expression is hooded. He slips on some skinny jeans and a sweatshirt, and with one brief glance back at Sienna follows you out to the Jeep.

* * * * *

You get up on the interstate, so that Reston won't be tempted to jump out at any stoplights during your negotiation, and head east, not because there's anything interesting east of town but because that's the closest and easiest on-ramp to take. You quickly come to the point. "I talked to Dwayne Macauly yesterday," you say. "About his business, about the Warehouse, and about you."

Reston says nothing until you glance over at him, and then he just says "Uh huh." He is squinting against the sunlight, which is another advantage you've got since you're wearing shades.

"How much a month do you clear?"

"None of your fucking business, Chen."

"I'll guess anyway. You pay Dwayne fifteen hundred a month for the privilege of making a profit on the product he sells you. So I figure you're making at least a thousand for yourself. Jesus, Reston. With that kind of profit, the fuck are you doing living in that kind of a dump back there?"

"That's also none of your fucking business."

"Yeah? Well what if I made your fucking business into my fucking business? What if I gave you fifteen hundred a month to give me your fucking business?"

Again, you have to look over at him when he doesn't reply. He looks baffled. "The fuck are you talking about?"

"I'm talking about taking over your Warehouse business. Actually, I'm talking about taking over from Macauly. You're buying sixty ounces a month from him. Instead, you start buying sixty ounces a month from me."

"You're fucking nuts," he says, and sounds scared. "Macauly would -- "

"Macauly would be okay with it. I'd be buying him out too."

"Jesus! You can't afford to -- "

"Can't I?" You give him another glance. "Do you know all about my fucking business that you know I couldn't afford it?"

Reston's mouth hangs open. It takes him a moment to find his voice. "I don't believe you."

"We'll talk it over with Macauly, you'll see. You just have to tell me you're interested. Are you?"

Reston is silent for a very long time. The changing expressions on his face suggest that his thought processes are very painful. "I don't understand," he says at last.

"Then let me give you some numbers." You're coming up onto a country road that services some of the farms in this part of the country, and you exit onto it and park on the shoulder so that you can give Reston your full attention. He flinches back as you reach past to take a notepad and pencil out of the glove compartment. Quickly you scribble out a rough income statement.

"I don't know what you're selling at," you say, "but Macauly tells me you're buying fifty-four ounces from him at two-fifty-five an ounce. I'm not including the six ounces you're selling at cost to Erik Carstairs." You hold his eye, and he blinks once, heavily. "You're also paying Macauly fifteen-hundred a month on a debt he's carrying for you. That means you're paying him fifteen-two-seventy a month." You circle the figure.

"To cover those expenses, you've got to sell those fifty-four ounces for at least two-eighty-three an ounce, and I bet you're selling for least three hundred an ounce inside the Warehouse, which tells me you're making a profit of at least nine-thirty a month, and probably closer to a thousand." In fact, you suspect he's selling for a lot more than three hundred an ounce, but you're opening with a low-ball bid. "Now, how far off the mark am I?"

"It's still not your business."

"Are you so fucking hung over you can't come up with anything else to say? Then let me make you an offer. Step one." You cross out the number "1500." "I make your debt to Macauly go away. You don't pay him anything. You never see him again. You're totally square with him because I've paid it off for you. How happy would you be to be out from under that debt? Because you know what he'll do to you if you're ever short on paying it."

Reston swallows again, and his eyes dart about.

"Step two, you stop buying ganja from Macauly." You cross out the number "13770". "I take over the business inside the Warehouse, which means you don't got to buy anything anymore. Bingo-bango, your expenses go to zero." You draw a big circle on the notepad.

"Then comes the magic. Step three. I pay you a thousand dollars a month to do squat. Jack shit. Nada. To sit in your fucking room and jack off five times a day. A thousand bucks a month just to get the fuck out of my way so I can have the run of the Warehouse." You write "1000" in the notebook. "That's yours, every month, no expenses, no debt, no work. Fuck, I'll won't even bitch if you keep hanging out at the Warehouse every month, playing your shit licks and getting shitfaced every night. I'll pay you a thousand bucks a month just to get out of the way and let me run the Warehouse business."

Reston's eyes are starting from his head now. "You're out of your fucking mind. Why the fuck would -- " But he stops and licks his lips and gets a shifty look. "And how long would you pay me a thousand a month," he sneers.

"As long as you want me to. Would you take this deal?"

He doesn't answer. When you lower your shades, you notice that he's trembling.

Finally he shrugs in a spasmodic way. "Sure. I wouldn't turn down free money. But there's no fucking way you're -- "

"So you'd take a thousand a month from me to give me your Warehouse business."

He takes a deep, shuddering breath. "Yeah, I guess, but I don't believe your shit -- "

You put out your hand. "Shake on it. Promise me right now you'll take this deal, and then I'll explain how we work it. You'll see, it'll work out fine. You'll make a thousand a month without doing hardly any work, no debt to Macauly. You don't like the mechanics, you can back out. But shake on that number. A thousand a month."

You hold your breath as he hesitates.

Then Reston takes your hand in his. His grip is weak, but strong enough you think you can hold him to the promise.

"The way we work it is this," you tell him. "You keep dealing in the Warehouse, but you buy sixty ounces a month at two-ninety an ounce." You scribble out the numbers. "You just got to sell it at three-oh-seven an ounce, and you'll clear a thousand a month profit. Can you sell sixty an ounce at three-oh-seven?"

He stares, like the moron he is. "Uh, sure," he croaks.

"Then we have a deal."

He hesitates again. But when you put out your hand again, he takes it.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Takeover Artist, Part 2

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You don't give Reston a chance to argue or second-guess himself, and you don't make any inquiries about the way he runs his Warehouse operations. You throw the Jeep in high gear and race back down the interstate to town. On the way, you call Macauly to set up a meeting. Reston makes a few mewling noises, but you keep a stony silence all the way back. By the time you pull up in front of Dwayne's, Reston's spirit seems broken, and during the subsequent meeting he totally surrenders to your "buyout" of his interest in the Warehouse operation. There are no papers to sign, of course, but in the conversation that follows, everyone's obligations to everyone else get spelled out clearly. The other two are very quiet at the conclusion of things, and you can tell from Dwayne's expression that he's not sure you'll be able to pull things off.

You're confident, though. You've got magical aces in the hole if things get rough.

You're happy with Reston, and you show it by treating him out to lunch at Pizza Hut. In a corner booth, you quietly talk and force him to disgorge all the details of the operation you'll be taking over.

"I've got it set up so these guys sell in different parts of the Warehouse," he tells you. "Like, Lane Overstreet sells in the bar, and Oscar Cantu sells up in the rooms. I sell them a set amount each month at a set price, like Dwayne sells to me."

"You parcel out the whole fifty-four ounces to them?"

"Uh huh."

"You sell them all at the same price?"

He swallows. "Yeah."

"What's the price?"

He hangs his head and stares at the table top. "Three-twenty."

"An ounce? So, less the fifteen hundred you were paying Macauly, you were actually clearing two thousand a month."

His voice is so soft it is almost inaudible. "Yeah."

"Okay. Well, I hiked your unit price, but you also shed that payment to Macauly, so net you'll still be pulling down around two thousand a month. More, if you find a way to jack your sales price up."

Reston's jaw falls open. "That's okay?"

"Why wouldn't it be?"

With exquisite caution he says, "But you thought I was only making a thousand a month."

"So I was wrong, so what?"

His caution increases. "You're not going to raise your price to me because -- ?"

"No. We made a deal and I don't go back on my deals." That's a lie coming from Gary Chen, but you think it's better here to pretend otherwise. "The deal still works for me with the numbers I used. I want the Warehouse, and I got other deals that will make it pay off to me good. Gonna make me a metric fuck-ton with those other deals, but I need the Warehouse to make 'em work."

You lean in close to him. "In fact, I need you to manage the Warehouse business for me. That's worth two thousand a month to me, or however much you make. So let's just keep things going the way they are. You sell to your guys, in their territories, the same way you're doing now. Hardly any work. Only you buy from me at my price, and you leave it to me to bust kneecaps if someone needs a kneecap busted. The other changes I want, I'll let you know when they happen."

Reston's eyes widen. "I don't own the Warehouse, Chen. If you've got some other plans -- "

"I do, but they won't get me in trouble with Macauly or whoever actually runs the place. They won't even notice. But you keep your mouth shut anyway about what I just said. I'm just telling you that so you don't give me any shit when I make those new moves. Oh, better give me a list of all the guys you sell to, and what their areas are."

It's not a long list. Overstreet deals in the bar; Jeffries deals in the booth area; Oscar Cantu in the bedrooms upstairs; Alex Massey and Michael Hurst partner on the dance floor; anyone playing in a band gets theirs from Dean Stratton. Reston looks puzzled if you ask if these "areas" are in force only on weekends, and you can see a dawning memory that Adam Karter asked him a similar question. "There's never any demand on weeknights," he says. "I guess Lane could sometimes sell to whoever shows up."

"Then that's the first change. Start chopping it up by days of the week. Tell your guys that they can only sell on weekends. They gotta get another license, buy more supply from you if they wanna deal on weekdays or weeknights. And you're not selling any of those licenses, not yet."

"You got plans for the weeknights?" A light dawns in his eyes. "Is that how you're going to make that metric fuck-ton?"

"Something like that."

"Because the guys who run the Warehouse might not like it -- "

"I told you, it won't bother them, and you let me worry about it if it does."

Reston looks dubious but acquiesces. Over the pizza, he gives you some more details, which you absorb attentively even though they don't really affect your plans.

* * * * *

After dropping off Reston back at his place, you call Trantham and the Karterbot to a brief meeting at Hochstetter Park, where you tell the fake Trantham that Karter will be taking over Trantham's distribution business and that he needs to inform all the guys he sells to of this fact. Evans accepts this dutifully, then asks how long he has to keep playing Trantham. "Until the next time we make a distribution through Eastman," you tell him. "So that all of Trantham's customers are used to the idea that Karter is supplying them."

Then you send him away and take the Karterbot in hand. Carefully you explain your desires while trying not to pin the -bot down too rigidly. He needs to get all of his friends, and people who want to be his friends, to start habitually meeting at the Warehouse on weekdays after school. First he should concentrate on his bandmates and on people he will be selling weed to. He needs to get them used to the idea of regular, casual, but informal after-school parties at the Warehouse, and then to gradually broaden the base of people who will be hanging out there. He should sell to anyone who attends. To make sure he doesn't get in any trouble, he should talk to Matt Reston about getting a license. You've already told Reston to talk to you if anyone inquires about a new license, so things should go smoothly on that end.

Then after sending him away, you turn to the last bit of business necessary for taking over Reston's operation. You call Erik Carstairs.

He can't see you right away, and in fact it's nearly nine-thirty at night -- which leaves you courting trouble at home -- before you rendezvous with him at McDonald's. His eyes narrow when you inform him that from now on he'll be getting his weed from you. "I don't think I like this," he says.

"Doesn't matter if you don't like it," you tell him bluntly. "The business has changed. Reston's retired, so I'm taking over."

"No one says we have to buy anything from anyone."

"Does this mean your guys aren't working at the Warehouse anymore?"

He freezes. "What's that got to do with -- ?"

"Isn't that the deal? Your guys are the muscle out there, and in return you get ganja at a discount."

"Then I guess you know about it all."

"Fucking right I do. I had it explained to me on account of Reston's quitting. So what's got your ladybits in a twist about it?"

"My problem is I didn't want to fucking deal with you, Chen, which is why when we set it up with Macauly we had Reston handling it."

"What's your problem with me?" You feel you temper blossoming.

He smirks. "Let's just pretend it's because I don't like monopolies. And now you got a monopoly at school."

"It doesn't matter if I do. Your deal is still with Macauly, not with me. I'm just the fucking delivery boy, and I don't even get a tip."

His smirk widens. "Maybe we could toss you a couple of extra dollars, and you could throw in some eggrolls."

You grip the edge of the table. "Macauly's changed the delivery method, that's all," you say, trying to keep the fury from your voice. "You accept delivery from me, or you tell Macauly you're quitting."

Carstairs holds your eye, then shrugs again. "Fine. As long as you're reliable."

"I am."

"I'll send someone to pick it up from you when it's time. When are you making the next delivery?"

"Last day of the month. No later."

His face tightens. "Reston was delivering in the middle of the month."

"Then you get a double delivery this month."

But he shakes his head. "I can't pay that much that soon."

"Then I guess you can wait. It'll keep."

Carstairs sighs, and ends the meeting soon after that.

His manner leaves you feeling thoughtful, and it reminds you of certain mysteries you'd not had time to ponder. Such as: Where is he getting the fifteen-hundred a month for the six ounces he's buying? According to Mendoza, Carstairs passes it out free to his teammates, so he's not collecting contributions from them. You don't think he comes from a rich family, or has the kind of afterschool job that would support that kind of habit. And why is he so reluctant to deal with you?

You could get answers to those mysteries, perhaps, by moving Mendoza out of Kevin Hall and into Erik Carstairs. Indeed, there might be advantages to controlling a large portion of the football team.

Or dangers, since it would be Mendoza under the Carstairs mask.

Unless you made another -bot.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Done Deals

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Put in a ringer for Carstairs? It's a passing thought, but only that. You don't need to domesticate Erik Carstairs to make that end of the business work.

Afterward, you check the texts that came in while you were dealing with him. One is from Tanner Evans says he'll give you the three-sixty he owes you at school tomorrow. Mrs. Chen wants you home -- now! And Chelsea is reminding you that Karol Mathis needs your final order for November (and the money to pay for it) by Monday at seven o'clock.

* * * * *

"What have you got to be twitchy about," Kirkham asks Monday afternoon just before AP Stat is about to start. The question and the voice he asks it in -- stainless steel wrapped in velvet malice -- would have terrified you back in your own skin. As Gary Chen, though, you hardly notice it.

"Just got a lot on my mind," you tell him. You stop drumming the desk with your fingers. A moment later -- when you realize you've just shifted the fidget -- you stop your leg from jogging.

"So go jack off and get it out of your system. You're making me nervous."

He's nervous? He should try being you. You've meeting with "Chelsea" this afternoon -- passing along money for the November supply -- and you're not sure what you're going to tell Chen about the weekend's deal-making.

It would be easiest to say nothing: what he doesn't know won't hurt him, and it certainly won't hurt him to know that you've just doubled his volume of business and might be on your way to tripling it. Besides, you fear that if he realizes what a great job you're doing, he'll just force you to keep doing it.

On the other hand, the whole idea is to get his business running so good that heasks for his life back. And if you don't tell him the kind of profits you've set him up to make -- and if your projections aren't wildly off the mark, it looks like you're set to triple his profits -- he may think that you're ripping him off if and when he discovers what you've been doing behind his back.

All morning you've been leaning first one way and then another about what to tell him. You're still preoccupied with it now, so that not until Joshua Call is actually next to you and leaning in to mutter in Kirkham's ear do you notice that he's come into the classroom.

You can't make out what he says to Kirkham, or what Kirkham says in reply, but neither of them pay any attention to you. Once Call's gone you lean over to your friend. "The fuck was that about?"

"Exactly."

"What?"

"Fucking."

"Are you and Call getting romantic?"

You can feel Kirkham's eyes shift onto you behind his shades. He removes the toothpick from his mouth. "If you're feeling jealous, I'll let you suck my cock any time you want."

"I'll leave that to Call." You slouch. Another emotion has started squirming wormily in you gut, and you're horrified to recognize that it is something like jealousy. Kirkham is supposed to be your friend.

Jesus, maybe I really am turning into Chen, you think darkly.

* * * * *

Then a last minute hitch in plans leaves you scrambling. Even after Evans -- very white in the face -- passes you the money he owes you, you're still almost four thousand short of what you need for Mathis to get all the weed you're planning to sell. Of course, that's the money that you're supposed to get from Andrea Varnsworth, but she hasn't brought it to school, and she refuses point-blank (in her text messages) to let you come over to her house to get it: u said midweek, its monday.

So on your way out of school you risk a call to Dwayne. "I need some dimes," you tell him.

"How many?"

"Three-sixty. Like, now."

"Three hundred and sixty {i]dimes? I don't carry that kind of pocket change. Talk to Reston, he's due to -- "

"He doesn't pick up his stuff until the second week of the month. He's not going to pay me in advance for shit I'm not due to deliver in two weeks."

"You pay Mathis in advance. Reston can pay you."

"It's all going into your pocket anyway, man. It's the money I need to cover this month's order."

"Doesn't matter, I told you, I don't have that kind of -- Talk to Reston anyway. If he kicks, tell him to call me and I'll make it square with him."

You're dubious, but you call Reston anyway. He's at the Warehouse, naturally, but he agrees to meet you at his flophouse. And to your surprise he doesn't kick when you tell him you need thirty-six hundred dollars from him in advance of when he's usually due to pay. In fact, he's bright-eyed and smiling, and you can only figure he's so relieved to be out from under all his obligations that's he's forgotten to be paranoid and grasping.

"I'll pay you back Wednesday," you tell him. "And I'll toss in a little on top to show my gratitude anyway."

"I guess we'll talk about it," he says evasively. "Make yourself at home, be back in a bit." He goes to the door.

"What? Where are you -- ?"

"You don't think I keep that kind of shit around here, do you?" he asks at the open door. "I'll be back in -- "

You scramble after him. "Listen, you don't have to show me where you keep it, but let's meet somewhere else for the hand off." You think fast. "How about the Railroad Museum. You know where I mean?" He doesn't, so you give him directions. "It's close to someplace I have to be anyway." You check the time on your phone. "Shit, I'm running late as it is."

"Whatever, man." He glances at you queerly. "You really don't want to hang around here?"

"Toldja, I'm late for another appointment. Besides," you can't help adding, "if you don't like keeping your money bag here, I don't much like picking it up here."

* * * * *

Reston meets you twenty minutes later at the museum, and with the balance of the money for Mathis you race off to Ben's BBQ to meet Chen. What with the extra stops, you're almost fifteen minutes later.

Chelsea is quietly fuming in a back booth. "Where the fuck have you been?" she demands as you scoot into the seat opposite her. "Never mind. Names have come rolling in for the squad tryouts all weekend, and guess who's name is not on it?"

"Mr. Walberg?"

"Is that supposed to be a funny?" she snarls. "I'm talking about Andrea."

"Oh, right. I haven't talked to her about -- "

"Why not?"

"I been too busy getting laid."

Her mouth drops open.

"Anyway, I got the rest of -- "

"Bullshit. You're not getting laid." Then she turns shrewd. "You're not, are you?"

"You know better than to ask a dumbass question like that. But you're finally getting laid, aren't you? Ten times a day, I hear, and not that I'm dying to know, but exactly how delicious is Seth Javits' tiny, tiny dong?"

Chelsea flushes to pink, then to red, then to purple. You take advantage of her fury-choked silence to press on with regular business. "You still insist on being the one to pay off Mathis?" She doesn't reply, except for a sloppy kind of swallowing noise. "Because I got it all here, and if he asks where the extra cash came from, you're going to have to improvise."

She finally masters herself, at least enough that she can speak, even if it is in a kind of croak. "Extra cash?"

"Exactly. I told you. Or you told me. Gotta step up the sales, you said, so I'm buying eighteen-kay this month."

"Where did you get the money?"

"You know where. I told you. Well, after you told me."

She looks puzzled for a moment, then hard fire comes into her eyes. "That's right. Andrea. Speaking of which -- "

You reach under the table and jam a fat envelope into her hand. "I have to be at the country club in a little while. I hope you remember that too, you fucking bint." You get up.

"Prescott! I mean -- Gary." You half turn at your name. Both of them.

She is trying very hard to master her temper. Her color is still bad, and she gulps one breath down, and then another. She closes her eyes and she pushes the corners of her lips into a smile. "Gary," she says very sweetly when she opens her eyes again. "Please sit down. I know you have to be someplace, but I'd really like to talk to you."

"What about?"

She shrugs. "Us?"

"I don't -- "
