"And who's playing him now?"

"A robot. Didn't Call tell you about those?"

"He might've mentioned something, but I wasn't paying a lot of attention. What are they?"

"It's like a fake version of someone, a robot, that takes care of yourshit for you while you're someplace else. I have robots in for Evans and Thomason and Mendoza, and Call and a couple of others."

He gets a shrewd look on his face. "Took you awhile to get the programming right, didn't it?"

"What do you mean?"

"Now that I think about it, there was something goofy about some of those guys." The shrewd look softens into a thoughtful one. "Been a couple of people gone goofy lately, now that I think about it."

"I had to do some experimenting, but the formula's perfected. Go talk to Thomason, the robot, I mean. You won't find anything 'off' about him."

Kirkham grunts. "But speaking of Call, what kind of punishment did you have in mind for him?"

"I haven't thought of one yet."

He raises his chin at you. "If you won't give me Kelsey to play with, how about taking Jelena from him and giving her to me?"
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Making of a Reluctant Partnership

choices: Abandon your plan to roboticize Kirkham
Make Kirkham, not Justin Roth, your alter ego
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
What's the difference is between Gary Chen and David Kirkham? The difference is that Kirkham really would try pulling off this dumb-ass idea about selling young bodies to billionaires. Because that's Kirkham all over, all mouth and swagger. He'd go see this tech billionaire and show him the masks and make him an offer, and then he'd find out real fast how easy it is for a billionaire to fuck him over, because no one -- you're pretty sure -- gets to be a billionaire by making square deals with dipshit high school seniors.

But Gary Chen has both brain and guts. That's how he got to be dealing tens of thousands of dollars of weed every month without even Kirkham guessing what was going on. Gary Chen is too smart to go for Kirkham's idea.

But Gary Chen is also too smart to piss Kirkham off by swatting his idea down completely. That's the thing about swaggering morons like David Kirkham: there's no telling how much they can fuck other people up, even accidentally.

So you play it cool.

"That's not a bad idea," you tell him. "A little bolder than I was thinking. You really think we can fuck with a billionaire?"

Kirkham shrugs. "What's the downside? If these things work the way you and Call say they work -- " His face goes taut, as though he's assailed by a sudden doubt. But with another shrug he dismisses it. "I wanna fucking try it out, though. Who you got I can use when we make the approach?"

"You mean someone new? Or someone I already got replaced with a fake? 'Cos if it's someone new then I have to make a copy."

"How about someone at that fucking country club," he says. "Disguise us up as one of them, and we can get in to see that tech guy easy."

"Sure. I can think of the perfect dumbass for you." You grin. "Martin Gardinhire."

"Fuck." Kirkham's lips curl around the toothpick. "Maybe I don't wanna go in disguise."

"Sure you do. In fact, I'll get on the prep work immediately. Meet me back here this afternoon, around five."

"What for? What prep work?"

"I'll explain it to you then. But I got to pick up some supplies, and I won't be done doing that until around five." The bell rings. "Come on, we better get to class."

But you don't go to class. After a leisurely walk with Kirkham back to the school building -- during which he swears and mutters against the idea of pretending to be Martin Gardinhire -- you sprint hard for the parking lot, leap into the Jeep, and race back to the elementary school.

The Chenbot, once activated, complains bitterly about not getting to skip the day with you. You kick it out of the basement with orders to return to school and avoid Kirkham, and to put him off with phrases like "We'll talk about it later" if he does try to talk about things, and to be back at the clubhouse as soon as possible after last bell.

You settle down to making another mask.

* * * * *

Kirkham's in a bad mood -- even for Kirkham -- when you find him again at five o'clock. "So what's this fucking prep work that took you so goddam long," he asks. He's squatting by the corner of the portables and stabbing his leg with the blunt end of a pen. "I got stuck here with Roth and his boy toys."

You leap to the defense of the guy you plan to turn yourself into. "What's wrong with Roth? And he's no fag, not that I've heard."

"Who knows what he does when he gets high." Kirkham stands up. "Or who he does. Guy's got no standards. And you can't fucking tell me Small and Semple aren't pining for his cock. Coupla girls is what they are."

"You're talking like you're jealous. But I got the stuff now, come on, I'll show it to you."

Into one of your regular portables you lead him, and set a sack down on the teacher's desk with a clunk. "Okay, we gotta do two things," you tell your erstwhile would-be co-conspirator. You take out one mask. "Second of all, we gotta get this thing onto the guy you're going to impersonate. But first of all -- " You take out the newly made mask. "We have to put this one on to you."

Kirkham visibly stiffens. "What for?"

"To make a robot of you. Didn't Call explain that to you?"

"Yeah." Kirkham folds his arm and glares at you from behind the shades. "Why do I need a robot?"

"Two reasons. First, you don't want to disappear, right? You still got a mom and a little brother who'll miss you, and school, and all the other shit."

"It'll only be for an afternoon."

"Maybe, but maybe not. If we're gonna do this thing like you want, using disguises to talk to this billionaire, we're probably not gonna sell it to him in one afternoon. He's gonna want to think about it, and he'll be wanting to call us back. We're gonna have to be in these disguises for a little while, maybe a couple of days at a time. You need a robot to cover for you. And the robot also gets rid of the original."

You can hardly believe it, but Kirkham actually flinches. You don't think you've seen him ever flinch at anything, no matter how heinous. But he flinches now, and repeats your words. "Get rid of?"

"Sure. You don't see two versions of Jelena Petrovich running around, do you? Just the Joshua Call version."

The light's too dim in the portable to be sure, but you think Kirkham has changed color.

"But when I say 'get rid of', I don't mean like you're worried about. See, your robot is inside the mask -- " You point to the mask you're holding. "But it's like software or something, it needs something to run inside. If you put it onto a person, it'll change that person into you. Like, a perfect copy. They totally forget that they were ever someone else, and they act just like you. So that's where Jelena is. I put her inside a mask of Joshua Call, and that turned her into a Joshua Call robot. It hides her so that Call can walk around being her."

Kirkham rolls the toothpick around in his mouth, but doesn't relax. "And this is, like, permanent?"

"No. You can take the mask off anytime, and the person will wake up. I've tested it out. They wake up, but they don't remember anything. They don't remember being the other person, they don't remember anything after you slapped the mask onto them. It just puts them to sleep and hides them at the same time."

Like he did this morning, Kirkham takes a slow walk around the portable. Halfway around the room, he says, "You didn't explain this to the dipshits."

"It's not their business. But you're my paisano, right?" You hold your hand up, inviting him to clasp it.

"Right." He works that toothpick, then advances on you earnestly. "But we swap blood on it."

You jerk a little. "What?"

"You heard me. You take a blood oath seriously, fucker?"

You blink. "Sure."

"I mean it, asshole." He puts his hand out to grasp your shirt collar, but catches himself. "You wanted to do that once, right, back in our sophomore year?"

"I remember." You say it grimly. The memory still aches. Chen wanted to cement his friendship with Kirkham with a mingling of blood, and Kirkham slapped him away with a sneer.

"Well, I'll do it now." He holds your eye, and his face is so close to yours you can smell the cinnamon on his breath. "'Cos this is the most fucking important thing I've ever done."

The moment hangs, and a prickle goes up your spine as the import of what he's saying sinks in. You nod gravely. Still, neither of you move. The breathless air suddenly feels very dry.

Then it's like a thread snaps between you, and you both stir. Kirkham takes out a pocket knife, and you put the mask on the desk. You jump when Kirkham grabs your wrist, and you glare up at him. He returns your glance with a masked one of his own. You force yourself to relax, and open your hand palm upward.

He pulls the knife blade hard across your palm. You grit your teeth to keep from crying out, and your limbs shake. Blood wells up, and you suck in a hard breath as the sharp air stings the wound.

"Okay, you do it to me," Kirkham says, and hands you the knife. With a sticky hand you grab his wrist, and with the other hand drag the knife tip across Kirkham's palm. He also shakes and makes some hissing noises.

"Now shake," he says.

And you clasp bloody hands together tightly. "You're my blood brother," says Kirkham. "We don't fuck with each other. We fuck up anyone who fucks with one of us. We fuck up anyone who tries coming between us."

"A-fucking-men," you say, for you have no idea what kind of thing you're supposed to say in reply. But it satisfies him, and you hold each other's hand and each other's gaze.

Until Kirkham says, "Okay, now it's getting gay," and releases you. "So what's the next bit?"

"Better lay on the floor, 'cos the masks knock you out when you put them on," you tell him. As he turns and drops to the dirty linoleum, you reach out for the mask with your blood hand. But you catch yourself at the last moment, and pick it up with your clean hand. You grimace at your bloody palm, and wipe it across the giant desk calendar -- now seven years out of date -- that covers most of the teacher's desk.

"Is it gonna hurt?" Kirkham asks as you kneel beside him.

"You won't feel a thing." You drop it onto him.

Your plan, of course, is to turn him into a 'bot by filling that mask with rockbot paste. But this blood oath gives you pause. Will it interact with the magic? Do you dare find out?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Making of a New Partner

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You can't afford to linger over David Kirkham's unconscious body. True, he will be unconscious for fifteen to twenty minutes, but if you're going to carry out the rest of your plan, you can't spend it worrying about --

Fuck it. That "blood oath" is just a bunch of words that guys say to each other. It's not magic, not like the magic of the masks. There's nothing to worry about. You flop out next to Kirkham, grab Chen's face, and rip it from your own.

* * * * *

"You're gonna get it, Prescott," Gary Chen says. His voice is a poisonous whisper. He squats on the floor of the portable, his forearms resting on his knees. He inches closer to you. "You are so fucked. You fucked with my brother after taking an oath. You fucked with him and you fucking broke your oath. That means you're double fucked. And when it's time for you to pay the price, I'm gonna -- "

Yeah yeah. The Chenbot's been going on this way for a couple of minutes now, ever since you dropped the mask onto Kirkham. It started up the moment it opened its eyes -- and yes, that gave you a really queer turn because for a couple of seconds you thought it really was Gary Chen, or maybe even David Kirkham, awake under the mask, and that you really had fucked something up. But it let go of your arm when you told it to, and that proved the thing is just the same dumb old Chenbot. So you let out a nervous laugh and let it keep cussing at you while the two of you swapped clothes.

You're out of the Chen mask now because at almost the same moment you decided to break that "blood oath," you also decided to make Kirkham your vacation spot. His home life isn't nearly as tough as Chen's, and no one fucks with him, so he's got all of Chen's advantages and none of his disadvantages. Plus as him you can hang out close to the Chenbot.

And since you're going to be playing Kirkham for the next little while -- you need to straighten out Call's shit, and a double visit by Chen and Kirkham will be more effective -- you don't need to put any rockbot paste inside his mask, so you just seal it up instead. You're getting the finishing touches into it now.

"You can be quiet now," you tell the Chenbot as you blow the mask dry. "You made your point." It falls silent. "Go watch outside to make sure no one comes in."

The 'bot mutters something but complies, popping a cigarette into its mouth as it shuts the door behind it. You shake your head. You've had your fun teasing the Chenbot, but if you're going to be David Kirkham you are going to have to straighten out its attitude. It can't treat its "blood brother" like an enemy.

You pause for only a moment, regarding the name inside Kirkham's mask, before pushing it into your face.

* * * * *

It's like breaking the surface of a very deep, silent, and black pool of water: breaching the waking world again with the mind of David Kirkham wrapped about yours. You gasp and lurch halfway up, then lay back down again and groan. You rub your eyes.

Fucking Chen. Fucker. You should have seen the double cross coming. Scorpions gotta sting and so does Chen. Fucking oath-breaker.

The thoughts and resentments are cold -- the corpses of the real thoughts and feelings that David Kirkham would have. His mind clings to yours like wet clay. You sit up and roll your shoulders, and flex and grip a rounded bicep. You wobble a little as you spring to your feet. They are bare -- Kirkham's shoes were too small for Prescott's boat-like flippers -- and you dizzily stagger as you bend over the snatch up the socks and sneakers. You hop onto the teacher's desk and pull them on.

Fucker. Now, if you had that book, one of the first thing's you'da done is show it to Chen, 'cos say what you will about that slit-eyed motherfucker, he's smart. You'd've trusted him with it and made him a partner, and the two of you would've gone out and had some smart, profitable fun with the masks. But the asshole had to be the one to find it, and he doesn't share. No, even when he gives you free weed it's just so you'll keep still and not go digging around and find out that he's selling weed by the fucking truckload instead of those shitty little baggies he's been pretending to deal.

You slap your hands together, like you're bitch-slapping the moron.

And you smile grimly. These are all the thoughts David Kirkham would have if he were awake and alive to the treachery of the situation. But he hadn't a clue it was that ass-clown Will Prescott who's been pulling the strings, and that the real Gary Chen is asleep somewhere too.

It's hilarious.

But you don't laugh. You're staring at your palm -- the one you slit open with the knife for that blood oath.

The cut is still there, but the wound is clean. There's no smear of blood, no sticky mess, no oozing, crimson ichor. You peer at it closely, and press it. A little blood starts to flow, and you brush it away with your thumb.

In two huge, loping strides you're at the door, yanking it open. Gary Chen whips around, flicks the butt end of that cigarette away, and sneers at you with glittering contempt in his mica-like eyes.

"Lemme see your hand," you mutter at him, and grab for his wrist.

"The fuck?"

"I said give me your hand, you fucking wonton!" You grab his wrist, and for a moment there's a struggle as the meaning of your words registers with the Chenbot. Then it relaxes and lets you pull its hand toward you. "Open it up, lemme see the palm."

It's just like yours. A fresh cut, but no blood until you press at it, when some more comes out.

"What's your fucking deal," the 'bot snarls.

"I dunno," you mutter. A chilly miasma of fear fills your heart, and overflows it, and sends tendrils -- like a fog of dry ice -- spilling down into your gut.

But you're not so scared you don't lose track of what you need to do. You clutch the Chenbot by the side of the head and pull it close, throwing your other arm around its strong shoulders.

"I'm your fucking bro, you got that," you murmur to it, and you knead its neck. "We're blood brothers. We took an oath. We mingled blood." You feel its hot breath on your cheek, and feel your own hot breath wafting back off it. "Anyone fucks with you, I will fuck them up. Anyone fucks with me, you will fuck them up. We are fucking blood brothers, and we will never. Never. Fuck with each other. You love me, fucker. You love me like you love yourself."

It feels good -- it feels very good -- to say this and to mean it. Because it feels exactly like you mean it. The thought may have been yours -- something to corral the Chenbot, finally -- but it filtered through David Kirkham's brain and came out his mouth, and in some strange alchemy it came out as something that you meant. Your heart beats hard, your fingers squeeze and caress the Chenbot. You pull it into a hard, manly embrace.

It does the same to you.

You give it one hard squeeze, then separate. "Don't want it turning gay or nothing," you mutter. Nervously, you look it in the eye.

The contempt and loathing is gone. Its glance is still hard and sharp, but it shows admiration and loyalty now. Those ebbing feelings of affection for this blood brother surge again in you. "We're gonna have so much fun fucking people up," you tell it.

It smiles by raising its upper lip, showing white fangs. "Starting with Call, right?"

"Fuckin'-a. Lemme get the stuff." You go back inside the portable. "We'll drop my wheels at my house, then you can drive us to Andrea's. Fuck, how you managed to let her sweet pussy go unmolested all this time -- "

You're not sure you mean those words, but you're not sure you don't. Maybe there was something in that "blood oath" business that's addled you. But your chatter breaks off abruptly as you're scooping up the bag of supplies off the desk.

In your rush to handle the Chenbot you almost forgot all about the weird business with the blood, but it comes back to you now like a slap in the face, for that teacher's desk calendar is clean. That smear you left when you -- as Chen -- wiped the blood off your palm is gone.

Chen has followed you inside. You snap your fingers at him. "Open those shades, will you, we need some light." He pulls up the busted Venetian blinds, letting some late afternoon sunlight spill onto the desk. You squat next to it, studying the surface as the light gleams dully on the old paper. You can make out the track your hand left in the dust. But of the blood you left there is no trace.

You pull a fresh toothpick from your pocket and pop it in your mouth. The cinnamon hit energizes your brain.

"What's the deal?" Chen asks. He also squats and peers at the calendar.

"You remember wiping your hand over that calendar? You left a mess, but it's gone."

Chen runs a fingertip over the calendar, then looks at it, and looks at his wounded palm. "I'm starting to bleed again," he says. "A little."

"So'm I. But not like I shoulda been." You straighten up and roll the toothpick around a little. "I don't like this."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Blood Brothers

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"We need to check out that grimoire, that's what we need to do," you mutter more yourself than to the Chenbot. "Except -- Fuck! That thing ain't been good for nothing 'cept cookin' up masks and crap since -- "

Since you ran into that torn page. You've been too busy with the life of Gary Chen to do anything about that. But as David Kirkham you can have a lot more time.

"What about Call?"

"Call can keep for a couple of hours. We can fuck him up any time. Right now I wanna figure out -- " You grimace and rub the deep cut in your palm.

* * * * *

Chen follows you back to Kirkham's place and idles the Jeep long enough for you to leave a note on the kitchen table about being out all evening. Kirkham lives alone with his mom and little brother -- another student at Westside -- and is often in charge of fixing evening meals. But tonight his mom had to work a late shift at the hospital, and "the brat" (as Kirkham calls his kid brother) is staying with a friend. The note is only in case someone comes back early.

Then you swing into Chen's Jeep and let him drive you out to the elementary school.

You pull at your nose thoughtfully as you look over the elementary school basement with Kirkham's eyes. It was good enough for Prescott and Caleb, and for Chen too. For Kirkham? It's got its possibilities, but you find yourself worrying a little more about the chances of getting caught.

But as with Joshua Call, that's a worry for later. Right now you snatch up the grimoire, hop cross-legged onto a desk, and bend over the book.

Before opening it, you summon the 'bot over. "I wanna run a little test. Think you can squeeze a little blood out onto -- " You snag aa loose sheet of paper that you'd been scribbling some notes on. "Here."

"I've mostly stopped," the Chenbot says. With a grimace he rubs his palm hard on the sheet, and manages to leave a thin trail of red.

You do the same. "Now go sit over there and watch it," you tell him. "Yell if something funny happens."

"Like what?"

"Like anything, dumbass. Mostly, though, in case either of those stains disappear."

Then you turn your attention fully to the book.

It's the same book as you have been using all this time. Taller and wider than most books. Bound in red leather, its sheets stiff and heavy. You close it and study the leather and the golden threads, feeling at it. There doesn't seem to be anything occult about the stuff its made of.

Inside you study those weird, metamorphosing faces. It's strange, but you can't catch them actually changing. They're like those duck-rabbit or old woman-young woman illusions, or the spinning ballerina, where it looks exactly like one thing and it's impossible to make yourself think it looks like anything else, until suddenly it does look like something else and you can't get it to change back. Only these faces flip between both sexes and all ages, and sprout beards, lose their hair, and shift from one race to another.

Page by page you go through it, studying the careful line work of the words and holding them up to the light to see if they disclose anything that would suggest the presence of invisible ink. You're very conscious of the fact you have no idea what you're looking for, and that you have no idea how to set about finding anything even if you did. Mostly you're just hoping something will leap out at you, or that the book will reveal something new, the way that the faces at the front are constantly revealing something new. But the ink inside the book is stubbornly unchanging.

"Hey, I got something," Chen calls. You look up as he wheels around to thrust the paper at you. "Some of that blood just disappeared." He points.

Sure enough, where there were two streaks, now there is only one. "Was it yours or mine that went?" you ask.

"Pretty sure it was mine. I put mine onto it first. Yours might -- Whoa!"

Whoa indeed. As you watch, the second streak evaporates off the page. It doesn't fade, but gives the impression of having dissolved into nothingness. You peer at the sheet very closely, but can see nothing to show that there had ever been any blood there at all.

"Okay, let's see now if it's just blood that does that." You suck on a tooth, trying to figure out what to try for. Well, there's spit. Mucous. Tears. Ear wax. You sniff. Come to that, there's also piss, shit, and cum.

You can try that stuff later, if it seems worthwhile. For now, you pull out a booger and stick it onto the paper. You spit on it. Tears, did you say? You pluck a nose hair, and tears pop into your eyes. A fingertip transfers a small drop onto the page, and for good measure you lay the nose hair alongside. "Now watch these things," you tell Chen.

"Do I have to watch?"

"Keep an eye on them. Jack off, if you want to. You can add some Cantonese cum to the collection if you get anywhere." Chen makes a face, and carefully transfers the paper to another desk.

You return to studying the book.

* * * * *

Two hours later you are stiff all over -- except in your cock, which as flaccid as an overboiled pasta noodle -- and hungry and have gotten nowhere, except in regards to studying what happens to your secretions. You haven't pulled any teeth or plucked out any eyeballs, and you haven't spent yourself in an Kleenex. But it does look as though everything that comes off either your body or Chen's disappears after a few minutes. Blood, tears, sweat, spit, boogers, dandruff, scabs, nail clippings and hair all evaporate. Chen even went in the corner and squeezed out a little ball of shit and pissed into an empty plastic bottle. They disappeared too. "You know what this means," you observe. "Forensics would have a hell of a time pinning us down to any crime scenes."

But the book itself yields up no new secrets or insights.

You try peering at the lettering that shows beneath the torn page, but you can look at it for only a few seconds at a time before the rippling line work nauseates you, so if there's anything to be gained there, you're not going to find it that way. So you turn your glance to the words written on the top of the torn page.

You've looked at them before, and recognized them to be a list of ingredients. But you've not studied them closely, on account of there doesn't seem to be any point in it. Every spell has required a sigil, and the sigil for this spell apparently went missing along with the rest of the page. But now you read through the list carefully.

A feeling of deja vu creeps over you. You've used these ingredients before, in the exact quantities and order that they are listed here. You flip back a few pages. Yes, they are the ingredients for one of those metal bands that copies memories and personality. You flip rapidly back and forth between the two pages. Not only are the ingredients the same, but the ink marks -- hand lettered, not printed -- look identical too.

There is only one extra line on the later spell, and when you run it through an internet translator it brings back a gnomic instruction. "Use me, solve me."

Use me? What is this "me" to be used? The book? You've been using the bloody book for weeks now. The spell? You can't use the spell if most of it is missing. The ingredients for the spell?

That gives you pause. It's the only thing you haven't tried.

You hop to your feet --

And you look around. "Yo, dickface, where'd you get off to?"

"I'm back here. Fuck!" A bookcase wobbles, and Chen puts his dirty face out from behind it. To keep him occupied, you'd order him to explore around, to see if there was anything interesting. Aside from the dusty bones of some small animals, nothing much has turned up.

"Just checking. You need to be home soon?"

"I texted my mom, told her I was studying with you."

"Shit, we had homework to do, too. Well, fuck it, we can go one day without. We got any -- ? Yeah, here we are." Inside one of the desks is the makings of another brain band. "Keep exploring."

"I'm gonna need a flashlight."

"You got one out in your glove compartment," you start to say, but Chen is already running up the stairs to the door. You flick on a desk lamp, and start putting the stuff together for a brain band.

* * * * *

It doesn't take very long. The actual crafting of the band is only a few minutes work. The hard part has always been the carving of the runes into the metal, which follows on putting the band inside the sigil. But when you follow the instructions without employing a sigil, the runes go into the metal much faster.

You're not sure if this means that you are doing something right, or something very, very wrong. As insurance, you prepare a second metal band.

But you're still not done when Chen yells your name, followed by a muffled, "I found a door!"

A door? You thrust your tools away, and bang yourself up in a couple places as you squeeze through the furniture. Chen has shifted lots of desks and chairs around, and pulled bookshelves and cabinets away from the wall. You have to call his name a few times before tracking him down a narrow corridor he's made along the back cinderblock wall. He pushes himself farther into a corner, and turns a flashlight onto the wall.

Sure enough, there's a dusty door.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Locked Doors

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
It's almost pitch black here in the back of the elementary school basement, behind all these bookcases, and the door Chen has found is itself darker than the wall it sits in. The flashlight he's pointed at it shows no detail on its flat surface.

You brush your hand over it. It's made of metal, and it's cold. You feel all over it, and find a round knob. It's locked.

"Huh. Must be a closet or something," you say.

"Why would a basement have a closet?"

"I dunno. Why would this dumb school have a basement? But it's locked, and I don't see a way in."

"The hinges are on this side," Chen says. "We could take it off that way."

"We don't got the tools. Or the space or the light. Eh, maybe tomorrow we can check it out. Right now I got something to finish, and you gotta go home."

"We also got Call to fuck up."

"I didn't forget, but I think I'm on to something and I wanna try it out. Come on."

* * * * *

It wasn't a good stopping point when Chen interrupted you, but scoop your stuff up anyway, and Chen hauls ass getting you back to your house. You exchange a complicated handshake before jumping out.

David Kirkham lives in a dumpy house on the edge of one of the more run-down neighborhoods, but it's reasonably well kept. There were once a couple of big trees in the front yard, but those got taken down in a windstorm ten years ago, but that's just as well because it's easier to keep the lawn mowed that way. The paint is peeling and the box of flower beds by the front door is just dirt, but there's no trash in it.

And although Kirkham is a bully and an asshole, he works hard at school and around the house to keep things looking as good as can be expected. The guy's got a terrifically stiff neck when it comes to putting up a good front.

The lights are all on in front, but no one answers your call. There are some dirty dishes in the sink. Your mom's car isn't out front, so that means --

You march into the back and without knocking slam your little brother's door open. It gives you a kind of double deja vu to have a little brother again. But Kirkham's brother, Tad, is older than your brother.

He's sitting at his desk, head phones on, leaning forward tensely with his hand between his legs. The video is hard to make out, but you're pretty sure you know what it is he's watching. Without announcing yourself, you lean against his desk with folded arms.

He glances over at you, does a violent double take, and flies backward. The 'phones come out of the jack, and some female but very unfeminine groaning comes blasting out of the speakers. Tad flaps at the keyboard, trying to shut it off.

You close the top. "Hey, all the lights in the living room and kitchen are on," you say.

His eyes are wide. "Yeah?"

"Yeah. So go turn 'em off. If you're not using them."

"I'm not -- Uh -- "

"Are you using them?"

He has turned very red, but he is also turning belligerent in a way that he shares with his older brother. "Maybe I'm going to. Uh, move out there -- "

"Like we want you doing it out there. Take it in the bathroom, you miserable little tick. After you turn off the living room lights." You stalk toward the door.

"Where are you going to be?"

"In my bedroom. With a good book."

"I know what kind of -- !" But the rest of his words are lost after you slam his bedroom door on him.

In your own bedroom you make a show of spreading out school books, but you hop onto the bed with the grimoire and tools for making brain bands. After a quarter hour you've finished the lettering on the second one you've made today.

Great. Now you've got two pieces of metal and no idea what to do with them.

You lean back against the headboard of your bed, the grimoire in your lap, feverishly clicking the button of a ballpoint pen as you try to come up with a brainstorm. That one line on the torn page -- "Use me, solve me" -- is so strange, so different from the other lines of directions in the other spells, that you have to wonder if there isn't more to this spell than meets the eye.

And speaking of "meeting the eye", you have to pinch your eyes shut after they drift down toward that blinding maelstrom of ink below the tear in the page.

While you're muttering in irritation and frustration and rubbing your eyes, you hear a thumping and crashing from outside. Fraternal instinct -- the need to save your kid brother from his own stupidity, and the need to beat him savagely for being stupid in the first place -- yank you off the bed. The book falls open, and you drop the metal band onto it. Your bedroom door almost comes off its hinges as you wrench it open.

Around the corner, in the living room, is Tad, looking like he's just pulled his fingers from out of an electrical socket. A floor lamp -- its shade busted -- lies on the floor, with a heavy book next to it. "There was a spider!" Tad yells.

"What spider?"

"On that book!" He points. "I went to pull it down, and there was a spider crawling on top of it!" He shakes his hand like he's still trying to fling it off.

You pick up the book -- a very heavy one from a set of history books that your grandfather left to your mom. "The fuck are you reading The Age of Voltaire for? You don't even know who Voltaire is."

"It's for a school report! And you don't either!"

"I don't fucking have to know!"

"But I do! I mean -- "

Something in his manner raises your suspicions, and you open the book. From inside it spill a couple of joints. Tad dives for them, but you block him with a solidly braced forearm to the throat. You pick them up. "Where the fuck did you get these?"

He was pale when you came in, but he's very red now. His eyes water, and he says nothing.

"I said, where did you -- ?"

"Just a guy! At school!"

"And you were going to smoke one? Here?"

Tad doesn't answer. But you see the answer in his face.

You grab him, and he howls and bats at you. You twist him around and hurl him onto the sofa, then clamber onto him and feel at his pockets. He kicks and writhes, but a few blows to his kidneys quiet him enough that you can pull the baggie out of his jeans. The two joints he had in there are bent and shredded. You slap him across the back of the head before letting him. "You fucking moron."

He turns on you with a snarl. "You know where that fucking egg roll you hang out with gets his pocket money, don't you?"

"Yeah, that's why it's fucking stupid of you to be buying from somebody else. Come here. I said, come here!" You grab him by the shirt and drag him to the floor. He flops and fights with you, but you haul him and head rush him back into your bedroom, throwing him against a wall. He pants heavily and angrily at you, but you shut him up with a snarl and rip the middle drawer out of your dresser. You dump its contents on the floor and flip it over. Taped to its bottom is a zip lock bag containing your most recent tribute from Chen. You thrust it -- drawer and all -- in Tad's face.

Anger turns to stupefaction. His jaw drops. "You -- You -- "

"I what? I use? Sure, a little. Chen gives it to me for free, I go out by the river with a joint and a beer and take the edge off. That's why it's fucking stupid of you to be buying it. You're throwing your money away, you fucking moron. How much are you buying?"

"Not much." His face has gone slack with shock. "Uh, maybe an eighth? A week?"

"I'll give you three a month, I'll set it up with Chen to give me some extras, but you fucking lay off the purchases. Jesus, you had me scared." You wipe your forehead.

"Scared of what?"

"That you were trying to deal, or that you were using a lot more. God damn it. What else are you doing that I should know about?"

He turns green. "Nothing."

"You sure?"

"Only what you caught me at, uh, a little while ago."

"Are you buying condoms?"

He gasps. "No!"

"Should you be?"

"David!"

"Alright. Just go clean up that mess you made in the living room. Bring me the rest of your shit from out there, too. Jesus, what are you hiding them there for?"

"Because no one looks at those old books. Dur!"

"Just go put things back. You're not getting wasted too, are you? Got the DTs, that's why you're feeling spiders running all over you?"

"Fuck you!"

"Whatever." You hurl a pair of socks at the back of his head as he goes out the door, then put the dresser drawer back in order. Christ, you think as you drop onto the bed and rub your eyes. If it weren't such an insult to your mom, you'd wonder if Tad had a different father than you.

Than David Kirkham. You sigh. Not even six hours, and already you're thinking of yourself as David Kirkham.

You reach for the grimoire and the metal band. But though the book is there, the band is gone.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Things That Appear and Disappear

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You look all over the bedroom. On the bedspread, under the bed, between the wall and the bed and behind the headboard. You look on and under the desk, and on and under the dresser. You look inside the dresser that you repacked. You look in the corners of the room and even on the windowsill.

You even pick up the book and look underneath it. Nothing.

That metal band you were playing with on the bed is gone.

It's not the loss of the band that bothers you. You made two of them -- the other is sitting on the desk, and in a spasm of anxiety you snatch it up and tuck it carefully into your jeans pocket -- in case of an accident. No, what bothers you is that it sure seems like you've had just such an accident.

You tell yourself that you're imagining things, that it was just carelessness. Obviously you just dropped it someplace and it bounced away.

You fall into the chair at the desk and tug at your lip. You close your eyes and visualize what you were doing when you last had it. You were on the bed, the book in your lap --

You scoot over and feel at the bed where you were sitting. You go out in the hall and into the living room again. Tad has got the lamp back up and is trying to straighten some bad kinks out of the lampshade, and he gives you a querying glance. You ignore him and scan the floor. Nothing.

Back in the bedroom you gingerly get back onto the bed, and lean back the way you'd been sitting, with the book in your lap. You flip its pages, to see if it got stuck behind one of them. Nothing falls out.

You lay your head back and close your eyes. You were in just this position, playing with the band between your fingers, when you heard a crash. You jumped up --

You jump up now, lay the book on the bed and mime tossing the band onto the open book. It should have landed on that open page.

Unless --

The hair on the back of your neck rises. The book is open to the torn page. It couldn't have fallen into the book.

Could it?

With infinite care you brush your fingertips over the exposed page beneath the tear. It's firm to the touch. You close your eyes and repeat the experiment. Except for the bump where the tear is, you can't tell the difference between the two pages: the one above and the one below.

How could a piece of metal fall into or through a solid book?

And yet it seems like that's the only thing it could have done.

You shiver at a goofball thought. That fucked up line work beneath the tear -- maybe that's the sigil. Maybe by dropping that band onto the book you accidentally --

And as you're standing there speculating, all need for speculation ceases. Like a telescopic image coming into focus, the blurry lines of ink resolve into the shape of that metal band, sitting on the torn page. Then they blur again beneath it.

You blink, and touch the band. You pick it up.

Okay, this is all way too freaky, you decide. It's one thing to make a mask that can copy other people's bodies and brains. It's totally something else when one solid thing vanishes inside another.

Yeah, you're not making any sense, even to yourself. You just want to put the thing away and not screw with it anymore. The waters are starting to get a little deep here, and your feet aren't touching the bottom of the magical pool anymore. You got this far without getting past that torn page, you can just keep going by letting things lie. You shut the book firmly, set it on top of the dresser, and slap the metal band onto it.

The band vanishes from under your fingers. If you weren't already so freaked out, you'd shriek. You look at the book, you look at your fingers. The band evaporated like a sliver of ice.

And now where did it go now? Back into the book? Will it come back out in the same place, like a coin falling through a trick box? You open the front cover and flip through the book. Past the spell that makes a mask. The one that makes a sealant. The one that makes a brain band. The one that glues the band to a mask. The one that makes a rockbot. The one that turns people into rockbots. The one that makes a rockbot paste. The one that --

You catch yourself in mid turn. You're holding the bottom of the torn page.

But it's not torn anymore. It's whole. You are holding the bottom of a page that wasn't there a moment ago.

You fly back as though stung.

But curiosity gets the better of you. You return to the book, and examine the page that has magically repaired itself. It contains a list of instructions, and the gnomic instruction "Use me, solve me." There's nothing else.

You swallow and turn the page. There's no explanation on the back. This is disappointing. But at least you're into a new part of the book. The next spell is waiting for you, and the lettering that had danced manically before is now dead and readable.

To keep from being disturbed as you study the new spell, you tell Tad you're making an early night of it, and turn off the lights and stuff some clothes against the crack under the door. Then you sit at your desk, turn on the desk lamp, and open the book again.

You're up until almost two, and you would have been up much later except for the damnedest bit of luck.

* * * * *

The girl falls insolently against the locker next to yours. "Hey, how's it hanging," Jelena Petrovich asks, and looks down her nose at you.

God, she's sexy. A pedestal-like neck, the mane of dark hair, the lips that she bites provocatively when she catches someone looking at her. Today, despite the cool weather, she's in a sleeveless red jersey and jeans cut off halfway down her thigh. She's wearing boots, too. She looks like she's on the hunt for faces to stomp.

She picked wrong if she thinks she can stomp on you. "Glad you showed up," you mutter. "I wanna talk to you."

"'Bout that shit we talked about yesterday? That was fun." She wipes the side of her mouth with a fingertip.

"Yeah. I talked to Chen, decided you're not a lying whore."

She grins. "Cool. So, is Chen setting you up?"

"I wanna talk to you first. Fourth period, out at the portables."

"Sure." She brushes past you as she goes, scraping her breasts across your torso. "I'm popular, but I got time for you." She plunges back into the crowd.

You text Chen, telling him about the meeting.

* * * * *

Jelena's on time, but you're early, so you get a nice eyeful of her swaggering up. You also notice the jealous looks you're getting from a cluster of juniors out at the corner of the portables, but you turn your back on them to put your arm around her waist and lead her to that one portable that is rapidly turning into your office.

"So's Chen got a face picked out for you?" she asks. "Or is he letting you pick? 'Cos he made us take these girls."

"Something wrong with those girls?"

"No. 'Cept dumbass Rennerhoff is gonna flunk all his classes if he doesn't start studying."

"What about you?"

"I don't give a shit about classes, I'm only here to show off." She snickers. "This ain't permanent, you know, and if Jelena has to drop out when she comes back from the dead, the fucking bitch deserves it, plus the reputation I'm giving her. Meanwhile, I'm -- "

Whatever Call was going to say next dies in his throat as you open the portable door. Gary Chen is standing inside with his arms folded.

You push her inside to join him. "Siddown, fucker. Yeah, I'm talking to you," you add when Call turns a dumbstruck face on you. "In that chair."

"What's the -- ?"

"He told you to sit down," says Chen. He grabs Call by the arm and hair and shoves her into a seat.

You step behind Call and grab her slim wrists, holding her helpless. "Get the mask off," you tell Chen. "Then we'll explain to him how much fucking trouble he's in."

Call kicks at Chen, but Chen sits in her lap and with a leer puts his hand to Jelena's face. He mutters and pulls, and mutters and pulls, and mutters and pulls. The leer slowly disappears. "Quit fucking around," you tell him.

"I'm not!" he says, and resumes pulling at Jelena's features. You stare at him hard, and he swallows and starts to sweat.

"I can't get it off him," he finally mutters.

You swear at him, release your hold on Call, and shove Chen aside, taking his place in Call's lap. Goddamn it, let a pro do it. "Grab her hands," you yell when Call tries clawing your face. Then: "Maybe you better start screaming, sweetheart," you tell Call as you cup her face in your hands. "You'll want to, soon, on account of everything we're gonna do to you."

She's flushed, and her eyes roll. "What did I do, why are you -- ?"

"You need to learn to keep your fucking mouth shut about this stuff, you cunt. You don't tell anyone -- anyone! -- about it." Froth suddenly fills your mouth. "You had one job, to keep your cocksucking hole shut, and you fucked that up. So now we're gonna fuck you up."

You refrained from striking the girl, but once you've got the mask off Call, you and Chen kick and beat him mercilessly all about the portable. He nurses and weeps silently over broken bones at the end.

Then you put the mask back on him. Jelena doesn't show so much as a bruise.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: And Things That Reappear Only to Disappear Again

choices: Tell Caleb that Kirkham is your new spot
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You were pretty sure that's the way it would work with the masks. Call has a couple of busted ribs, and his flattened face is black and red with cuts and bruises. But once Jelena's mask is on him, he can stand up and move without showing any hurt at all. A lot of his old, stubborn life flares back into his eyes when he stands, but at least he's docile. "You're still on the team," you tell the girl. "But you're on parole. Next time you need a good kick, we're just gonna take that mask off you and give it to someone else, and you can crawl your way to the hospital. Or maybe we'll do something else to you. 'Cos who's in charge around here?"

Jelena glares between you and Chen. She licks her lips. "You are."

"That's right." You put your arm around Chen's neck, and he reciprocates. "You don't go making any decisions about who needs to hear about these things. 'Cos you never know what they're gonna do when they find out." You get right in her face, and grab her by the jaw. She tries to tear away, and her arms fly up, but she stops at the last moment. ""That's right," you tell her softly, and stroke her hair. "If want to touch you, then I get to touch you. Wherever and whenever I like." You let that horrible thought settle into her. Then you push her away. "Not that I'm gonna." You wipe your palm on your shirt. "You fucking disgust me. Now get the fuck out. And tell those dykes you're hanging out with that the same goes for them!"

Silence descends in the portable after she's gone. You clench your fists and force yourself to relax. It was quite a workout you had on Call, and you've still got the adrenaline going. When the silence becomes too oppressive, you look over at Chen.

He's looking at you with open admiration. "Jesus, Prescott," he says softly. "I didn't know you had it in you."

It surprises and appalls you that the Chenbot still knows who you really are. And the praise would be sweeter if you'd earned it instead of programming him to be your best friend. But you can't resist the pleasant tickle of flattery. You shrug it off, though. "You gotta do what you gotta do. You know that. Fuck, we both know that."

Middle school wasn't pleasant for either of these guys, but you don't dwell on that.

Chen knows what you mean too, and nods. Or maybe he's thinking of something else. "We still moving Evans into Kelsey?"

"At some point. I'll leave it to you, but -- What happened that you couldn't get the mask off Call?"

"I dunno. I thought I was doing it all right, just like I always do. But it wouldn't come off."

You point at your face. "Can you get this one off me?"

"I can try."

So you sit down. Chen pulls and pries and mutters and sweats. You guide his hand to the proper spot. You rehearse the words with him. None of it makes a difference. At the end you have to surrender to the fact that Chen -- at least when it's the Chenbot -- can't get a mask off anyone. And that rules out letting the Chenbot handle replacement duties. "Well, just keep Evans stringing along for the next day or two, tell him there's a hitch. You heard any more from Mendoza?"

"Just his contact info, and he says there's a party at Kelsey's this Saturday."

"If we could get in -- " Except it wouldn't be "we", it would only be you, because the Chenbot can't go in under a disguise.

Or could it?

It would be worth a try, but just then the bell rings, signaling the end of lunch.

"So you got work tonight," you muse as you trudge back to the main building. "I'm gonna work some more on that new spell."

"Any idea what it does?"

"No. You know what to do or say if any of the dipshits call or text, right?"

"Pretty sure I do."

"Good man." You clap him on the shoulder, as David Kirkham would with his best friend and blood brother, Gary Chen.

* * * * *

You let your homework slide one day, and you can't let it slide anymore than that, so directly after school, you go to the school library, kick a chair loose from a table, and spread out your homework. Efficiently and methodically you work out calculus, chemistry, and statistical problems in Kirkham's neat handwriting. It's a remarkable thing, and you feel its oddity even while sitting comfortably inside Kirkham's skull. He's a thug and a reprobate, but he is very good at some very hard classes, especially math, where he's taking both AP Calculus and AP Stats. Beyond the hard math and sciences courses there's his skill on the cello -- he's even studying with a university professor. Only a weightlifting class and a blow off "Asian Alphabets" class keep him from running the table with kickass classes his senior year.

But then, he kicked his way through the AP social studies classes his junior year.

And what's he going to do with it? Go to college, get a degree in mechanical engineering probably, make some serious coin in a serious business working alongside other serious people. As equations stack and evolve neatly at the end of your pencil, you reflect that that is what pisses him off so much about high school and about shitheads like Will Prescott: their utter lack of serious purpose.

That and the fact that the ball-playing team-sport faggots won't acknowledge the place he deserves in the upper echelons of the school pyramid. Really, he ought to be running things --

And the tip of your mechanical pencil breaks as with mounting fury you press it too hard against the paper. You cuss under your breath, replace the old toothpick with a fresh one, and continue after forcing your attention back to the math.

* * * * *

By five o'clock, when the school library closes, you're done, and decamp for the old elementary school basement. You ignore the door that Chen found -- easy to do, since it's still hidden from sight behind the disordered bookshelves -- and settle down with the grimoire. Before getting to work, though, you check the text that hit your cell on the drive out.

It's from Chen. cj wants 2 no when youll be roth.

Shit. There's that to deal with, too. Why does Caleb want to know? Does it make a difference to him? Your reply: tell him theres a hitch, youll let him know.

ok did that already

You unbend enough to send back a smiling face emoticon, then turn the phone off. You've got more than enough to concentrate on.

You would have quailed when you saw that long-hidden spell, but David Kirkham doesn't quail before anything, not even a fucking magical grimoire. No, David Kirkham only gets mad when he meets a frustration. And with his psyche riding shotgun in your head, you weren't about to let a wall of words knock you back on your heels.

That's what confronted you: No list of ingredients, no instructions for putting them together, and no sigil. Just a wall of words. You translated them -- and the book, which previously didn't like it when you copied down what it said, let you in this case -- and only got gibberish.

If you had tackled the problem during the daytime, you probably would have got stuck, and who knows if you'd ever have got past them. But as luck or fate would have it, you were studying the spell at night, at a desk, in front of a window. The bedroom faces out into the back yard, and when you heard a noise you opened the window to look out. A dumb and thoughtless move, and useless, for you saw nothing, not even with the waxing moon blazing down from a clear sky.

You turned back to the book, and to the sheet of paper you'd laid out for taking notes. Your throat tightened: silvery figures were forming on that paper.

You held your breath in for as long as possible, then let it out slowly and inhaled slowly again. It was resting on the book, on the page with the new spell, and like blotting paper absorbing a pattern from beneath -- a pattern that wasn't on the page. You didn't disturb anything until all movement on the page had stopped. You had to bend close to examine it, for the line work was silvery --

Silvery like the moon. You've read books where some kinds of invisible ink respond only to moonlight .

-- and hard to make out. But it formed a disk, and that disk was a recognizable sigil.

Gingerly you picked it up. And whether it was some trick of the light or whether the magical ink actually changed, but the lines darkened until the sigil was as solid as the ones in the book.

You put in your top dresser drawer and went to bed. When you woke up, the paper was still there and, more important, so was the sigil.

You have it with you now.

Again, luck (or something like it) is with you now. You solved the last puzzle by putting a brain band onto a weird scrap of magical paper. You have the other bands you made yesterday, and now, as if guided by some instinct, you set one of them on this new sigil. While your fingertips are still touching it, you feel a characteristic thrill, and the band has acquired a shimmer when you pick it back up again.

A shiver runs through you. Why is it all working out so simply? You can tell without testing it on the grimoire that you've completed the spell.

It's worked out so well, in fact, that you don't want to push the day's luck any further. You press the paper with the sigil between the pages of the grimoire, pocket the band, and hightail it back to your house.

On the drive back you force your concentration onto Caleb's question. Do you add Roth to your collection? Or tell him that Kirkham has become your vacation spot?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Help From an Unexpected Quarter of the Sky

choices: Finish the new band by using it on yourself
Finish the new band by using it on Patterson
Finish the new band by using it on Chen
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You go round in a circle a couple of times before deciding what to tell Caleb. What finally settles you is the simplicity of leaving Justin Roth out of it. It's enough to have two aliases, after all, and Caleb would wonder why you had to give yourself a third if you told him about both Roth and Kirkham being part of your crew. He'd wonder why you added Kirkham, and that might unravel your other secrets. And if you kept Kirkham a secret from him, that would be another complication.

* * * * *

After you've fixed supper for you and your mother and your brother -- a simple chef's salad; far from being your favorite kind of thing, but there's nothing else in the kitchen -- you plop onto your bed with the grimoire and text Caleb, telling him you have to talk. Then you open up the grimoire and take out the sigil you made last night, and the band that you made this afternoon. Time to figure out what it does.

But when you press it against the book, the page doesn't turn.

Hmm. That means there's one more step to the spell. You study the new band thoughtfully.

It looks just like the brain bands you've already been making, only you were able to make it much quicker -- the rune work went on before you set it in the sigil, which seems to have sped up the carving process. You vaguely wonder if that's the purpose of this spell: to give you a new and quicker technique for making these bands. Maybe you need to use it on someone first. The obvious candidate is yourself. It would be a waste of material, but the thing was quick and easy to make, and you're more interested in using it as a key to open the next spell in the book.

You're still thinking about this when your phone tweets. It's a text from Andrea Varnsworth: need 2 talk.

Fuck. Must be about Call.

u got my number, you text back.

Your phone rings a minute later. Andrea's voice is very tight. "So what the fuck happened this afternoon?"

"What do you think happened?"

There's a pause. "You know what I'm talking about, right? Because Jelena told me -- "

"Oh, is it 'Jelena' now? Call's got her pussy on that tight?"

"Whatever." Thomason is either very angry or very frightened. Maybe both. "I didn't know you were running things with Chen."

"I am. At least, I am now. And that's why we fucked up that whore. Didn't Call explain, or is she too fucked in the head to understand our message?"

Another pause. "He only said that you kicked the shit out of him because he told you what was going on. Which makes no fucking sense at all."

"It makes perfect sense. But I guess I have to explain it to you, and then you can explain it to those three fuckbrains. Get yourself some finger puppets and teach it to 'em Elmo style." You have to get off the bed to pace, because the anger is beginning to stir in you again.

"Chen put those fucks there for only one reason, so they could help you move his product. They're not there to think or to come up with ideas of their own. They're supposed to be exactly as dumb as those dumb cunts they're pretending to be. But then Call got the fucking bright idea to tell me what was going on. Did he ask you if he could or should?"

"No."

You're pretty sure that's a lie, but you let it pass. "Then he's even stupider than I thought he could be, because you'd be smart enough to tell him not to, right?" She doesn't answer your question. "But he tells me, and I talk to Chen, and now, yeah, I'm part of things. Me and Chen are running things together, because obviously we're the only two guys with anything resembling brains. And that means both of us understand how stupid Call was. I don't care why that cocksucker did what he did. He opened his mouth, and anytime you open your mouth about this shit you risk fucking us all up. So we had to teach him not to ever do it again." You let those words sink in. "That's what I thought we did. Are you telling me now that he still doesn't understand that?"

"I think he understands. But he's really upset."

"Dykes get 'upset'. Guys get pissed. So if he's only 'upset' -- "

"So he's pissed! I'm telling you that!"

"So what's he going to do about it?"

"I don't know. He doesn't know. But you oughta know what's going on with him."

"That's good, Thomason, that's smart of you. Thanks. You're keeping your head. You keep me informed, and you keep Chen informed. By the way, why'd you text me instead of Chen?"

"I did text him. He told me he was busy at work, I should talk to you."

"That's right. He's got a lot on his plate, so you better talk to me about this stuff. What you need to do is to tell Call to calm down, that he'll be okay as long as he just sticks to following orders, and his only orders are to have fun and keep his mouth shut about this magic shit. Okay? 'Cos that's the only thing that'll get him fucked up. And what me and Chen did this afternoon, that's nothing. You ain't seen a tenth of what Chen's learned to do. Fuck, he's even got me scared of some of this stuff. I mean, think about it. If he can turn you into Andrea Varnsworth and Call into Jelena Pussyface, think what worse he could do. Long distance, too."

"Really?"

"Want me to tell him to demonstrate on your ass?"

"No. Alright, I'll tell him. But you know Call, he's not the kind of guy who'll roll over this this."

"Is that a fact? Then I'll tell that to Chen when he gets off work. We'll take care of Call so he doesn't have a chance to make another boneheaded mistake."

"No! Don't do that!" There's no mistaking the fright in her voice. "He won't do anything! I promise, I'll talk to him, get him to see that he has to -- "

"I don't think that'll work, Thomason. I think you're right, I think he's gonna go full retard on us and tell someone -- "

"He won't, I swear! Whatever you do to him, you can do to me if he goes retard on you!"

You'd be touched by Thomason's loyalty to his friend if you actually had any respect for Thomason. "I'll hold you to that. Is there anything else?"

"I don't think so. Are you, uh, also partnering with Chen on the sales thing?"

The sales thing. Jesus. "No, the ganja business is all his. Bother him with stuff about that, he knows what's going on there better than me. Now hang up, because I got another text coming in."

You don't actually, but talking to Thomason has given you indigestion.

* * * * *

You spend the rest of the evening waiting impatiently for a reply from Caleb, and for Kirkham's family to go to bed so you can get out of his mask and use the band on yourself. It's nearing nine o'clock when you realize that it's probably your fault that Caleb hasn't texted back: You sent him a curt gotta talk 2 u from Kirkham's cell. Why wouldn't Caleb ignore a text from Kirkham?

So you send another. hey its Will, in kirkham now, gotta talk 2 u.

And that gets an almost instant response, telling you to come up to the loft. Kirkham's mom doesn't mind him being out late, so you just give her a curt "Gotta run see a guy" as you haul ass through the living room while pulling on a windbreaker.

Caleb has already opened up the side door to the gym, but you make sure to yell loudly for him as you cross the empty basketball court, for you don't want to bust in on him and the Chelseabot. At any rate, they are both fully clothed when you push open the loft door with your foot. Javits jerks his chin in greeting; Chelsea gives you a simpering smile.

Your friend crawls over to the dorm-sized refrigerator. "So what's the deal," he asks. "Having one asshole wasn't enough, so you got yourself another?"

"Something like that. Kirkham was being a problem. It just seemed the easiest way to do things by making him my spare."

"Seems painful to me." He tosses you a beer. "Christ, Will, what is it with you and these psychos?"

"You're one to talk, Seth."

"No I'm not, not until I've got myself a second asshole."

Something in the way he says it catches your ear. "What do you mean?"

"Well, I'm thinking you got two faces now. Why can't I? Gimme a mask I can use on Patterson." He turns to the Chelseabot. "Wouldn't you like to find out what Steve's cock feels like?"

She squirms and pants, "You're enough for me, Seth."

"Jesus, Caleb, didn't you leave her any of her original personality?"

"It was repulsive. But come on, we're partners, right? Gimme Steve as a second. Fucker brings so much pussy up here." He shakes his head in wonderment.

You put your hands in your pocket, and that's how you discover that you'd absentmindedly brought one of those new brain bands with you. Well, why not? you think. You need to try the thing out anyway. You just need to make a mask to go with it. "I guess I could do that," you say.

"Awesome. So tomorrow night -- "

"Do we have to talk about this in front of Chelsea?"

He looks at his girlfriend. "Her?"

"You got another Chelsea up here?"

"Whatever. Hey Chels, go hang out downstairs till I call you."

She gets up and sways past you, that horrible smile still plastered to her face. You shudder. "That thing is creepy," you mutter.

"Not as creepy as what's under it, right? And not as creepy as being Kirkham. Seriously, dude, what's the deal with the new face? What happened to Roth?"
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Creepy Girls

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You tear your attention away from Chelsea to answer Caleb's question. "It's just convenient, using Kirkham. You know? 'Cos he's close to Chen, gets in his face and shit."

"You don't have to be Chen," Caleb reminds you. "So you don't have to be Kirkham."

"Yeah, I know." You sigh impatiently. "I could be Patterson, I could -- "

"Nah, that door's closed." Caleb grins. "I took the hint, and now I want that fucker all for myself. Christ, you know he had Kelsey up here the other night?"

That jars something loose in your head. "I thought she was doing it with Hennepin."

"I guess she's doing it with both. Anyway, that's what I want." He stretches his arms, flexing them hard, and grunts. "Can start lettin' you up here, too, once I got -- Hey, maybe we could each get a third. You could be Delacroix, he wouldn't look outta place up here."

"I'm good," you say evasively. "Look, I'll get the shit together tomorrow for your mask by six."

"Gimme the key to the basement, and I'll take care of it."

You hesitate. "That's okay. Tomorrow's the Chenbot's night off. I can mix the shit up tonight, actually, and just have him do the polishing. Easiest on both of us."

That basically ends the conversation, and you wish him a good fucking night after he asks you to send the Chelseabot back up as you leave.

* * * * *

You told Caleb you could make the mask up tonight because you're still thinking about the new brain band. That's the first thing you do when you get to the elementary school basement after leaving Westside: You pull off the mask of Kirkham and put it to your forehead. You're expecting to be knocked out, but you're not prepared for the nausea you feel when you awake. It's as though you've been pulled inside out.

But the brain band is finished, as you confirm when you use it to unlock the page. To your disgust there is no writing on the other side of the page that explains what it does.

And you kind of wish there was an explanation, because apparently this is a different kind of brain band. It now bears your name -- WILLIAM MARTIN PRESCOTT -- but the letters are a fiery red instead of the familiar blue.

You finger it for a very long time, then with a shrug lay it aside. You hadn't pinned a lot of hopes or assumptions on it, so you turn instead to the next spell.

Ugh. The previous spell consisted solely of a wall of words. This one consists solely of a sigil, and it's the most complex sigil you've seen yet. It looks like several sigils -- at least three -- enclosed within each other. Without much hope you tug at the corner of the page, to see if it will turn loose to give you some instructions, but it doesn't. Even after you've put Kirkham's mask back on and stared at the spell with his eyes and brain, you can't figure out how to approach it.

Well, you've one idea, but it doesn't bear any fruit. After prepping a mask for tomorrow, you go home. You set the grimoire open next to the window and lay a sheet of paper over it. An expectant wait yields no result as no mysterious moon writing appears. But you leave it out overnight, even after you've stayed up until 1:30 carving up a new brain band to go with Patterson's mask. You use the traditional recipe instead of the new one.

* * * * *

Caleb's bit of gossip about Kelsey has reminded you that you need to move Evans into Kelsey's spot, and you're thinking about how to make the switch when you pull into the school parking lot the next morning. You overslept and are late, so you're hustling around the back of the school to first period, when you get an idea after passing the natatorium. You shoot a text to Andrea, asking if she can get Kelsey out alone to her house.

The reply comes near the end of first period. maybe, y?

You start to compose a reply, then realize it would just be easier to talk. She has the same lunch as you, so you set up a meeting.

But you conduct it by phone from different parts of the school -- it would look odd for Kirkham and Andrea to be seen together. You hide yourself in a portable; she tells you she's in a corner of the stacks in the library. "I'm moving Evans," you tell her. "Into Kelsey, but I need a place to do it. If you can get Kelsey out to your place, where it's just you and the dykes -- "

She interrupts. "Have you talked to Evans about this?"

"No, but I'm pretty sure he can come out whenever."

"What I mean is, is he okay with changing into Kelsey? Because I don't think he's going to like that."

"The fuck does 'liking' have to do with it? He does what he's told." You'd assume that's the end of it, but something in her silence makes you think there's something else going on. "Doesn't he? You heard something that would make that a problem?"

"Just the way he talks about us. About me, the fact that I'm -- " Thomason lowers his voice to near whisper. "That I'm wearing a mask of a girl. I think he'd have a problem with putting one on."

"Oh Jesus." Your contempt for Evans blossoms anew. "Like what kind of fucking problem?"

"Like the kind of problem some guys have with putting on dresses. But more so. You know?"

"It's a fucking costume. Tomorrow's Halloween anyway. I'm not gonna bust his balls about being a girl. Fuck, he hasn't got any balls to bust anyway. Have I been busting your balls about it?"

"No, but you only got into this stuff yesterday. Would you put on a girl's body?"

"Hell, yeah. I know what you get up to in that thing, Thomason, I'd -- "

"So why don't you turn yourself into Kelsey?"

That knocks you back on your heel. "I like keeping my head clear," you stammer. "I mean, I'd like to play with what you got. But Chen and me talked, and he convinced me I don't need to fuck myself up worrying about what clothes to wear or who's sucking off who." The truth of course, is nearly the opposite. You feel like you need to keep inside Chen's head, or Kirkham's, to have the instincts and the stamina to control guys like Thomason. "But is this really gonna be a problem for Evans?"

"It might. You've already got Call unhappy."

"Christ. Okay, let's meet to talk about it. Up here at the school about six. Can you do that? I'll talk to Evans and Mendoza, see if they can make up here too. We'll talk, figure something out."

Talk. You snarl the word to yourself as you hang up. You'll fucking make Evans take whoever you want him to take, that's what you'll do.

But in the back of your mind you know it's better to give him some say. And if he digs in his heels and refuses to take the part of Kelsey, maybe you can juggle the other two around.

* * * * *

Caleb came by your locker early in the day to ask if you could get the mask for Patterson ready by five o'clock -- "We're gonna hang out upstairs after practice" -- so immediately after school you send the Chenbot to get leftovers from his aunt's restaurant while you drive over to the elementary school to put things together. You've got the supplies together and have started buffing the mask when the 'bot shows up. Your turn the job of polishing up the mask over to it while you make yet another mask for use on Kelsey -- or whoever. The 'bot has finished the polishing job by four-thirty, and after attaching the brain band to the mask and gathering up sealant and the rockbot paste that uses Caleb's hair, you set it to polishing up the next mask.

Caleb meets you downstairs at the doors of the gym to take the stuff from you, but you demur from helping him out with Patterson: "I got an errand to run. Besides, you know how to take care of it, don't you?" He gives you a dirty look, but doesn't kick when you leave. You circle back around to the parking lot, then keep going around the back of the school to the portables.

Chris Trantham -- really Tanner Evans -- is already there, and under harsh questioning digs in against taking on the guise of Kelsey Blankenship, offering no reason other than a mulish "I don't want to." Nor does he want to take over for Andrea. He shrugs when Douglas Canfield shows up and you point to his sorry ass as a possible identity. To your relief, Mendoza says he's okay with taking on Kelsey's mask. By the time Andrea shows up, you've got thing set, and you send Thomason and Mendoza home while calling the Evansbot to come up to the school: Evans will go back to being himself until Andrea can set up an ambush for Kelsey.

By six o'clock you're done and home and doing homework. A text comes from the cell of Steve Patterson, confirming that the switch went off without a hitch, and bragging that he'll be spending the evening in the company of Olivia Byrne.

Fuck. You pull off your glasses and rub your eyes. Not for the first time you wonder how it happened that you got the grimoire, but Caleb wound up getting all the ladies.
