1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You tear your attention away from Chelsea to answer Caleb's question. "It's just convenient, using Kirkham," you reply. "'Cos he's close to Chen, gets in his face and shit."

"You don't have to be Chen," Caleb reminds you. "So you don't have to be Kirkham."

"Yeah, I know." You sigh impatiently. "I could be Patterson, I could -- "

"Nah, that door's closed." Caleb grins. "I took the hint, and now I want that fucker all for myself. Christ, you know he had Kelsey up here the other night?"

That jars something loose in your head. "I thought she was doing it with Hennepin."

"I guess she's doing it with both. Anyway, that's what I want." He stretches his arms, flexing them hard, and grunts. "Can start lettin' you up here, too, once I got -- Hey, maybe we could each get a third. You could be Delacroix, he wouldn't look outta place up here."

"I'm good," you say evasively. "Look, I'll get the shit together tomorrow for your mask by six."

"Gimme the key to the basement, and I'll take care of it."

You hesitate. "That's okay. Tomorrow's the Chenbot's night off. He can do the polishing."

That basically ends the conversation, and you wish him a good fucking night after he asks you to send the Chelseabot back up as you leave.

* * * * *

Caleb's bit of gossip about Patterson and Kelsey Blankenship has reminded you that you need to get Evans moved out of Chris Trantham and into Kelsey. You text Andrea before going to bed, asking what her relationship with Kelsey is like. That's the only avenue you can see for getting to that rich cunt.

You don't get a reply until the next morning, and it's equivocal: ok. y? You call her once you get to school. "Is there any way you can get her out to your house, alone, or with just you and the dykes? Oh, by the way, did you straighten Call's shit out?"

"I think so," she says guardedly. "But you ought to try smoothing things over with him a little."

"I'm not gonna smooth over shit. Fucker'll take it as a sign of weakness. If he wants to be on my good side, he's gonna have to come up with something that'll make me happy. But Kelsey, can you get her out to your place?"

"Maybe. Why?"

"Because I'm going to put Evans in for her, so I need her someplace where I can do the switch."

There's a long pause. "Have you talked to Evans about this?"

"Fuck no. Why would I hafta to do that?"

"Because I don't think he'd like it."

Now it's your turn to fall silent. "The fuck does that have to do with anything?"

"I guess it's got nothing to do with it, man," she says with an aggravated sigh. "But you know, Chen was at least giving us choices. He gave me a choice -- "

"Chen put me in charge of staffing, so if I tell Evans he's going to -- "

"I think he's got a real phobia about it," Andrea interrupts. "Hang on, can I call you back? Traffic's getting bad and I shouldn't be talking. When I get to school -- "

"Fine." You slap the phone off.

Where the fuck does Evans get off on deciding where he can or can't be? But then you calm yourself. That's Kirkham talking, and Thomason is right, you were giving them choices originally. And it's bad enough that you've got Call in a pissy mood. You should listen to what Thomason's got to say. He's actually the smartest of the fucketeers, and he's done some good work for you.

So when your cell rings again, you are a lot more pleasant. "So what's got you worried, man? Be fucking blunt about it."

She still sounds guarded, though. "It's things he's said. You know, we do sometimes get together, or meet. Evans gives me shit about being Andrea. I don't mind," she grumbles, "but he says things like, 'No fucking way Chen could get me to put on one of those things'. So I just think he won't want to do it."

"He don't like tits and pussy? What is he, gay?"

Another long pause. You crane your neck to see if you can spot her car. "I think it's the opposite," she says in a hard voice. "You know, like he wants to have a cock and balls so he can shove himself inside a pussy."

"Whatever. Listen, you three get together, you and Evans and Mendoza. Did he tell you where he is? Dougie Canfield? Right, so get together and talk about who's willing to put on which mask. Find out if Evans is okay with being Kelsey. If he's not, find out if he's okay with being that Canfield fucker, and if Mendoza wouldn't mind being Kelsey. Or, I dunno -- " Kirkham's patience is starting to come apart at the seams. "Maybe you want to be Kelsey, and Mendoza wants to be Andrea, and Evans can be Canfield. You know? Tell Mendoza to think of some candidates for Evans, some dipshits in the junior or sophomore classes, just in case. Just talk over lots of possibilities so I know who's willing to be whoever instead of -- " You run out of breath and patience simultaneously. "You get it?"

"Sure. I think that's a good idea. We'll get it to work."

"You better. But you know that I make the decisions. Right?"

"I know."

You break the connection and put the phone away, then turn at the sound of a car door closing. It's Andrea. She catches your eye briefly, but otherwise doesn't acknowledge you.

You can't resist, though. As she approaches, you blow a lewd kiss at her. "Lookin' fine there, girl."

She tilts her chin imperiously and quietly says, "Go fuck yourself, David" as she glides by.

* * * * *

It's a normal, boring Thursday at school. Javits leans in at locker after first period to ask in a low voice if you can have the mask ready by five, "'Cos me and Steve'll be hanging out upstairs after practice." You assure him that you can have a mask by then. Then late in the day you get a text from Andrea, telling you that she's set up a meeting with the other two fucketeers. After school you snag the Chenbot and have it follow you out to the elementary school basement. There you make the mask, then give it to the 'bot to polish. You'll be cutting it a little close getting everything done by five, but it'll be doable. To work on homework while the car buffer whines loudly nearby.

By four-forty you're on the road back to school, with the polished mask and everything else you need for the ambush. That includes the grimoire, and you bite your lip as you mull the one awkward spot to come in the afternoon. You're going to have to get the new brain band onto Patterson separately, and then without Caleb noticing you'll have to get it into the book to unlock the next spell.

That is, unless you tell him that you've gotten farther along in the book.

Fuck it, you decide after some momentary doubt. You've already got enough secrets from him. One more, for a little while, can't hurt. You'll just have to finesse it somehow.

At school you loiter by the gym until you see the basketball squad heading out. You count off the members, and you've just decided they've all gone but Javits and Patterson when you get a text from Caleb: where ru?

downstairs outside come meet me, you reply.

A minute later the doors open. A frowning Javits puts his head out. Before he can speak, you hand him the new brain band. "Get this on him, then text me when it's clear."

"The fuck? Where's the rest of the mask?"

"Better this way," you hiss back. An idea suddenly comes to you. "He'll see a mask coming if you try that on him. You can keep the band hidden until the last minute. Then once he's out we can put the mask on him, then glue them together. This way we don't have to tackle the asshole."

"Yeah, not a bad plan," he nods, and disappears.

Your heart thumps as you wait, and Caleb gives you plenty of time for your nerves to twist up. For whatever reason, he's taking his sweet time getting the band onto Patterson. You're about to head in and see if he's in trouble when the "all clear" text comes.

You take the stairs three at a time to the loft, and a distressing sight meets your eyes when you burst in: Patterson, unconscious, next to an unconscious Caleb Johansson. It takes you a moment to realize that Caleb has simply taken Seth's mask off in preparation for a switch.

You unpack the gear, and coat the inside of the Javits mask with some of Caleb's rockbot paste for when he puts it on Patterson. Long minutes pass, then the new brain band comes out of Patterson. Quickly you press it into the book, confirm the next spell is unlocked, then shove the book away into your bag. The blank mask goes onto Patterson, and a minute later Caleb sits up with a groan. "Wanna make the switch right away, huh?" you grin at him.

"Sure. Fucker told me he's got Olivia Byrne coming up here tonight. Again. Boo-yah!" The exclamation sounds a lot sillier coming from him than from Javits. "So where are we on the copying?"

"Got the band already. Just waiting for the mask."

Caleb gets you a beer, and you talk a little more, mostly about the pussy he's anticipating. It's clear he can't wait to don Patterson's identity. "I'll get out of your way as soon as the mask is done," you promise him. You don't want to be up here alone with Steve Patterson, even if it is your best friend underneath.

Caleb hands you the mask when it comes out. You slide a bit of glue across its inner surface and attach the brain band. You're about to seal it shut when you freeze.

STEVEN PERCEVAL PATTERSON, says the band. But the letters are red, not the blue you're used to seeing.

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You barely hear Caleb's question: you're concentrating on Chelsea Cooper as she walks away.

And you're concentrating on the girl, not on her ass or on her legs or on the way her golden hair cascades around her shoulders.

You don't know Chelsea Cooper, and in your time had as little to do with her as she found possible. So you don't know what she was like originally.

But you can't imagine she was anything like the simpering girl you just saw. It was creepy, and it gives you the chills.

"Hey, I'm talking to you." Seth Javits's voice breaks in on your attention, but he doesn't break your concentration on the girl. The door closing on her doesn't break it either. "She's pretty fine, ain't she," Caleb says. "It's a good thing I'm not jealous."

You turn on him. "You think I want to do your robot?"

"Yes."

"Can that be arranged?"

He turns shrewd. "Maybe. Have to set it up special. Patterson and I have to keep out of each other's way, and he gets more time up here than I do." He raises an eyebrow. "That's another reason I should get Patterson, that way I get total control over -- "

"Sure, we'll do that. I'll make the mask for you, but you gotta polish it up yourself."

"Lemme have the key to the basement, and I'll do all the work myself."

You hesitate. "Nah, I'll get it started for you. Besides, things are all upside down out there. Me and Chen -- the Chenbot, I mean -- we've been moving stuff around. Did you know there's another door in there? Anyway, I'll take care of the basics, then bring it up to you to finish."

"You gonna help me take Patterson? He's a big, mean son of a bitch."

"I'll try to fit it in." There's an awkward pause between you. "So, I guess that's it."

"Really? What did you need to see me about?"

"Well, I didn't have to, though I guess it's kind of nice. I just wanted to tell you where I was, what I was doing. So you'd know I'm here and not -- "

"Is this where you're going to be, more or less? Inside David Kirkham?" He looks amused.

"I dunno. I'll keep you posted on my whereabouts."

"Jesus, Will," he says, and he sounds more like the old Caleb. "It's not like we're on fucking Facebook and I need a status update from you every time you move."

And with that the conversation peters out. Caleb asks you to send Chelsea back up. You set the unopened beer on a box, and leave.

The staircase is deserted, and not until you're down in the gym do you spot where the Chelseabot is. There's a glow on one of the bleachers. It's gotta be from her phone. You trudge over to her.

* * * * *

Even after she'd gone, you were thinking about her, and how much it bothered you the way she was looking at you. It felt like an act, like she was only pretending to be this docile slave, and she couldn't help overdoing the effect. It gave you the shivers, for it led to a horrible thought: Maybe Gary Chen isn't asleep under that mask, maybe he's only pretending to be asleep, and he's still guiding that body and plotting against you.

That was the suspicion that came to you as she brushed past you, and it renews itself now. Your hand tightens around the new brain band. Because that's how you could figure out if Chen is only playing possum. Get the mask off him, put the band on him, and then get a look at what he's been plotting all the time he's been under that mask.

You're on top of the girl before she looks up. You can't make out her expression, but her tone is friendly. "Are you and Seth all done?"

"Yeah, but I need to talk to you a moment." Despite Kirkham's bravado, you suddenly feel very scared. It's like when you're watching a horror movie, and one of the characters goes up to closed door. You know something scary is going to happen, either when the door opens or right after.

You've got that same feeling now, only you're not watching a movie, you're in one. And the monster is Gary Chen.

Chelsea looks at you, then puts her phone down. "Yeah?"

"Uh huh. Hold still. I gotta do something to your face."

"What are you -- ? Hey!" She struggles, but not hard, as you cover her eyes with one hand and brace the back of her head with the other. You grit your teeth around some magical words, and pull.

It's almost like pulling a plunger out of a toilet, the way the mask comes away in your hand. Chelsea's head gets very heavy, and she lists to the side. Only she's not Chelsea Cooper anymore. You let Chen sink to the side onto the bleacher, and quickly get the new brain band onto him.

It's a long wait, and you pace nervously. You keep expecting Caleb to appear, looking for his girl. If he does, you'll just tell him the truth, or something like it: that Chen had some info in his brain that you needed, so you were using a brain band to get it out. There's nothing very secret about what you're doing, and if he hadn't sent the Chelseabot away, you might have done it upstairs with him there.

In fact, you come real close to dashing upstairs to tell him what you're doing. But you decide that's unnecessary. Besides, it's only guilt that's tempting you, guilt at keeping so many secrets from him.

At any rate, you're still alone when the band reappears, and the Chelseabot says nothing when the mask is back on and she's sat up again. "If Caleb asks where you were," you tell her, "tell him he can text me and I'll explain. Not that it's important."

"Caleb?" she says.

"Yeah. Oh, that's right. I meant Seth."

"That's a funny mistake to make," she says, and gets up.

You get the shivers again as you watch her dim outline walking toward the stairs to the loft.

* * * * *

You do hear from Caleb on the drive home, but it's green lights all the way and you're parked in front of your house before you can check the text. whats the deal w chelsea?

needed thing from chen's memories, you text back. used brain band on him.

smthing wrong w chenbot?

no, i wanted 2 know what chen was doing while in chelsea.

anything i need 2 know?

dont know, tell u later.

You pause, wondering if you should warn Caleb not to let the Chelseabot see those texts. You decide it's probably too late. If anything, she was probably looking over his shoulder while he was tapping them in. Or she was sucking him off while he lay back and --

Ew. Best not to visualize anything like that.

Inside the house, you pause in the living room to say hi to your mom. She's sprawled on the sofa, looking exhausted from her shift at the hospital, and watching a news program. She looks irritated, but it's not on account of the arguing pundits, as you learn when she hits the mute and points at the lamp. "What happened there?"

Oh. The lamp that Tad busted when getting down his stash. "Sorry, I meant to mention that. I knocked it over while getting a book down."

You don't know why you took the blame yourself instead of putting it on your little brother. Just one of Kirkham's reflexes, you guess.

"How'd you manage that?"

"It was one of those heavy books up there, and it slipped out of my hand when I was turning around. I did my best to get the dent out, but -- " You can only shrug helplessly.

Her face slackens back into exhaustion. "Well, we'll put it on the list as something else to get replaced, eventually."

"I can bring my lamp down from my room, switch it out with this one. I don't mind."

"I don't mind either, honey," she says, and she sounds too tired to mind it even if she'd like to. "Just be careful."

A prickle goes up and down your back. In the Prescott house, you'd thrust all blame onto the deserving Robert, and do it as fast as possible. The prickle turns into a burning blush as you feel Kirkham's contempt for your native cowardice. "Fuck you," you murmur at him. "You're not better than me."

Inside the bedroom, you take out the new brain band, giving it a cursory glance before --

You frown at it. There's something wrong.

It's the name. Oh, it's got those same goofy Chinese characters that are inside the Chen mask, the characters that spell out Chen Guangli. But in all the brain bands you've made before, the lettering has been blue.

These characters are a bright red.

So it's not a new-style brain band. Or if it is, it's a different kind of brain band.

Okay, time to check the book. You lay the band onto the spell page, give a few seconds to register, then lift and turn.

The reverse of the page is blank. You flip back to the spell. It's just that wall of text.

You sit back with a grimace.

It's clear now that you're getting into some pretty advanced stuff, the kind of stuff that's beyond the capabilities of a high school student. You turn the previous page -- the one you magically repaired. It was a sheer fluke that you got past that, and now you're wondering if it wasn't a test or a trap -- something to separate the idiots (like you) from the masters.

Maybe you should take the hint and stop now.

Or maybe you should you get Caleb to put it on, to see what it does to him.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Layers of Creepiness

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You shouldn't experiment on your friends. No, if you're going to keep playing with these items, you're going to have to do the manly thing and take the responsibility yourself. Like you did just now, with the lamp downstairs.

Testing the new brain band will require you to take off Kirkham's mask. (At least, you assume so.) So it seems best to wait until the house is dark and everyone is asleep. That shouldn't be too long, to judge by what you saw in the living room. The trouble would come if Tad got nosy during the night. He doesn't do that -- unlike your own little brother -- but tonight would be the night that he broke a long-standing habit.

You kill the time as best you can on the computer, but you can't concentrate on anything, and you keep making short trips out into the hall to see if things are shutting down. You're pleasantly surprised when you see the lights out under Tad's door, but they remain on in the living room.

And they remain on.

As midnight creeps closer, you start to worry. So you look in. The TV is still going with the sound down low, and Kirkham's mom has her head back on the couch with her mouth wide open, snoring softly. You're tempted to leave her there, but a simulacrum of devotion takes over, and you lean over to gently wake her. She doesn't want to come up out of it. When she does wake, it's with a start and a groan. "It's almost midnight," you tell her. "You fell asleep out here."

"Oh." She sighs and yawns. "I should go to bed then."

"Let me help you."

"No, that's -- "

But you insist, and you do most of the work getting her onto her feet. You also support her most of the way down the hall until she gets enough energy to make it on her own. You wish each other good night, and separate into your bedrooms.

Finally, you think when you get the door shut.

Except you're really not relieved. You're rather dreading what comes next, because you don't know what it will be. You prepare by putting the brain band and your cell phone under the pillow: that's where Kirkham keeps the latter at night anyway. Then you take off your clothes and put out the light and crawl between the sheets. You settle in, trying to get comfortable. After all this delay, your nerve almost fails you at the last moment, but with a spasm of resolve you seize your own brow and pull.

* * * * *

You wake with a start, looking wildly around. It's pitch dark. You scramble about, feeling for the cell phone, and cuss groggily as you pull it from under the pillow.

It's almost exactly five minutes after you put out the lights. Jesus. You were half convinced you'd slept most of the night away.

You lay back down and blink at the dark ceiling. You touch your face -- the long thin whiskers on your chin and lip -- and you hair. It feels just the way you remember it feeling, but you shiver and sink down under the covers some more. It's a kind of horrifying thrill: You're in David Kirkham's bedroom, in his bed -- and you're naked, too! You shiver again, thinking of the magic that has brought you to such an unlikely spot.

Then you pull the new brain band from under the pillow. You look at the Chinese characters on the band. Their glow looks more sinister than usual. Probably that's because they are red and not blue. And the significance of that is?

Only one way to find out.

With a deep breath that's almost a gulp, you lay the band onto your forehead.

* * * * *

A hole opens in the center of your forehead, and the universe rushes into it.

* * * * *

You come halfway out of the bed with a stifled yelp, grabbing at air. Your heart batters at your ribs, and you pull in deep breath after deep breath. You are trembling all over.

Nausea washes over you, and you fall forward with a groan, covering your face with your hands, fighting the urge to throw up. Something is aching inside your brain. It's a foreign presence, a parasite. You spasm once, then again, and fall over onto your side.

It's Prescott. That's who's inside your head. Hatred like a blowtorch blasts your brain, but it can't burn him out. He only retreats, like a shadow springing away from bright light.

Yes, that's all he is, a shadow, like that shadow mind of Chelsea that you carried around with you after putting her mask on. It was creepy having her draped around you, but it was useful too. When you were with her family or friends, running things in the cafeteria or on the gym floor, you could wrap her around yourself, like a little kid wrapping himself in a sheet to pretend he was a ghost. Then she covered you completely, and you could prattle and sneer and preen and simper just like the real girl while still peeping out through the eyeholes. Sometimes it was fun.

Oh hell, it was almost always fun.

But now Chelsea's gone and it's Prescott in here with you, and that's a lot less fun. If he was outside you could beat the shit out of him, but there's no getting ahold of him here.

Here. You blink.

Shit.

Gingerly, with deep loathing because you know what you're going to find, you put your fingertips to your face. It's Prescott's face. That nausea, which had swiftly ebbed, just as swiftly rolls back in again.

Oh, God damn it. Prescott's memories, after fleeing your onslaught, rush back in. Instantly, hideously, you see it all from his perspective. He made a new toy and he tried it out and --

You flop onto your back, your eyes straining as you try to see the solution in the dark.

Somehow the new brain band pulled you out of your own body and put you into his.

Oh, Christ! He pulled off a reverse possession! Instead of possessing someone else's body, he got himself possessed by you! Or it was a real body swap.

You laugh out loud, once. You don't know whether to be horrified or ecstatic.

Horrified, because you're trapped in a body that makes you want to vomit.

Or ecstatic, because in Prescott's body and with his memories and his toys, you're running the show!

You inhale and exhale deeply, and now when you run Prescott's flabby hands over your chest it's with a mix of loathing and malicious glee. The fucker outsmarted himself!

Being in his body brings other problems than the obvious one -- that it's a sickening place to be. You can't show his face in public, since there's someone else -- Gordon Black of all people! -- parading around in it. But a mask can hide it.

Trouble is, the only mask at hand is the one he made of Kirkham. Christ, there's another reason to punch your new body in the face. But come the morning, at any rate, you're going to have to put the mask back on and go to school as Kirkham.

Once you're there, though, you can get your old face back by taking it off Kirkham, and then you and he can --

Now dread washes over you. Yes, you and he can be partners, right, like he and the fake you were working as partners. Can't you? Except, how can you explain to him that you're really Gary Chen, when you've got Prescott's body?

And what happened to your old body? The last thing that Prescott saw was Chelsea Cooper walking away to be with Seth Javits. Who was guiding the thing? No one, because it was just one of these robots he's been making? Or is Prescott inside your old body the way you're inside his? Was it an actual body swap?

No, that couldn't be what happened. Because Prescott was himself all the way back to Kirkham's. The possession didn't happen until a little while ago, when he slapped that new band onto himself.

You drape your arm over your forehead, and reflect that there are advantages to being inside Prescott, but there are disadvantages, too. The main disadvantage being that you are just as ignorant about these things as he was.

* * * * *

You can't sleep for all the problems and questions to think through, but you can't think through them either because you are so ill and tired from fear and frustration. It's closer to morning than to midnight when you finally give up and try to catch a little sleep. And then it turns out to be easy to get to sleep. All you have to do is put Kirkham's mask back on.

The alarm wakes you at around six forty-five, and you're out of bed and wondering why you went to sleep nude before you remember clearly who you are and how you got to be here. You surprise yourself by feeling no embarrassment at being in Kirkham's naked body, but that's largely because you two got used to being stripped down in athletic changing rooms. You shower, dry yourself and your hair, look yourself over in the mirror, and with a guilty resentment reflect that Kirkham shouldn't -- and doesn't -- have nearly as much trouble as you do at getting laid. Then you get dressed, fix breakfast for yourself and Tad while sparing him only as many words as you can get away with, and see him out the door to catch a ride with his best friend, Aidan Baumgarten, who lives just half a block down the street. Then you get in Kirkham's car and take a much more circuitous route to the same school as you do some thinking.

You've got a couple of choices. One is to get the mask off your best friend and try to explain things to him. Maybe he'll understand. Another is to keep muddling through, as Prescott had been muddling through. Maybe you can get the Chelsea mask off your old body to see what's up. A third possibility is to see if there's any expert help out there to draw on.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: A Total Change of Mind

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Westside High doesn't look any different as you approach it. It's the same blank wall of the gym facing the student parking lot, and the same athletic fields off to the right. You don't feel any different about it either. As far as you're concerned it's the same dumb concentration camp it's been since you entered it as a freshman, with the same dumb mix of kids. The dumb brainiacs and the dumb party dudes and party girls, the dumb jocks and the dumb troublemakers. Girls who are prissy and girls who are easy and girls who haven't decided which one they want to be.

Only the toothpick rolling around in your mouth feels different. That's Kirkham's toothpick, and you're walking into the school under the face of your best friend. You have no choice, not after the way Prescott fucked him over.

You have to give that little douchebag some credit, though. He's done a smart job of expanding your trade network, and has seeded masks in all the right places. It was your brain that was helping him, of course, same as it was your nerve that gave him the courage to swap in some more morons and to put the Eastman trade into the hands of that robot. The same nerve that led him to kick the shit out of Josh Call when that fuckwit told Kirkham what was going on, and the same nerve that handled your best friend when he tried horning in.

And it was the same nerve that let him drop the boom onto you by trapping you under that mask of Chelsea. You should have seen it coming. You should have known that if he had the guts to run your business he'd have the guts to handle you. But he played it cool and didn't let you see how good he was doing. He just took everything over, and when he finally got tired of your shit he got rid of you too.

But you got the last laugh, didn't you?

And you'll definitely have the last laugh once you figure out how to get back into your body. Then you and Kirkham will really start running things.

But you'll have to wait to tell your friend what's going on. Telling him now, when you're still trapped in Prescott's body, would carry too many risks. It would be really easy for him not to believe you.

That's the final plan, though. But to get there you'll need help. You'll need to find someone who can at the very least interpret sigils and tell you what you're getting into. You're not going to make Prescott's mistake and dive further into the book without understanding what you're getting into.

* * * * *

Kirkham's schedule isn't lighter than yours, but it's less intensely demanding. It opens with a study hall, which gives you a chance to catch up on your sleep before going in to AP Calculus. After that you get the muscles warmed and stretched and tested in a weightlifting class in the gym, and then you let them cool off in Chemistry II. Lunch brings another respite before sixth period AP Statistics.

That's the class that Kirkham and Chen share. You're nervous while waiting for him to show up. You're not sure what it's going to be like to sit in a desk next to yourself. Kirkham's memories are one thing, but to experience it yourself?

You're irritably clicking your mechanical pencil when he comes in the door. It gives you a start.

The first thing you notice about Chen Guangli is his nervous, thrusting energy. He doesn't walk into the classroom, he sizzles in. His eyes flash about, taking in the room. The pack is off his shoulders before he's halfway to his chair, and he swings it easily onto the back of his desk. He slips into his seat and turns to give you a gleaming, slant-eyed smile.

His eyes are curved like scimitars, and his upper lip curls when he smiles, showing his teeth. Strands of dark hair stick out from under his gray ski cap; they are curved and pointed like Oriental knives. He's in a heavy camo jacket, and the cuffs of his cargo trousers are stuffed into the tops of heavy boots. Everything about him screams "Don't tread on me."

He's almost impossibly sexy.

"So what do you hear?" he asks in a low hiss.

You shrug, for you can't get your tongue and throat to work. You're deeply conscious of the fact that your cock is springing up hard.

"Yeah, everything's okay with me too." He slaps a jacket pocket. "Heavy trade today."

"Is it?" You can form those words only because they don't require any effort. You're horrified by how excited you are to be sitting next to this guy.

"I'm fucking stoked, man. Where was this business last year when I really needed it?"

"It was sitting in a used bookstore."

"What do you mean?"

"Nothing." You lean forward to hide your erection, but that just squeezes it harder, and you pinch your eyes shut. "We should hang out this afternoon, I got some research to do."

"Sure, I got time before work. What kind of research?"

"That book. We need to find someone who can read it."

"We do? I thought we were reading it."

"I mean, really read it, not just Google translate it. I almost had a disaster last night."

"What happened?"

"Nothing. It worked out, it worked out in a fucking awesome way, but we can't take any more risks."

"So how do we -- ?"

But class starts. You can barely pay attention, for your eyes keep straying over to Gary Chen. And it's like your dick is straining at him too, like a dowsing rod.

* * * * *

You're relieved to head into Chamber Orchestra after that. That's actually Kirkham's favorite class of the day -- a chance to play some challenging music with people who are up for it.

But mostly you're glad for the chance to figure out what the hell was going on with you in Statistics.

You are pretty fucking sure that Kirkham isn't gay. He doesn't look at gay porn or ogle passing guys. His tastes seem pretty normal, inclining toward girls with big bosoms and firm asses. You wrack his memories and can't come up with occasions in which Kirkham felt anything but a brotherly affection for Gary Chen.

You'd love to pin it onto the Prescott personality you're lugging around. But a quick glance in there finds only a healthy fear and loathing of "Gary Chen," and certainly nothing like a tongue-tied lust for him. Besides, you've got Prescott's personality completely cowed, and not once in any of your classes have you felt an impression or a fleeting emotion whose alien nature could be connected to your erstwhile tormentor.

So that only leaves one explanation: you have a jonesing for yourself.

You force the issue aside during Orchestra, and relax to the strains of Mozart as you practice one of his string quartets with the other advanced students. Sometimes Kirkham wishes he was closer to the other guys in there, so that he could relax and joke around with them. But ever since he beat up Andrew Harding -- the viola player -- in the tenth grade, no one in the class says more to Kirkham than they have to. But that gives you time to get into the music and to let your troubles drift away on the melodies.

But your last class, the blow-off "Asian Alphabets" language class, brings you back to the problem of your reaction to the doppelganger. You decide finally not to be bothered by it. Surely it doesn't mean anything, that you were physically attracted to yourself. It was a matter of the look, surely. You take a great deal of care in dressing yourself and projecting an image, and it's an image you calculated not only to be intimidating but to look good while looking intimidating. You've scoped yourself out in the mirror plenty of times. How much more exciting should it be to see a reflection you've made sexy to your satisfaction when it's alive and not mirroring your own movements?

Only at the end of class do you remember what happened to Prescott when he put on Jamie Rennerhoff's mask and went to the Warehouse. And if you got alone with your robotic doppelganger, what would happen then?

* * * * *

The memory of that sufficiently alarms you that you cancel your after-school meeting and flee to the Keyserling college library instead. There you spend a couple of hours browsing the internet with your phone, and using leads picked up there to try finding books on magic and its different school and techniques. Armed with this info you hunt down what books there are in the library. There are a lot of them in the school catalog -- a lot more than you'd hoped to find -- but most of them have been checked out. You get a lead on a couple of professors who might be able to help -- the ones in medieval studies are the most likely to know something useful -- but by the time you've moved on to them it's too late to go find any, and you go home for supper and homework and another desultory glance through the grimoire. The next spell settles it for you: it's nothing but sigils enclosed in sigils, with no regular words at all to translate. You're not going to tangle with that.

You go back online to check out when and where you might meet up with those university professors, and cuss to find that although they have hours set aside to meet with students, they are all during the weekday hours when you'll be in class.

All except one guy, a professor who includes in his specialties "non-Romance languages." The sigils surely count as a "non-Romance" language, and his office hours begin at four o'clock. But the rest of them? Ten o'clock, eleven-fifteen, one-fifteen. They don't have hours on Saturdays or Sundays, either.

So, there it is. You can skip class tomorrow, or you can meet this Dr. Terry Lipscomb after class tomorrow and hope that he can give you a lead.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: That Unexpected Object of Desire

choices: Use the band on this guy -- now!
Let him talk first -- he might be useless
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Friday the Thirty-First.

It doesn't sound as scary as "Friday the Thirteenth," despite "31" being a transposition of "13." But it's Friday, October the Thirty-First. And it being Halloween makes it even scarier.

Because that means all the dipshits at school will be dressing up. And that's probably why, at the last minute, you jerk the wheel to the right instead of to the left and drive out to the university instead of to school. You can afford to skip a day.

Hell, you might even get one of the professors to write you a note saying that you were up at the college talking about taking some classes. That might get the school administration -- if they even cared -- to expunge the absence from your record.

If they even keep records of such things. Sometimes you wonder if anyone really pays attention.

* * * * *

Your first lead is a Dr. William Steed, who is the sole scholar in Keyserling's Medieval Studies Program. It's good that you headed up to the college early instead of stopping for coffee and a donut someplace, because it takes you forever to find a parking lot that doesn't require a permit. It does leave you enough time, though, to walk over to the student union, where you buy a small black coffee at the Dunn Brewin' inside the food court.

Then it's over to the History building, where Steed has his office. It's an ugly old building from the 1970s: a cube that's four stories tall and is as wide and squat as it is tall. The lower three floors are entirely windowless, being covered in stone panels engraved with things that look like hieroglyphs. Why someone would erect such a building you don't understand; and the only people to work in it, you're sure, are people who have incurred the university president's displeasure.

Maybe Steed is one of them. He's on the third floor, squatting in an office that's a little bigger than a janitorial supply closet. And he is literally squatting, for he's on the floor scooping up papers that have apparently toppled off a tall stack of folders at the corner of his desk. It also surprises you -- though why should it? -- that he is African American.

You knock, and he turns wide, almost wild eyes up at you. "Dr. Steed?" you ask.

"Yeah?" His voice is a rich baritone. You bet he drives the ladies wild with it.

"Do you have a minute to talk?"

"Sure. That's what office hours are for. Be with you in a minute." He resumes pulling the papers together, glances through them with a sigh, and looks around for a place to put them. Every surface in the office is covered with books or folders. With a helpless gesture, he points to a chair in front of his desk, which is similarly stacked with papers. "You can move that onto floor," he says. "Just be careful, okay?"

The title on the top sheet catches your eye, though it doesn't mean anything to you: "The Newt and the Sparrow: Metaphors of Medievalism and Modernism in Confrontation." "I'm editing a book," Dr. Steed says. With a wry smile, he adds, "Though 'randomly shuffling' might be a better way to describe it. Are you one of my students?"

"No. I'm in high school."

"Oh. Are you interested in history?"

"Uh -- " You don't know if honesty will get you credit or not.

"Well, never mind. You're not selling band candy, are you?" He waves a large hand with long, graceful fingers, and you can't help noticing the flutter -- like the different sides of an aspen leaf -- of the dark of his hand against the light of his palm.

"No. It's about an old book I found in the used book store."

"They're usually old if they're in a used book store," he observes as you lean forward to open your back pack.

"Well, this one was in the special collections area." You pull out the grimoire. "Most of it's in Latin, but some of it uses some other kind of alphabet or something." You hesitate before handing it over to him. "I guess I want to know if anyone can read it, or if it's just decoration." You lay the book in his outstretched hands.

But he doesn't set it on the desk. Instead, he peers at it, and tickles the cover with his fingertips. "It doesn't appear to be a medieval work," he says. "A modern reproduction, likely. Has it got a name?"

"I'm not sure. It's in Latin, and I don't see anything that look like a title, like, uh -- " You glance down at that other manuscript. "The Newt and the Sparrow," etc., doesn't seem like much of a title either.

"Yeah, they were pretty long-winded back then," says Dr. Steed. "They believed that you should know exactly what you were getting when you picked up one of their books." With that, he opens the grimoire.

He pauses on the first page, the one with the metamorphosing faces. He stares at them with an unchanging expression. You keep your own face neutral. Dr. Steed doesn't look up at you, but he does let out a quiet "Whew," and turns to the next page. "Handwritten," he says. "If it's a reproduction, it's a very good reproduction, done up to look like an early modern manuscript. Where did you find this?" Now he does look at you, piercingly.

"At Arnholm's, in the special collections. It was two hundred dollars."

"Then it's certainly a reproduction. I can't imagine Ted Arnholm letting something original go for a price that low."

You'd feel disappointment, but your interest in the book isn't monetary. "The stuff I'm talking about -- "

You lean forward to turn the page, but Dr. Steed blocks you. "Tch tch tch, let me do it my way, if you want my opinion. Your book would have been called the Libra Personae, I can tell you that much, though the actual title is much longer and descriptive. He rests his chin in his cupped hand. "It's a work in neo-Aristotelian natural philosophy, if it's describing itself accurately. Alchemical and astrological in particular." He chuckles. "You might have found yourself an authentic grimoire. Or a reproduction of one."

You pretend to be surprised. "Really?"

"No. More likely a parody. The late nineteenth century was thick with pretended antiques." He turns a page. "It might be worth a monograph if we knew who the author was." He turns another page.

"Can you tell me what that is." You point at a sigil.

Dr. Steed studies it closely. Then he reads the rest of the page. "Yes, this is definitely pretending to be a grimoire. That thing is supposed to be part of the magic. You'll summon a demon or something if you use it right." He traces the edges of the sigil with a fingertip, and his lips work. Then he shakes his head. "No, this isn't an alphabet, or if it is, it's not one that I recognize." He raises his head to study the bookshelf behind you. He grunts. "No, I don't have anything that would -- No, sorry." He smiles sadly at you. "If this were the Ecole Nationale I could tell you exactly who to talk to. But at Keyserling?" He shrugs, and sits back in his chair.

So you thank him and go on your way.

* * * * *

It's the same story with Dr. Lisa Potowski in the History of Science program, and with Dr. Samuel Chidwick in the Philosophy Department. You're down to that linguistics professor whose office hours begin after Westside's classes are over, but Dr. Chidwick catches you just as you're leaving. "I'll tell you who you should talk to," he says. "Aubrey Blackwell, over in the Anthropology Department. He's interested in alchemy and stuff like that." You thank him and head across campus.

Dr. Blackwell's office door is closed, but the departmental secretary tells you he's in a meeting with the chairman and will be out in a little while. You slump in the hall in front of his door so he'll have to pass you on his way back.

It was always going to be a long shot finding someone locally who can read and understand the sigils, or even point you in the right direction, so you try not to feel despair as you're down to your last two possibilities. But you're not inclined to waste time on hopes that won't bear out, so you've already got the book out in your lap when a bulky figure looms over you.

He's a tall man, with a fat, apple-like belly sticking out inside a black pullover sweater that's encased in a tweed jacket. His face is pasty and a few lank hairs cover a bald scalp. A badly trimmed goatee and moustache disfigure his mouth. "I beg your pardon," he says with patent insincerity as he tries to step around you. You scramble to your feet.

"Dr. Blackwell? If you've got a minute I -- "

You stop, for he's clearly not listening to you. His eyes are fastened on the book in your hand, and a bit of green is showing in his cheeks.

Slowly, he raises his eyes to meet yours. "Ah," he croaks. "You are here to see me?"

You shiver all over. "Yeah. It's about this book."

"Then please come in." He jams a key at the lock, but misses it completely because he can't keep from glancing down at the book you're holding. He pokes at it a few more times with the key, and finally gets the door open. He draws a labored breath, and turns on the light.

You did not come to this meeting unarmed, and you did not go looking for someone who would read and explain the spells to you. You went looking for a brain to copy, the way that Prescott and Johansson copied Mr. Leavey's brain when they were trying understand the Latin. So last night you made up a new brain band. One of the classics, not the new kind.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Does Anyone Here Know How to Play This Game?

choices: Move what you need out of it
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You're pretty sure you've found your guy, just from the way Dr. Blackwell was looking at the book. It was the look of man shocked and excited by something he recognizes. And since the grimoire is not obviously an antique -- Dr. Steed immediately thought it looked like a reproduction -- then this Blackwell character must be excited by the book itself.

So you don't wait for Blackwell to turn around or even to get across his office. You reach up behind him and put the brain band to his forehead. He instantly folds up and falls to the floor. Thank God he didn't fall backward against you. The man must weigh at least two hundred and sixty pounds.

You close his office door and lock it, and roll the toothpick around speculatively in your mouth as you wait for the job to finish. You hope you didn't misjudge the man, and your immediate excitement quickly wanes as you wait for the brain band to reappear. But a feeling of anticipation returns as you ponder where and when to test the new band out. There's no rush to get inside the professor's mind; the grimoire can wait, even if Blackwell's brain can help you out. But you want to settle the question of whether he even can help you out, so you decide to find yourself a private spot here on campus so you can get the brain band on as quick as you can.

Minutes pass. There's a stealthy sound outside, as of someone approaching the door. It's locked, so you don't worry about being found like this, but you tense anyway. The person, if they did pause at Blackwell's door, moves off. You perch on the edge of the desk and will the brain band to finish and to finish quickly.

It doesn't obey you, but it eventually reappears. You check it, and there's Blackwell's name in the familiar, spooky blue lettering. You sneak out, closing the door softly behind you.

A fast trot downstairs and across a small parking lot brings you to the college library. You run inside and take the stairs to the uppermost floor, then into the back where there is a set of restrooms. You lock yourself in the furthest stall of the men's room, wedge yourself between the wall and the toilet, and pull Kirkham's face off.

* * * * *

You've fallen over and are staring into the toilet when you wake, and that gets you alert again real fast, because there are brown streaks at the bottom of the bowl. You rub your eyes, and dig Blackwell's brain band out of your pocket. Without pausing, you put it to your forehead.

You don't lose consciousness, not fully, but you sink into a deep lethargy, for it feels like the man has brought his fat body with him and is squeezing the whole thing into your brain. It makes you glad that the masks knock you out when you put them on, because it's no fun to have the actual memories thrusting themselves inside of you. It's like your own brain is being stretched and pulled apart -- uncoiled and untwisted like a Slinkie -- so that the new ones can fit inside. The actual experience is like remembering a forgotten dream, only far bigger and more intense, like a whole lifetime coming inside that dream. Incidents and impressions crowd in and cohere, feeling both novel and instantly familiar, knitting together into a remembered life of Aubrey Blackwell. You cover your head in your arms and shut your eyes and gasp as the inner portrait of this man comes into focus.

And then all of him is there and he's settled in and watching you malignantly from the back of your skull, waiting to be interrogated.

You turn your face toward the toilet again, in case you have to vomit.

* * * * *

You definitely found your man. You found more than your man. You found the grimoire's previous owner.

The Libra personae is not a famous book, except in certain very exotic circles, where it is an object of fascination and intense desire. Professor Aubrey Blackwell -- he does not have an actual doctorate, and earned his position at Keyserling through the sheer force of his learning -- definitely moves in those circles. It would be far less accurate to refer to him as an anthropology professor who dabbles in the occult than a black magician who supports himself by teaching anthropology.

Earlier that summer, the professor heard rumors of an odd book found among the effects of a dead woman in upstate New York. He got himself up to the offices where her effects were being collected for distribution, and managed to abstract the Libra from them. Never mind the precise means he used, for they were no more complicated than switching labels on packages. But by early August he had the Libra at his house and had begun to go through it.

But he had not gotten as deeply inside it as Prescott had before a clumsy work-study student accidentally sent it off to Arholm's Used Books. The professor had raced to the bookstore as soon as he learned of the error, but found that the book had already been sold, and that it could not be further traced.

He had become resigned to its loss, until just a few minutes ago, when a very young college freshman had shown up at his office, holding the Libra and desiring an appointment ...

You get shakily to your feet, and Kirkham's mask clatters onto the tile floor. You know exactly what Blackwell intended to do to you once he had you inside his office, and you know that he knows what you look like. Well, what David Kirkham looks like.

And you know perfectly well how implacable the professor is, and what he would do to you if he caught you.

You could hide. You could let David Kirkham go missing and put yourself under a different face, maybe back under your own. But Kirkham is your friend, and after what that cocksucker Prescott did to him, you're not going to do worse by erasing his existence.

No, you have to strike at Blackwell first.

And if you pull off that strike, you'll collect more dividends than you ever dreamed of collecting when you set out for the university this morning.

* * * * *

Blackwell doesn't know Will Prescott's face, so you're safe enough returning to your car just by packing Kirkham's mask into your backpack. You don't run -- you don't want to draw attention to yourself -- and you don't take the subsequent drive recklessly. But you need to get out to Blackwell's house before he can get home, and you can't be sure he's not on his way there now.

And of course, you know where you need to go and what you need to do when you get there, because you've got the professor himself inside your head to help you out.

He lives in a two-story stone villa on the western edge of town, close to the river. It sits out mostly by itself -- the nearest house is a quarter mile away -- on a low rise behind a whitewashed stone wall. The professor's car is not in its accustomed spot out front, and nor is it in the back, which is where you park. There's no time to scout further; you'll just have to chance the house being really and not just apparently empty.

There's a culvert dug into the hillside behind the villa's grounds. Its entrance is shut up behind a metal gate that shrieks on its hinges as you open it, but carries no further alarm. Inside that culvert is a tunnel that serves as a secret back door to the house. The tunnel grows pitch dark as you feel your way cautiously forward. You trip onto your destination -- a set of brick stairs that lead up to a very ordinary looking door. Like the gate, it is unlocked, and leads you into a very suburban laundry room.

The professor only appears to be careless about keeping his property secure. He has a kind of guard dog prowling the place, but you know what to expect and are not going to let it faze you if it starts acting up. Quickly you make your way to the pantry in the kitchen, where behind some carefully placed boxes of mac-and-cheese and Saltine crackers are jars of pastes and ointments labeled with Hebrew characters. You take out one of these jars, pull some rubber gloves from a drawer, and go to the front door. A quick peek outside shows the coast is clear.

With gloved hands you coat the outside handle of the front door with the slimy ointment from the jar. Back in the kitchen you replace the jar and carefully peel off the gloves, tucking them deep inside the trash. Then you go upstairs to the spare bedroom in the front of the house where you can watch from behind closed curtains for the professor's arrival.

It turns out you didn't have to hurry quite so much, for it's forty minutes before the professor's car pulls up outside the front gate. During that time you ignore the patter of footsteps in the hall and above the ceiling, the insistent scratching against the wall, and the shadowy limbs that appear outside the windows. It's an effective display, and you have to steel yourself against them in order to concentrate on the business of watching and waiting and figuring out what comes next. The professor's a great help to you there too, and his memories of the things he has done help firm up your intention to deal with him exactly as he deserves and exactly in a way that will make you safe from him.

But you go on alert as he comes into his front yard. You can't make out his expression from behind the thin curtain across the window, but his tread is very determined. From downstairs you hear a rattle of the knob --

And then silence.

So much for Professor Blackwell. The question now is, do you make his house your new base of operations?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Another Brain for Your Collection

choices: No, there's work to do with masks
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Whether or not you make Blackwell's villa your base, you have the professor himself to take care of. There's no rush, but you can't help taking the stairs three at a bound on your way down to the foyer. The front door is open, and you can't help snickering at the sight of the professor sprawled outside on the stoop. It's almost like a running gag: Every time he gets close to you, you knock him cold.

You avoid touching the booby-trapped door handle as you step over the unconscious man and run around to the side of the house where the old mausoleum is. The door resists being opened, but you shove it back and let fresh air into the dim and musty interior. There's an empty stone bier inside, just waiting for Blackwell, but you'll need help moving him onto it, so you send a text to your 'bot, telling him where to find you. Then from the kitchen pantry you get a less concentrated aerosol of the same knock-out ointment and spray it over Blackwell's face. That will keep him out for hours.

Inside the library --

And though Blackwell's memories have prepared you for it, you still pause just inside the threshold. It's a very large and impressive space, and is the main reason for moving your operations here. The ceiling floats two stories above, and from the floor to the ceiling on three sides it is covered in bookshelves: a wheeled ladder on a railing is necessary to get the books that are high up. Three desks occupy the wide space in the center of the room, and tall windows and a French door in the fourth wall let in the afternoon sunlight.

But despite the high ceiling and clear glass, an air of gloom and oppression settles on the room. Some if it is due to the discordant ticking of two grandfather clocks in one corner. Then too there are the alcoves from inside which stare balefully the stuffed corpses of impossible creatures. One of these -- a small orangutan-like monkey with a single, Cyclops-like eye in its forehead and razor-like teeth in its grinning mouth -- is out of its alcove and sitting on a table. You put it back in its spot while muttering a spell that Aubrey Blackwell knows but that Gary Chen doesn't. With that, those uncanny noises should cease to disturb you.

From another alcove you take down a stuffed two-headed dog and wedge it under your armpit. Then you twist a candlestick stuck incongruously in one of the walls. A door disguised as a bookcase unlatches with a click and swings open. Ah, the classics!

Inside is a narrow, hidden basement lit from a single window. A metal spiral staircase leads down to a concrete floor. This hidden dungeon has held a number of prisoners over the years -- you grimace to think of the last prisoner, and her ghastly fate -- but now only holds the supplies that Blackwell had accumulated when he still had the Libra. These include most of the ingredients you will need for finishing off the professor; the one ingredient you lack can be got next to one of the tombstones next to the mausoleum.

Then, once you've got the stuff set out in the library, you sit down to write a letter. With Blackwell's brain band you can, after a little warm up practice, imitate his handwriting perfectly, and on some of his stationary you compose the following:

My dear doppelganger,

This letter will find you after I have already departed. I have not time now to explain where I have gone nor to issue instructions in person. You know what you must do, and what will happen to you if I return to find you have been derelict or rebellious. I will contact you when I can, but I can as yet not anticipate my return date. Remember that you know not the time or day that may be!

This letter comes to you from the hand of a young man I have just engaged. He has my complete trust, and you may trust those for whom he vouches. Again, I have not time to explain, but you will notice that he is in possession of the Libra personae, and it is due to that book that he and I have experienced a perfect meeting of the minds. You are to give him every aid that he asks for.

In addition, and I hesitate to trouble you with it, but there it is: The roost under Sister Theresa's whatnot has another tear in the elevating/latching lever. You ought understand.

You sign it with a flourish, tuck it in an envelope, and seal it with wax.

You go to put Kirkham's mask back on, but stop just in time. Blackwell's ghost reminds you that you are already wearing one brain band, and that it is bad idea to introduce another -- as is contained in Kirkham's mask -- above it.

You cuss. That means that you're going to have to show Prescott's fucking face to the world when you are using Blackwell's memories.

* * * * *

It's another hour before you Jeep slides to a hard stop next to Blackwell's car. Your 'bot at the gate before you're out the door. "Christ, what the hell is this place," he exclaims, then does a double take at you. He grins. "What the fuck happened to your face?"

"Fuck you, that's what happened. I need your help moving this asshole."

Your 'bot takes off his sunglasses and whistles down at the professor, who you've laid out a little more neatly on the porch. "What happened here?"

"Fucking point is what happens next. Help me pick him up."

He blanches, but complies, grasping Blackwell by his wrists while you take the feet. Your 'bot, despite his strength, can't get Blackwell off the ground, and the professor's fat ass drags across the dirt across as you haul him to the mausoleum.

Three bodies collide at the door to the crypt before you squeeze inside. Chen tries to flatly refuse moving Blackwell onto the bier, but he can't argue with his master, and together you manage to roll the whale-like prof up and onto the stone slab. "What now?" Chen asks. "Lock him in?"

"Nope, I'll be right back." The struggle to move Blackwell has winded Prescott's scrawny frame, but snapping and twanging nerves give you the energy to run back inside for the ingredients. In the mausoleum you sprinkle Blackwell with dirt and incendiary chemicals. "Okay, now I need to take your face off a minute."

The end result -- after you've added some of David Kirkham's hair to the mess, and lit it on fire -- is a stone-like statue, much like the one you made when Prescott performed the same spell on your grandfather.

The difference is that this 'bot -- "golem" would be the word Blackwell would use -- won't be under your control. It will be under David Kirkham's control. That's only an academic point, since Kirkham is now back under the Chenbot mask and so isn't around to give orders. But making him the master of the new golem builds in a failsafe in case this new golem proves troublesome.

And why didn't you use Prescott's hair, so you could control the golem as you control the others?

Because Aubrey Blackwell's brain told you that would be a bad idea.

* * * * *

"I must say I am surprised, very surprised," says Aubrey Blackwell when you and he and Chen are standing in the library a little later. Blackwell himself, while he had the Libra, had made a mask of himself, and you retrieved that mask from the hidden basement and set it on the new golem in order to revive a copy of the professor. Its memories will be almost a week out of date, but it can muddle through.

Right now the trouble is that it is being argumentative.

"Look, it's all there in the letter," you say, pointing to the note the golem is holding. "At least, I assume it is. He told me to give it to you, that it would explain everything."

"It certainly does not," snaps the Blackwell-bot. He looks between you and the letter, with a side glance at Chen. "It is most suspicious."

Blackwell's replacement makes no allusion to the gnomic closing lines of the letter, and since you are not supposed to know what the letter says, you don't allude to them either. But it bugs the shit out of you that the 'bot is ignoring their obvious meaning and trying to disbelieve what the letter says.

"I don't know how to contact, uh, Professor Blackwell," you say. "But if he calls me, do you want me to have him call you?"

"Certainly," says the golem, but it also seems a little discomfited by the idea.

"I will. Meantime, you get to be the professor, but you also have to do what the real professor says. Don't you?"

The golem makes a face, but allows that that's true.

"Okay, I'm supposed to work here after school, going through the books and making notes for the professor. You got a spare key?"

"Somewhere. But there are certain things you need to know if you're going to be here without my supervision." A nasty look comes onto its face.

"Professor Blackwell already explained them to me. You mean the gwarcheidwad, right?"

"Among other things." Now the golem screws its face up into a look of sour disappointment. "I'll get you the key. By the way, what is your name, young man?"

You hesitate. You'll be working here under Prescott's face, but you don't want to use Prescott's name in case the golem gets nosy. "You can call me 'David Kirkham'."

Chen chortles up the back of his nose, and you shoot him a hot glare. "Well, Mr. Kirkham," says the Blackwell-bot, "I will get you the key. Will you be starting this afternoon?"
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Prep Work for New Digs

choices: Study up on the soul-swapping spell
Study up on how the masks work
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Before you can answer Blackwell's question, Chen nudges you in the side and mutters in your ear. "Your friend Johansson wants to know when he's gonna get that thing you promised him."

The mask to use on Patterson. "God damn it. I should fucking shove him -- " You stifle yourself as the Blackwell-bot raises an eyebrow. "I can fix it up here, I guess. I got the shit, but I need you to get the stuff out of the elementary school and bring it over. You got time to do that before you go to work?" Chen nods. "Good. Go do that. I'll text my mom, tell her I'm spending the night at your place."

"Your mom?" asks Chen with a grin. "Whose mom is that, Prescott?"

You'd forgotten whose face you're showing, and you show your double the bird. "Kirkham's mom, then, if you wanna be fucking technical. Tell her I'm staying at your place -- "

"You can't stay at my place, Prescott, there's no -- "

"It's just a fucking story for her, I'll be staying here. Christ."

The Blackwell-bot frowns. "I don't think that's a good idea, er, Mr. Prescott. The gwarcheidwad -- "

"I told you, I know all about it. I'll be fine. Look -- " You jerk your chin at the monster's reliquary. "I even put it back in its alcove when I came in. That's how come we don't have it howling around our ears right now."

"Ah. Well then." The golem grimaces bitterly. "I will fetch you that spare house key." He glowers, then pads from the room.

You pull Chen out the front door. "Jesus, man," he hisses as you trot down the front walk, and glances back at the villa. "I know it's fucking Halloween, but did you hafta pick out the perfect house to spend it in?"

You know what he means. The house -- especially its library -- is thick with occult forebodings. "Get used to it, man. This is our new place."

"This?" You're surprised and a little disappointed at the way he squeaks when he says it. "The fuck is wrong with your house? Or mine? Or that fucking basement? We were having a lot of fun there."

You open the Jeep door for him and push him inside. "You don't remember trying to kill me out there?"

"Sure I do! Like I said, we had some good times."

"Well, we're going to do it here. Dude, bro, honored sibling." You lean in the window to put your face close to his, for you still feel the electric thrill of being close to yourself. "That is a real haunted house, and that is a real warlock who lived there. He's got a magical library of the blackest shit, and we are going to use it to pound the fuck out of anyone who gets in our way."

The grin that splits your face is so wide and gleeful you think the top of your head might fall off. Chen stares at you, then grins back. "Whoa. I like the way that sounds."

"You sure as fucking better. Now go bring our shit out here. And text Johansson, tell him you'll have his mask ready tomorrow morning."

Your other face falls. "If we're gonna pound the fuck outta people, can't we start with him?"

"He's on the list, but I gotta figure out what we're gonna do and when. Until then, we gotta keep him happy and stupid."

"At least he's already stupid," says Chen. He slips on his sunglasses and starts the Jeep.

* * * * *

The Blackwell-bot is waiting for you in the foyer, and hands you a key like he's handing over a cheap tip to a server who's badly disappointed him. You've been dealing with the thing for less than an hour, and even with the professor's own personality knocking around inside your head you can hardly stand the company of his replacement. If you hadn't been in such a goddam hurry at the university, maybe you could have gotten used to the idea of making a pet of the professor before actually doing it.

But you didn't have time at the university. If instinct hadn't led you to spring yourself at Blackwell, it'd be you as the petrified 'bot under a mask.

And that just gives you more reason to loathe the golem. "How long will you be in residence," it asks you. It's tone is odiously polite, and it doesn't bother to hide its sneer.

"As long as it takes." You try to force yourself past, but the golem blocks you.

"Doing what?"

"Cataloging the library, doing research -- "

"The library is already cataloged." Suspicion deepens on the golem's brow. "I did it myself."

"Your boss has a whole new set of cross-references that have to be put in. He got the Libra back, and that's going to upend the entire organization. Plus there's research to be done, and you're not set up for that kind of work." You poke it in the chest. "I am."

You can see the snarl inside the golem's beard, but it steps aside with bad grace. "I do wish that my original -- " The word grinds inside its throat. "My original had had the foresight to put his wishes and information into the, ah, system. It would have only taken a few minutes with a mask." It glowers at you.

"He was out of town by that point. That's how come he had to send a letter."

"I wonder why not a text message."

"Those are easy to fake. Was it his handwriting on the letter? That tells you it was him."

"I can imitate his handwriting," says the golem. "And much else. It proves nothing." Its look becomes very shrewd.

You wonder if the thing has discerned how you could have played the trick by using a brain band of the professor. Blackwell himself did not hazard that experiment, but it's an obvious one, and you're sure the golem is capable of seeing the possibility, if it only thinks long enough about things. You still want the house for your base, but you before you do that you may need to find a way to tame the golem.

For now, though, you just shrug at the thing and continue down the hall.

But it's not done with you: "Has a similar set of instructions been left for Miss Vredenburg?" it calls at your back.

"I don't know. You'll have to ask her. Or maybe she didn't get them yet."

"And why would my original send my instructions through you, but give that girl her instructions directly?"

"I don't know," you say, losing patience. "Maybe your original likes her better than you. I know I would. Or maybe -- " You wheel on the golem. "Maybe, asshole, it's because I had to slap a fucking mask onto a golem in order to revive you. Lucy Vredenburg was already in circulation."

"Yes." That nasty smile reappears. "And where did that golem come from, hmm? Miss Lucy's mask is riding around on the only one that my original made. Whence the substantia upon which my present imago rides?"

That's the 'bot's fancy way of asking where the fuck Blackwell got another golem so he could put his doppelganger back to work.

"Beats the fuck out of me. I got a note saying I'd find one inside the mausoleum, and fuck me if I didn't find one there. I'm really starting to wish I hadn't."

"There would not have been time for my original to cast another." The 'bot speaks with a note of triumph. "My own memories are as recent as last Saturday night, and in that time -- "

"I said I don't know! Jesus! Maybe it's in the Libra. I'll let you know if it is, because Christ it sounds like you won't leave it alone until you find out!"

"The squeaky wheel gets the grease, or so the old adage has it," replies the golem.

* * * * *

It lets you alone after you get back into the library, and you're grateful to it this far: It's reminded you in a timely way that there's yet another fake running around town. Lucy Vredenburg -- that would be Cindy Vredenburg's older sister -- fell prey to Blackwell while he had the book, and he replaced her with a duplicate. And since he hadn't mastered the book as far as being able to put golem paste into a mask, he had had to eliminate the real girl using more old-fashioned methods. Methods that didn't leave much physical evidence behind. But that's another "assistant" that you'll have to neutralize.

You can write a note to her later. For the next while, you've got research you want to organize.

If it weren't for the copy of Blackwell's brain you're carrying around, you would have no idea where to start in his library. And even with his brain to guide you, you're not completely sure where to start.

Despite the "advantages" Blackwell had seized while he had the Libra Personae, the man had actually been more interested in it for its research value. The sigils --

And yes, Blackwell knows how to read and use and create sigils like those in the grimoire.

-- in the book are reputed to be masteries of their kind, constructed almost four hundred years ago by one of the greatest practitioners of the art. Blackwell wanted the book for the masks, and for the knowledge of how to make them, but more than that he wanted to study how the sigils themselves were constructed. You yourself feel the tug of his curiosity.

But it's not only of academic interest to you, as it is to Blackwell. You are desperate to know how that metal band that Prescott made managed to suck your soul out of your body, and how you might get yourself back into your body. To do that, you'll have to study the sigil that made that brain band.

And that's not all. Until you can get back into your own body, you're not only trapped inside Prescott's body, you're stuck using Kirkham's mask to go around in public, because you don't dare use that bot-making paste again. In fact, Blackwell's brain tells you it's incredible that Prescott didn't kill himself a long time ago while using it.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Even Black Magicians Can Have Management Problems

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You have solid reasons for boning up on the spell that makes the rockbot paste. David Kirkham's mask does not have any rockbot paste inside it, which means that the only person who can safely wear it is you. That leaves you stuck wearing it in public, which isn't bad, but it limits your ability to move around and manage your enterprises. Sure, the 'bot that Prescott made of you is smart and aggressive and knows what to do, but you wouldn't be Gary Chen if you didn't want to manage things directly.

So it's a problem that you can't make any more rockbot paste because of certain risks that Prescott didn't know about. You don't dare make any more until you understand those risks better.

But your talk with the Blackwell-bot has given you a more pressing goal: understanding the spell that pulled you out of your body and put you into Prescott's.

Because if you can figure out how that spell worked, then maybe you could show it to the golem and -- thanks to the brain band you're wearing -- maybe you could convince it that you are the real Aubrey Blackwell who got himself swapped into the body of this high school boy. The golem wouldn't be forced to obey you -- it has to obey the body of Aubrey Blackwell, not his soul -- but maybe it would be less of an asshole to you.

And showing it that spell would be perfectly safe. Golems can't perform magic. You know that now, thanks to Blackwell's memories. That fact, incidentally, explains why the Chenbot couldn't get the mask off Joshua Call back in the portables on Wednesday.

That's neither here nor there, though. You lean back against a table, study the bookshelves, and try to remember where certain books would be that contain certain necessary information ...

* * * * *

Sigils for Dummies. That, you wryly reflect, is the kind of reference book you could really use.

"There are sigils and there are sigils," Blackwell might explain to you in one of his pompous lectures. (You're going to be used to his presence inside your brain long before you're used to the fact that he's even a presence in your life.) "Any fool can make a magic symbol, and many fools do. But the real art and craft of sigil-making is something known only to the smallest number of cognoscenti, of whom, ahem, I may include myself as the most humble and ignorant of members.

"One may find sigils mentioned in the cheap paperback books on occult metaphysics that clog bookstore shelves. Perusing these books is like reading about tigers, a thing much different from hunting or touching them. In the crude fumbling of Aleister Crowley or Jack Parsons one may see attempts to recreate at its foundation an edifice long since erected by finer and more learned hands. In the, ah, putatively lost works of Hermes Trismegistus and Simon Magus the science was explained without technical examples, and certain alchemical works whose real meaning is obscure to the supposed experts in the field will actually employ sigils without explaining what they are.

"But that peculiar ability to craft a living sigil, and to understand wherein its life resides, and the evolutions that will yield higher specimens of the form -- these are secrets jealously guarded and put down only with many ellipses. Only George Abney was known to have attempted an encyclopedic accounting of the sigils and of their kinds, and that manuscript -- But bless me, why should I describe it when I have it here at hand?"

You pull out the old book -- written in a spidery hand on parchment and closed up between cheap boards of thick paper. "Yes, poor George Abney. At least his manuscript escaped even if he did not." You shudder slightly to remember the price Blackwell paid to get this book, one of the rarest treasures in the world. "And yet there are very few who would recognize its value or worth." You flip to the desired page in Abney's encyclopedia, and compare the figure there with the figure on your paper. "And yet I may do better than him, by putting his foundational work together with the Libra."

The latter certainly is a masterful work of art. Of course, you're looking at it with Blackwell's eyes when you say that. To you yourself, the sigils it contains just look like someone mixed Chinese and Hebrew characters in a rock tumbler and dumped them out onto the page. And yet, bit by bit you are beginning to discern the meanings of each sigil, and to see the new meanings they acquire when they are put next to each other, and the larger meanings created when they are fully assembled into a finished set.
