You remember that shitty hotel room where Reston was holed up, and the idea of sharing it -- and maybe even a bed -- with someone else with a sausage between his legs was just odious. You'd probably kill each other after a day or two. And though there'd be space and separation if you used Johansson and Prescott and disguises, that was a really unappealing suggestion. You don't want to see Prescott's face in the mirror every time you go to shave, and Johansson was dancing on your last nerve the last time you saw him.

So that left the Garners as the only good option. As sisters, they naturally will see each other at home after school, but they won't be hanging out with each other all the time.

Besides, you have some fond memories of playing Chelsea Cooper, and you think you could have some fun with the body of another cheerleader.

* * * * *

It's almost eight o'clock, and you have long since broken down and had that pizza delivered, before Joe returns. He doesn't look happy, and he flushes when you ask him if something is wrong.

"No, everything's all set up. Goddamned finally. You really fucked things up at school, you know that?"

"What do you mean?" you retort. "I got the business going great, I got -- "

"I don't mean the business, I mean all those fucking masks you had scattered all over the fucking -- " He draws a deep breath, and calms down. "I keep telling myself I have to remember who you think are, which team we're all playing for," he says after making an ugly face at you. "We all think we're Chen, and you can't help who you look like. Well, I guess you can, after we've changed." He has a backpack on his shoulder, and he slings it onto the floor. "You wanna change here, or up at the school?"

"Change?" you ask, puzzled.

Then it hits you. They must have already taken care of the Garner girls.

Automatically, almost, you draw back from the backpack. "Is that them in there?" you squeal. "Eva and Jessica?"

"Don't scream. Who else would it be?"

"Jesus! How'd you get them so fast?"

Joe rolls his eyes. "You think it's hard? All we had to do was get that mask of Javits off Johansson -- "

"You did what?"

"Shut. The fuck. Up. Or at least settle on one question and stick to it," he snaps, then crouches to unzip the backpack. "Which one do you want? Jessica or Eva? They're interchangeable."

"Jessica," you blurt out. "She's, I dunno, more pert," you explain when he cocks an eyebrow at you. You mime grabbing some breasts.

"I don't remember studying her tits that closely, but I won't argue." He mutters something else. You don't ask him to repeat it, because you think you catch the word Prescott in there.

"So we called up Johansson," he continues as he spills masks and clothes all over the floor. "He was a fucking prick about it, too, but he met us over at that fucking basement. Frank grabbed him and I pulled the mask off him, and then we used those ropes and that bag to quiet him down. It was after that we called you, got that report so we'd know what we needed to handle." He glances inside a mask, sets it aside, then tosses you the other one. "So, Frank's gonna be taking over the shit you set up -- " he continues.

"You mentioned that."

"No, I mean all those other masks you scattered around. Johansson and Javits and Chelsea -- Mm." He grabs his crotch. "The others. We're using that golem paste to keep 'em all in line."

"You mean you're going to turn all of them into golems?"

"Sure, it's the only way to straighten things out safely. Gonna make 'em into slaves for Frank's. We already put his golem paste inside that mask of Javits that Johansson was wearing. We also made a new mask of Johansson, put Frank's paste inside it, and got it back in circulation by slapping it on the real fuckface. So -- " He ticks off two fingers. "Javits and Johansson are both slaves now. Then I used Javits's voice to get Eva and Jessica to come out, and we got masks of them and turned them into golems too. The kind like you did granddad, those statue things. We hid 'em behind all that junk in the basement. I don't think we'll need them, but they'll be useful to have around."

"What are you doing about Chelsea?"

"Well, her and Matthias and Black were the tricky ones to deal with. We made up some new masks and bands -- which took fucking long enough, we shoulda brought you out to help but Frank doesn't want to look at you -- then went around and got each of them one at a time. But what we did was, we copied the real people into the masks and bands, but then we switched them around, so as to keep the goofy stuff more or less the way it is now. I dunno, we might undo it later, but for now we're keeping things as close to the way they look as possible. So, like, we copied Chelsea brain but put it into Matthias's mask, then coated it with Frank's paste and put it on Matthias. So, he's under his own mask, and he's a 'bot that Frank controls, but he's got Chelsea's brain and is going to keep doing the kind of shit she was doing under his mask. Except we can boss him around if we have to. Did the same thing with Black's mask -- put it on Black, but with Matthias's brain band, and put Gordon's brain band in that mask of Prescott." Joe snorts. "Speaking of which, you wanna change now? So we don't have to keep looking at you like that?"

"What did you do with, uh, Chen?"

"The real guy? Same thing. Made a new mask of Chelsea, and a second brain band of the real Chelsea while we had her, slathered Frank's paste in it -- " He grimaces. "And he'll be able to jam himself up her anytime he wants 'cos she's his slave. And then we put it onto Chen.

"So, those are the details," he concludes. "If you can't keep 'em straight, just understand that Frank's got a golem gang of his own now." He ticks off his fingers: "Prescott, Black, Matthias, Chelsea, Johansson, and Javits. We can use Javits and Chelsea, I guess. The others are pretty useless, unless Frank puts himself into one of them and runs it himself."

"Are we gonna make any more golems?"

"Oh yeah." Joe picks through the clothes, separating them into two piles, and pushes one pile toward you. "We're supposed to get a mask onto Marc when we get back to the Garners', so Frank has a place to hang out if he needs to conference with us. Anybody else, it'll be a case of whether they're useful or not."

"I've been trying to get Kelsey Blankenship. She's got money, and she's a big buyer for her weekly ganja parties."

Joe mulls this. "That's a good idea. Moneywise, the real score woulda been back at Eastman, but he's too obvious and we wanna stay out of that school, 'cept for Karter. But we'll take Kelsey's parents, too, so we get total control of the house and money."

"You know, Kelsey's throwing her weekly weed party tonight, and the Garners should be at it. If you got another mask -- "

"Christ, man, I wore myself out making all the other ones, just to clean up your fucking mess!"

* * * * *

But you've got to change anyway. Joe stays in the living room, while you take Jessica's things into a musty bathroom. The t-shirt and shorts you're wearing will be big enough to hold Jessica's smaller frame, so you don't disrobe before laying in the bathtub and putting her mask on. A little thrill goes through you just before settling the mask in place.

You can't tell right-side up from upside-down when you wake, and grab onto something cold and hard as your head spins. You sit up sharply. You're in a bathtub. The fuck?

Twin streams of memories come rushing back. One of going into the bathroom with a mask of Jessica Garner after talking with Joe, and that after spoiling a good Saturday in a strange house, and that after being hijacked by two magic users whose personalities are now dominated by yours -- that of Gary Chen. The other stream brings memories of having a sack shoved over your head after driving out with your sister Eva to meet Seth at some dumpy old elementary school because he wanted to talk about Cindy, and that after an afternoon of shopping where you picked up the clothes that you were wearing, and which are now -- oh God -- scattered on the filthy floor of something that smells like a gas station restroom. With finicky care you crawl out of the tub.

From outside, somewhere, you hear a feminine voice barking something that sounds like a curse word. You open the bathroom door and go hunting.

Eva, wearing a guy's clothes, is standing in the living room. She's glaring at a cell phone. "It's Kelsey," she says, "wanting to know if we're coming or not. I vote 'no'," she continues, "unless you think we can fake our way through."

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
It's a really dangerous idea you're playing with -- slapping the anima band onto the kid who took you prisoner. The band contains a copy of Gary Chen's anima, and if you put it on the kid then he'll be convinced that he's Gary Chen, the way you're convinced of it. Can the world stand to contain two guys who think they're Chen? You don't even want to let the real Gary Chen out, even though you and he would have the same thoughts and feelings.

"I brought you some water," the kid announces as he comes back in. You let the anima band slip from nerveless fingers, and it drops to the floor. "And this box of cookies was still sealed up. Made by Keebler elves, but if they're bad for you its for solid nutritional reasons. Oh hey, what's this?"

He bends over to pick the metal band up from off the floor. "Chen Guangli," he says, and it shocks you that he can read it and pronounce the name perfectly. It surprises you almost as much as the fact that he even spotted the band on the floor, for you were sitting between him and it. You blink as he fixes you with a sharp glance and drops lazily into the chair on the other side of the desk. He leans back and crosses his legs and steeples his fingers as he looks down his nose at you. "Chen Guangli," he repeats. "What did you say your name was? I mean, what were the names you think are yours?"

You lick your lips and look around, calculating whether you can get out the door and through the yard and over the wall and down the road before this guy can catch you. The way he's sitting, you're pretty sure it'd take him a couple of seconds to get onto his feet.

But when you look back at him, there's no missing the gleam in his eye. It's like he's reading your mind, and daring you to try to make a break for it. "Look man," you say, trying though it kills you to go for the pathetic angle. "I don't know what's going on -- "

"I know, I know, it's all a muddle, that's what you told me outside. Now tell me those names again. There was a 'Chen' in there, right?"

"Yeah. Gary Chen. But I'm not Chinese. Obviously."

"It says 'Chen' here," the kid says, showing you the band. "Can you at least read Chinese?" You shake your head. "Well, it's the name Chen Guangli, which is pretty close to 'Gary Chen'. We'll figure out what it is later." He tucks the band into his shirt pocket. "Now what about this?" He shows you the grimoire. "Know anything about this?"

"I don't know anything about -- "

"Let's keep this simple, whatever your name is. Just answer the questions simply and directly and truthfully. You don't have to try putting the pieces together, I'll do that for you. Have you seen this before?"

You decide to fudge it. "Maybe, I don't know. There's a lot of books in here."

"Do you work for Aubrey Blackwell?"

You'll have to improvise. "Sure. He hired me to do some library work for him."

"When?"

"A couple of days ago."

"How do you know Blackwell?"

"I don't, not really. I heard that he was looking for somebody to do this cataloging job."

"Who told you about the job?"

"Um, a girl at school. Cindy Vredenburg. Her sister -- "

"Who did she tell all this to? About the job?"

"I don't know who else she told."

He smiles thinly. "I mean, she told you, right?" You nod. "What was the name of the person she told?"

Your throat freezes, and a chill goes down your spine when the kid smiles. You're trapped. You have said that you don't know who you are. So how can you so confidently say that Cindy Vredenburg told "you" about a job that "you" took with Aubrey Blackwell?

"You need some practice, if that one stumped you," says the kid. "Try a tongue twister, see if that gets you limbered up. Round and round the rugged rocks the ragged rascal ran. Say it," he insists after you frown. "Repeat after me. Round and round the rugged rocks -- "

"I know how it goes, I'm not gonna -- "

"It'll help, believe me," he says gently with a grin. "It'll help us figure out one thing, at least. So say what I say. Round and round the rugged rocks -- "

You loose an exasperated sigh, but give in, and follow him through the tongue twister like it's a round of "Merrily We Roll Along" or "Frere Jacques." "Well that settles one thing," says the kid. "You're not Chen Guangli, or you could never have said it so good."

"Why's that?"

He sniggers. "Lound and lound the lugged locks the lagged lascal lan," he lisps. A short, hot flame goes off inside you. "I'm joking, of course," the kid continues. "But let's see what that opens up. What's your name?"

"I don't -- "

And your head jerks to the side as though you've been punched in the face. Your jaw twists and your tongue swells. "Will Prescott Gary Chen" are the words that squirt out of your mouth.

The kid's eyebrows shoot up. "Say that again."

"Will Prescott," you say with great deliberation, but your mouth refuses to clamp shut afterward, and "Gary Chen" comes out immediately afterward. And even then you can't stop, and say "Will Prescott" and follow it up with "Gary Chen." Your head jerks back and forth, and you have the terrifying thought that you've been possessed. A spasm runs up your back, and your breath becomes very short.

The kid leans forward, an alarmed look on his face, and he snaps his fingers. Your mouth, thank God!, relaxes. The kid stares at you gravely. "You really don't know who you are," he says softly.

"The fuck is -- "

"Shut up," he says softly. He holds up the grimoire. "Have you ever seen this book before?"

"Yes," you say instantly.

"Where?"

"Here. At school. At my house. At the used bookstore." It's not like it was a moment ago, with different words fighting to control your mouth. But these are true words, and they leap unbidden from your brain to your tongue and into the air.

"When did you first see it?"

Your head jerks back and forth. "At the used book store. At David Kirkham's house. At the bookstore. At Kirkham's. At -- "

Again, a snap of the kid's fingers breaks the spasm. He gets up and sits on the table, his eyes boring into you. "You've got a real problem with the truth," he says, and he says it like he's lodging a very serious judgment against you. "You won't tell it voluntarily, and you've got too many truths inside you for me to compel them from you."

You're scared and you're angry and you're sick of this guy's shit. You've recovered from the session in the back of his truck, too, and he's by himself and though he looks like he's got some big muscles, you're pretty sure you can make him hurt. "And who the fuck are you that you get to bust in here and even ask me shit?" you demand.

"Who am I?" He hops to the floor and takes a few steps back while holding your gaze. "Who am I?" You only notice now -- as he turns to open it -- a backpack in the floor near the doorway to the library. From it he takes a packet of cigarettes. It's new, and he tears the cellophane off and drops it casually to the floor. He pushes the lid back and thumps the bottom and pulls a cigarette out with his teeth. He drops the pack on the table. "Who am I?" He shoves his hands in his pockets and sucks on the cigarette.

Glowing ash forms at its tip and smoke wafts out. Behind that curl of smoke, the kid's eyes glow. "I'm the burning light of truth, baby. That's who I am."

* * * * *

It's a good parlor trick, you'll give it that much, and it robs you of your voice for a few seconds, during which the front door to the house slams back and slams shut. An instant later a tall, dark-haired kid looks into the library. "I got you coffee and an Egg McMuffin," he says, and remains in the doorway, looking very watchful. "I got another McMuffin too," he adds when the blonde kid says nothing, but continues presenting his back to the doorway. "For me or for the other guy, if you think -- "

"I think he needs to go on ice," says the blonde kid, his eyes locked on you.

"What are you smoking, Joe? Unless this kid is -- "

"I think he is. At least, he's not cooperative and I can't get him to be."

"You try a tongue twister on him?"

"I know my business, Frank." Now he turns around. "I'll get the bag. You tie him up again."

The one called Frank shrugs and comes into the library as the one called Joe exits. You stand up. "Listen, asshole, no one's -- "

"Shut up," says Frank, sounding tired. You'd argue back, but something that feels like a giant invisible claw clamps around your torso, squeezing your arms to your sides. Frank doesn't even hurry as he wraps those silver ropes around you. And then your arms are numb again.

"I hope no one misses you, kid," says Joe when he returns. "But according to you, there's already two kids answering to your name out there, so I suppose you can disappear for a few days." He's holding that silver cloth bag, and though you jerk your head back, he slips it easily over your head, and all thought instantly ceases.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Burning Light of Truth

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You need to figure out how Prescott got your soul out of your body and into his, but your gut -- seconded by Blackwell's brain -- tells you that that's going to be a very hard problem, one that will take a lot of time and intense research to puzzle out. So right now, for personal and business reasons, you're going to concentrate on a different problem.

How the hell did that dipshit Will Prescott manage to make so many golems?

That's what the rockbot paste actually is. It's the same stuff that makes a rockbot, only it goes inside a mask. That's a really smooth and ingenious technique, and Blackwell's persona marvels at the elegance of the solution. Instead of making a rockbot and a mask and putting one over the other, and having to find a way to get the original person out of the way so the doppelganger can take over, the golem-in-a-mask trick creates an obedient copy of the person to be replaced and at the same time hides the original. Prescott has been pulling that trick all over the place. He has an actual golem that is impersonating your grandfather; and thanks to the masks he also has golems of yourself (Gary Chen), Adam Karter, Joe Thomason, George Mendoza, Tanner Evans, Joshusa Call, Jeff Spencer, and Jamie Rennerhoff. That's nine.

But golems obey a master because they've got a little bit of their master inside them. That's what the hair is for. (Though it doesn't have to be hair. It could be a flap of skin or some drops of blood.) The hair carries a person's essentia -- the stuff that makes the person into that person and not some other person -- and when it is used in a spell like the one that makes a golem of any kind, part of the person's essentia passes into the golem. The golem becomes a part of the person, filled with the person's will, and so it obeys the will of the person who contributed the essentia.

But people only have so much essentia to spread around. If you spread too much into too many golems, the result can be a catastrophe, the magical equivalent of getting squashed and spread out by a steam roller.

And from everything that Blackwell has read, most people can only divide themselves five ways before there will be such a failure. Some very hardy types can split themselves six ways. Seven ways might be possible, but it is as a practical matter unheard of.

Counting his own body -- the one you are now inhabiting -- Prescott split himself ten ways. That should be beyond the realm of any possibility. If you make any more using Prescott's essentia, well, you're inside his body, and that means if you make one to many golems, you'd be history.

Or so you assume.

Even if that's a false assumption, even if Prescott could survive an eleventh division, or you could survive the failure of his essentia, you are not going to hazard the experiment to find out.

Not until you figure out what the hell is going on with Prescott's essentia.

* * * * *

Your replacement has long since come and gone, and deposited the rest of Prescott's supplies in the mausoleum, and the hands of the clock are nearing nine when you lay aside the seventh book that you've consulted without result. The problem is that although the authorities have a lot to say about what essentia is used for, and the dangers of handling it, and though they have a lot of speculations about where it comes from and how it develops, they have nothing to say about oddballs like Prescott, whose essentia is so remarkably durable. With a grimace you glance over the shelves, looking for any other books that might help, but you're now too tired to care. "Fuck it," you mutter, and trudge outside to the mausoleum to dig into Prescott's supplies. You need to get a mask to Johansson in the morning.

You've passed around the corner of the house when you hear the noise above: a sound like pebbles rattling over slate. You freeze, and a chill runs all over you.

You'd almost forgotten about the gwarcheidwad.

It's Blackwell's watchdog, though he had nothing to do with it. It came with the house. It's a guardian entity -- Christians would call it a "demon", pagans would call it a "spirit." But it is what it is, and nothing else. It is a thing that is hungry, and will eat anything that falls into its grasp. Between the hours of midnight and dawn it walks at liberty over the grounds and inside the walls of Blackwell's villa; and during the day, when its reliquary is not in its niche, it can prowl inside the house, assaulting intruders but not able to abscond with them. Its power is waxing now, and though it cannot actually harm you at the moment, you've no desire to run into it. Blackwell has had many a bad fright when he's run into the thing after dark.

So you step back around the way you came, and when you're inside you wipe a little perspiration from your forehead and try to still the beating of your heart. You're not ashamed to have retreated, either. You're not a coward, but you're not reckless.

Besides, you now remember, Blackwell has some unused masks of his own, relics from his earlier ownership of the grimoire, which he kept down in the hidden basement with the old golem. But there will be time enough in the morning to fetch one up. Right now you repair your shaken nerves with some food from the kitchen. Low sounds are coming from the living room, and when you look inside, the Blackwell-bot looks up from the TV to give you a hooded glance. You don't want its company, and go upstairs to look over the bedrooms.

Of course, you know what you will find. Blackwell's own bedroom is the only one that is decently furnished, but the others, though dusty and unaired, have beds with sheets on them. You check the door frames to insure that the protective spells that keep the gwarcheidwad at bay are still potent. You end up choosing the bedroom in which Blackwell had kept the golem and the masks. It seems like a fitting choice for you.

Back down in the library, you amuse yourself by writing a letter in Blackwell's voice to the Lucy Vredenburg golem, one that will explain your presence at the house and direct it to give you help. You beguile yourself with fantasies of getting it on with Lucy. She was a senior a few years ago, and was even more devastatingly beautiful than Cindy. Like her little sister, she had sheets of platinum blonde hair cascading down the sides of her head to frame a face with the complexion of cream. She was a cheerleader like Cindy, too, and had the legs and the butt and breasts for it, as well the amazing acrobatic moves. Your cock swells as you think of her, and of her availability now.

Except she won't be available. Like the Blackwell-bot, she must obey Blackwell's body. And as that has been transformed into a golem, she has no one to obey but herself. There is no way she would give Will Prescott -- or even Gary Chen or David Kirkham -- any attention or love.

You could only get inside her by getting literally inside her -- inside her mask, the one that Blackwell made. It has no rockbot paste inside it, so you could put it on and explore her. Blackwell explored her plenty, and every weekend liked to go out and party as a popular and arrogant college cheerleader. Pervy old cocksucker, and you mean the epithet literally!

* * * * *

As midnight closes in, you put things away. But you take Kirkham's backpack, the note to Lucy, the Libra, and the most encyclopedic of the books on essentia upstairs with you. You prop up in bed with the latter and, safe behind the magically locked door, ignore the sounds of heavy, clawed footsteps in the hall after the downstairs clocks have struck twelve. You're just beginning to grow sleepy and are thinking of putting the lights out when you bolt upright at a passage in the book. You have the feeling that someone has just placed a long-lost key into your hand.

You can't see what it is, though. The passage is just talking about the different "flavors" of essentia, for it can be "charged" in various ways. Not until you've scanned it half a dozen times, and felt gingerly around that opaque idea, do you center on the word "alchemical", on the fact that essentia can be "alchemically" charged.

Alchemy, of course, is related to the metals -- the transmutation of such -- and a short and serendipitous chain of free associations takes you from "metals" to the "stars" that are associated with certain metals.

If Blackwell's mentalia had hands, the knuckles would now be whitening, for "stars" reminds him of the Stellae Errantes, a band of occult practitioners that are the bane of black magicians such as himself. And what does Will Prescott have to do with the Stellae Errantes?

Only this, maybe: The Stellae, it is rumored, are born with alchemically charge essentia from which they derive certain occult prodigies that link them to the planets of the solar system: the Sun, Mercury, Venus, the Moon, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune, and Pluto. Ten planets. Prescott was able to split his essentia at least ten ways.

Coincidence? You put out the light and lay awake in the dark with Blackwell's suspicions a very long time.

* * * * *

You are awoken very early by a loud crash. Early morning light shows in the window as you sit up and listen with a beating heart. Is it the gwarcheidwad?

Footsteps sound outside, and a hoarse yell. A door across the hall is kicked in, and Blackwell's sudden shout is muffled.

It sounds like a police raid.

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Before you can answer Blackwell's question, Chen nudges you in the side and mutters in your ear. "Your friend Johansson wants to know when he's gonna get that thing you promised him."

The mask to use on Patterson. "God damn it. I should fucking shove him up -- " You stifle yourself as the Blackwell-bot raises an eyebrow. "Yeah, I'll take of that. I'll be back tomorrow morning to get started," you tell the Blackwell-bot. It has the grace not to look pleased, and only purses its lips. "Until then, just hold down the fort."

"And Miss Lucy?" the thing asks with icy, impudent politeness.

It comes back to you, from Blackwell's brain band. "Miss Lucy" is Lucy Vredenburg, and the sister of your classmate Cindy Vredenburg, she who "Seth Javits" dumped in order to take up with Chelsea Cooper. It suddenly feels like a tight, constricting ring of coincidences: When he still had the Libra, Blackwell replaced Lucy -- a student in one of his classes -- with a Lucy-bot.

"I don't know," you tell the golem. "Maybe the professor sent her a letter too. Maybe not."

"Perhaps I should call her and inquire."

"Do that," you say. "Though if she hasn't got a letter from him, maybe she just didn't get it yet."

"Mm. I suppose that's a possibility." The golem's voice is soft, and you don't like the expression of sly suspicion it wears. You can only helpless retreat under his gaze. From the house you retrieve the few items you'd left behind that you'll need, and chase Chen back out.

* * * * *

It's lonely work down in the elementary school basement after Chen has gone to work, but it's preferable to keeping company with that creepy Blackwell golem at that creepy house. You'd have second thoughts about turning it into your base if your mind didn't keep reverting back to the library there. And to the safety and privacy that would go with working there. You can't shake the feeling that someone is going to discover the school basement eventually, and that you're going to get in serious trouble for breaking and entering and trespassing. But you can make the move tomorrow.

As mentioned, it's lonely work down in the basement. Or it would be if you didn't have so many personalities inside your skull with you as you make and polish a new mask, and follow it with a brain band. Prescott's personality is mostly quiet, because you kick hard at his instincts and his memories when the crop up, but after you remove the Blackwell brain band he comes more to the fore. He didn't ever worry about being found in the basement, but now that you've thought of the possibility he won't stop jumping nervously at every noise. To quiet those nerves you slip Kirkham's mask back on, and then you're back to three voices babbling in your head. Finally you give your own brain a rest, let Kirkham's instincts come to the fore, and he makes quick, vicious work of ghost-Prescott.

It's a good set up and gets ghost-Kirkham's approval, and you think some more about maybe letting the real guy out. He's your best friend, and he's smart. The only trouble -- the trouble you keep coming back around to -- is that you're stuck in Prescott's body, and once you show him the brain bands he'll have no reason to believe you're really you. He'll think you're Prescott pretending to be Gary Chen.

Tell him to try taking a brain band off you, one of the other voices inside you says. That'll prove you're really inside Prescott's body, that it's not a cheat.

That excites you briefly, but then another voice replies: He'll just think it's some new trick Prescott found that keeps brain bands from being taken off.

So show him the soul-swapping spell.

He can't read it. Hell, even Blackwell can't read it without that library.

So show him how it works. Pull his soul out of his body --

A deep shiver starts at the base of your spine and ripples out, like a stone thrown into water. If you pulled Kirkham's soul out of his body and put it into another body, then he'd see that you might actually be on the level.

But he'd be pissed at you for doing that.

Hey, it was your idea! You don't know who you're shouting at, but you shout it anyway.

Pull Kirkham's soul out of his body, put it inside --

You grin. Put it inside Johansson. Then he'd have control of Johansson's rockbots. Turn the Prescott-Johansson partnership into a Chen-Kirkham partnership.

It's still a very tricky idea, and you're not totally sold on it. But you've the entire evening to work. It's late when you finish up the mask for Johansson, but still well before midnight. You text Chen, telling him to stop by the basement when he gets off work, and start making up another one of those goofy bands that Prescott made.

He's come and he's gone by one o'clock. And in that time you took the mask off your best friend, and put his soul inside the new band.

* * * * *

You wake up around nine o'clock the next morning and text Johansson to set up a meeting, but he's not up yet or something, so to kill time you drive out to the Blackwell villa. Until you hear from Johansson -- and until you decide what to do about with that soul-swapping band you made last night -- you can look through Blackwell's library for books to help you out.

You've two immediate goals. The first is to figure out how the soul-swapping spell works, and how to reverse it. The other is to figure out how it is that Prescott was able to make up so many golems.

You want to get out of Prescott's body and back into your own, as that's the only way you can get amputate his personality. You thought you saw a way to do it last night, after you went to bed: When Prescott put that soul-band onto his forehead, it never came out. So maybe it's still there, and if you ripped that band off your forehead, and put it back onto the forehead of your real body, it would reverse things. But you'd need somebody's help to do that, and you can't think of anyone you trust.

Also, you have to figure out why Prescott could make so many golems, because Blackwell's memories told you that that should have been an impossibility. When making a golem -- and that's what the Thomason-bot and the Call-bot and the Karter-bot and Chen-bot all are -- the golem maker puts a little of himself into them. But a human being only has so much of himself to spread around, and Blackwell's best knowledge was that people can only split themselves into six or at most seven pieces. Prescott split himself into ten. He should have died the night he was making the Call-bot, the Spencer-bot, and the Rennerhoff-bot.

If you make another golem using Prescott's hair, there's an awfully good chance he'll die, and since you're in his body, you would die too. Even now you feel like you're living on borrowed time.

Yes, it's all very confusing, and that's why you need Blackwell's library. To help you figure out the incredible mess that Will Prescott, Harry Potter's retarded cousin, has gotten you in.

So occupied, you arrive at Blackwell's almost before you know it. And you're startled to see Prescott's white pickup truck parked out in front of it.

* * * * *

You circle around the house and park near that culvert where you broke in yesterday, and sit behind the wheel of David Kirkham's car -- and inside David Kirkham's body -- and drum your fingers thoughtfully. The sight of Prescott's truck at the professor's is a horrible surprise, and you shudder inside and out.

It makes no sense that he would be there.

You are inside Will Prescott's body, and Will Prescott's body hasn't been near that truck in almost a month. For about that long Gordon Black has been trapped under the mask of Will Prescott, fumbling his way through that asshat's life. And Gordon Black knows absolutely nothing about the Libra and what has happened to him.

So how did Black get to know about this place, and to know about it now? You don't have the professor's brain band on you or with you, but you don't remember anything from it that suggests that Aubrey Blackwell, professor of anthropology, knows anything about Will Prescott, and would have called him out.

Then it comes to you: The golem. It must be the Blackwell-bot that you left behind. It started digging around and, God only knows how, it got onto Prescott's trail!

You lick your lips. The Blackwell-bot isn't under your control, so you can't order it to desist. Only the body of David Kirkham could do that. If you revived him in another form --

But it might be too late for that, if Gordon Black has learned everything that's been going on.

Instead of retreating, maybe you should walk into the tiger's den. It's still early. Maybe Black just got here, and there's time to head things off.

Or maybe you should just get Kirkham's soul into Johansson pronto, so that you have an ally in case the worst is already coming to pass.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Dilemmas of a Soul Survivor

choices: He just vanished, and that's the end of it
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Whether or not you make Blackwell's villa your base, you have the professor himself to take care of. There's no rush, but you can't help taking the stairs three at a bound on your way down to the foyer. The front door is open, and you can't help snickering at the sight of the professor sprawled outside on the stoop. It's almost like a running gag: Every time he gets close to you, you knock him cold.

You avoid touching the booby-trapped door handle as you step over the unconscious man and run around to the side of the house where the old mausoleum is. The door resists being opened, but you shove it back and let fresh air into the dim and musty interior. There's an empty stone bier inside, just waiting for Blackwell, but you'll need help moving him onto it, so you send a text to your 'bot, telling him where to find you. Then from the kitchen pantry you get a less concentrated aerosol of the same knock-out ointment and spray it over Blackwell's face. That will keep him out for hours.

Inside the library --

And though Blackwell's memories have prepared you for it, you still pause just inside the threshold. It's a very large and impressive space, and is the main reason for moving your operations here. The ceiling floats two stories above, and from the floor to the ceiling on three sides it is covered in bookshelves: a wheeled ladder on a railing is necessary to get the books that are high up. Three desks occupy the wide space in the center of the room, and tall windows and a French door in the fourth wall let in the afternoon sunlight.

But despite the high ceiling and clear glass, an air of gloom and oppression settles on the room. Some if it is due to the discordant ticking of two grandfather clocks in one corner. Then too there are the alcoves from inside which stare balefully the stuffed corpses of impossible creatures. One of these -- a small orangutan-like monkey with a single, Cyclops-like eye in its forehead and razor-like teeth in its grinning mouth -- is out of its alcove and sitting on a table. You put it back in its spot while muttering a spell that Aubrey Blackwell knows but that Gary Chen doesn't. With that, those uncanny noises should cease to disturb you.

From another alcove you take down a stuffed two-headed dog and wedge it under your armpit. Then you twist a candlestick stuck incongruously in one of the walls. A door disguised as a bookcase unlatches with a click and swings open. Ah, the classics!

Inside is a narrow, hidden basement lit from a single window. A metal spiral staircase leads down to a concrete floor. This hidden dungeon has held a number of prisoners over the years -- you grimace to think of the last prisoner, and her ghastly fate -- but now only holds the supplies that Blackwell had accumulated when he still had the Libra. These include most of the ingredients you will need for finishing off the professor; the one ingredient you lack can be got next to one of the tombstones next to the mausoleum.

Then, once you've got the stuff set out in the library, you sit down to write a letter. With Blackwell's brain band you can, after a little warm up practice, imitate his handwriting perfectly, and on some of his stationary you compose the following:

My dear Lucy,

This letter will find you after I have already departed. I have not time now to explain where I have gone nor to issue instructions in person. Not that I have any instructions to give you, for your orders are merely to carry on as you have been.

You pause, wresting with whether to say more. The "Lucy" you are addressing is Lucy Vredenburg, older sister of your classmate Cindy Vredenburg, the cheerleader whose heart Seth Javits broke when he exchanged her for Chelsea Cooper a few weeks ago. Like her younger sister, Lucy is a cheerleader, but at Keyserling College, which she attends, and like Cindy she is a bosomy, platinum blonde with sensational legs and an arrogant attitude.

But it's not Lucy Vredenburg you are addressing, not really. It's only a magical 'bot -- "golem" would be the word Blackwell would use -- that the professor made when he had the Libra. Lucy was the unlucky student that he chose to experiment on, and after murdering the real girl he replaced her with a Lucy-bot that's under his control.

After struggling with temptation, you decide to split the difference between not telling the Lucy-bot of your existence and full-on encouraging her to spread her legs for you. You continue:

This letter comes to you from the hand of a young man I have recently engaged. He has my complete trust, and you may trust those whom he vouches for. You are to give him every aid that he asks for.

You write Blackwell's name at the bottom with a flourish, tuck it in an envelope, and seal it with wax.

You go to put Kirkham's mask back on, but stop just in time. Blackwell's ghost reminds you that you are already wearing one brain band, and that it is bad idea to introduce another -- as is contained in Kirkham's mask -- above it.

You cuss. That means that you're going to have to show Prescott's fucking face to the world when you are using Blackwell's memories.

* * * * *

It's another hour before Gary Chen's Jeep slides to a hard stop next to Blackwell's car. He's at the gate before you're out the door. "Christ, what the hell is this place," he exclaims, then does a double take at you. He grins. "What the fuck happened to your face?"

"Fuck you, that's what happened. I need your help moving this asshole over there." You point first to Blackwell, then around the corner of the house.

"What happened?"

"Go fuck yourself, man, that's what happened. Help me move him."

He blanches, but complies, grasping Blackwell by his wrists while you take the feet. Your 'bot, despite his strength, can't get Blackwell off the ground, and the professor's fat ass drags across the dirt across as you haul him to the mausoleum.

Three bodies collide at the door to the crypt before you squeeze inside. Chen tries to flatly refuse moving Blackwell onto the bier, but he can't argue with his master, and together you manage to roll the whale-like prof up and onto the stone slab. "What now?" Chen asks. "Lock him in?"

"Nope, I'll be right back." The struggle to move Blackwell has winded Prescott's shit body, but snapping and twanging nerves give you the energy to run back inside for the ingredients. In the mausoleum you sprinkle Blackwell with dirt and incendiary chemicals. You don't bother with hair, for you are permanently disposing of the man. You use your 'bot's lighter to set him ablaze, and a minute later Blackwell is a stone golem -- the same kind that Prescott made of your grandfather. With some scissors you snagged in the kitchen, you cut the clothes off him so that he'll just look like a hyper-detailed statue of the man. Then you close the door of the mausoleum on him.

Chen's eyes are wide when you're done. "So you gonna explain what all that was about, or is it a secret?"

"I'll tell you about it when you get back from your errands."

"What errands?" His eyes narrow suspiciously. "You know I got work tonight."

"You also got time to swing by the Panda Garden and pick us up some food. Also, the box store. We need -- " You go through the pockets of the professor's shredded pants to find his wallet. "As many book boxes as you can buy with this," you say, thrusting a fistful of cash at Chen.

He whistles. "This'll buy a lot of boxes."

"Start with fifty. We'll get more tomorrow if we have to."

"Fifty? What are we gonna do with -- ?"

"Just get the fuck going." You clap him on the shoulder and propel him back toward the Jeep.

* * * * *

But where are you going to keep all the books and supplies that you're going to take from Blackwell's house? It would seem simplest to keep the house and use it as your new headquarters. But that would require the professor's presence. He made a mask of himself, and you could set the mask onto a golem, but it would not be under your control, and you don't want to have to fight it or negotiate with it. So you're going to raid the house for its value and leave it abandoned.

You start by pulling out all of the late professor's magical supplies. Some of these are in that basement -- and it includes a blank mask, ready for use -- and some tools from a workshop in a third-floor cupola on the back of the villa. These you pack in some spare boxes and set by the front door.

Then you turn to the major task: sorting through the library.

Even with Blackwell's memories, it's a prodigious task, and you are loathe to leave any of the volumes behind. But there is simply no place to keep so many books, except by renting a storeroom someplace. So you are going to have to make do with Kirkham's closet for the most important books, his garage for the slightly less important books, and the elementary school basement for the rest.

You start by pulling out the books that Blackwell consulted most often when working with the Libra.

And as you work you wrestle with another problem: How to handle the disappearance of Professor Blackwell. The simplest thing would be to let him vanish: Today he was at the university; tomorrow no one heard from him ever again.

But he has money, and he made a mask of himself, which you could wear. Maybe he should abruptly resign from the university, and you could use his form when you need access to his bank account and credit cards.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Piracy on a Magical Scale

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You're still not decided about what to do with the late Professor Blackwell, but it's worth keeping some of his stuff around as a back up. You break from sorting through the books long enough to run upstairs to his bedroom.

The professor lived alone -- Morticia Addams being unavailable, no one else would have him -- and he only half maintained the house. The downstairs is reasonably well appointed, with a plush living room, a fine dining room, and a well-stocked kitchen, for the professor enjoyed his food. There was the library, of course. But upstairs there's only one bedroom with any real furniture. There's a king-sized bed, a large wardrobe, a dressing table and a desk, and several chairs. The whole thing is also done up in crimsons and brocades, like a Westerner's idea of a Chinese brothel.

There's no need to pack much: one suit of clothes, shoes and socks and underwear. From the stone corpse you have his watch and wallet, and from the bedroom desk you extract a check book and some extra credit cards. The information that will get you into his financial accounts you copy it down in one of the man's own notebooks.

You're still at this when you hear a noise downstairs. "Hey!" the Chenbot calls. "I need some -- "

Then there's a screech, a shouted "Jesus!" and the sound of something hitting a hard floor.

You grab up the gathered material and race downstairs.

There's a mess on the floor just outside the living room: dropped cartons of Chinese food. You whirl around, calling your own name out. An answering shout comes from outside.

Pallor is showing under the bronzed cheeks of Gary Chen when you find him on the patio. "Christ, what's going on in there?" he gasps, and a shiver goes through him.

"Man up, there's nothing -- "

"Fuck you, there was a thing in that library, it popped out at me when -- "

"A thing? What kind of a thing?" His obvious fear sobers you up.

"I don't know." He shudders. "But I saw all these teeth and some wings and -- "

"Wings?" you say, and spin around to frown at the house. "I thought I put it away."

"Put what away?"

"The thing that you saw."

His eyes bulge. "The fuck are you talking about? You know all about it?"

"Sure, come on."

"Uh uh. I'm staying out here. Besides, I hafta get to work."

"Are you disobeying me?"

He grimaces, hard. "No," he says in a very tight voice, and he takes a few steps toward you, as though being pulled along on the end of a rope. "I just really don't want to -- "

"I'm sure it's okay. It can't hurt you, not in the daytime. It can only scare you."

"It's pretty fucking good at its job." But he follows you inside.

The gwarcheidwad is in its alcove, where you'd put it. It can't ever be completely leashed, Blackwell's memories tell you, and even when put it away it is capable of making some startling lunges inside the library. But Blackwell has no memory of it ever being really intense. But a little later on, Chen mentions some of the costumes that people were wearing at school, and that reminds you that it's Halloween. The thing will be much more powerful, it occurs to you, on that very occult day, and you put the incident down to that.

That's after the 'bot has cleaned up the mess he made when he dropped the food, and you have brought in the book boxes. Most of the meal was salvageable despite the accident, and there is time for the two of you to gobble it down before your 'bot has to leave for the country club. He also asks about the books and where you're going to put them all.

"Not sure," you say. "We're not staying here -- "

"Good."

" -- but I don't know where else to put them except that basement."

"Why don't we use that rockbot paste on someone's family, someone with a big house? Then we'd -- "

"Can't. Can't make any more rockbots."

"Why not? We were making them all over the place just last week."

"That was last week, and it's complicated why we can't do that now."

He relents, then reminds you that Johansson still wants a mask to use on Patterson. You start to give him the blank mask that Professor Blackwell left behind, but pull it back. "I'll handle it myself," you tell him. "For fucking reasons, that's how come," you explain when it wants to know why.

* * * * *

Your various reasons for various actions you reflect over after you've started to pack up boxes.

For a start, you can't make any more rockbots because of reasons that you've learned from Blackwell's brain. It has to do with the way that golems work.

A golem -- whether it is a featureless pile like what Prescott and Johansson made; or a transformation like what Prescott did to your grandfather; or a paste put inside a mask -- most obey its creator because it contains some of the essentia of the creator. Essentia is a mystical component of people, and it is extracted from the hair -- or semen, or skin, or blood -- that the creator puts in the mix. As it contains part of the creator, it must obey the creator because the creator is sharing his will with it.

But a human being only has so much of himself to spread around, and if he spreads too much of himself he will die. Most human beings can only divide themselves five or six ways; a lucky few have been known to have divided themselves seven ways.

Prescott divided himself ten ways, which is absolutely unheard of. He should have died when he started making 'bots of Call and Spencer and Rennerhoff. So you're not going to take a chance on creating an eleventh golem, not while you're inhabiting Prescott's body.

As for why you'll give Johansson the mask yourself instead of passing it on through Chen, that's just a practical caution. You want to talk to Johansson. Also, he will need to mix up a rockbot paste to use in the mask of Patterson, and you want to make sure that he does it right. The Chenbot, though it knows how to make a paste, is a golem, and golems can't perform magic -- that's why it couldn't get Jelena's mask off of Call. So there's a chance things could go wrong if it's put in charge of making the rockbot paste tomorrow.

Not that you'd be unhappy if something went wrong for Johansson. You just want to make sure any such accident doesn't cause a problem for you.

* * * * *

Packing up books isn't hard work, but it's tedious driving them out to the elementary school, for you've packed too many to take them all in one trip. It's this part of the job that wears you down, and causes you to scale back your plans for removing much of the library. By the time eight o'clock comes, you're ready to end the move after using the last five book boxes, and after making the last delivery to the old school you settle back in the car with a groan and start to raise David Kirkham's mask to your face.

You catch yourself at the last minute -- a just-in-time warning from Blackwell sounds in your skull. You're still wearing his brain band, and you take it out before putting on the mask, which contains Kirkham's brain band.

And when you wake you feel refreshed and energetic, for David Kirkham hasn't spent the evening moving boxes. So you make one last trip to Blackwell's, and add one last box of magical supplies to the great mass packed inside the basement. Then you go home.

* * * * *

You must have been worn out still, even under Kirkham's mask, for you sleep late the next morning. You eat a late breakfast with his mom and brother, and outline a day that will be spent around town in the company of Gary Chen, a boy that Mrs. Kirkham doesn't know that well but of whom she has a high opinion -- largely based on stereotypes about his ethnicity, it must be said. Tad, luckily, has a friend in the neighborhood he can drive around with when he wants to goof off in town. You all separate with minimal drama, though you and Tad get into a shoving match after colliding with each other just outside the bathroom.

Despite what you told your mom, your plans actually take you up to the high school, where Johansson via text has told you he wants to meet. He's in the unlocked gym, shooting free throws. He looks like a natural athlete, which he naturally should, since he's gotten very comfortable inside the face and body of Seth Javits. "So how was your Friday night?" he asks with a leer.

I'm Will Prescott, you remind yourself. Not Gary Chen, not David Kirkham. I'm this fucking loser's friend. "Fucking lonely. Just me and Kirkham's fantasies."

He laughs. "Well, me and Chelsea -- "

"I don't need to hear about it, fucker. Unless Patterson was there and you sucked his cock, and you sucked it so good that's how come he's coming up here to get himself turned into a robot. Is that what happened?"

Johansson blinks, then turns a little red. "No, he's just coming up to hang out. But are you sure you didn't hang out with someone?"

"Pretty sure. Why?" You don't like the puckish expression on his face.

He shrugs. "I just wondered, since I saw you out driving around, and you were in Kirkham's car but you weren't in Kirkham's face."

It takes a supreme act of will not to swallow at this. "Yeah, where'd you see me?"

"Just driving around. You stopped at some big house by the river. I would have stopped too, but -- " He rolls his tongue in his mouth. "I figured I'd see you today, and Chelsea was begging me to get over to her house."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Of Clearances and Clean Slates

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Johansson's naked boasts about Chelsea --

Naked? This is full-frontal dick-wagging.

-- pisses you off. It's bad enough that Prescott's butt buddy get to fuck Chelsea Cooper. Worse is that he's angling to turn himself into Steve Patterson so he can get even more pussy. And now he can't resist bragging about it to you.

Only the fact that you have no way of controlling him keeps you from ripping the mask off him now and kicking his ass all the way back to his shitty little house and his shitty little life.

Maybe he catches your mood, for he pulls himself back abruptly. "So why don't you get yourself a mask of someone who can get laid?" he asks. The sniggering brag is still in his tone, however.

"Oh, I can get laid," you tell him. "Kirkham's got no troubles there."

"You're fucking with me," he chortles. That's more fuel to a fire that is already dangerously close to raging out of control. "Dude, are you seriously bragging about Kirkham's prowess with they lay-days?"

"You're only getting pussy because you're wearing Javits's mask. And because we turned Chelsea into a 'bot."

"Sure." He shrugs. "That's how come you should do the same. Come on." He shoves you in the shoulder, and grins. "I'm just winding you up so you'll go out and get some of the same fun I'm having." He swivels on his heel and sinks a three-point shot with a swish.

"You think I can't do anything with Kirkham?" You bridle at his mirthful expression. "Or Chen?"

"Can you?"

A dare. You think you can get some satisfaction -- less than the satisfaction of breaking his face, but more than nothing -- by winning a challenge. "What about Javits's old fuck? Cindy? Think I can't get anywhere with her?"

A couple of expressions wash over his face in quick succession: surprise, anger, amusement. "Not without doing to her what I'm doing to Chelsea."

"Bet you I can. And not a one-night lay. Full on relationship."

"No! Ha!"

"Sure. That way you know I'm not cheating. I get her to go out with me, we make it a thing, you check her for masks and shit, and you'll see it's the real girl. What do you say?"

He looks you over, then thrusts out an open hand. "You don't try to fuck it up for me, either," you say, and he nods. "You got her phone number?"

"You're gonna try her now?"

"I'm getting a raging boner just thinking about all the times I'm going to humiliate you by satisfying her."

His lip curls, but he gives it to you.

You get another dividend. In the heat of that raging competition, he forgets to ask you for the blank mask he can use on Patterson. And if he remembers it later, he's too resentful to send you a text reminding you about it.

* * * * *

Actually, you don't need Cindy's phone number, for by chance you've got a perfect way to run into her, assuming she's home. You go back by the elementary school basement, where you pick up the letter that you wrote for Professor Blackwell's Lucybot. After finding the Vredenburg's address online, you drive out to their house.

Mrs. Vredenburg answers the door when you knock, but she tells you that Lucy isn't home. So you ask if Cindy is around. She is, and Mrs. Vredenburg calls her down.

Don't give me that face, you bitch, you think when Cindy appears. The distaste shows all too obviously. "Hey, I've got a note I'm supposed to pass on to your sister," you tell her. "Are you going to see her around?"

"Later on."

"Do you know where she is now?"

"No."

"You mind passing it along to her for me?"

Cindy puts out her hand with a shrug. There's a tug of war as you both pull at the letter.

"On second thought," you say. "I supposed to give it to her directly. If you don't mind, I'll come back later."

"Doesn't bother me," Cindy says, and an irritated weariness shows in her eyes.

They also look a little puffy around the eyes. "Hey, are you okay," you ask.

"I'm fine."

"You wanna talk about it?"

"Talk about what?" Her lip curls.

"Whatever's bothering you. There's something, right?"

"It's none of your business, uh, David."

"I didn't say it was. I only said you might want to make it my business. I don't care. Wouldn't bother me. Or am I keeping you from something?"

"Actually, you are." She puts her hand on the door to close it.

"Is it your friends? Are they here, because I didn't see any cars out front. Are you texting with them instead of talking with them?"

"Yeah, actually." You could scratch a diamond with the edge in her voice.

"Those are some real friends, if they won't get together with you. You know, I'm going out to get some coffee, something to eat. Come along, talk to me about it. An actual flesh-and-blood person. I'll even pay for it."

"Are you asking me on a date?" Well, those are her words. Her tone suggests a meaning closer to Are you seriously trying to put your cock in my mouth?

"No. I'm just a school friend who dropped by, saw you, decided you looked like you needed someone to talk to, and made an offer. Does that piss you off? Does it piss you off that someone might want to help you out by listening to you?"

She looks like you whapped her in the middle of the forehead with a two-by-four. She starts to stammer. "No. It -- It just -- "

"Great. If it doesn't piss you off, come go with me. Now. I'm starving, and you look like you need a person, not a cell phone to talk to."

She sways on her feet, but can come up with no counterargument. A few minutes later, she is awkwardly sliding into the front seat of your car.

And almost as bad as the way Johansson was bragging to you earlier about his ability to score with girls, now Kirkham is gloating to you about how he can talk them into going on a date.

* * * * *

You take her to The Crystal Cave, which is one of those kooky eateries close to the Keyserling campus. Like a lot of spots in town it is built around an occult theme. But where Besandwiched overwhelms with occult iconography, and The Flying Saucer spooks with weird architecture, the Crystal Cave only uses an astrological seating arrangement. The front of the restaurant -- an open space with a concrete floor and an open ceiling with bare support beams -- contains the cafeteria-style restaurant stations and the coffee bar and ten tables, each marked with a different symbol associated with one of the ten planets. The back room contains twelve booths, each with a symbol of the zodiac painted on the wall next to it.

After getting your food -- a panini sandwich for yourself and a bean-and-pasta salad for Cindy, and some coffee for you and hot tea for her -- you lead her into the back and slide into a booth, where you take direct control of the talk. You kept to school stuff on the drive out, but now you hit the main subject. "So what's making you all puffy?" You wipe at one of your eyes.

She looks away. "Nothing. Really. I'm okay."

"So why'd you come out with me? You think I'm charming, you wanna hang out with me?"

She stiffens.

"Yeah, you don't wanna answer that, do you," you observe. "That's okay, I'll ask you again later, maybe. But you came here with me because you were upset and I said you should talk to me. At least you can tell me what you were texting about with your girlfriends."

"I just needed to get away from it, that's why I came with you. Out of sight, out of mind, you know."

"Is it embarrassing? Is that why you don't want to tell me?"

"Look, don't badger me, David. Let's talk about something else." She squeezes some lemon into her tea.

"Sure, whatever you want. What do you want to talk about?"

For a long, dreadful moment, neither of you say anything. She busies herself with stirring her tea, hanging her head, and darting her eyes about.

It's a miserable sight. Cindy Vredenburg is a beautiful girl, with a pale complexion, blue eyes, strong cheekbones in her pie-pan face, and straight hair that shimmers like pale gold as it drops to her shoulders. Her breasts are large and firm, as are her thighs and calves, and her torso curves like a vase between her hips and her chest.

"So I heard you broke up with Seth," you say at last. "Have you found a replacement yet, or are you still auditioning guys?"

She gives you a dirty look. "I don't audition guys."

"So what are you doing with all those guys lining up around the block to go out with you? You telling 'em to send an application and you'll get back to them?"

"It's not like that," she says in a very cold voice.

"So what is it like? Seriously, I'm dying to know."

Her complexion curdles, and her eyes are like gimlets. "Are you really."

"Sure. Why wouldn't I be? I don't get to see the auditioning process, or however you do it. Is it a process, or are you just winging it? How do you handle the attention, how do you pick a guy out?"

She rolls her eyes. "It's not like that," she says again.

"So what's it like?" you repeat. "I try to imagine it, and nothing comes."

"Oh, I bet you try to imagine it," she mutters, and pushes her plate away. She hasn't taken a single bite of her salad.

"Well, answer me this, then. Have you gone on a date with anyone since you broke up with Seth?"

"No."

"Good. 'Cos I'm not interested in a girl who's just on the rebound."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Date on a Dare, Part 1

choices: Change your plans for Evans and Kelsey
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Cindy Vredenburg settles back in her seat. Her blue eyes are very hard. "What did you say?"

"You heard me. You know exactly what I said."

"So you're auditioning to be my boyfriend?" she sneers.

"You told me it's not an audition. I'm just declaring an interest, Cindy. Well, maybe. It depends on if you're serious about not being on the rebound."

Her stare is unblinking. You return it steadily, until you see the quiver of a swallow in her throat. Then you flash her just the tiniest smile. She looks away.

"If you're really serious about me, David -- " She breaks off. "This is really awkward."

"You mean we're not having fun."

"No we're not."

"That's only because we're talking honestly. Or I am. You wanna talk about something else, we'll talk about something else. But lemme say something first."

You lean back and look at her coolly. You've got a huge advantage over her, because your eyes will be mostly invisible behind your tinted lenses. But still you can't stop yourself from playing with the fork as you talk.

"You don't give a shit about me, Cindy. You don't give one fucking thought to me. I'm not in your little circle jerk of jocks and cheerleaders. I'm just a guy taking AP Stat and AP Calculus and Chemistry II, and I can talk about the late Beethoven string quartets, or show you how the Bach cello suites work, if you're into that shit. Also, I played dentist on Victor Anaya my sophomore year, used my fist to remove two of his teeth. And if you ask that dumbass who was dumb enough to dump you a couple of weeks ago, he'll tell you that I once punched him so hard for hassling me in P.E. class that he shat a little brown peapod into his underwear. But I dunno." You shrug. "That's all I gotta say for myself. From now until we go, you can talk and I'll just listen. 'Cos I like listening to you, and you can tell me anything you want, and I won't say anything back unless you ask me to. This ain't a date, Cindy, but that don't mean we can't be nice to each other. I mean, I'm no fucking good at being nice, but I'm sitting here trying hard to be, if you can believe it, because I want to be nice to you."

You're not sure, but you think Cindy's pupils have dilated during this speech. A flush has crept up her throat, and her nostrils are flaring lightly. Even some of the life and bounce has seeped from her hair.

She swallows openly, and shifts in her seat. She looks around.

Then she sags. "I can't -- " She pinches her eyes shut, and some of the red from her throat puffs out around her eyelids. She puts her elbows on the table and rests her chin in her hands.

You mirror her pose. She jumps a little when she opens her eyes and finds you staring at her, only inches from her face.

She wilts. "I just want to kill everyone at school," she says in an aching voice.

* * * * *

The dam doesn't break all at once. It's more like a glacier cracking up, calving off big chunks one at a time.

It's school, and her romantic life. She and her friends got forced off the cheerleader squad. Her boyfriend broke up with her hard, then jumped into the arms of the girl who drove her off the squad. Betrayal and backstabbing everywhere she turns. She had such ambitions for her senior year, such dreams, and they've all been smashed and shat on.

You ought to feel guilty about this. It's all your fault. It isn't even Prescott's fault. Sure, he put you inside Chelsea Cooper's body after you made that bargain. But it was you -- not the real Chelsea -- who obliterated Cindy and her friends, and used a magic mask to steal Cindy's boyfriend.

But you don't give a fuck. Truth to tell, you don't even really give a fuck about Cindy's happiness. But it would be fucking awesome if you scored with her, got her to be your girlfriend, even if you have to do it from inside the likeness of your best friend.

So when she asks you what you think, you say, "I think you don't have any friends."

She starts at that. "What do you mean? You think Lin and Yumi -- ?"

"They're not your friends, are they? You said everyone's stabbed you in the back."

"I didn't mean them." Her face is hot with embarrassment.

"So they really are your friends? Then okay, you've only got two friends in the whole fucking world. Naturally you should feel sorry for yourself."

"I didn't say -- "

"Name me one more friend you've really got. Bet you can't."

Her eyes flash. "Eva and Jessica Garner, and Jenny Ashton -- "

"So that's five. So what?"

She purses her mouth into a grim line. "I know what you're trying to do, David. You're trying to get me to count my blessings or some shit like that."

"And you don't want to. You'd rather feel sorry for yourself."

She slumps and glares at you.

"Do what makes you feel good, Cindy. If feeling rotten makes you feel good, then keep thinking about all the stuff that makes you feel rotten. I know I'd rather feel good by doing stuff that actually feels good, but I guess you've invented another way."

Her eyes widen. You tap her plate -- food still uneaten, as is yours -- with your fork. "You wanna get this to go?"

"No. I'm not hungry."

"Then get it to go. 'Cos you'll be hungry later if you're not hungry now. I'm getting mine to go, I can't afford to let it just fuck off." You get up.

"Are we leaving," Cindy asks, sounding startled.

"Oh yeah, we're done here." As she scoots out of the booth, you grab her hand and haul her to her feet. You pull her close, clutching her hand in yours and resting your other hand on her hip. "I'd love to help you feel good, Cindy. But you have to want to feel good before I can help you out. So think about it. Think about whether you want to sit in a puddle, or if you want to find some new way of feeling good. A way I can help you with." You release her, and swagger off to the front to get a couple of Styrofoam containers, leaving her to stare haggardly at the floor.

* * * * *

Conversation between you and Cindy is muted as you drive her back home. She thanks you as you pull up in front of her house. "For what?" you ask. "Listening," she replies. "And for the, uh, pep talk." "You're welcome. What are you doing Wednesday?" "I don't know." "Hang out with me after school."

She gives you a quizzical look. "Why Wednesday?"

"So you won't think it's a fucking date and get all defensive. And it'll give you time to get used to the idea of hanging out with me. Which, yeah, takes some getting used to."

She turns a little pink, but this time it looks like pleasure, not anger.

You've noticed a new vehicle in the drive, a big SUV. Cindy identifies it as Lucy's when you ask. "Let me come in a minute," you say, shutting off the engine. "I still got that note I'm supposed to give to her." You follow her in.

Lucy, when she appears, is even more luscious than Cindy. She's got the same pale, blonde looks, the same intensely huggable shape, the same sexy legs and calves and bosom. But somehow she's just twenty-five percent more everything that a guy wants. Maybe it's because she's a fully ripe woman, while Cindy is still a little green. You want to bite great chunks out of Lucy, and to rub yourself all over with her juice.

She takes the note with a skeptical "tch", and reads it with a frown. She looks u you up and down. Then she shrugs. "Any reply?" you ask her.

"No. I guess I'll see you around," she says with faint and undisguised distaste.

Yeah, I guess you will. She's just a mask on a golem, and her hauteur makes you want to take her off alone, rip the mask off her, put it on, and get it on with one of your alter egos. Teach the fucking cunt some respect for Chen Guangli and his power.

But you just nod, and with a soft "Catch you later" to Cindy you take off.

This very girl-heavy afternoon has got you thinking about girls, and about getting laid, and the jealousy you feel toward your best friend Kirkham and the way he manages to score more often than you ever did. The few girls you've scored with, he's managed to get, and quite a few more. None of them have been anywhere near the caliber of Cindy Vredenburg, though. It depresses you and excites you both, the prospect of snagging her. You marvel that you did as well with her at lunch as you did.

Meanwhile you've gotten a text from your robot replacement, asking about plans for the day, and reminding you that you need to move Evans out of Chris Trantham and into Kelsey Blankenship. You tell him to meet you at the elementary school basement. You've only one blank mask on hand, and will need more soon, so you'll make some there.

Probably that was a mistake. You're addled by all the thoughts about girls and sex, and it's made worse by sitting in the basement with your double: all those feelings of sexual fascination come flooding back, and you can't shake a fantasy -- horrible and exciting at the same time -- of putting on a girl's mask and doing it with him. You gruffly distract him, finally, by telling him to call Evans and outline the plan for him.

It's a conversation that ends with yelling and threats. "He says he won't do it," Chen says when he hangs up. "He won't play a girl."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Date on a Dare, Part 2
