1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Fucker." You grab the phone away from Chen and are about to call Evans back when you stop. "What exactly did he say?"

"That he's not gonna be a girl," Chen tells you. "He said he didn't care what I did to him, he wasn't going to do it."

You bet that between Gary Chen and David Kirkham you've got the persuasive muscle to convince Tanner Evans that he'd love to be Kelsey Blankenship, but you decide it'd be better if he was playing someone he likes and not something he hates. "Fuck it," you say. "We'll put him in for Roth. Or for someone else."

"We still need someone for Kelsey," Chen says. "Or are we giving up on that?"

"I'll think about it," you say, and hand Chen the mask you just finished casting. "Go polish that up while I think." You start making another mask. And you'll make another mask after that.

* * * * *

It probably wouldn't have occurred to you if you weren't so fixated on the sex-and-girls things. You've been using guys in all your schemes because they're friends -- of a sort -- and you feel like you can dominate and control them. But there are some girls that might be useful too, if slotted into the correct spot.

Molly Shaw, for instance.

Kirkham has had her a couple of times, the fucker. Once after he heard how easy she was, and discovered that rumor did not exaggerate. A second time when, to his delight, she came to him with an itch that she insisted only he could scratch. A third time, when he had an itch, and after some intense pressure got her to yield. And a fourth time when she was adamantly against it until he relaxed her with some of his stash and she became very friendly indeed.

Oh, and a fifth and sixth time when he pulled the same trick. He's not been much interested in her since then. She gets these intense pregnancy scares, and makes life hell for whichever guy she's convinced herself is the father.

The main reason you're thinking of her now, though, is because of what she said one time when they were stroking each other and the subject of Kelsey somehow came up. "Bitch! Cunt! I wish I had her fucking money and she had a feather up her thing, then we'd both be tickled." There was a lot more in that vein, though Kirkham had tried to get her off the subject, all of which showed how venomously jealous Molly was of that stuck-up snob, and how she felt slighted by her.

Replace Kelsey with Molly Shaw? You bet she'd go for it. And you bet she'd put out afterward, too.

After you finish two more masks -- giving you a total supply of four -- you leave your robo-bro to finish polishing them while you take off with the book and some supplies for making mind bands. You'll do that tonight, but you have a stop or two to make first.

* * * * *

"I didn't know you were part of this," Chris Trantham -- really Tanner Evans -- says. He's looking fatter and pastier than the last time you saw him, and the weedy blonde moustache on his upper lip looks like a hairy caterpillar that's dying of mange.

"Well I am," you retort. "Me and Chen are partners on this. What he says, I mean, and what I say, he means. Got that? Two heads, one brain. So what's this shit about you not playing a girl."

"I'm not playing a girl," he replies with quiet truculence. "You're not giving me a pussy."

"Don't you want a pussy to finger? Don't you want a couple of fucking globes to fondle?"

He tries to shrink down inside himself. "I'm not," he says in a voice that's soft and pained.

"You'll do as you're fucking told. But you're lucky this time." You jab him in the shoulder. "My brother Chen talked to you before I told him I had somebody else lined up for Kelsey. Now if you fucking beg me -- " You grab him by the hair, and with a knee to the groin force him to kneel. "If you fucking beg me, you cocktail of puke and dirty cum, I won't change my mind! I won't make you put on Kelsey's face and blow me! But you gotta beg!" You choke on a bolus of fury, and knee him in the chest. "Beg, cocksucker!"

Trantham gasps and snuffles. "Please," he whispers.

You are almost nauseous with contempt. "You're gonna do it, aren't you, you fag? I'm gonna make you, because you already have a pussy where your balls should be. Well, fuck you!" You shove him away.

You're out by the water towers east of town, and you and Trantham aren't the only assholes out here. But you're off by yourselves, and the gangbangers loitering a couple of dozen yards away are only eyeing you furtively. So you're not expecting any trouble, and certainly not after the way you've forced the tall and chubby Chris Trantham into a fetal huddle on the grass.

"You fucking pussy," you snarl at Trantham. "The only reason I'm letting you off is because you'd be shit in the part. You playing Kelsey? Fuck that. I told my bro you were a fucking loser and I'd find someone else for the part. But he went and called you anyway." You blow a wad of spit onto the side of Trantham's face. "The first time I ever had to bitch slap my bro, and it was on account of your fucking ass. Fuck me, but that's typical of you." You drop to one knee and press his head into the ground. He weeps with fear. "Thank me for going easy on you, cocksucker. I said, thank me!"

"Thank you," he gasps, and starts sobbing in a low whine.

"Now we just gotta figure out a spot we can put you where you can actually do some good. I was thinking about giving you Justin Roth's face, but Roth's a fucking cool guy, and it makes me want to puke all over myself to think of you there. You got any ideas?"

He whimpers and shakes his head.

"Me neither. Fuck."

You sit next to him and let him cry and snivel until he runs out of tears and snot and self-pity. It's a long process, and even after he's quiet again he remains curled up like a immense, blubbery baby. When you're on the verge of losing patience again, you tell him to sit up. He does, and rubs at the dried streaks of tears that stain his cheek.

"Okay, you know I'm in charge, and you're going to do what I fucking say. Right?"

Trantham nods.

"You're going to play Kelsey."

He gapes. Then his face twists up. You're sure he's about to start crying again, but with a couple of deep gasps he fights it down. "Okay," he says quietly. "I'll play Kelsey."

"Good man." You slap him lightly on the cheek. "You said the right thing, so I'm gonna let you off. You don't have to be a girl. Like I said, I found a much better person for the part. But if you kicked again, I was going to make you. But you can be a guy. You don't got any ideas of your own?"

He is in such shock that it takes him a moment -- and a couple of swallows -- to recover, and to shake his head again. "You know what we want?" you ask him. "We want someone who can move product directly, or who can help us move it by making it look good. That's why we got Andrea Varnsworth working for us now, it's how come we want Roth on the team."

"I wouldn't mind being Roth."

But you don't think it would work. Looking at Evans, you're pretty sure you've broken his spirit, and you don't need a spirit-broken Justin Roth. If anyone is going to play Roth, it has to be someone who is happily on board with you and your program. Evans needs to be slotted in someplace where he can do a job.

Someplace like Douglas Canfield.

Now that you think about it, you've got Mendoza and Evans in the wrong spots. Mendoza's got spirit and guts -- of the three fucketeers, he's the one you like and respect the best -- and he's shown a lot of good sense and worked for you faithfully. You'd trust him with a spot like under Roth's face. He's wasted in the role of Douglas Canfield, whose only job it is to take orders by pulling more of the junior class into the AP snobs' "reefer parties." That's a job that Evans could do.

"I gotta think about this," you tell him, and to show him that the storm has blown over, you put out a hand and help him to his feet. "I want you to do what we tell you, but I also want you to be happy. I mean, Jesus. That's how come Chen was going to put you in for Kelsey. I mean, besides her being a total score for us, he figured you'd have fun playing with her boobs and driving her BMW. What's the fucking matter with you that you don't want that?"

"I just don't," he says. "Being a girl is -- " He shrugs.

"I'd do it."

"You would?"

"Fucking hell, yeah. But me and Chen, we got to keep our heads out of other people's heads, keep our thoughts clear and pure. We got some other girls you could be, though. You sure you don't want -- ? Yeah, okay," you say when he shakes his head. "Well, come on, let's get you an ice cream or something, make the tears go away."

* * * * *

"Craig Wyatt," Mendoza suggests later, when you ask him of any juniors it would be good to acquire.

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Who's fucking Craig Wyatt?" you ask in answer to the suggestion. You're at home, on the phone, talking to Douglas Canfield. It's George Mendoza answering from under the mask, of course. You called to ask him who he knew down in the junior class that would be a good disguise to help expand the network in the eleventh grade.

"Who is he, or who's he fucking?" Canfield replies. "No one, I think, except he's dating Bailey Keane, but she's not the type -- "

"No, who is this asshole?"

"He's Craig Wyatt. He's in the band. Plays clarinet."

You try to put a face to the description. Something very hazy comes. A tall blonde kid, maybe? "Why him? Is he a stoner? Is he popular?"

"Well, he's got a lot of friends, but he's not one of those Mr. Popularity fags. He's in a couple of AP classes, he's in band, he shows up at all the parties -- "

"How's this help us move product?"

"The same way Andrea does. He's this clean-cut kid, but it's not like he's a fucking Boy Scout or choir boy about it. That's what makes him perfect." Mendoza is clearly warming to his idea. "This guy, he belongs to all the right clubs, you know, and he's got all the right shit about him -- "

"You're not convincing me, man."

"He's like an advertisement for making it respectable. Like, if he starts smoking a little weed, everyone's gonna be all, like, 'Hey, if Craig's doing it, it must be okay, I'll do some too.' So he'll make smoking weed look totally boring and safe, and then lots of people will start doing it too. At least, that's the way it looks to me."

This sounds promising. "Is he friends with Dougie Canfield? 'Cos I need someplace to put Evans, he's being a total pussy and I need someone who can manage him, which would be you."

"Yeah, I talk to the Wyatt fag, it won't look funny if we become best friends."

"Fantastic. I'm putting you in charge, then. Can you arrange to hang out with him after school on Monday? We'll do the switch then."

He promises to let you know, and you hang up. That done, you return to working on a brain band to go with one of the new masks that Chen dropped off a little while ago.

Four masks. You've got four masks either completed or in various stages of completion. One of them you're holding back against the time that Johansson decides that he has to get himself a copy of Steve Patterson. One you're going to use on this Craig Wyatt guy. One you'll use on Kelsey. And the last?

You still think that Justin Roth would be a good recruit, but you're less and less enamored of that part of your program. You've already got things set up to move huge volumes through Westside and Eastman and at the Warehouse. You're gearing up a network in the local junior class. Really, instead of nailing down every angle in the Westside senior class, you ought to be pushing into the junior class at Eastman and finding new customers at the university, which is still where you sell most of your product.

Trouble is, you haven't got any agents that you can put in those places.

You sigh. No, what you really need to do is to get back inside the head of Aubrey Blackwell and start figuring out the grimoire. The deeper you get into it, the more toys you've discovered inside it. The long-term answers are there.

But that's a job for tomorrow, when you can go hide somewhere after taking Kirkham's mask off.

* * * * *

But you're solidly distracted the next day when Chen calls with a message: Douglas Canfield got himself an early play date with Craig Wyatt, and is going to have him over at his house that afternoon. You call Mendoza, get the details on the meeting, and scramble to get your shit together so you can be there. Evans sounds relieved when you call him and tell him he'll be working closely with Mendoza for the foreseeable future.

The ambush is slated to go off at two o'clock, and the first thing you have to do is meet with Evans and Trantham. These two are inhabiting each other's form, of course, so it's an easy swap to make when you meet at one o'clock out back of Westside. The trickiest part is keeping them inside separate portables so that Evans won't see how docile his replacement is. When he asks how you're going to get masks off one victim and then onto the other, you tell him to shut up and trust you.

The swap itself is just a matter of ordering each person, in their separate portables, to give you their clothes. You transfer each wardrobe to the other portable and have them put the other clothes back on. You get the mask off Evans first, wake him up, and send him back to the parking lot with directions to Douglas Canfield's house. Then in the other portable you take the mask of Evans off Chris Trantham and quietly steal away to follow your confederate.

Canfield lives in the same general neighborhood as Chelsea Cooper, on the southeast part of town about a mile from Fort Suffolk. It's a ritzy section of the city, and the Canfields live on the very edge of town in the largest house in the development. Dougie -- looking dopey as ever in a sloppy gray pullover and baggy cargo pants, lets you in and introduces you to his parents before taking you upstairs.

"Nice fucking bedroom," you observe when you're inside it. It's very large, with a king-size bed, a tall book case, a large desk with an executive chair, an entertainment center and two bean bags. You nod at Evans, already arrived earlier and sitting quietly in a bean bag in front of a flat-screen TV and game box. "Bed's big enough for a three-way. You ever have one of those up here?"

"You asking to have one now?" Canfield retorts

"Keep in character," you tell him sharply.

"That is in character," he honks. "I'm kind of obnoxious."

"Only 'kind of'?" You nod at Evans again. "You tell him where he's going, what he's doing?"

"Yeah." Dougie's brow creases anxiously. "How's this going to work? I mean, if Craig wakes up here inside Tanner's body -- ?"

"Leave that to me. Chen discovered a little trick that gives 'em a little bit of amnesia, just enough to cover things. Oh, that reminds me," you continue when the other two exchange an anxious glance. "This partnership that me and Chen are running. He's taking care of the product, so if you've got any questions about supply or sales, talk to him. The masks and the magic, and who pretends to be who, I'm in charge of that."

They nod. "I heard you're doing something with Kelsey Blankenship," Canfield says.

"Yeah, but don't worry about that. Where's this fucker Wyatt? Is he always late?" It's after two by now.

It's another ten minutes before he shows. You recognize him once he's inside, though you don't know anything about him. He's not such an obvious dillweed as Canfield is, and your instant reaction is that Mendoza probably has him pegged correctly. He's a tall kid, skinny, with reddish-gold hair and dark eyebrows that slope down at the corners over sleepy eyes. Everything about his face is long -- long nose, long jaw, long chin -- but his looks are handsome enough without being very distinguishable. He's wearing tan slacks, a blue Oxford shirt and a red and blue tie, probably the leftovers from going to church. The impression of WASPy respectability is offset, though, but the way he's got the tie loosened, the sleeves rolled up over his elbows, and a Boston Red Sox ball cap perched brim backward on his head. "Hey," he says in a very casual tone. But the guarded look in his eye tells you that he recognizes David Kirkham, and recognizes him as bad news.

So you don't waste time. "Grab him," you tell your confederates. Canfield immediately springs at him; Evans lumbers a little more slowly.

"What the -- ?" Wyatt barely has time to twist inside the others' grasp before you smash a blank mask onto his face.

"Get him undressed," you order. "And try not to give yourself a chubby while you do it."

* * * * *

"How long did it take you to get into character as Trantham?" you ask your newest imposter as he finishes getting dressed.

"I dunno. A couple of hours?" Craig Wyatt cinches the knot of his tie, then loosens it. He sweeps the ball cap off the bed and sets it on his head. He blinks sleepily at you. "Like, six or seven?"

"Then maybe you better call home, tell your folks you and Dougie here will be out sucking each other's cocks until eight or nine o'clock."

"He's got a girlfriend," says Canfield.

"I do?"

"Doesn't matter," you tell Evans. "And steer clear of her until you get some idea of how to treat her."

"I know how I'd treat her," says Canfield, and grabs his crotch.

"Like that, huh?" Wyatt laughs. "Now I know what to aim for."

"That ain't your job," you remind him. "Remember where you coulda been. I'm doing you a big favor, fucker. This cocksucker's a lot better looking than your last carcass, or your real one." You glance out the window in time to see the Evansbot getting into his car. "Me and Chen have a lot invested in you. Don't fuck us over. You're gonna smoke weed, and you're gonna get all your friends to start buying it too."

"Sure. But can I start with my girlfriend?"

"If that works for you."

"I'll make it work," he smirks.

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Molly Shaw is not an unhandsome girl, so it's no surprise there's a line around the block of guys willing to give her a reason to worry that she's come down with a baby. But there's room for improvement. And even if she wasn't quite so pudgy, or didn't wear her mousy brown hair in that unflattering flat drape, or showed a little less of her very tall forehead and showed a little more skin instead of wrapping herself in cheap and shapeless fabrics --

Well, she'd be prettier even if she just carried herself with more poise and confidence.

Kelsey Blankenship has poise and confidence to spare. What Molly Shaw could do with those.

"Why do you want to see me," she asks, her voice roughly edged with suspicion, when she answers your call.

"Just to see you, girl. Well, that's not true. I got something special to show you."

"I've seen your special thing before, David."

"Not this special thing. It's awesome, trust me. My God, it'll change your life."

"Where?" She's sulky, but at least she's willing.

"How about up at the school, the portables back behind -- "

"Oh God, no!" Her voice rises to a shriek. "I know what you want, David, you're not -- "

"Alright, alright! Jesus! The library, then, the city library. Someplace public, because it's not that. It's something else, but I want it someplace people can't see us. Four o'clock. I'll buy you ice cream afterward," you add when she's silent. "If you don't like what I show you."

"Is it just going to be you?"

"Just me. Though a third person might show up." You hang up before she can ask what you mean by that.

Next you call Andrea. She's busy socializing, but you don't have to threaten her to break off and meet you at Westside. Thomason can keep busy in a portable with Andrea's cell after you've divested him of the mask, and while he's unconscious you shave some of his hair off and save it in a small plastic baggie.

* * * * *

Molly is wearing clothes even more shapeless than usual when you meet her: baggy jeans and a fuzzy blue top that's a little like a sweater and little like a sweatshirt without managing to be either one. Her cheeks seem slightly swollen, and she looks at you from under her eyebrows with a hooded resentment. Brown hair hangs limply past her shoulders. She's tried something new, and tied a braid across the top of her forehead. It looks like a dead garter snake.

"You're looking nice," you tell her, and stroke the side of her head. She jerks away. "Hey, what's the matter?"

"Nothing," she says. "I'm just mad."

"Who at? Maybe I could talk 'em into making it right for you." You cough into a clenched fist.

"Never mind. What do you want?" She squints peevishly at you.

"I got something to show you. Come on with me, we don't want anyone seeing."

You lead her up into one of the wings and back behind the stacks, to a spot where you know of some sofas and comfy chairs. They turn out to be taken, but the second set, off in a distant corner, are empty, and you point Molly into one of them as you set your backpack down. She falls into it like an avalanche of old clothes.

"My friend Chen made this," you tell her as you unzip your bag and take Andrea's mask out. "You know Chen. You sometimes buy from him." She makes a face at you. "Well, he found this kind of arts and crafts book, and it taught him how to make things like this." You display the mask.

The curl of her lip tells you she's unimpressed. "Yeah, so? It looks dumb."

"It's a mask. You put it on."

She rolls her eyes. "Halloween was Friday."

"Except this is a magical mask. Really and for true. Here, see?" You turn the mask around. "It's got Andrea Varnsworth's name in it."

"So what?" Then she does a double take it, and stares. Her nostrils flare. She puts out her hands and takes the mask and tries to touch the letters that float over the attached metal strip. "I guess this is pretty cool," she says.

"It's more than cool. It's magic. Look, let me show you." You take the mask back from her. "I'm going to put it on your face. Just press it right up close." You spread your palm over your own face to illustrate. "It's going to stick to your face. And then it's going to give you Andrea's face." You grin.

Molly stares, then snorts. "I don't have time for bullshit, David." She starts to get up.

"Hey, hey." You press her back down. "Just humor me. Just let me -- " You push the mask toward her, slowly and gently at first. But when she tries twisting away, you ram it home.

Then you sit back with a heavy sigh. It wasn't likely she was going to sit still for the demonstration, but you would have rather talked her around. Either way, though, you get to stare for a couple of minutes at the severely beautiful face of Andrea Varnsworth as she slumps inside the clothes of Molly Shaw.

* * * * *

"Jesus! Oh Jesus God!" Molly gasps and trembles and with quivering fingertips touches one cheek while staring at the picture on the screen of her iPhone. "Is that really -- ?" She blinks wet eyes.

You had to hold her back when she woke up, and talk her gently through the discovery of the change. You started by assuring her that it was only temporary, that you could take the mask off her any time, and then had her look at her hands. Then you had her feel her breasts, which are much larger than they were before. Only then did you have her take out her cell phone and look at herself in it.

"It's a perfect disguise," you tell her. "All the way down to your toes. There's no seams or buttons or zippers, and no way to get it off without using the magic words."

"Yeah?" Her tone and expression are both dazed.

"And up here." You point to your temple. "There's even memories. It takes a couple of hours for them to come through. But if you spent the night inside that mask, then when you woke up tomorrow you'd remember everything Andrea remembers, know everything she knows. You'd have all her abilities, too. You could swim like her, dance like her, sing like her. Instincts, too. You'd be able to act just like her, if you concentrated. Walk around inside that thing, and tell yourself you're Andrea Varnsworth, and you'd be able to act just like her so perfectly that no one would ever guess you weren't."

Her eyes are dilated, and she's been staring at the cell phone, so you're not sure she even heard you. "Molly?" you say.

"Sorry? I mean, I heard what you said, but -- " She touches herself again. "I just can't believe this." She touches her teeth. "Wow, they're just like hers. I mean, they're not like mine!" She runs her tongue around inside her mouth. "It feels funny."

"You'd get used to it."

She lowers the cell phone, but still stares off distantly. "Why are you showing me this?" A flush begins to creep up her neck.

"I want you to do a job for me."

"What kind of job?" Her eyes harden.

You smile. "I want you to replace someone. I want to turn you into another girl, and I want you to take over her life."

Her eyes go very big. For a moment you're terrified that she's going to jump up and run screaming from the library. But in a very quiet voice she only says, "Who?"

"Kelsey Blankenship."

You hold each other's eyes. Molly doesn't move for the longest time, and when she finally does move, it's only to blink once. "Why?"

"To help out some friends of mine. You barely have to do anything. See, Kelsey has these parties every week at her house, where she and her friends like to hang out and smoke a little weed."

"I know about those," Molly says, which surprises you.

"Oh. Well, I've got some friends that would like to start hanging out with her at those things. But you know what a snob Kelsey is. She doesn't want to hang out with them. But if you were Kelsey, you'd be nice and let my friends hang out at these parties. Right?"

Her wariness deepens. "Who are these friends?"

"Some juniors, maybe a few sophomores. Kelsey's type, they're AP students like she is. But she's a snot and only wants seniors and maybe only one or two juniors hanging out. It would just be a really nice thing if you opened her parties up a bit and didn't make her such a stuck up bitch."

Deep skepticism shows in her eyes. "You don't care about people that way, David."

"No I don't, but it would be useful. 'Cos my friend Chen, he's the one who sells Kelsey her weed. If she starts inviting more people to her parties, he sells her and her friends more weed."

Molly blinks again. "Oh," she says, and with that you realize you've convinced her. Until her suspicion reappears with the question, "But happens to Kelsey? If I'm replacing her -- "

So you explain about how you'd make a mask of Molly herself, and put Kelsey inside it, and how the new Molly would act just like the old Molly and would forget that she was Kelsey. "And you could stay Kelsey as long as you want. Rest of the year. Or after. Go to college, marry some rich fucker who works on Wall Street, become a fucking socialite." You shrug.

Envy and morality fight for possession of Molly's face. But you judged your girl well, and the fight doesn't last long. She only surprises with one question, but it's a real bombshell: "Can Joe Ysleta come with me? I mean, as one of Kelsey's friends?"
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Mollycoddle

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Ysleta?" you exclaim. "Why him?"

Molly's expression turns hooded. And since she is wearing Andrea's face, it's a sexy and smoldering kind of hooded, the kind that makes you want to take her face in both hands and chew her lips off. You restrain yourself, though, and silently repeat the question to her.

It's a weird request. First, because you don't see why Molly would want to bring a friend along if she makes a switch. Second, because you don't know why she'd want to bring Joe Ysleta along. He's not her boyfriend or anything. In fact, he's the boyfriend of --

Ah. Maybe that's it. "Are you trying to steal him from -- ? Who's he going out with now? Victoria?"

"I'm not stealing him," she snarls back. "He'd want to do this."

"So you want Victoria to come along too?" You stare at her evenly when she doesn't answer. "The answer's 'no' unless you tell me what this is about."

"Then don't do it," she says, and starts to get up. A bad move: Molly's baggy pants fall off Andrea's much slimmer frame, and she grabs them up and falls back into the chair.

You'd rather have Molly happily on your team than unhappily on it. This isn't like with the fucketeers or with Call and his dipshits. You can't beat her or threaten her, not the way you can with them. This needs persuasion. So you grab Molly's hands between yours and look deeply into her eyes.

"I'll do it for you, babe. I don't want you to be lonesome, and if showing this shit to Ysleta and giving him a mask will make you happy, I'll do it. But you gotta explain it to me so I know what to do and how to do it. To make you happy and make him happy. I mean, won't he want Victoria in a mask, so he doesn't get lonely?"

Molly bites her lip and gets a very pinched look, which is just about the only expression that doesn't look good on Andrea's face. "It has to do with -- She doesn't understand him!"

"What's not to understand? Ysleta's a good guy. He's not my kind of guy, but if you like marching bands and hot air balloons, then he's -- "

"Look, he'd just like it, that's all. He'd like -- Well -- " She squirms. "These masks." She pales. "Wait. Can a girl wear a mask of a guy? Or a guy wear a mask of a girl?"

"Sure. You can cross-dress that way if you want." You want to keep things compartmentalized, so you don't tell her that you've already got three guys pretending to be girls.

"Don't laugh about it, David. Okay?"

"Laugh about what?"

She takes a couple of heavy breaths. "Joe, he -- Well, he likes -- " She turns very red. "He'd like it if he could put on Victoria's clothes. Or Kelsey's clothes. Skirts, I mean."

It takes you an absurdly long time to realize what she's implying, and she turns even redder as she waits for you to make the connection. "You mean he's into cross-dressing?" you finally ask. You're still not sure what else might explain her statement.

And she nods. "Victoria doesn't understand. Wouldn't understand. He's never told her. But -- "

"How do you know all this?"

She hangs her head and mumbles something. "Because I caught him once," she says when you ask her to repeat it. "There's a couple of guys at school that like it, and they get together, and I found out about it and caught them at it."

"Who?"

She flares. "I'm not going to tell you! I'm only telling you about Joe so you'll understand that he'd like this."

"Okay, that's cool," you say, even while you're sniggering very hard inwardly. Joe Ysleta, despite his Hispanic genes, is one of the squarest and whitest guys you know at school. "So you want him along because it would make him happy to go all the way?" She nods. "And Victoria doesn't know, and he wouldn't want her to know?" She shakes her head. "And he'd give her up if he had a chance to turn himself into a girl?" She only turns even redder at that. "Look, unless I know for sure that he'd be into this -- "

"He would," Molly says fiercely. "Because of what you said, about these robot people. Couldn't he switch back and forth, sometimes being himself and sometimes being, uh, the other girl? Who could he be?"

"I'll have to think about it. Both things." You lean back and tug your lip. It doesn't take you long: you just have to look at Molly's face to realize that the only way to keep her happily on this team you're expanding is to do this for her. "Yeah, I guess it could work. But he has to do what I tell him. He has to be a team player," you say sharply. Molly nods. "And you have to tell me why you want him specially. It's not just 'cos he likes to dress up in girl's clothes. You're not that nice, just like I'm not that nice to those junior and sophomore cocksuckers I'm trying to get into Kelsey's parties."

Molly twists some hair about her index finger -- a tic completely out of character for Andrea. "I'd like it better if he just left her," she says in a small voice. "But if I could get him away from her, even for just a little bit -- " She trails off.

"You want him for yourself?"

Her lower lip trembles. Then she shakes her head violently. "I just want her to lose him!"

* * * * *

Well, really, do you give a shit what Molly's reasons are? You pull the mask off her, and then you have her call Ysleta. He cheerfully accepts her invitation to the library, though only after Molly says she's slowly getting people together for a social thing that evening.

Ysleta doesn't swagger into the library, exactly, but he's the kind to walk with a loose swing in his hips and arms. He's not intimidated by you, either, and nods a cheerful greeting when he sees you. Without waiting for an invite, he throws himself into the third of the chairs in the corner and dangles a leg over the arm.

Joe Ysleta is a shortish guy, which means he's about your height (whether as yourself or as Kirkham). His complexion is naturally dark, and his hair is almost jet black. He keeps it trimmed into a modified buzz cut -- long on top, with gel to help it stand on end, and shaved close above the ears. His grin is wide and easy and perpetual, and he has a habit of stroking the side of his jaw with his thumb with an air of faux-thoughtfulness.

In short, he's a sociable twat who is only about 85% as cool as he thinks he is.

But at least he keeps his cool while Molly in her halting way tells him about the masks, and he grins coolly when you show him Andrea's mask, and he actually does a really good job of keeping it in stride after he's woken up inside her mask. Like Molly, he spends a very long time staring at himself in the cell phone and stroking his cheeks and playing with his hair. "And what are you doing with this?" he asks in a low voice.

"He's going to turn me into Kelsey Blankenship," says Molly. "I'm going to be Kelsey Blankenship," she repeats in faintly awestruck tones.

"What for?" he asks. You thought you detected a little bit of horror in his voice when he spoke before. Now you definitely hear it.

You explain things to him, and can tell it doesn't make him feel any better about the proposal. Thank goodness then that Molly puts her hand on his forearm and tells him that he can put on a mask too. A mask like Andrea's, she says.

"Molly tells me you like to put on girls' clothes," you tell Ysleta bluntly, and he rears back. Molly gives you a dirty look. "Which is fine with me. I don't care. I can see the attraction. But Molly wants a friend with her, someone I guess she can talk to like herself." You pause. This is the first time you've really thought that maybe you don't like the idea of Molly having a confidante. That's the way conspiracies can form. But it's too late now. "So she wants you to come along, to be one of her friends." To be her pervy friend, you think but don't say aloud.

Ysleta goes back to staring at himself in the cell phone, and stroking himself. Then he lowers the phone, and like Molly had, he stares at the far distance over your shoulder. A few times Molly starts to speak, but you motion her to silence. Ysleta's inner struggle takes longer than Molly's does, but when you quietly explain again how you would disguise the real girl, and how she could come back again with no harm done, he accedes. He does so with a very heavy sigh and gulp, though, and you're pretty sure that you need to bundle him into a mask quick before he changes his mind.

But a mask of who? Someone who would hang out with Kelsey, and would be a help in moving product. Someone who does not have a boyfriend -- Ysleta might like to dress up like a girl, but you're pretty damn sure he doesn't want to fuck like one -- or who can dump her boyfriend without it looking weird. And probably someone who dresses up in nice things.

No obvious candidates spring to mind, so you ask Ysleta who he would like to be. Now his eye turns inward. You glance at Molly, and she blurts out, "What about Olivia? Byrne, I mean."

She's a swimmer and a part of the AP crowd, so that makes sense, if only as a holding spot. It also, by a chain of free association, suggests Catherine Muskov as a candidate to you.

Then Ysleta himself upsets everything with the name "Cindy. Cindy Vredenburg."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: A Friend in Need

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Not Cindy," you tell Ysleta.

"Why not?" he asks sharply.

"Fine, Cindy it is. If you're willing to put out for me."

Ysleta's eyes -- Andrea's eyes -- widen, and she flushes a little.

"I'm trying to go out with her," you explain. "I had a date with her yesterday and I'm gonna have more. I'd rather do it with the real girl."

Ysleta looks amused. "Really? You and Cindy?"

"Something wrong with a picture of me and Cindy?" You raise your chin.

He stifles a laugh. "No. Good luck to you, though." He gives you a sidelong look. "Olivia would be okay, I guess."

"What about Catherine Muskov?"

That name catches his attention, which surprises you. You'd expect him to instantly reject her on account of her boyfriend. Which would be disappointing, because now that you think about it you can imagine her opening up some new markets the way Andrea has.

"That might be fun," he says. "Except she's dating Michael Allen."

"So dump him. Dump him hard."

His eyes widen at your brutality, and he looks away for a bit before leaning over to murmur at Molly. "Would that be okay with you? Catherine?"

What's it matter what she thinks, you want to ask him. "Okay, Catherine," says Ysleta when Molly nods. "So how, uh -- ?"

"Would it be funny if you called her, set up a place to meet and talk?" Ysleta seems like the kind of social butterfly who could pull off a direct approach, and sure enough, he nods. "We need to do it someplace super private, not like here. Clothes are going to have to be changed, you know."

"We could do it at my place, I guess," Ysleta says, though he sounds a trifle unsure.

"Mine," says Molly more firmly. "My mom's out of town."

"You mean you're there by yourself?"

"No one'll bust in on us."

You and Ysleta exchange a glance. Then you shrug. "That's great then. I'll get this off of you -- " You stretch your hand to cover his face. "Then I'll go get the shit we need to do this."

* * * * *

It's a tedious round of errands. First to the school to give Thomason the mask of Andrea back. Then to your house to pick up two mind bands. Then to the elementary school basement to fetch blank masks and sealant. You also have to make a fresh batch of the latter. After pulling the mask off Ysleta, you snipped off a chunk of his hair -- explaining to Molly it's part of the spell-casting process -- for use in the robo-paste. You can't use Prescott's hair anymore, and you've changed your mind about using Thomason's. You're just going to have to let Ysleta and Molly control their own 'bot replacements, since it sounds like they are going to want to switch out between identities. A huge part of you resents this diminished control. But you have recruited them solely because they can help you advertise product, not because you need any real positive help from them.

While you're driving around, you get a text from Molly, giving you her address and telling you that Catherine will be at her house at six-thirty. You wind up cutting it close, but Catherine is late getting to Molly's anyway, and you beat her there.

Molly lives a couple of blocks away from Andrea Varnsworth, in a house that is roughly the shape and dimensions of a trailer -- an ugly, square, utilitarian thing with a dead lawn and a chain link fence whose rusted "Beware of the Dog" sign long ago ceased to refer to any such beast. Ysleta is already there too, and he and Molly both look very pale and ill at ease -- on the very edge of backing out, you can tell. It was one thing, after all, to tease them with the idea of swapping identities with someone more alluring and with more advantages. It's quite another, surely, to carry out such a scheme so soon and on such short notice. If you didn't slap a little encouragement back into Ysleta by telling him about the awesome boobs he's about to acquire, you're sure he'd tell you that he wants to sleep on the idea first. Maybe for a couple of years.

Around six forty-five there comes a sharp knock on the door, and some cheerful greetings when Molly opens it. "Is this where the party's supposed to be?" a low, thrilling female voice calls. Catherine Muskov comes in, and if her face falls a little at the sight of you, she still manages a smile.

She's tall -- a little taller than you, actually -- and carries herself with confidence and poise. Her smile is calm, and there's just a trace of satire in her eyes. She wears her long, thick, brown hair loose, sweeping it up and over and behind her ears to pour down the back of her neck without the aid of any ties or pins or gel. You've always liked looking at her, and it's not just because her boobs are big enough to be an imagined handful without being overwhelming. You like the way she massages her neck when she gets a crick in it, and the way she pushes her hair around and gets it to stay in place. You especially like that she prefers wearing sports tops, even under short jackets.

She's wearing a tight, sleeveless red top now, despite the cool evening air, and it complements her rose-and-earth complexion. It clings very tight to her, ending above her belly button, inviting you to peel it carefully off her. She's in hip-hugger jeans as well, and dark heels. You can't help wondering what Molly told her to get her over here dressed like this. And without her boyfriend, too.

Well, there's not really time to speculate. You return her greeting with a nod, then march up to her and in one motion grab her about the waist and push a blank mask to her face. She sags into your arm, taking you to the floor with her. "Oh, Jesus. Guys, help me get her up," you call as Molly and Ysleta look on with stunned expressions.

When she's off the ground, you lift her by the shoulders and Ysleta takes her feet as Molly frantically and uselessly guides you down a short hall to a dingy bedroom, where you toss her onto an unmade bed. Molly mutters "Oh Jesus, Jesus, Jesus" and covers her mouth with her hands as you unceremoniously pull off Catherine's shoes, her jeans, and her panties. Ysleta recovers from his paralysis in time to tug off her top.

You've seen Catherine out jogging, so the sight of her strong, smooth thighs and calves is no surprise. Nor is her stomach, which is trim but soft with a padding of baby fat. You can't help staring at her breasts, though, which have the largest and rosiest nipples you've ever seen. It's all you can do to keep from cupping one and sucking on it right then and there.

"Here, I need you to put this on," you tell Ysleta, and hand him the other blank mask. (You should have done that while you were waiting for Catherine.) You press up close on him when he hesitates. "Come on, man. This is what you asked for." He takes a huge breath, sinks to the carpet, and puts on the mask.

"Stop that," you tell Molly roughly as she starts whimpering. "There's nothing hinky going on here. The masks just knock people out while they're copying them."

"Okay. But what we're doing -- " She sniffles, and her eyes turn puffy.

"You asked for it. Didn't you say you wanted it? It'll be Kelsey laying there tomorrow. It'll be a mask copying Kelsey. And when it's done copying her, you'll put it on, and then you'll be Kelsey Blankenship. You understand that? You'll have her stuff. You'll have her clothes and her jewelry and her friends. Her BMW. You'll have everything she's got. It'll be yours." You hold her by the arm and shake her. "People will look at you and they'll see and hear Kelsey Blankenship. When you look in the mirror, you'll see -- "

"Okay, I get it," Molly shouts. Her expression has hardened, but something like horror still shows in it. "I just -- Seeing it, like this." She covers her mouth again.

"Hey." You pull her into a hug. "I want what's good for you, baby. This is good for you. That's why I thought of you. And we had some fun in our time, right?" You put your mouth to hers, and kiss her, though she doesn't kiss you back. "This is to pay you back for it. There's no fucking reason Kelsey the Cunt should have all her shit. She's a spoiled snot, and we're just balancing things out. Right?"

"No, I get it." She peers around your shoulder, and her eyes bulge a little. Then she buries her face in your chest. "I just want it to be over, so I don't have to -- "

You pull her close, and squeeze her until the masks have come out of Catherine and Ysleta both.

* * * * *

"So, are you gonna be able to get together with Kelsey?" you ask the girl. A 'bot that looks like Joe Ysleta has dressed and gone already, but Catherine Muskov is still turning this way and that, looking at herself long and steadily in the mirror. Her expression is grave, and she pulls her tousled hair back with one hand, and strokes the side of her jaw with a thumb. "Hey! Guy with the rack! I'm -- "

"I heard you. Jesus." Her voice is as throaty as it was when she came in. She catches your eye briefly in the mirror. "I don't know. I don't have any of her, like, memories and stuff." Her brow furrows.

"Yeah, it'll be awhile before that comes. What I mean is, even if she's not friends with Kelsey, have you got the balls to phone her, set up a meeting?"

"I haven't got any balls," she says. "But sure, I can do it." She drops her arms, and hugs herself with a groan. "I'll do anything you ask, man."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Mugging of Catherine Muskov

choices: Go see Johansson when this is done
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You are suddenly very nervous. Fucking Ysleta is acting like a drunken toddler with a new toy. Your gut tells you there's no trusting him with Catherine Muskov's body, not as a long-term play, and not as part of any plan of yours. You deeply regret letting Molly talk you into showing him the masks, and you're starting to really regret showing them to Molly herself.

But it's too late now. Damage control is the best you can do. "Just get dressed and have fun," you tell the new Catherine. "Help Molly out tomorrow. She's the one that got you this, remember." Catherine nods. "So tomorrow you set it up so that me and Molly can get Kelsey alone. That's all you gotta do for me."

"Are you sure?" There's a twinkle in her eye.

He's not serious. You're sure the fucker isn't serious, but you're sorely tempted by the body he's wearing, with its firm curves and soft padding. Catherine has a body you could work yourself down inside and rock yourself to sleep with. "Yeah," you say, and clear your throat of the frog that's crawled up inside it. "And don't do anything dumb until we can talk later." You allow yourself the briefest touch of her skin -- fingertips on her elbow -- as a farewell. To Molly you say, "You two try to figure out how you're going to handle things tomorrow."

Molly looks bewildered. "Is that all? Are you going?"

"You don't need me, and I have stuff I have to do to get ready for you tomorrow. So, you two just have a slumber party or some girl talk or whatever. You have my number? Text me if you have any problems, and when you know how you're going to handle tomorrow."

Your mind is a whirl of desire and second-guessing as you leave.

* * * * *

You're very late getting home, because you have to stop by the elementary school first to make and polish a mask. Kirkham's mom yells at you for skipping out on dinner, and she doesn't believe you when you say that you were working on a school project. At least Tad has the sense to keep his mouth shut as he watches and listens to the volley of angry words you and she exchange. It only worsens your mood, but you gradually calm yourself as you chisel way at the memory band for the new mask.

You also get a little perspective on your doubts about Ysleta. The problem, you decide after listening to your gut, is that the idiot probably thinks he's just having a lark -- that he's hijacking Catherine's body for a few days or maybe a week, and then he'll give it up. Unless you've seriously misjudged him, he's not really a transgender case, he's just a guy who likes to put on girl's clothes, in which case he'll soon get bored with putting Catherine's clothes onto Catherine's body. And what are you going to do when he tires of the charade?

If Ysleta is part of a group that likes to cross-dress, then maybe he can point you to someone who'd like to go farther, and to go farther permanently. But that would mean trusting Ysleta and trusting the new person, whoever he is. Or maybe you could give the mask to Johansson. He probably wouldn't mind having a Catherinebot under his control. (Which reminds you that you still have that fucker to deal with.)

Or maybe you can just worry about it when the time comes.

You try putting it out of your head before going to sleep, but it's like a rash and just keeps biting at you until you fall into an exhausted doze.

* * * * *

You don't have any classes with your two newest collaborators, but you keep an eye for them in the hallways. You spot them, but they don't see you; you also spot whatsisname, the junior beanpole you shoved Evans inside. He nods a quick acknowledgement at you, but scurries away. You hope things are okay there, and that's the reason you haven't heard from him. But you make a note to yourself to check up on him.

Among the usual pointless streams of texts that hit you during the day comes one from Seth Javits's number: u owe me something? And, a little later: hows cindy?

You ignore them until after seventh period, which is when you feel a gentle squeeze to your shoulder when you're at your locker. You turn. It's Catherine Muskov.

She has that same grave twinkle in her eye as you saw yesterday, and she runs a quick tongue over her lower lip as she smiles at you. "What are you doing this afternoon?" she asks.

"Whatever plans you got."

She nods, and glances over her shoulder as someone jostles her. "Kelsey's giving me a ride home. Would that work for you?"

"Is that all? Just a ride?"

"I told her something came up with Michael. We're leaving at five, when she's done with tennis and I'm done with track."

"I'll have to think about it. Text you after last period. How's your head?"

"It's all there." She smiles broadly, gives you another twinkle, and edges back into the crowd.

At least Ysleta sounds happy for the moment. But then, he's wearing a navy blue dress that hugs every curve, and is balancing himself expertly on some tall heels, so you guess he's got reason to be.

* * * * *

Johansson also lobbed a text at the Chenbot, asking about the mask he's owed, so after last period you trudge into the gym to watch the basketball team practice. There's always a small crowd there, and today is no exception.

The surprise to you is how many girls are attending. The Chelseabot is there, for instance, which is a change from when you were running Chelsea. You didn't attend the practices when you were inside her mask. Probably it's a new thing that Johansson is having her do, since he got her turned into an obedient robot. Anger builds in you again as you think of the way he and Prescott caught and imprisoned you.

She's surrounded by her cheerleader cronies, including the new members of the squad. Anger and regret burn harder in you now as you watch them. You were so looking forward to running those tryouts, the ones to replace Cindy and her friends after you kicked them off the squad. You were really salivating at the prospect of watching dozens of girls in their uniforms tumbling and twisting and showing off their bodies to you. But it was robo-Chelsea who made the calls. You don't even recognize most of the girls sitting with her now, except for Kendra Saunders and Maria Vasquez.

But there are lots of other girls too, which makes the bleachers a much more interesting spectacle than the court. You bemuse yourself with them and their breasts and their hair, for most of them are pretty good looking. Probably they're preening for the guys on the court, declaring themselves interested. Probably they're auditioning for Patterson. The smug asshole must be getting so much pussy since Gordon disgraced himself and he took over as team captain.

That's obviously why Johansson is now so frantic to get a mask of Patterson. He sees you from the floor of the gym, of course.

But you leave before practice is over. You send him a text first, though. making a mask ths aftnoon. ready by 9. You weren't in the gym to talk to him, only to kill time until the real business of the day. You'd set that business up with Catherine right after eighth. It was a text from her -- the first signal -- that takes you out of the gym.

Your heart is beating as you walk back into the main building -- now almost empty -- toward G wing, which is where the ambush is to take place. The timing is almost perfect. As you enter the sciences wing from one end, Kelsey and Catherine enter it from the other. Kelsey is talking in that loud, shrill voice of hers, and she pays no attention to you as you pass them going the other way. Both girls, coming in from exercise, are in shorts and skin-revealing tops, and they are flushed and sweaty -- delectable. You pause at a locker -- not your own -- and watch out of the corner of your eye as they disappear into the girls' restroom.

A minute later Molly comes into the wing, and hurries up to you. "Is it ready?"

"Just about. We just have to wait for -- " Your cell dings with a text from Catherine: ok. That means the two girls have it to themselves. "There it is."

You swing your pack off your shoulder, unzip it, take out a blank mask. A quick trot takes you to the door to the restroom and you bust in hard. Catherine and Kelsey are at the sinks, and both swing around. Your victim hasn't time to react, and you don't even have to shout a signal, before Catherine throws her arms around Kelsey and holds her tight. In three quick bounds you're at her and smothering her belated shriek with the mask. You grab onto both girls and help Ysleta lower Kelsey to the floor.

"Okay, you and Molly -- " You look over your shoulder. Molly is in the doorway, staring down at Kelsey with bulging eyes. "You two stand outside and make sure no one comes in. Tell 'em some girl's in here puking her guts up. Or she's having her period. Anorexia and period at the same time, I don't -- "

"Don't be gross, man," Catherine says as she leaves. She has to pull Molly -- who is still staring at Kelsey with fascination -- after her.

You straighten out Kelsey's limp form and pull the rest of your supplies out. You met up with Molly before heading to the gym, and you already have her imprinted in the other mask.

While you're waiting for the mask to finish copying Kelsey, you get a reply from Johansson. meet asap, help w mask.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: A Passing Pageantry

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You take one, two, four, seven, nine deep breaths before you master your temper. You are so tired of Johansson and his fucking interference. You would really like to settle his shit for once and all.

But you haven't got the tools to do that. You need to get into the grimoire with Blackwell's brain band before you can do that.

And before you can turn your attention to that, you have to get your new recruits in place.

So you send Johansson a short but placating message: cant, busy, working at home, call me at 9. Your one concession to fury is the angry stab you give the screen as you send the reply. Then you wipe your forehead with another sigh and turn to prepping Kelsey.

* * * * *

The girl is a fine specimen all the way around. To start with the invisible, there is her brain -- she's an AP student for a reason -- and her wealth. Her family owns Blankenship Motors, one of the largest car dealerships in the city, and she's reputed to live in an amazing house. She drives a fucking BMW and dresses in a stylishly bohemian way: lots of jeans skirts and denim tops when it isn't silk blouses or cotton shirts printed with Native American designs. In fact, the Southwest is a recurrent motif in her look, and she sports lots of silver and turquoise bracelets, necklaces, pendants and earrings, and is constantly prattling about the art that her family collects.

Physically she isn't quite the knockout that she wants to be or pretends to be. She is in excellent shape, as she should be for someone who was on the cheerleading squad until the start of the year. Her arms and legs are lean but strong and are still brown from the summer. Her face is similarly lean. Her nose and her chin are a little too pointed for your taste, and in those resentful moments when you're hating her for her snobbery -- which is quite often -- you imagine that when she's old she'll acquire the conventional witch's face, with the nutcracker nose and chin. But at her present age it just gives her a bold appearance that she softens with long, straight brown hair that she controls with an array of pins, clasps and ties so that it falls about her ears in swooping lines.

You start by stripping Kelsey of her jewelry -- two stud-like earrings, half a dozen bracelets, a ring on one hand -- and tennis shoes, then pull off the rest of her tennis clothes. Kelsey's breasts are disappointingly small, but she's toned all the way around. You pull her into one of the stalls so she'll be out of sight. You end by tidying up Kelsey's things, and that keeps you occupied until the mask finally finishes copying her. After that, it's just a minute of sealing up her mask, and then you gesture the two girls in.

Catherine is very calm, but Molly is vibrating, and she looks like she might throw up. "Go sit in that stall," you tell her, pointing to the one next to where Kelsey is lying. "Take your clothes off and push 'em into the next stall. Sit on the floor, then put this on." You hold out the mask.

Molly reaches out to take it, then draws her hands back a moment before darting out and snatching it. She turns it over, and her breath comes whistling out her nose as she reads Kelsey's name on its inner surface. She murmurs something, and with a kind of hopping walk goes into the empty stall. You manage not to roll your eyes.

"We'll let her get started," you tell Catherine quietly, "then you can help me finish up. Tell me about how you're doing."

"I'm doing great," she says with that wide, quiet smile. "It was an awesome day." She runs her palms down her sides. She's in a sports bra and thin, baggy running shorts. "I got up early to get all made up and dressed -- "

"And the memories are okay? No one caught you making any mistakes?"

"I got home later yesterday than Catherine was supposed to get home," she says. "I didn't know about that. But I wasn't in bad trouble. And by the time I woke up -- "

"Everything okay with your friends?"

"Sure." She's starting to look puzzled. "I had to push Michael off me a couple of times. Even with, you know -- " She rubs her temple with the heel of her hand. "I'm not into him. Not that way. Why are you asking?"

"Just making sure things are okay with you. How long do you want to keep this up?"

"I don't know. I haven't thought about it. Why?" Now puzzlement is shading into suspicion.

You glance at Molly's stall, which is very quiet. "Think about how long this is going to go for you," you tell her in a very low voice. "Because Molly in there wants you to be a friend with her, and she's planning on making the Kelsey thing permanent, probably."

Catherine's eyes widen, and she loses a tiny bit of her color as she looks over at the stall. "There might be a way of handling it," you continue, "if you decide you're getting bored with being Catherine. But try not to get bored. Hopefully it won't be a problem, because" -- You jab her in the breastbone -- "Catherine's about to become Kelsey's best friend."

She nods gravely, as though the import of what is happening has just sunk in.

"Okay, go into Kelsey's stall and put this on her face." You hand Ysleta the mask of Molly. "It's gonna turn her into Molly. Tell her to get dressed while I take care of your friend."

You push inside the stall where Molly was huddling. But it's not Molly Shaw anymore. It's Kelsey Blankenship. Her mouth is hanging open and she's drooling.

* * * * *

They threw you out after Molly was awake again. That was okay, you'd gotten enough of Kelsey's naked body. And you'd taken care of the Mollybot. You didn't have time to make up any of that robopaste using Molly's hair, so you used some of Ysleta's paste instead. It didn't matter. You don't think you're going to be using the Mollybot for anything, and if you do need it for something, you can probably get Ysleta to give the orders for you.

That's how you handled the Mollybot in the restroom. He told it to get dressed while you hovered over Molly and pretended to work, and when Catherine shouted "What now?" you said, "Tell it to go home and act natural. You know. Tell it to act just like Molly would, and not to tell anyone about this."

"Can't you tell her?"

"You got to look her in the face and say it, otherwise it doesn't work," you lied. So you gave Ysleta the words and he passed them on to the 'bot. And he didn't question you about it.

Now you're prowling about outside the restroom, up and down G wing. Mr. Gelding does a double take when he comes out and locks his classroom door. He demands to know what you're doing, and when you tell him you're waiting for some girls to finish in the restroom, he pushes open the door and yells inside for them to get done and get out. You're grateful for that.

Ysleta has changed into a fresh pair of shorts and a t-shirt, and has resettled her hair in a tall thing that's halfway between a bun and a ponytail. Molly is much more disheveled and panicked looking, and looks ill at ease in Kelsey's clothes. Catherine puts an arm around her waist. "We'll go to my place," she says in a low voice. "Thanks for waiting for us, David," she says to you. You fall in next to them.

"I don't think I can do this," Kelsey moans.

"Sure you can," you say. "You get through the night, and when you wake up tomorrow you'll have her memories and everything."

"But until then! I don't know what to do!"

"Jesus. Go with Catherine. Use your phone to figure out where you live. Then read your texts. All the ones that need answers, tell them that you're suddenly sick and can't talk or see them. Text Kelsey's mom, tell you won't be home until late. Wait until the last minute to go home, then go home and go straight to bed. You'll be fine."

But she keeps whimpering all the way out to the parking lot. You're filled with disgust long before she reaches her car, and even there she shrinks back from the BMW and moans that she'll get into a wreck with it.

* * * * *

So when your cell chimes fifteen minutes later, as you're braking for a yellow light, and you glance over to see that it's Catherine calling, you almost ignore it. But it's a notoriously long light you'll have to wait for, and it's a phone call, not a text, so you pick it up. "Yeah?" you wearily demand.

It's Kelsey's voice on the other end: "I've got a huge problem, David. Kelsey's mom! I found the texts from her, and I'm supposed to go out to some university thing with them! So I can't stay with Catherine, and I don't know what to do if I go out there and -- !"

She's on the verge of hysterics. "Hey! Hey! Shut up!" You wish you could reach across the ether and slap her. "Give me a minute to think."

Voices murmur, and Catherine comes on. "I can go with her, David," she says with blessed serenity. "I can help her out."

That would be good. You could leave it to Ysleta to help Molly through the evening while you take care of that mask for Johansson. On the other hand, you could temporarily take over for Molly. Prescott's body has the weird ability to adapt fast to masks. You could spend the evening as Kelsey Blankenship.

Then your cell dings, and you tell Catherine to hold on a minute. It's a text, and you almost drop it to see it's from Lucy Vredenburg. wheres blackwell? emergency.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Kelsey-Related Conniptions

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Who's fucking Craig Wyatt?" you ask in answer to the suggestion. You're at home, on the phone, talking to Douglas Canfield. It's George Mendoza answering from under the mask, of course. You called to ask him who he knew down in the junior class that would be a good disguise to help expand the network in the eleventh grade.

"Who is he, or who's he fucking?" Canfield replies. "No one, I think, except he's dating Bailey Keane, but she's not the type -- "

"No, who is this asshole?"

"He's Craig Wyatt. He's in the band. Plays clarinet."

You try to put a face to the description. Something very hazy comes. A tall blonde kid, maybe? "Why him? Is he a stoner? Is he popular?"

"Well, he's got a lot of friends, but he's not one of those Mr. Popularity fags. He's in a couple of AP classes, he's in band, he shows up at all the parties -- "

"How's this help us move product?"

"The same way Andrea does. He's this clean cut kid, but it's not like he's a fucking Boy Scout or choir boy about it. That's what makes him perfect." Mendoza is clearly warming to his idea. "This guy, he belongs to all the right clubs, you know, and he's got all the right shit about him -- "

"Are you gay for him?"

"Fuck you, no. What I mean is, it's like he's programmed to only do stuff that's respectable. So what if he starts smoking a little weed, you know, just totally casually like there's nothing wrong with it? Everyone's gonna think, 'Hey, Craig's into it, it must be okay, I'll do some too.' He's totally average, man, he sets a tone by being average and boring and safe."

This doesn't sound obviously right. "I'll think about it," you tell Mendoza. "You think some more about it too, come up with a coupla more candidates. I need a place to put Evans."

That done, you return to working on a brain band to go with one of the new masks that Chen dropped off a little while ago.

Four masks. You've got four masks either completed or in various stages of completion. One of them you're holding back against the time that fucking Johansson decides that he has to get himself a copy of Steve Patterson. One you'll use on Kelsey. One you'll use for Evan's new location. And the last?

You still think that Justin Roth would be a good recruit, but you're less and less enamored of that part of your program. You've already got things set up to move huge volumes through Westside and Eastman and at the Warehouse. You're gearing up a network in the local junior class. Really, instead of nailing down every angle in the Westside senior class, you ought to be pushing into the junior class at Eastside and finding new customers at the university, which is still where you sell most of your product.

Trouble is, you haven't got any agents that you can put in those places.

You sigh. No, what you really need to do is to get back inside the head of Aubrey Blackwell and start figuring out the grimoire. The deeper you get into it, the more toys you've discovered inside it. The long-term answers are inside it.

But that's a job for tomorrow, when you can go hide somewhere after taking Kirkham's mask off.

* * * * *

You need another hiding place.

That's what you decide as you're driving around Sunday afternoon. You're not thinking of the loathsome fact that you're stuck inside Will Prescott's body and having to pretend to be your best friend David Kirkham, though that's connected to it. What you need is someplace that isn't that elementary school basement and that isn't Aubrey Blackwell's old house. You're gonna get in caught eventually if you keep going out to the basement, and you really didn't like the feel of Blackwell's villa. But you need a private place where you can take off Kirkham's mask without the risk of being recognized as "Will Prescott." And right now the basement and the villa are the only two places you can do that.

You started off at the basement, which is where you'd hauled the key texts from Blackwell's library. For two hours you hunched at an old desk, poring through half a dozen of his books with Kirkham's mask at your elbow and Blackwell's brain band inside your head. Then you thought of another book that you should probably consult, and changed back into the form of David Kirkham to drive out to the villa to get it. And that's when it really sank in that you needed a new place to hang out in.

But there are no such places. No place that's big and secure with no danger of being interrupted by anyone who shouldn't see the fuckface you're currently forced to inhabit. Blackwell's is the only place that comes close to fitting that bill, but since you got rid of its owner, it's not a long-term secure place.

It's not a short-term secure place either, as you discover after you arrive.

You first sense that something has gone wrong when you walk into the foyer and are immediately assaulted by a sense of desolation. There's a hollow quality to the silence -- the silence of a house that has been abandoned. A house that is now a shell and not a home.

The second shock comes when you step into the library. You remember well the oppressive sense of malign watchfulness that fell over you the first time you stepped into it -- the almost jarring sense of stepping into room that was out of alignment with the rest of reality. But now it just feels like an ordinary room. A big room, a room of dull echoes, an empty room --

You whirl. Empty. The shelves are all empty!

You turn and turn and turn again. Every shelf, every book case stands bare. Those grandfather clocks, the ones with the discordant ticks and tocks, are also missing. Your eye travels over the walls. The alcoves are also empty. The cyclops-monkey with the vicious fangs -- the house's guardian -- has also gone.

With mounting chills you return to the body of the house and make a fast search up and down. The furniture is all in place -- sofas and electronics in the living room; chairs and table in the dining room; beds and dressers in the bedrooms. But Blackwell's clothes have all gone, along with any personal effects that you didn't carry off. The kitchen is still stocked with food, but the magic supplies are gone from the pantry. The upstairs workshop has been stripped.

In short, everything magical and occult, and everything that could be connected to Blackwell's own person, has gone missing.

You're in a numb haze as you descend the staircase. You can think of only one person who might know what's going on. You call Lucy Vredenburg.

"Hey, I'm out at Professor Blackwell's. What's going on?"

"I give up," she says coolly. "What's going on?"

"I mean, have you been moving stuff out? Like, stuff from his library?"

"No. Have you asked the professor what's going on?"

"I haven't seen him." You're at the front door, heading outside to glance in the mausoleum where you left his remains. "So you haven't been moving stuff out, or he hasn't asked you to move stuff out?" You're really hoping the answer to that last question is "No."

"Of course not. I haven't seen or heard from him since you gave me that letter."

"You haven't been out to his place?"

"Who put you in charge all of a sudden?"

I did, you want to snap back at her. Day before yesterday, when I took out your fucking warlock like a boss. "I came out here to see if I could talk to him, and the place is cleaned out. Not totally, but of most of the stuff." You freeze in front of the mausoleum, for the door is ajar. "I haven't heard from him. Things are fucking weird out here, and I wanted to know what you know."

"I don't know anything," she says in a voice that has grown increasingly waspish. "If I do hear something, maybe I'll let you know." She hangs up.

You drop the phone into your pocket, and with your heart in your throat you push open the door to the mausoleum.

It's empty. Of anything. The bier where you left the stone-like remains of Aubrey Blackwell, black magician, is bare.

Oh Jesus. Tell me he didn't get up and is coming after me!

You bolt from the grounds.

* * * * *

You're halfway back home -- like it would do you any good to hide under your bed -- when it comes to you that there's one more place to check. You detour sharply to the elementary school, where you retrieve the keys you took with you when you were moving things out of his house. They are house keys, car keys -- but his car was still in front of his house when you stopped there -- and the keys to his university office. You drive up to Keyserling.

It's Sunday, but the outside doors to the university's Anthropology building are unlocked, and you pass a couple of professors and graduate students as you prowl down the hall. You glance both ways when you come to Blackwell's door. The coast is clear, so you slip the lock open and dart inside. You close it, lock it, and look around.

A desk, a chair, a file cabinet, and four empty bookcases.

This is very odd. They were anthropology books only, nothing occult. But someone has swept them up just as they swept his house clear. The desk and file cabinets have also been cleaned out.

A suspicion gradually takes hold of you. Was the Lucybot being honest with you? Worse: Was she really the golem Blackwell left behind, or was she someone else --something else, a lich-Blackwell -- under the mask?
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Vanishing Assets

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"Fucker." You grab the phone away from Chen and are about to call Evans back when you stop. "What exactly did he say?"

"That he's not gonna be a girl," Chen tells you. "He said he didn't care what I did to him, he wasn't going to do it."

But you bet that between Gary Chen and David Kirkham you've got the persuasive muscle to convince Tanner Evans that he'd love to be Kelsey Blankenship.

"Here." You hand Chen the mask you just finished casting. "Polish this up while I go straighten out his shit."

* * * * *

"I didn't know you were part of this," Chris Trantham -- really Tanner Evans -- says. He's looking fatter and pastier than the last time you saw him, and the weedy blonde moustache on his upper lip looks like a hairy caterpillar that's dying of mange.

"That's right, you fucking puke," you retort. "Me and Chen are partners. Two heads, one brain. So what he tells you, I'm telling you too, and vice versa. So what's this fucking backtalk you're giving him about your new gig?"

"I'm not playing a girl," he replies with quiet truculence. "You're not giving me a pussy."

"Like you don't already got one. You'll do as you're fucking told, Evans." You grab him by the hair, and with a knee to the groin force him to kneel. "If I tell you to put on Kelsey's Blankenship's face and blow me, you do it. If I tell you to blow my man Chen, you'll do it. I tell you to blow fucking Walberg, you'll do it? You got it?" A bolus of fury nearly chokes you, and you knee him in the chest. "You got it?"

Trantham gasps and snuffles. "Please," he whispers.

You throw him back. "You're gonna do it, aren't you? Aren't you?"

"Why? Can't you give me someone else?"

"There is no one else! Not for you! The whole fucking point of this organization is that you do what you're told! And what's wrong with Kelsey? Your fucking brother Thomason's living the life of Andrea Varnsworth. You laughing at him? Are you laughing at him 'cos he's got himself a pussy to play with?"

"No," Trantham whispers. "I just don't want -- "

"You know it could be a lot worse, right? Me and Chen, we're actually taking care of you assholes. We're setting up a fucking beauty pageant for you! Andrea and Kelsey. Don't you want to be fucking rich? Fuck me, I'd squeeze myself up Kelsey's tailpipe if it meant getting to drive around in her BMW."

"So why don't you -- ?"

You put your foot on the side of his head. "What were you about to ask?"

"Nothing."

"That's right. You were about to do what I tell you. But I'll give you a choice instead. You know Keith Tilley, you know which ugly-ass loser he is?"

Trantham thinks a moment, then nods.

"You can be Tilley or you can be Kelsey. Which one do you want?"

The face of Keith Tilley -- Prescott's other loser friend -- would do you no good in your plans. Fuck, if people heard that he'd taken up with the ganja it would kill the market. But it delights you to think of doing something to Prescott's friend, and it's a good threat to hang over Trantham.

Trantham swallows a couple of times, and mumbles. "What's that?" you demand.

"I'll take Kelsey."

"That's right. You're not so dumb. Ugly as fuck, but only half as dumb." You lift your foot. "We're gonna put you in for Kelsey, and you're gonna queen it up. She's got half the school kissing her ass, which means they're gonna be kissing your ass, and why are you crying about that? Come on, get up." The way he's wallowing on the ground fills you with contempt.

"Now, here's what we're gonna do," you tell him. "You're gonna meet me up at the school, in the student parking lot in an hour. I'm gonna be waiting for you and I'm gonna have your old car and some of your old clothes there. We're gonna get you outta this mask of Trantham, and you're gonna put on your old clothes and get in your old car, and you're gonna drive to -- " You think a bit. "Out to the Starbucks on Orlando. You're gonna wait there with your phone on for me to call you again. I'm gonna give you directions, and you're gonna meet me. And when we part company again, you're gonna drive off in Kelsey Blankenship's car, because you'll be carrying her driver's license inside her purse as part of your ensemble because you're going to be looking just like her. And you're going to have fucking fun doing it, asshole." You jab him in the shoulder with a hard finger. "Because anyone who doesn't know how to have fun with that body and that money is a fucking loser. Are you a fucking loser?"

He actually hesitates before shaking his head. You snort. "Meet me at the school in an hour."

* * * * *

You're able to arrange all this because you gave it some good hard thought on your way out to meet Evans. It's going to be so easy it's embarrassing.

You start by phoning the Evansbot and telling it to meet you at Westside. It arrives, and you accompany it back to the portables. The walk -- a silent one -- gives you some pause. Evans was pretty goddamn easy to push around, and you wonder if it isn't because Chris Trantham got too far into his head. This creature walking next to you -- Tanner Evans's doppelganger -- is much more erect and confident, with a "fuck you" look in his eye, and the swagger that the neutered Will Prescott you've got in your head remembers of a terrible bully. Certainly neither you nor Kirkham nor any of your friends would have given Evans any respect if he really was the marshmallow that you beat on mercilessly at the water towers not an hour ago.

So if he got so deep inside Chris Trantham that he turned into a wuss, what will happen when he gets a good whiff of that arrogant cunt Kelsey Blankenship up his nose?

Well, you'll worry about that later. Right now you're at the portables -- deserted on this Saturday afternoon -- and you lead the Evansbot into one. You order it to take its clothes off. With only the briefest glower, it complies, and you take the gear back out to the parking lot, where you suck on a toothpick meditatively until Trantham drives up.
