You order Evans into his old car and have him disrobe. "When you wake up, get dressed and drive out to Starbucks. You might be there awhile," you warn him. "Nurse your fucking macchiato." You rip the mask from him, shove it in your backpack, and leave him there to wake as you return to the portables. Back there, you order the Evansbot into Trantham's clothes, tell it to lay down, and remove the mask. Chris Trantham -- the original -- appears. You tiptoe out, then hustle at a hard run back to the parking lot. Evans is still passed out, but he wakes with a start when you lean in and honk his horn. "Get out of here," you tell him, and hop into your car.

All that was tedious enough, but then comes the trip back to the elementary school basement, where the Chenbot is dutifully waiting. You tell him briefly of what you're going to do -- which leaves him chortling -- while swapping out materials from your bag, taking the mask for the Evansbot, the brain band of Professor Aubrey Blackwell, and the professor's house keys with you.

Then it's off to the professor's villa.

* * * * *

The plan is simplicity itself, assuming that Kelsey is as conceited as everyone assumes she is. From the professor's house will telephone her, pretending to be a professor who has heard rumors of her academic prowess and has the intense desire to see and talk with this most academically gifted girl. You will sprinkle hints of scholarships to attend Keyserling College and stories of college classes she can take in the spring for both university and high school credit. You will explain that she only has to stop by your -- the professor's -- house to discuss this, and that she should hurry over for a light tea and a short interview.

How will she be able to resist?

But though you have the professor's house keys, you park in the back behind the villa -- in case Kelsey should happen to recognize your car -- and enter it through the tunnel, the same way you did yesterday when you ambushed the professor.

You're in the hallway when you stop short at the strange feeling in the air. At first you assume it must the gwarcheidwad, but it takes only a moment to realize it feels nothing like that monster. In fact, it's the opposite feeling you're getting: a sense of abandonment and desolation.

Your frown of puzzlement turns into a gasp of shock when you step into the library. Almost all the books are off the shelves and stacked onto the desks next to half-filled book boxes. You look around, and after a shocked moment realize that the two grandfather clocks -- whose discordant ticking drove you nuts yesterday -- have gone. So have all the stuffed animals from their alcoves.

Including the gwarcheidwad.

No wonder the house feels empty. It actually is.

You tiptoe around, exploring. The furniture is undisturbed, as is the kitchen, including its supply of knockout compounds. You arm yourself with some of these before heading upstairs to look around. There is nothing amiss in the bedrooms, but the workshop has been emptied of its tools.

Is it the Lucybot? Has it been up to some kind of mischief? It's not under your control any more, and it probably knows everything the professor did.

You're at the top of stairs, about to come down, when you hear the front door open. A voice shouts inarticulately from the foyer.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Tanning Evans' Hide

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You put your hand in your pocket, and your fingers touch the brain band. But you hesitate to strike. Those bands are tedious to make, and you'd prefer not to waste one.

In the moments that you hesitate, the professor turns on the lights and moves behind his desk -- and then it's too late for you to strike. So you resign yourself to questioning him before making any move against him.

But he has the first question for you. "And what can I do for you, young man?" he asks as he sits behind his desk with a groaning sigh. That oily insincerity he used on you in the halls has evaporated, replaced an almost equally odious affability. There are spiders inside his smile.

"It's this book I found in the used book store. Arnholm's." You stammer a little as you set the book on the desk in front of him, and you really wish now you'd used that brain band on him when you had the chance. "I was wondering what kind of book it is and what the symbols inside it mean."

You're still talking even as he opens the book. He bends over to peer at the end pieces, then turns to the title page, then rapidly flips through more pages. You jump forward to lay your finger across one. "Those things especially," you say, pointing to a sigil. "What are -- ?"

"Tch tch tch tch tch," he says, just like Dr. Steed had. Firmly he pushes you back. "You must give me a chance to examine the entire thing." He shoots a beady, hungry glance up at you, then bends back over the book. He flips a few more pages, then pauses. He gives you another brief glance, and turns back to the title page.

"Well," he says slowly without raising his eyes. "Let us start with the title." He leans his elbow on the desk and rests his head on his hand; his other hand he waves lazily about in the air, his fingers fidgeting. "It is called the 'Libra personae'," he tells you, "which may be translated in a number of ways. As the Book of Persons, or the Book of Personaa, or the Book of Characters, or the Book of Faces. The Latin word 'personae' is much more difficult to translate than one might think." His fingers twine and waggle in a way that draws your attention. "It is best expressed in the phrase 'Dramatis Personae', which one sometimes finds at the front of playbills. The cast of characters in the story. The characters are, of course, fictional -- persons constructed for the purpose of the play. But that's rather the sense in which 'personae' is used in Latin. Each of us, you know -- all of us, in fact -- go through life as constructed personae -- characters that we play."

His fingers seem to weave patterns in the air. There's a waviness, a haziness about them, that fascinates you. You can't drag your eyes from them.

Then you forget that you ever wanted to look at anything else, ever, in the history of everything.

"Yes, we play different characters in our lives. The son. The brother. The father. The faithful employee. The generous employer. The helpful neighbor. The ardent lover." His voice becomes distant and indistinct. You can still hear his words, though. "Those who know us under one persona may be surprised to see us when we are operating under a different persona. They may not be able to reconcile that both personas belong to us. They are like masks that we take off and put on as suits our needs and interests."

And now, beneath and inside his words -- like the aural equivalent of an optical illusion -- you can hear other words. They seem to fit inside his droning and meaningless talk about masks and personas, but they are more forceful and direct, and they land like blows inside your head even as your mind is dulled into sleepy docility by his other talk: "Listen to me. Listen to me. Bend to me. Bend to me. Obey me. Obey me."

Then even these words become indistinct, and you can only feel them as pounding blows inside your skull, mashing your brain into mush and reshaping it. You feel them as fingers inside your mind, and then inside your body, inserting themselves into your limbs.

So when he stands, you stand; and when he puts his arm in yours and draws into the hall, you follow on numb feet. Still his words run through you, a murmuring brook that carries you down the hallway and the stairwell, out into the parking lot and into a sedan. His voice rocks and lulls you on a drive to the other side of town, then out of the car and up a walk and through the door of a large house. He guides you through shadowy rooms and through a door and down a spiral staircase. Only after you are seated on a cot do his words cease.

For a moment you stare at him in a daze. You've just time to see him blow a powder into your face, and then you know no more.

* * * * *

You are very stiff and cold when you wake. You sit up and rub your face.

That's when you notice that your glasses are gone.

You blink and look around. Your eyesight is more or less perfect, and when you feel the whiskers on your chin and glance down, you confirm the loathsome suspicion. The mask of Kirkham that you'd been wearing has been removed, returning you to Prescott's body. Your clothes are gone too, and you snarl at your nakedness.

Fuck. You really are in the shit now. That professor you found knows all about fucking magic, apparently. Enough to recognize the grimoire as something worth stealing, and enough that he was able to put some kind of a spell on you. A hypnotic one, at least.

And where are you now?

You're a in a very narrow room with a very high ceiling. The walls are of brick, and the floor -- which is stained a variety of nasty colors -- is concrete. There is one window, high up, in the wall opposite you, and it's the only source of light. You are sitting on a cot. When you move your foot it hits something. Christ, worse and worse. It's a porcelain chamber pot.

Well, clothes or no clothes, you've got to get out of here. Luckily, there seems to be an exit. A wrought-iron spiral staircase leads up to a tiny landing. Grimly you ignore the chill of concrete and of metal on the soles of your bare feet, and ascend.

You don't get far, though. About halfway up the air changes, becoming cold and foul, and a pit forms in your stomach. You pause to gather your strength, and take another step. The chill increases, and that pit turns into a fist pressing into your gut. One more step, and you falter, sinking to one knee with your head bowed.

The cold is bad enough -- a polluted, nauseating chill that sucks all the strength from your joints and limbs and fills them with poison instead. But it's the sickness that gets you. It's a sickness like you recognize from several memories, those of yourself and those of Prescott. It's a fist shoved up inside your stomach and held there. Bullies used to do that to you -- to both of your remembered personalities -- holding their closed fists inside your solar plexus until you felt the shuddering need to vomit. It's like this now. Something cold and powerful is pushing you back.

You raise your head, and almost faint as a wash of pain and intense dizziness floods your head. The thing on the next step, the one just above the rung you're standing on. You can't see it, and you can see no mark on the stair. But you know it's there. Or, if it's not a thing sitting there, it's the rung itself. Nothing -- no threat and no reward, no promise from heaven or terror from hell -- could get you to put your foot on it.

Your knees buckle and you slide back down the staircase, banging your shins and knees and nearly cracking your skull on the railing. The weakness and sickness, though, rapidly subside once you're at the bottom, and when you stand again a minute later, it's as though you had suffered no attack.

Still, you are not so foolhardy that you'll try mounting the stairs again.

The landing above is out of reach, even after you drag the cot over and try jumping from it to grasp it with your fingers. That leaves the window as the only way out.

Its lower sill is almost even with the bottom of the landing. But you discover that the brick wall beneath it is a lot rougher than the wall by the landing, such that when you lean the cot against it the legs rest fairly firmly against it. This gives you a makeshift ladder that lets you scramble up and onto the sill, where you are barely able to hang on with your toes and fingers.

But it's locked. The only way through it is to break the glass and to try to crawl through. That sounds really dangerous. You've fantasized a lot about hurting Prescott's body, but as you're trapped in it for the present you're really not keen on slicing it to ribbons.

On the other hand, who knows what horrors the man upstairs is preparing for you.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Another Body for Someone Else's Collection

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
You study the window carefully. The panes are very large, certainly large enough that you could get through them if there were no glass. As for dealing with the glass --

You come real close to losing your patience and busting through it with your fist, but you keep your cool. You leap off the ledge and look around for a tool -- any tool -- to use. Your eye lights on the chamber pot.

It's tricky, clambering back up the cot to the ledge with one hand because you're carrying the pot in the other. But you make it up. With gritted teeth, you strike the glass a hard blow, and it shatters outward. So far so good. Using the pot you clean out as much of the glass as you can from the frame, smoothing it down so that even if you accidentally brushed it on your way out, you probably would only nick yourself.

But that leaves all the glass on the ground right outside the window. Stepping on that would be a bad idea. You get another idea, though, and congratulate yourself on its cleverness. You place the pot on the ground atop the glass, open-side up, and put one foot in it. Then, very carefully, you crouch and pull yourself through the window. You brush the top of the pane with your back, but feel no sharp cuts or scrapes. On the other side you straighten up, bracing yourself on the window as you balance on the foot you've got stuck in the pot. You stretch the other of Prescott's long legs, planting a foot far from the glass, and step onto it. You clear your other foot of the pot --

--and then you're out.

But you're not safe yet. You glance around.

It's a big house, made of white-washed stone, rising at least two stories above you. To your left is a patio; to your right, the continuation of the house. Some yards away is a wall that looks too high to leap over. So you make your way around the house away from the patio. The ground is completely bare, composed of dirt and rock, and the stones bite your bare feet as you run. Though there is a watery warmth in the sunlight, the air is very cool, making you shiver.

You choice takes you the long way around the house, for you have to turn three corners before you spy a gate in the wall. The yard all about the house seems dead, except for a single, rather forlorn tree growing up close to the side of the house. You sidle up the porch, making sure that the front door is closed, before turning back to the gate.

This is the grim part. You are naked, in a body that is the double of someone else out there in the world. You appear to be out in the countryside, for there are no other houses visible. Your best chance of safety would seem to lie in getting picked up by the police. But what would you say to explain yourself or your identity?

Still, you bet it's better than getting caught by that university professor.

Then you pause. Johansson. That's who you could contact. If you could get Johansson to drive out here, and if you could meet him before he hit the front door, then you could get away with him in his car. But how to get to a phone to call him? If the cops picked you up, don't you get a phone call? You think it's supposed to work that way. Otherwise, your only real option is to go back inside this house and find one.

You're still wavering over what to do when you hear a noise behind you. You whirl. The front door has opened, and you gasp at the sight.

It's Will Prescott. And before you can react, he squirts you in the face with the spray bottle he's holding.

* * * * *

Your mind is a sloshing ocean of memories. It heaves and rushes and slopes upward into towering mountains of heavy memories before crashing in on itself. The bowl of your skull shudders and vibrates.

But you've no memory of these things when you wake, only the bruises. All things considered, you are feeling remarkably calm and rested when you open your eyes, to glimpse a very familiar ceiling. You raise up on your elbows and look around.

You are in a bedroom decorated in crimsons and ebonies with a slightly Chinese flavor. You are resting on a bed and you are quite naked.

But you are not alone. The high school student David Kirkham is here, sucking on his toothpick and sneering maliciously at you.

And standing beside him, eyeing you intently, is yourself.

Well, it isn't really yourself. You know enough to realize that. But it's very hard not to think of him as yourself.

For just as you were convinced up until this afternoon that you were really Gary Chen, high school student and drug dealer, so you are now convinced that you are really Aubrey Blackwell, anthropology professor and warlock.

You can't help wincing as you look at yourself through the eyes and mind of Will Prescott. You are not so egotistical that you think yourself physically attractive, even at a minimum level. Still, it hurts to see a fat, bald man with greasy hair and a tangled moustache and goatee. Your belly strains against the pullover sweater, and your feet splay out awkwardly.

"You seem to be in a ruminative state," says your alter ego after he has waited for you a bit. "Have you something to say?"

"I've several things I should like to say," you retort. "But the phrase you're looking for, if I remember correctly, is -- " In perfect Greek you recite the first line from George Abney's book on sigils. "Now, if we have established my identity to your satisfaction -- "

"Jesus, he talks like a fruit," sneers Kirkham.

"That's quite enough from you," you and your other self snap in unison. "Yes, I suppose that demonstrates it to a redundancy," says your other self. He hands you a robe. "You understand what and where you are?"

"Perfectly," you say as you wrap the seventeen-year-old body of Will Prescott in a dressing gown. "I suppose that, technically, I ought to apologize for breaking the window downstairs and thereby necessitating this hazardous experiment. However, it would be an insincerity, as I feel no identification with the person who broke it. That, I would say, admirably demonstrates the success of the amphora anima that you prepared." You bow. "I am you, my dear Aubrey."

"As I am you," you politely returns with a bow of his own.

"Oh, Jesus," Kirkham groans, and rolls his eyes.

* * * * *

You are not, of course, Aubrey Blackwell. You are really Will Prescott. But you have a copy of Professor Blackwell's mind inside yours, guiding your thinking and your instincts. Prescott, the dear boy, had made great progress, but he fell into waters much too deep for himself. It's actually a mercy that he came and found you. He will lead a much longer and more productive life as a result.

He just won't be himself anymore.

It's all on account of that metal band he made the other night using the new sigil. He thought he was making a tabula mentalia, a thing like a book that allows its wearer to read the mind of another without feeling any force from it. But instead he fashioned an amphora anima, a thing that copies the actual will and mind of another person. With it, he foolishly filled himself with the anima of Gary Chen, giving himself the illusion that he actually was Gary Chen, and had somehow been swapped into the body of Will Prescott.

So Aubrey Blackwell had gleaned after maneuvering the luckless Prescott/Chen hybrid into the secret chamber of his villa. Blackwell knew all about the Libra personae, as he himself was its former owner before a careless work-study student accidentally sent it off to Arnholm's in a load of books. But in the short time Blackwell had possessed it, he had made a number of masks, and even a golem -- one of those stone 'bots -- and still had some on hand. He had tried to put one onto Prescott -- onto you, as you must remind yourself with a sigh -- so as to copy your mind and body for investigation. That is how he found you were already wearing a mask -- a mask of Kirkham. He had removed that mask, made one of you, donned it himself, used some advanced memory techniques to quicken his mastery of your memories, and thereby come to learn your history. A few shrewd deductions, corroborated by a cursory examination of the pertinent sigil in the grimoire, had told him of your accident with the anima band.

By chance he had happened to see you skulking around the front of the house. After recapturing you, he found how you escaped. And since he could find no other way to control you, he had hazarded the great risk: he had prepared an amphora anima of himself and placed it upon you.

So here you are inside Prescott. Or, rather, that is what you can't help thinking. In fact, here Blackwell is inside you.

* * * * *

"And what do your instincts suggest we do now?" asks your partner. As hard as it is to not think of yourself as "Blackwell," it takes a wrench of willpower to think of him under that name.

"Well, there's his friend, Mr. Johansson," you reflect aloud. "It would be well to neutralize him. Many of his classmates should be returned to normality, as they are no use to anyone in their present guises."

"What of this one, and his drug-dealing friend?" Blackwell asks, indicating Kirkham.

You're not sure. You have little interest in them or their enterprise. But they are available as receptacles. There is another and more pleasant receptacle, however. Lucy Vredenburg, an unlucky college girl, is also available for inhabiting.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Another Change of Mind

choices: Find Blackwell a place in the high school
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"I think it would be best if we took this one in hand," you tell Blackwell, pointing to Kirkham. "I suspect there will be many opportunities to conduct experiments in the future, and their delinquent companions will make convenient subjects. I will resume the role of this one myself," you continue after a pause. "With Mr. Prescott's essentia and his memories I can manage things until we have a clearer idea of how we want to proceed."

"Very good. I will accompany you," says Blackwell.

"Accompany me?" you ask, puzzled. "Where?"

"Out the door. As Miss Lucy. It's All Hallows' Eve," he reminds you, "which is a convenient excuse for spending the night elsewhere."

Ah. Of course. The gwarcheidwad that guards the house will be at its most potent and active tonight. And though you trust the seals that keep it out of the bedrooms, it will likely be a very unpleasant -- at the very least a very noisy -- night.

Blackwell orders David Kirkham to disrobe and then to lie upon a desk, and removes the mask from it, revealing the golem that he had made when he still had possession of the Libra. It was the last spell he studied and mastered. He then goes upstairs to change himself as you sit in a chair and return Kirkham's mask to your face.

* * * * *

It is most disorienting to wake with a third mind inside yours. They are all very discordant.

There is, first of all, the mind of Will Prescott. It is actually your native mind, but it feels like an assumed personality -- an active but docile thing, easily cowed and subdued. Then there is mind of David Kirkham, which is a very different personality, but one that is also assumed though harder to control because of its native truculence. And over the other two is the mind of Aubrey Blackwell -- an invasive mind, but one that you cannot help but treat as your own.

As you dress in the wardrobe of David Kirkham, you reflect on the war within you, and how different it felt from when it was the mind of Gary Chen inside you in place of Blackwell's. That felt more like a single personality, probably on account of the way Chen had Prescott so cowed, and because his personality and Kirkham's meshed so well. But now, as you put Kirkham's glasses on and remind yourself to slide a toothpick between cheek and gum, you are going to have to figure out a new balance.

You're pulling together some books to take with you when another person comes into the library. It ought to be Aubrey Blackwell, and it actually is, but he's in a different guise. The girl has wide hips, large bosoms, and a pronounced hourglass curve to connect them; strong legs; long, platinum-colored hair; and a face colored like strawberries and cream. Her name is Lucy Vredenburg, and before she became a mask for the professor to wear she was a college student and cheerleader, and the older sister of the high school cheerleader Cindy Vredenburg.

Three different saps rocket upward inside you as she enters. All three personalities you carry inside you want to grab her, hold her, and molest her. You have to force your throat to relax when she asks you, "Are you ready?"

"I think so." You lift the books. "You gonna leave anyone in residence?"

Her expression turns quizzical and amused. "You've gotten into character quickly, haven't you?"

It takes you a moment to recognize her meaning, and to realize she is right. Your instinct was to reply in the manner and cadences of the mask you're wearing. That's something of a relief. You remember the Kirkham-golem's comment about your being a "fruit" when you spoke like Aubrey Blackwell. It would never do to speak like that among his friends while pretending to be David Kirkham. It must be a consequence of Will Prescott's peculiar affinity for masks and for the Libra itself. That's what you tell Blackwell, at least, and it takes a little effort to get past Kirkham's filters so as to express the thought.

"Have you any insights into why that might be? You are inside him now, and if you know anything -- "

"No, I don't," you reply, forcing yourself to answer in Blackwell's voice and manner. "I hope we don't have to take him apart -- or that we'll break if we do. But you haven't answered my question. Are you going to leave anyone in residence?"

Lucy looks around with a frown. "I wasn't planning to, but perhaps it's for the best." She sighs, and starts to unbutton the top button of her sweater. "I suppose I'll have to briefly change, and re-charge the mask."

You would love to see her in her ripe nudity, but you stop her by seizing her wrist. "You don't have to do that. It's just overnight, the golem doesn't need to know what's been going on in the past week."

She smiles as you continue to hold her. "What a manly grip you have," she teases. You blush, and release her. "But I suppose you're right. I'll go get it."

"I'll wait outside." Your brain is buzzing with a restless lust, and you need to cool it.

So as she goes up to fetch a mask of Aubrey Blackwell, so as to leave a puppet occupying the villa and conducting the professor's business, you pace the ground outside the walls of the villa.

The air, you discover, doesn't really calm you, not when Lucy Vredenburg's SUV is sitting right there. It's here because he called her out here: He had her mask riding around on his golem, and when he decided he needed the muscle of David Kirkham to handle the boy in his secret room, he called her out, swapped Kirkham's mask onto it, and took it downstairs to hold you while he switched Chen's anima out of Prescott and replaced it with his own. Now in her guise he'll have to drive you up to the university to get Kirkham's car, and you'll be riding across town next to her with her thigh only inches from your hand --

You have to wipe your palm on your jeans. Surely Blackwell would understand if you made a pass at him while he was wearing that mask. He kidnapped and replaced Lucy because he wanted a test subject, and because he himself recognized how luscious she was. He wanted that body for himself, to play with from both inside and outside, and he has taken it out almost every weekend and had lots of fun with it by letting boys insert themselves into him. You're just another boy, an alter ego, even. He wouldn't think it strange if you touched him in that body, and pulled him close. Would he? Why, he might even think it strange if you didn't.

You flinch at the sound of a door closing. He's coming. Your pace quickens as his footsteps approach. You put your hand on the door handle of the SUV, but pause. "Was he confused," you ask as Lucy closes the gate behind her.

"No more than usual. I told him he only had to keep the house warm until tomorrow afternoon."

"Afternoon?"

"I suppose you can come out early if you want. It's Friday night, and if I'm going to be out late, I'm not going to be up early."

Jealousy erupts within you. As she passes, you dart a hand out, grabbing her by the elbow and pulling her up close. The predatory sexual instincts of David Kirkham explode, like beer out of a shaken bottle.

She gasps a little as you grip her tightly about the waist with one strong arm. You take the toothpick from your mouth and fling it away. For a moment you savor the moment -- the moment just after you've declared yourself, and just before you go in to mark your prize with lips and teeth and tongue -- then put your mouth to hers.

She grabs you by the back of the neck and head, and returns your hard kiss with an even harder one of her own.

* * * * *

Lucy screams like an enraged animal as you pound away inside her. Her nails rake your back, and her thighs squeeze you so tight you can hardly breath. It's four o'clock in the morning, and this is the third time you've done it since checking into the motel. The girl is both insatiable and inspirational. The first time you charged into her recklessly, and spent yourself so hard that you doubted you'd ever be able to fuck again. After that she sucked and kissed you all over, refreshing and lifting you into a second paroxysm as powerful as the first. You had both rested after that, but her discreet nibbles and bites had roused you, and after hovering over her and running your palms and tongue over every surface and crevice of her body you had found -- or been given -- a strength equal to the earlier frenzies. After the last thrusting discharge you almost black out, but rouse yourself enough to push and scrape more deeply into her, as though to grind your seed into her raw, soft flesh.

She growls and chews your ear, and not gently either. "And I thought college boys knew what they were doing," she mutters. "God! If I'd known -- " She alternately gnaws and gasps at you. "What seventeen-year-old -- studs could do -- with their cocks and their stamina -- " She tries swallowing your tongue. "I'd have gone to bed with one -- every night -- from the time I was a high school freshman -- until now."

"I can find you a place at the high school," you murmur. "Got lots of bitches there, not enough witches."

"Mm. Cindy, maybe? But can any of them match Lucy?"
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Love in Multiple Guises

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"There are a lot of good places at the high school for you," you murmur as you kiss Lucy all over. "I like you here, too."

"I won't give up Lucy," she groans. "She was my first, you know. But a part-time place somewhere else -- " She kisses and bites your mouth. "To be close to you? That's an idea."

Your reserves are very low by now, and you soon leave off talking, and then you leave off kissing, and then you abandon consciousness completely.

* * * * *

It's fairly late in the morning when you wake, late enough that you have to dress and go out to the front office to pay for another night so you can stay long enough to wake up.

Besides, you think you can get some work done here at the motel. You brought the Libra personae with you, and a few more useful texts. While Lucy showers, you text Kirkham's mother and explain that you spent the night out with a friend and that you'll be hanging out with him all day. She is quite angry at your staying out, and says so. You truculently reply that it happened and that's all there is to it.

So there will definitely be a downside to this high school alias business. Two of the personalities you carry inside of you are used to answering to parental figures. But your governing persona, Aubrey Blackwell, has been an adult for fifty years, and he set a stepfather on fire as a youth when he tired early of being ordered about.

Nor will you have the pleasure of Lucy's company. Her kisses are more chaste after she out of the bathroom and dried off and dressed. "I have to put in a couple of personal appearances," she tells you. "And run some errands. I'll bring you back a treat when I'm done." You stare at her ass as she walks out the door.

Then, after calming yourself with a shower, you sit down and open a couple of books.

* * * * *

You start with the Libra personae, the crown of the library that Blackwell has painstakingly put together. He had many incentives to covet it. As you have learned from experience, it can grant great power and bestow many advantages on its owner. Blackwell understands the attraction there, as well as the simple pride in his ownership of such a sought-after book -- it would be like owning the Mona Lisa.

But it was also its technique that attracted him. It uses sigil magic, an intensely powerful and arcane form of magic, the pinnacle of occult practice. Blackwell himself -- as even he has to admit -- has only a tentative handle on it, and there are very few books that explain its finer points. The Libra, by repute and by confirmation after he had examined it, is the pinnacle of the science. Its sigils were composed by the greatest practitioner of the art, and a warlock would study them with the same awe and wonder and hunger as a musician would study the fugues of J. S. Bach.

That is why Blackwell had hardly progressed into the book before the folly of a work-study student took it from him. His practice was to study the sigil of a spell intensely, to try to discern its purpose and practice before executing it. He had only just used it to make a golem when he lost it.

It has been out of his hands for many months now, and his memories of it are crude and rough, so you start at the beginning with a refresher course, studying the spell that creates a corpus; the spell that seals it with a membrana; the spell that makes a tabula mentalia; the spell that makes ferrumen to bind corpus and tabula mentalia into a persona ...

You are well pleased to see how easily it comes back to you.

* * * * *

But you're not so absorbed in your work that you forget to give some thought to the affairs at the high school. It will all be very tiresome to manage the affairs of the cretinous Gary Chen and his collection of thugs and lowlifes. Yes, there are a few advantages to be had by taking his gang in hand. He has enslaved some muscle that could be useful and plausibly criminal in some circumstances -- the septet of Adam Karter, Joshua Call, Jeff Spencer, Jamie Rennerhoff, George Mendoza, Joe Thomason, and Tanner Evans -- but that is more than counterbalanced by the tedium of managing the originals. Not even the money to be made off Chen's network of trade is an enticement, for you -- that is to say, Aubrey Blackwell -- make a comfortable living from the university position.

But there's no need to wind the gang up just yet. As you offhandedly remarked to Blackwell, the original thugs would be quite useful as subjects of experiments. The golem of Gary Chen can manage the actual drug distribution apparatus in the meantime.

Yet you still give some thought to it in the back of your mind, because you're thinking that, if you are to give yourself a semi-permanent alias within the body of a high school, it would be pleasant to give your alter ego one there as well. He would, likely, spend a great deal of time in his own form -- like you, he would hardly want to occupy himself with mundane teenage matters -- but a golem in the high school would make a nice vacation spot for him, and a nice companion for yourself.

It's the middle of the afternoon, and you're idly mulling over candidates when Kirkham's phone rings. It's Blackwell -- naturally, for from anyone else it would be a text. "Speaking," say when you pick it up.

"Have you been to the villa?" Lucy asks. "Because someone has been moving books around," she says when you answer in the negative.

"Are you out there now? What are you doing?"

"Picking up some more material for us. I've been thinking about essentia." She stops herself before she can get sidetracked with metaphysical speculations. "Most of the books have been taken off the shelves and piled onto the tables. It looks like they're being sorted."

"Not by me. I'm still at the motel, but I'm getting ready to leave."

"Oh? Where?"

"The Acheson elementary school basement. There are supplies there that can be added to our own." And because I might make a mask while I'm there. "Doesn't the professor know what's going on there? The placeholder, I mean."

"He's not here, and he left my cell behind. I suppose it's him. He sometimes gets ideas, you know. Maybe he had a brainstorm. I can't imagine it's a thief."

"Is the gwarcheidwad out?"

"Of course. Given what day it is, it will be very weak, hardly a nuisance to -- " She trails off. "Well, I'm sure it's the placeholder, as you say. I can't stay long. I've got what I need. I just wanted to know if you'd been here and moved things around."

She hangs up. And as she's successfully derailed your train of thought, you pack up too and check out of the motel. You take a cab out to the university, where you pick up David Kirkham's car and drive down to Acheson, to the elementary school.

* * * * *

As you'd half intended, you spend the balance of the afternoon making a new persona. As you work, you ponder the possible identities for your other self.

Cindy Vredenburg would be the most logical and natural. Through the character of her sister, Lucy, the professor could easily replace her. Lucy would also double as a convenient conduit when he wanted to shift from the high school to the university. The sister are very similar, though Cindy has a slightly greener quality to her body. The great disadvantage there is that Cindy has been through some turmoil recently, having been dumped by her erstwhile boyfriend, Seth Javits, when Prescott's friend Caleb Johansson moved in and moved on to Chelsea Cooper.

Speaking of Johansson, there's someone else that needs managing.

Kelsey Blankenship would be another attractive acquisition. She isn't lusciously beautiful in the manner of some of the cheerleaders, and her physical charms lean more toward the athletic and the aesthetical, for she likes to dress stylishly. She is also imperious -- a real queen bee -- which would suit Blackwell's temperament, and she is more than comfortably well off as well as academically ambitious. And your bully boys have been wanting to recruit her anyway; Blackwell could lend you a small finger of aid in that regard from her perch.

There is one very good reason not to use anyone like Cindy or Kelsey, however. They are rather obviously out of David Kirkham's league, and you would like for your high school paramour to openly acknowledge a liaison with you for as long as you will be identifying with this role. That argues for a character from a different caste.

You mull this possibility until you hit what you think is a fine inspiration: Rachel Bell. None of the minds jostling inside you know her well, and she is striking to behold rather than notably beautiful. But she is pretty enough in a non-classical way. Mostly she is notable for her brains. Unlike the AP students, who incline mostly toward the humanities and only suffer instruction in math and science, Miss Bell excels in the STEM areas, even to the point of being regarded as something of a prodigy, it is rumored. Blackwell likes brains, and -- consulting your governing anima -- you decide he would like that one as an identity.

* * * * *

When you are finished, you telephone Lucy back and talk for some time. As you'd anticipated, Blackwell is agreeable to adopting a part-time high school guise for the duration of your own time at Westside, and after the two of you talk over the possibilities, you both light on the same preferred candidate:
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: Academic Exercises

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"There are a lot of good places at the high school for you," you murmur as you kiss Lucy all over. "I like you here, too."

"I won't give up Lucy," she groans. "She was my first, you know. But a part-time place somewhere else -- " She kisses and bites your mouth. "To be close to you? That's an idea."

Your reserves are very low by now, and you soon leave off talking, and then you leave off kissing, and then you abandon consciousness completely.

* * * * *

It's fairly late in the morning when you wake, late enough that you have to dress and go out to the front office to pay for another night so you can stay long enough to wake up.

Besides, you think you can get some work done here at the motel. You brought the Libra personae with you, and a few more useful texts. While Lucy showers, you text Kirkham's mother and explain that you spent the night out with a friend and that you'll be hanging out with him all day. She is quite angry at your staying out. You truculently reply that it happened and that's all there is to it.

So there will definitely be a downside to this high school alias business. Two of the personalities you carry inside of you are used to answering to parental figures. But your governing persona, Aubrey Blackwell, has been an adult for fifty years now, and he set a stepfather on fire as a youth when he tired early of being ordered about.

Nor will you have the pleasure of Lucy's company. Her kisses are more chaste after she out of the bathroom and dried off and dressed. "I have to put in a couple of personal appearances," she tells you. "And I have to run some errands. I'll bring you back a treat when I'm done." You stare at her ass as she walks out the door.

Then, after calming yourself with a shower, you sit down and open a couple of books.

* * * * *

You start with the Libra personae, the crown of the library that Blackwell has painstakingly put together. He had many incentives to covet it. As you have learned from experience, it can grant great power and bestow many advantages on its owner. Blackwell understands the attraction there, as well as the simple pride in his ownership of such a sought-after book -- it would be like owning the Mona Lisa.

But it was also its technique that attracted him. It uses sigil magic, an intensely powerful and arcane form of magic, the pinnacle of occult practice. Blackwell himself -- as even he has to admit -- has only a tentative handle on it, and there are very few books that explain its finer points. The Libra, by repute and by confirmation after he had examined it, is the pinnacle of the science. Its sigils were composed by the greatest practitioner of the art, and a warlock would study them with the same awe and wonder and hunger as a musician would study the fugues of J. S. Bach.

That is why Blackwell had hardly progressed into the book before the folly of a work-study student took it from him. His practice was to study the sigil of a spell intensely, to try to discern its purpose and practice before executing it. He had only just used it to make a golem when he lost it.

It has been out of his hands for many months now, and his memories of it are crude and rough, so you start at the beginning with a refresher course, studying the spell that creates a corpus; the spell that seals it with a membrana; the spell that makes a tabula mentalia; the spell that makes ferrumen to bind corpus and tabula mentalia into a persona ...

You are well pleased to see how easily it comes back to you.

* * * * *

But you're not so absorbed in your work that you forget to give some thought to other, very interesting discoveries. There is, to take a very salient instance, Will Prescott.

It continues to be hard to think of yourself as him. Aubrey Blackwell's is your governing intellect, and the mentalia of David Kirkham is also very strong. Prescott's body is also hidden beneath Kirkham's corpus. Mostly, though, it is hard to think io Prescott as yourself because it is easier to think of him as an object of study and potential experimentation. It has to do with the number of golems -- of 'bots, as he thought of them -- that he created.

There are nine of them, if one counts up the various types. There is the golem that he created from the body of Gary Chen's grandfather. Then there are the eight masks that he filled with a servus mobilia: Gary Chen, Adam Karter, Tanner Evans, Joe Thomason, George Mendoza, Joshua Call, Jeff Spencer, and Jamie Rennerhoff. This is an extraordinary number of slaves.

It is extraordinary because of the material out of which they must be fashioned. The spell -- as you confirm with a close study of the sigil -- calls for the use of essentia in their preparation. That is provided by the hair -- though it could equally be the blood or saliva or even the semen -- of the one who means to enslave the golem. Essentia is a vital metaphysical principle in a human being, or in anything else, and its presence in the golem is the reason that the golem must obey the person whose essentia is used: the golem is, at bottom, an extension of that person because they share the same essentia. And as the essentia of a person must obey that person's will, so the golem (which has no will of its own) must obey its master.

But there is only so much essentia to go around. Those who have experimented with it -- and Blackwell has several books recording those experiments -- show that essentia can only be divided a small number of ways. Sometimes it can only be divided five ways; more usually six; in rare cases seven. There is no record anywhere of someone making an eightfold, or greater, distribution of their essentia.

Including the essentia still present in his body, Prescott made a ten-fold distribution.

Then there is quite remarkable luck that he had in getting past that torn page, and in deciphering the spell that makes the amphora anima. It does look like luck, and if it were anyone else who had done it, you -- that is to say, Blackwell -- would have dismissed it as such. But in light of the just-mentioned prodigy, you have to wonder.

* * * * *

In fact, you wonder so intently that you soon distract yourself entirely from the business of the sigils. None of the books you have at hand offer any guidance on the problem of Prescott and his essentia, and the frustration soon leaves you frustrated. You call Lucy's cell. "Where are you?"

"Hanging out with friends," she says in her laziest college-girl manner. "Wanna come out?"

"I'm preoccupied. I'm going to return to the villa, resume work there."

"I was going by there in a bit," she says. "I can pick up what you need and bring it out to you instead."

You hesitate. "No. I need to fetch Mr. Kirkham's automobile from the university anyway. I'll take a cab," you add when she doesn't offer to come pick you up.

"I'll see you soon, try to distract you," she giggles.

Feeling very irritated, you hang up. Of course, she was putting on the act only for the delectation of her friends, you realize. Still --

Oh, but you can't stay mad at her, not when you think of her body and what Blackwell was able to do with it last night.

You gather up your books, check out of the motel, and call a cab. It takes you to the university, where you collect Kirkham's car and drive back out to the villa.

But you don't dismount, not right away, when you pull up in front of it. Blackwell's car is gone -- the golem must be out running errands -- but there is a strange truck parked in front. For a moment you think it must be the truck that belongs to Will Prescott, for it is the same color (white) and general size and shape as his. But this one has a strange bumper sticker on the back: "I Brake for Meteors."
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Surprising Place You Find Yourself

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
"I think that these can keep for now," you say, indicating Kirkham in your turn. "There will be opportunities to conduct experiments in the future, using their delinquent companions as subjects. But we have research to conduct first."

"Indeed," Blackwell says, looking and sounding pleased.

"So I will adopt the guise of Miss Lucy tonight," you continue.

Blackwell's face falls. "I do not believe you have any right to claim priority there," he says stiffly.

"I am not claiming priority. It is merely convenient. Mr. Kirkham here needs a golem to keep him in circulation, and we have him upon one now -- "

"You know, I'm standing right here, you fuckers," Kirkham mutters.

But you ignore him. "Someone needs to play Miss Lucy. She has plans for the evening, it being Halloween, you know."

"Yes," says Blackwell. "I was rather hoping to get out of the house for that very reason."

You understand quite well what he means. On this night waxes the power of the gwachardweid, the supernatural guardian who prowls the grounds between midnight and dawn, and though the seals on the doors have always proven potent to keep it at bay in the past, the night promises to be a noisy and unnerving one.

"But I know how to earn my keep," you continue. "Before I leave I will make sketches of the new sigils that young Prescott uncovered, and study them over the weekend. We will thereby be able to cover twice as much ground in that time."

This mollified Blackwell somewhat, and after directing the Kirkham golem to return home and resume the existence of its original, he fetches the mask and clothes of Lucy Vredenburg.

* * * * *

But you don't change into her identity yet. It would be a distraction, and you should demonstrate your sincerity first by doing the promised work. You follow him into the library, to work even as he pulls down books in order to begin compiling his own notes on a related set of problems.

Blackwell himself has long since grown used to the library, but it's a distraction for you, as you have Prescott's personality as well as Blackwell's. It's a shadowy and gloomy room, even with all the lights on. The ceiling vaults high above, and three of the walls are covered with bookshelves punctuated with alcoves containing impossible stuffed creatures. Two grandfather clocks tick discordantly in a corner, and a scythe-like pendulum suspended from the ceiling slices the floor between two shelves. Your eye frequently darts over to the French doors leading onto an outside patio. The gwarcheidwad will be prowling the grounds, and you keep thinking that you see movement without. Eventually you move to a new desk and turn your back to it, though this makes you nervous for other reasons, until you finally get your mind properly into the work.

And what a joy it is to get into the work! With the Libra under one hand and a sketchpad under the other, you begin to copy in rough form the sigil that makes the servus mobilia, the paste that goes inside a mask to make a golem.

The sketching of such sigils is often a tedious business. Sigils are the most difficult magic known to man. They are devices for creating and executing spells of astonishing complexity and power, and the secret of their construction and use is known to very few. You -- or, that is to say, Blackwell -- has only a shaky grasp on them, and depends upon a book compiled by the nineteenth century antiquarian/alchemist George Abney to understand them.

That was one of the main reasons he was so eager and so pleased to acquire the Libra. Aside from the power it grants in the form of masks -- and the egotistical satisfaction that Blackwell got from owning this most rare and valuable grimoire -- were the sigils it contained, which were reputed to be the most masterful expression of the art and science of sigil-writing perhaps in the history of all the magical sciences. It would be like acquiring the sketchbooks of Leonardo da Vinci, or the notebooks of Johann Sebastian Bach -- a chance to study the mind and product of a genius of the first rank. Greedily he had begun to take them apart, only to find that they were of such complexity that he could only sketch them incompletely. It is your aim to prepare similar sketches of the servus mobilia sigil, and perhaps another, before the approach of midnight forces you to suspend your labors.

Or, it was your aim. To your astonishment, you find that the job of sketching the sigil proceeds far more rapidly and with far more detail than your original had ever managed. Each element of the sigil forms quickly and perfectly under your pen, and your hand fairly flies as you only have to glance between the original sigil and the sketch pad for it to register in your mind and form on the paper. You are able to complete that most complicated sigil while still in the freshness of your labor.

Back you go to the sigil before that, the one that encloses a living body inside the shell of a golem. That one too you finish quickly, and also the one that makes a golem. Blackwell completed sketches of the others, but you complete your own copies of them, reproducing them in perfect detail.

Blackwell himself comes over to watch you work, and mutters in startled surprise. "Quite remarkable," he says. "And how are you managing this?"

"I really don't know. It may be a synergy between the anima and this body, or it might be -- " You break off.

"Or it might be Mr. Prescott," Blackwell finishes for you. "I am really beginning to suspect he is a remarkable find."

"Have you found anything yet that could shed light on him?"

"No. I would encourage you to lay this aside and help me, but the progress you are making -- " He shakes his head and returns to his own work.

It is the matter of Prescott's -- that is to say, your -- essentia that has piqued his interest, and yours. One of the things he noticed while consulting the contents of the personas he made of you and of Kirkham was the quite extraordinary number of masks and golems that you were able to put under your control. In crafting golems, or the masks that contain golems as a shell on the inner surface, the mask-maker must put part of himself into them. That is why they must obey him -- they are extensions of him. But there is only so much of a man -- of his essentia -- to spread around. To the best of Blackwell's knowledge, it is possible only to spread oneself into five or six or seven places. But you put your essentia in ten different places, if one is to include your original body.

That was the main reason Blackwell was willing to insert his anima into you, and thereby make you a partner of sorts. Anyone else he would have imprisoned and eventually killed, but he saw that you had at least one gift that he wished to study.

And now you have followed that prodigy with this prodigy, that of being able to sketch the complex sigils with amazing speed, accuracy, detail, and dexterity.

* * * * *

So you are yourself quite excited when you straighten up a little after eleven, pop a crick from your neck, and declare that you have finished copying the sigils. Blackwell studies them closely and with a little pallor in his face. "And what of this one," he asks, pulling out a loose sheet of paper that was tucked into the Libra.

It's the paper with the sigil for the amphora anima -- the one that formed when you laid a blank piece of paper atop the spell under the light of the full moon. The spell itself consists simply of a lot of words arranged in nonsense patterns. You tell him it doesn't make any sense to you. "And this one?" He turns to the last unlocked spell, the one containing sigils inside of sigils. "I'd have to study it," you tell him.

"Take it with you," he tells you quietly. "The Libra and the Abney." You're startled at his suggestion that you take this book, recovered so luckily and unexpectedly, from the safety of the house, and you tell him so. "Yes, but you are making such remarkable progress. I don't want to see you stop."

"You just don't want me to go out and party," you retort. He gives you a pinched look, and starts to take the grimoire from you. But you pull it back. "You're right, I'm on a roll. I'll talk to you tomorrow. Or perhaps Sunday. It depends on how far I get before I'm exhausted." You gather up the Libra, the Abney, a few more books that might be of technical help, Lucy Vredenburg's mask and clothes, and leave him.

Your desire to get into the body of Lucy Vredenburg notwithstanding, you are actually more interested in continuing the research that has so excited you. And since the house that she shares with her parents and sister would be a distraction, you leave the costume off and drive her SUV to the Layzee-Nites Motel on Highway 126 on the southern part of town. It's a cheap and rundown dump, but you're able to pay for a room using the cash in Lucy's billfold without putting a debt on her credit card. You set up a study area in the room, and are soon utterly absorbed in your work. You ignore the texts that come in on Lucy's cell, and don't go to bed until nearly four.

* * * * *

The next morning you go to the office and add another day to your stay. Aside from three food runs, you don't set foot outside the room. The excitement you feel at your progress leaves you almost ill.

Sunday passes as Saturday did. You wonder a little at not hearing from Blackwell.

Monday comes, as do newspaper headlines describing the fire that destroyed Blackwell's villa on Sunday.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Prodigies of Will Prescott

choices: Keep quiet -- bad things will happen soon enough
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
Time passes very slowly, and you restlessly pace the narrow confines of your cell. Several times you come close to making an attempt on the window, and once you even heft the chamber pot in your hands, gauging whether if thrown with enough force it would shatter the window. But each time the thought of the danger -- of the glass that would be stuck in the panes and which would litter the ground -- dissuades you.

* * * * *

All light has failed, plunging the room into a gloomy darkness, when you hear a click high above, and you crane your neck to look up at that landing. A crack of light appears, and then a rectangle. A silhouette looms within it, and then a figure is gazing down at you. "Prescott," it hisses in a soft and menacing voice.

It's the voice of your best friend, David Kirkham.

But it won't be Kirkham. A chill of horror runs through you, fighting a hot flare of anger. "Who the fuck are you?" you yell back at it.

It doesn't answer before stepping aside as a larger figure enters. You can't make out its features, but the general shape suggests it's the professor. The silhouettes move away from the door, and the metal staircase groans. You retreat into a corner and hunch, your fists balled up. You're naked and in a shit body, but you think you can make a fair account of yourself before you're overwhelmed. Kirkham will be the deadly one, but the professor with his great gut can probably make his weight felt. If you can dodge them and get up that staircase --

But then you remember that thing on the middle rung.

A circle of light flickers on, stabbing you in the eyes. It takes you by surprise, so that you're not prepared for the rush. Hard, strong limbs close around you, and you're thrust into the middle of the room. You kick and twist viciously, and bang your head against something yielding. Then an arm wraps about your head, locking it in place while a hand goes over your forehead. You grit your teeth and struggle to pull away, but then something yanks at your brain.

It's like having your guts torn out.

* * * * *

You wake with the feeling of having been through an earthquake. You are groggy, and sick, and shaking all over. For awhile all you can do is lay in a pile and shudder and shiver all over.

You eventually feel well enough to move; your bare skin scrapes across a cold, rough floor. Ugh. Concrete. You open an eye and sit up. It's very dark. High up in the wall, where that window would be, the darkness is a little paler, but it casts no light into the room.

You feel about, and find the cot. A thick quilt has been dropped onto it, and you wrap yourself inside it and lay down. A depression, cold and nauseating, fills you.

How did you get in this mess? You found a book of magic tricks, and started fooling around with it with Caleb. You made some masks of each other, had some fun.

Then you lipped off to Chelsea Cooper. She sent her goon boyfriend after you. Caleb copied him into a mask, talked you into trapping Gordon inside a mask of yourself. From there is was just one pratfall after another, until you wound up playing the part of Gary Chen --

Whoa. You bolt upright, then lean over hard to keep from vomiting.

Chen!

He's gone.

All his memories and all his personality -- that angry, confident, strutting, seething personality -- have vanished. You don't even have the fake personality that you wore while wearing his mask.

You are yourself again.

You are Will Prescott.

You loose one or two dry heaves at the floor, but nothing comes out. You snuggle back inside the quilt and shake all over with fear, wonder and confusion.

What the fuck happened to you the other night, after you put that band onto your forehead? It was like --

No, it wasn't just "like," it was actual fact, for you remember the thoughts of, the conviction of, the enraged horror of Gary Chen flowing out of that band and into your mind, displacing your soul with his.

You were possessed by the spirit of that psychotic bully!

And now he's gone? Gingerly you feel at your forehead, and pull. You recite mystic words and grapple with your own brow. Nothing comes away. You fall onto your back, a chill sweat trickling over you. It's like a fever has broken, leaving you weak and wrung out.

Yes. He's gone. You remember everything you did as him, you remember even the thoughts and motives and desires and impulses as though they were your own. But they are gone now.

You laugh weakly in relief. If the professor ripped Chen out of you, then he did you one favor at least.

So maybe he's not all bad?

Except for the fact that he's imprisoned you.

Still, the realization that Chen is gone warms you, and the warmth and relief and the exhaustion of a very busy day send you into a slumber.

* * * * *

You wake with a start to the impression of an explosion. A dying echo is ringing through your heads and you stagger upright, grasping at consciousness. Was it a dream?

No, you hear voices and running feet above you. Something falls over with a great bang, and there's a rumble like falling crockery.

You sit up. Very pale daylight is showing in the window, and you hop across the icy concrete floor to peer up through it. You seen nothing but a soft blue sky. Cautiously you take a chance climbing the stairs, but even before you reach the fatal rung you feel its presence, and slink back down. After that flurry of noise upstairs, a silence has fallen. You wrap yourself again in the quilt, sit on the cot, and listen.

Nothing happens for a very long time. Then ... voices? You strain your ears, but can't be sure.

Then a much longer silence.

* * * * *

You jerk awake. How long were you asleep? The light in the window is a lot stronger.

You yawn and shake yourself awake. You stretch. You wait.

And you wait and you wait.

You try napping, and maybe you fall asleep, but you're not sure.

Then you hear muffled sounds from above. Thumps and thuds.

You listen some more.

An impatience borne of boredom nearly overwhelms you. It's impossible that your captor would have forgotten about you, and you're pretty sure you'll hate it when he starts paying serious attention to you. But maybe it would be worth it to annoy him a little by yelling.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: An Imprisonment, And a Release

1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.content:
It would be possible, but not at all entertaining, to describe the tedium that follows. Suffice it to say that you ignore the muffled noises from upstairs until they cease late in the afternoon.

Neither the professor nor Kirkham ever come down again. You relieve yourself in the chamber pot, and try to forget the hunger that gnaws at you. You give some thought anew to breaking through that window, but can never nerve yourself to try, no more than you could yesterday, when you at least had Chen's guts to help you.

Night comes, and you sleep. The next day you wake with a very empty feeling. Nothing happens, and you hear no noises. The day passes with no relief. Night comes again.

It is a sleepless night. You weep several times out of fear and self-pity.

Morning comes. You spend it in a daze.

Relief of a sort finally comes when with a great cracking and rumbling the roof caves in. You are in terrible pain for some seconds, and that is all.
1112111311222221222222122121211212112211.title: The Fate of Foolish Little Boys

11123.content:
Caleb's eyes nearly bulge from his head. "Are you crazy? We can't try these things on ourselves!"

"Yeah, that probably would be kind of dumb," you admit. "Okay. Do you know Jeremy's phone number?"

He slaps your chest and takes out his phone. "That's the spirit.".

"But I want that other mask to use on Geoff," you continue. "You know, if this one works on Jeremy." But Caleb is already dialing.

You take up the other mask and start polishing it while Caleb talks to your old friend. It doesn't sound like a happy conversation: Caleb has to do some serious cajoling and make some pretty lame sounding promises about the "cool stuff" you and he have found inside the old elementary school basement. But with a curt "Great, see you in a few," Caleb drops his phone and winks at you. "We better get this place straightened up. You know, make it look like we haven't been camping out here."

You're more bothered by the lack of a plan. "So how do we get him to try on the mask?" you ask as you help rearrange and hide the stuff that you've hauled over from your house.

"Oh, I dunno," Caleb says as he moves some tables around. "We'll just show it to him and suggest he try it on."

"Why would he do that?"

"Well, why wouldn't he?"

"Would you, if he showed you a strange mask?"

"Sure. Maybe. I dunno."

"You don't know," you repeat. "This is going to be a disaster."

"Well, it was your idea."

"No, it was your idea. My idea was—."

"Okay, so how were you planning on getting Geoff to try on a mask?"

"I told you, I was going to sneak up on him. We can't sneak up on Jeremy. The guy's too big."

"Oh, just give it here." Caleb grabs the mask. He looks around, then wedges it in a corner against the wall. "There. We'll pretend to find it and then dare him to try it on."

You look down at the second mask, the one you've been polishing. A sudden urge comes over you. "I wonder what happens if you try on mask that isn't fully polished yet."

"Well, for fuck's sake don't try it out now," Caleb exclaims. But it's too late. You've already pressed it to your face.

Nothing happens, except that you get a brief, strong sense of claustrophobia. Caleb is giving you a look of alarm when you lower the mask. "What happened?" you ask.

"Nothing. But it was a stupid thing to do."

"Oh, I don't know," you say, having a sudden thought. "It might be useful having a dummy mask around." You drop it onto a table not far from the finished mask.

* * * * *

You're worried that you'll still be cleaning up when Jeremy arrives, but it's almost an hour before he does. He doesn't look very pleased as he's unbending himself and exiting his car. "So what's this awesome hiding hole you promised to show me?" Neither you nor Caleb are particularly short, but Jeremy is nearly six and half feet tall, and he seems to tower over you.

"Nice to see you too, Richards," you say. "You getting enough oxygen up there?"

"Oh, for fuck's sake," he snorts. Caleb nudges you.

"It's over this way," Caleb says, leading you and Jeremy over to the basement door. Richards has to duck to get inside. As you look around, you can't help but guess that he is deeply unimpressed.

"It's a junk heap," he snorts as he glances around the dark, dusty basement.

"Well, yeah," Caleb admits. "But it's got all kind of hiding places and little mazes in it. I think it's cool."

"It's cool if you're a rat," he growls, and remains in the doorway.

"But a lot of this stuff is, like, really vintage furniture. How old is this school, Will?"

"I think it was built in the 1920s."

"Yeah, and a lot of it's in pretty good shape," Caleb continues. "I can't believe they're just sitting on it."

"Because even they know it's just a lot of junk," Jeremy retorts. He looks over his shoulder; clearly he just wants to leave.

"We're not sure, but we think there's an old pool table back behind this stuff," Caleb says. "Help us move some of it, will ya?"

Jeremy groans, but starts helping you pull desks and cabinets away. It is actually a corner you've not explored, and you've no idea what Caleb is talking about. But after a few minutes clearing stuff away you do find something—but it's just an old conference table.

"Oh. Well," Caleb says, pretending to hide disappointment. "Still, it would have been neat if it was a pool table. We could have had our own private pool hall down here."

"It's a stupid place for games," Jeremy snorts.

"Depends on what kind of games," you say, remembering last year's Halloween. "We could turn it into a haunted house. Oh, cool, check it out." You flick on a flashlight and turn its beam onto the masks that you and Caleb had set up earlier. You hurry over and snatch up the "dummy" mask. "This must be some old drama club stuff." You press it to your face. "Do I look like the guy in Scream?"

"You look like a dork," Jeremy says, but he's followed you over. "Lemme see."

"Get your own," you reply, tilting your chin at the other mask. "Maybe you could take it and use it as part of a Halloween costume."

"Oh, say, that's an idea," Caleb exclaims as Jeremy cautiously picks up the other mask. "Get yourself some black rags and drape yourself all over, and then wear that. You could be like the Grim Reaper."

"Try it on," you urge Jeremy. You feel your heart beating in your chest.

He looks at you and at Caleb and at the mask, then puts it close to his face. But he doesn't hold it all the way up. "There aren't any eyeholes," he points out as he lowers it again.

"So make some," says Caleb. "Try it on, see how it fits. If it fits, then you can take it and punch some holes in it."

Jeremy raises it back to his face. For a brief moment he holds it there. And then his hand falls to his side.

"Look out!" Caleb shouts as Jeremy sways on his feet. "He's going over!" You and Caleb grab him as he falls, but he's a dead weight, and the two of you crash to the ground under him.

"Shit, get him off me!" you yell. His knee, or something, is jammed in your stomach. Caleb kicks and pulls and jabs you in the side of the head. "Watch it!"

"Okay, I'm out. Fuck, where's the mask?"

"Never the mind the mask! Just get him— Oof!" Jeremy has flopped over senselessly and knocked the wind out of you. You pull back, banging your head on a desk, but finally worm out from under him. You're catching your breath while Caleb shines his flashlight over the ground.

"The mask! It's gone!" he shouts. You look around. Jeremy is lying on his back, dead to the world it appears, and the mask has fallen from his face. You scramble around, looking for it.

"Get him up. Maybe he fell on it."

"Shit." Caleb helps you drag Jeremy into the middle of the floor. While you feel under him, to make sure the mask hasn't been dragged along under his body, Caleb scours the floor near where he fell. Both of your searches turn up nothing.

"Fuck, this is bad," you say as you stare at each other.

"What's up with him? Is he breathing?"

You check. Jeremy's eyes are open, and he is staring into nothingness, and his breathing is shallow. But appears to be alive.

"Is he in some kind of trance?" Caleb wonders. "Maybe it's part of the magic?"

"I don't know. Shit. Why couldn't the book have said something about what to expect?"

"Probably because the guy who wrote the book knew exactly what to expect," Caleb says. "He wasn't interested in talking to amateurs."

"Fuck," you repeat. "I guess we wait and see if anything happens."

Luckily for all, you don't have to wait long. You're glancing around, looking to see if there is a hand truck or something else you can use to get Jeremy out of the basement if the worst comes to pass, when you feel Caleb grip your arm. You turn around, just in time to see a glow fade from Jeremy's face, and to see the mask materialize there. You stare at it a moment, then snatch it up.

It appears to be unharmed, but you're actually more worried about Richards, so you lay it aside and bend over him. He's breathing more deeply now, and you shake him. With a groan, he opens his eyes.

"Are you alright?" Caleb asks.

"What the— What happened?" He sounds groggy.

"You passed out. You put that mask on, and then you fell over like a stack of bricks."

He sits up and rubs his head, then puts his face between his knees. "I feel sick."

"Let's get you out in the air."

He waves you off and stands on his own, though he sways. With increasing confidence, though, he strides over to the basement door. Once outside, he kneels down with his head very nearly at his ankles. You watch him in concern.

"Thanks for a fucking lousy waste of time, assholes," he suddenly snarls as he gets up. His face is beet red. "Next time you want to prank someone, find someone else." For a moment you think he's going to punch you, but he just wheels and stalks back to his car. You watch for a few moments after he's driven away.

"What a dick," Caleb mutters. Then he brightens. "Come on, let's see what we got!"
11123.title: A Friendship is Renewed, Sort Of

choices: Insist on returning to the theater
111231.content:
"Thanks for a fucking lousy waste of time, assholes," you snarl down at Caleb.

He laughs. "You sound just like him, too!"

You look back into the mirror, which you'd found back behind some junk in another corner of the school basement. It's Jeremy Richards—or a flawless duplicate—who looks back at you: messy brown hair; droopy, stoner eyes; and toned arms and chest. You'd had to win four rounds of rock-paper-scissors (after Caleb insisted on a best of two out of three and then a best of three out of five) for the right to have this reflection, for a little while at least. "I wonder if I'm as strong as him," you wonder.

Caleb motions you over to some kind of cast-iron frame. You doubt you could have moved it before; it's still a strain, but you lift it more cleanly and easily than Caleb can when he gives it a shot. "I'd say that settles it," he says. "I don't think his own mother could tell the difference."

"God, I hope his own mother doesn't know what he looks like naked," you reply. "Actually, I'm not thrilled to know it either."

You are, of course, absolutely starkers, having discarded your own clothes before pressing the mask to your face. It took you awhile to get even that far. When you and Caleb dove back into the basement you'd found the mask undamaged but subtly altered: its glimmering surface now showed in its depths a pale but recognizable face, that of Jeremy Richards. You'd then used the altered mask to open another page in the spell book, where you'd found more information about the mask and its uses, as well as the start of another spell. As it happens, it was necessary to master that other spell before you could use the mask. But it was a fairly simple spell that made a sealant that could be applied to the inside of the mask, and it required only the materials you had on hand. Then it was a contest between you and Caleb to see who would get to try on the Jeremy disguise first. It knocked you cold when you put it on, but Caleb woke you with a couple of hard slaps to the face, and you sat up to find yourself nearly six inches taller than when you woke this morning.

"Okay, let me give it a try now," Caleb says.

"Wait your turn, I just got it on." You fondle your new balls. Disguise or not, everything is as sensitive and responsive as it should be.

"Dude, you're not going to jack off, are you?"

"Of course not! Well, not unless you think it would be a good idea to check that everything works."

"Look, that's your business." He backs away. "Just don't ask me to help."

You do a little tentative jumping and fake free throwing. "I suppose it would be too much to hope that the mask copies skills. It doesn't copy memories." You fall back onto your feet awkwardly. "No, I guess not." You bend over and scratch your new calf thoughtfully.

"So we won't be playing basketball as him. I can live without that."

"Yeah, but what will we be doing?" You put your hand to your nose and temple in the prescribed gesture, mutter the prescribed, odd-sounding words, and pull—

* * * * *

—and wake again with a stinging sensation in your face. "Jesus," Caleb says. He's leaning over you, and you sit up on an elbow to find yourself sprawled on the floor. "Okay that's something to remember. Lay down before you put one of those things on or take it off. You almost crushed me when you fell over."

But that's the extent of his sympathies, and as you sit up with a groan he's already tearing off his own clothes. Then he lays down and drops the mask onto his face.

The change is almost anticlimactic: One moment it's Caleb laying there, the next it's Jeremy. You slap him awake, as he'd done to you.

"So what do we do with this thing?" you ask again when Caleb is checking himself out in the mirror.

"Huh? Oh. Well, we were talking about using a Jeremy disguise to get close to Javits to copy him, right?"

"Yeah, but then what would we do with a Seth Javits disguise?"

"Get close to his girlfriend. Dur."

"Yeah, but how do we make sure the real people don't interfere? Caleb!"

"What?" He turns with a frown to stare down at you with one of Jeremy's dark, truculent sneers. It makes you blink, remembering when Jeremy was a skinny middle-schooler like yourself, and you wonder how come people who start out so similar can turn out so different.

"Suppose you get yourself all done up as Seth and go over to Cindy's," you explain to him. "What happens if the real Seth calls, or actually shows up at her house?"

"Then I guess you can play Jeremy and keep Seth away."

"And who keeps Jeremy away from us?"

"Will you stop worrying about these things?" He turns back to the mirror. "We'll figure out something."

You bite your lip. "Anyway, we can't use the second mask on Javits."

He looks down at your reflection. "How come?"

"Well, you wanted to use one of the masks on Jeremy, and we did. I want to use the other one on Geoff Mansfield."

He whips around. "Oh, dude, come on! When you suggested Jeremy I thought you were giving up on Mansfield and the whole Lisa thing!"

"No, I went with Jeremy because you wanted to try it on him. And because I wanted to see if it would work first before trying it tomorrow on Geoff."

Caleb takes your insistence with bad grace—"You're gonna fuck it up for us!"—but you are adamant. At least he doesn't fight when you snatch up the blank mask and take it home with you.

* * * * *

The next day, after church, you call Lisa to see if you can pick her up for the movie, but she tells you Geoff will be having that pleasure.
